  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "She was so occupied with admiring those gentle blue eyes, engaging in conversation, and constructing these various plans in the intervals, that the night passed with unusual speed; the supper-table, which invariably concluded such gatherings and for which she was accustomed to monitor the proper hour, was fully arranged and positioned near the hearth before she realized it. With a promptness exceeding the usual motivation of a spirit that was nonetheless never unconcerned with the reputation of performing tasks skillfully and considerately, and with the genuine enthusiasm of a mind pleased by its own concepts, she proceeded to preside over the meal, offering and suggesting the minced chicken and scalloped oysters with a persistence she understood would be welcomed by the early habits and polite hesitations of their visitors.

During such events, poor Mr. Woodhouse’s sentiments were in a state of painful conflict. He enjoyed having the table set, as it had been the custom of his younger days, yet his belief that suppers were highly unhealthy made him somewhat regretful to see anything placed upon it; while his sense of hospitality would have invited his guests to partake of everything, his concern for their well-being caused him to lament that they would consume it.

Only another small bowl of light gruel like his own was something he could, with complete personal approval, suggest; although he might force himself, while the ladies were contentedly finishing the more appetizing dishes, to remark:

“Mrs. Bates, allow me to suggest you try one of these eggs. An egg boiled quite soft is not unhealthy. Serle understands how to boil an egg better than anyone. I would not suggest an egg boiled by anyone else; but you need not worry, they are quite small, you see—one of our small eggs will not harm you. Miss Bates, let Emma serve you a little bit of tart—a very little bit. Ours are all apple-tarts. You need not fear unhealthy preserves here. I do not suggest the custard. Mrs. Goddard, what do you think of half a glass of wine? A small half-glass, mixed into a tumbler of water? I do not believe it could disagree with you.”

Emma permitted her father to speak—but provided for her guests in a far more pleasing manner, and on this particular evening took special delight in ensuring they departed content. The joy of Miss Smith was entirely consistent with her expectations. Miss Woodhouse was such a significant figure in Highbury that the anticipation of the meeting had caused as much anxiety as excitement; yet the modest, appreciative young woman left with deeply satisfied feelings, thrilled by the kindness with which Miss Woodhouse had interacted with her throughout the evening, and having actually shaken hands with her at the conclusion!


CHAPTER IV


Harriet Smith’s friendship at Hartfield quickly became an established fact. Prompt and resolute in her methods, Emma wasted no time in inviting, urging, and instructing her to visit frequently; and as their friendship deepened, so did their mutual contentment. As a companion for walking, Emma had recognized quite early how beneficial she might prove. In that regard, the absence of Mrs. Weston had been significant. Her father never ventured past the shrubbery, where two sections of the grounds were enough for his long stroll, or his short one, depending on the season; and since Mrs."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_002",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The inventory she compiled when merely fourteen—I recall believing it reflected so well upon her discernment that I kept it for a while; and I suspect she might have produced a quite respectable list at present. Yet, I have ceased anticipating any consistent reading habits from Emma. She will never yield to anything demanding diligence, perseverance, and the subordination of imagination to intellect. Where Miss Taylor failed to encourage her, I can confidently assert that Harriet Smith will accomplish nothing.—You were never able to convince her to read even half as much as you desired.—You are aware you could not.”

“I suspect,” Mrs. Weston answered with a smile, “that I believed so then;—but since we have separated, I cannot recall Emma ever neglecting to perform anything I requested.”

“One hardly wishes to improve upon a memory such as that,” Mr. Knightley remarked with emotion; and for a brief interval, he remained silent. “But I,” he presently continued, “having experienced no such enchantment cast upon my perceptions, must continue to observe, listen, and recollect. Emma is ruined by being the most intelligent of her household. By age ten, she possessed the unfortunate ability to resolve inquiries that baffled her sister at seventeen. She was consistently rapid and confident: Isabella, sluggish and hesitant. And from the time she was twelve, Emma has acted as mistress of the residence and of you all. In her mother, she lost the sole individual capable of managing her. She possesses her mother’s gifts and must have been under her authority.”

“I would have been distressed, Mr. Knightley, to rely upon your endorsement, had I departed Mr. Woodhouse’s household and required another position; I do not believe you would have offered a favorable recommendation for me to anyone. I am certain you always considered me unsuitable for the role I occupied.”

“Indeed,” he replied, smiling. “You are better situated here; highly qualified to be a wife, but entirely unsuited for a governess. Yet you were training yourself to be a superb wife throughout your tenure at Hartfield. You may not have provided Emma with the comprehensive education your abilities seemed to suggest; however, you were acquiring a very sound education from her regarding the essential marital duty of yielding your own desires and obeying instructions; and had Weston requested that I suggest a wife for him, I certainly would have proposed Miss Taylor.”

“I thank you. There will be very little virtue in being a good wife to a man such as Mr. Weston.”

“Well, to be candid, I fear you are somewhat wasted, and that despite every inclination to endure, there will be nothing requiring endurance. We shall not lose hope, however. Weston might become irritable from an excess of ease, or his son might annoy him.”

“I trust not that.—It is improbable. No, Mr. Knightley, do not predict trouble from that direction.”

“Certainly not I. I merely mention possibilities. I do not claim Emma’s talent for predicting and speculating. I hope, with all my spirit, the young man may possess a Weston’s character and a Churchill’s wealth.—But Harriet Smith—I have not nearly finished regarding Harriet Smith. I consider her the absolute worst variety of associate that Emma could possibly select.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_002",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The list she created when she was only fourteen—I remember thinking it showed such good judgment that I kept it for a while; and I imagine she could put together a very good list now. But I have given up expecting any consistent reading habits from Emma. She will never commit to anything that requires hard work, patience, and the discipline of logic over imagination. If Miss Taylor failed to motivate her, I can safely say that Harriet Smith will accomplish nothing. You could never convince her to read half as much as you wanted. You know that is true.

“I imagine,” Mrs. Weston replied with a smile, “that I thought so then; but since we have parted, I cannot recall a single time Emma failed to do anything I asked.”

“It is hard to argue with a memory like that,” Mr. Knightley said with feeling, and for a moment or two he fell silent. “But I,” he soon added, “who have not been under such a spell, must still see, hear, and remember. Emma is spoiled because she is the smartest in her family. At ten years old, she had the misfortune of being able to answer questions that baffled her sister at seventeen. She was always quick and confident; Isabella was slow and shy. Ever since she was twelve, Emma has been the mistress of the house and of all of you. In her mother, she lost the only person capable of managing her. She inherited her mother’s talents and must have been kept in check by her.”

“I would have been sorry, Mr. Knightley, to rely on your recommendation if I had left Mr. Woodhouse’s family and needed another position; I do not think you would have put in a good word for me to anyone. I am sure you always thought I was unfit for the job I held.”

“Yes,” he said, smiling. “You are better placed here; very well-suited to be a wife, but not at all for a governess. But you were preparing yourself to be an excellent wife the entire time you were at Hartfield. You might not have given Emma the complete education your abilities seemed to promise, but you were receiving a very good education from her on the essential marital skill of yielding your own will and doing as you were told; and if Weston had asked me to recommend him a wife, I certainly would have named Miss Taylor.”

“Thank you. There will be very little challenge in being a good wife to a man like Mr. Weston.”

“Well, to be honest, I am afraid you are a bit wasted, and that despite your willingness to endure, there will be nothing to endure. We will not lose hope, however. Weston might become irritable from sheer comfort, or his son might annoy him.”

“I hope not that. It is not likely. No, Mr. Knightley, do not predict trouble from that direction.”

“Not I, indeed. I am only mentioning possibilities. I do not claim to have Emma’s talent for predicting and guessing. I hope, with all my heart, the young man may be a Weston in character and a Churchill in wealth. But Harriet Smith—I am not half finished talking about Harriet Smith. I think she is the absolute worst kind of companion Emma could possibly have.”"
}

“To tell the truth, I am afraid you are a bit wasted, and that despite your willingness to endure difficulties, there will be nothing to endure. We will not lose hope, however. Mr. Weston might become grumpy from having too much comfort, or his son might annoy him.”

“I hope not. That is not likely. No, Mr. Knightley, do not predict trouble from that direction.”

“I certainly will not. I am only mentioning possibilities. I do not claim to have Emma’s talent for predicting and guessing. I hope, with all my heart, that the young man has the character of a Weston and the fortune of a Churchill. But Harriet Smith—I have not finished talking about her. I think she is the absolute worst kind of companion Emma could possibly have.”"
}

“It is hard to argue with a memory like that,” Mr. Knightley said with feeling, and he paused for a moment. “But I,” he soon added, “who have not been charmed in that way, must still see, hear, and remember things as they are. Emma has been spoiled by being the smartest person in her family. At ten years old, she had the misfortune of being able to answer questions that baffled her sister at seventeen. She was always quick and confident, while Isabella was slow and shy. Ever since she was twelve, Emma has been in charge of the house and all of you. When her mother died, she lost the only person who could handle her. She inherited her mother’s talents, and she would have been under her mother’s control.”

“Mr. Knightley, I would have been sorry to rely on your recommendation if I had left Mr. Woodhouse’s family and needed a new job. I do not think you would have said a good word about me to anyone. I am sure you always thought I was unfit for the position I held.”

“Yes,” he said, smiling. “You are better placed here; you are very well-suited to be a wife, but not at all for a governess. Yet, all the time you were at Hartfield, you were preparing yourself to be an excellent wife. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_003",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "You do not require any encouragement to compose a response that conveys regret for his misfortune.”

“You believe I should reject him, then,” Harriet remarked, casting her eyes downward.

“Should reject him! My dear Harriet, what are you implying? Are you truly uncertain about that? I assumed—but I apologize, perhaps I have been mistaken. I have certainly misinterpreted your position if you are questioning the intent of your reply. I had supposed you were merely seeking my guidance on how to phrase it.”

Harriet remained quiet. With a slight air of detachment, Emma persisted:

“I gather you intend to provide an affirmative response.”

“No, I do not; that is, I do not mean—What should I do? What would you suggest? Please, dear Miss Woodhouse, tell me what I ought to do.”

“I will offer you no counsel, Harriet. I refuse to be involved. This is a matter you must resolve according to your own emotions.”

“I had no idea he was so fond of me,” Harriet noted, studying the letter. For a brief period, Emma maintained her silence; however, fearing the seductive flattery of that note might prove too persuasive, she decided it was necessary to speak:

“I maintain it as a fundamental principle, Harriet, that if a woman questions whether she should accept a suitor, she ought to decline him. If she can waver regarding a ‘Yes,’ she should respond with a ‘No’ immediately. It is not a commitment to be undertaken with reservations or a divided heart. I felt it my obligation as a friend, and as your senior, to share this with you. But do not think I wish to sway your decision.”

“Oh! no, I am certain you are far too generous to—but if you would only suggest what is best—No, no, I do not mean that—As you say, one’s mind should be entirely settled—One should not be indecisive—It is a grave matter.—It might be safer to say ‘No,’ perhaps.—Do you think I should say ‘No?’”

“Not for anything,” Emma replied with a pleasant smile, “would I advise you one way or the other. You must be the ultimate judge of your own contentment. If you favor Mr. Martin above all others; if you consider him the most charming man you have ever encountered, why should you hesitate? You are blushing, Harriet.—Does anyone else come to mind when you consider that description? Harriet, Harriet, do not mislead yourself; do not let gratitude and pity carry you away. Of whom are you thinking right now?”

The signs were promising.—Instead of replying, Harriet turned aside in confusion and stood pensive by the hearth; and although she still held the letter, she twisted it absentmindedly without notice. Emma awaited the outcome with eagerness, yet not without significant optimism. Finally, with some reluctance, Harriet stated—

“Miss Woodhouse, since you will not provide your perspective, I must manage as best I can on my own; and I have now fully decided, and truly almost resolved—to refuse Mr. Martin.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_003",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "You don't need to be prompted to write with the appearance of sorrow for his disappointment.”

“You think I ought to refuse him then,” said Harriet, looking down.

“Ought to refuse him! My dear Harriet, what do you mean? Are you in any doubt about that? I thought—but I beg your pardon, perhaps I have been mistaken. I have certainly misunderstood you if you feel uncertain about the intent of your answer. I had imagined you were only consulting me on how to phrase it.”

Harriet was silent. With a slightly reserved manner, Emma continued:

“I gather you intend to give him a favorable answer.”

“No, I do not; that is, I do not mean—What shall I do? What would you advise me to do? Please, dear Miss Woodhouse, tell me what I ought to do.”

“I will not give you any advice, Harriet. I will have nothing to do with it. This is a matter you must settle with your own feelings.”

“I had no idea he liked me so very much,” said Harriet, contemplating the letter. For a little while Emma remained silent, but fearing the seductive flattery of that letter might be too powerful, she thought it best to say,

“I consider it a general rule, Harriet, that if a woman doubts whether she should accept a man or not, she certainly ought to refuse him. If she can hesitate about saying ‘yes,’ she ought to say ‘no’ immediately. It is not a situation to be entered into safely with doubtful feelings or half a heart. I thought it my duty as a friend, and as someone older than you, to say this much. But do not imagine that I want to influence you.”

“Oh! no, I am sure you are much too kind to—but if you would just advise me what I had best do—No, no, I do not mean that—As you say, one’s mind ought to be quite made up—One should not be hesitating—It is a very serious thing.—It will be safer to say ‘no,’ perhaps.—Do you think I had better say ‘no?’”

“Not for the world,” said Emma, smiling graciously, “would I advise you either way. You must be the best judge of your own happiness. If you prefer Mr. Martin to every other person; if you think him the most agreeable man you have ever been with, why should you hesitate? You are blushing, Harriet.—Does anyone else come to mind at this moment under that description? Harriet, Harriet, do not deceive yourself; do not be carried away by gratitude and compassion. At this moment, who are you thinking of?”

The signs were promising. Instead of answering, Harriet turned away, confused, and stood thoughtfully by the fire. Although the letter was still in her hand, she was now twisting it absentmindedly. Emma waited for the result with impatience, but not without strong hopes. At last, with some hesitation, Harriet said—

“Miss Woodhouse, since you will not give me your opinion, I must do as well as I can by myself; and I have now quite determined, and really almost made up my mind—to refuse Mr. Martin.”

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_004",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Is she, at seventeen, just stepping into the world, only starting to be noticed, to be questioned because she does not accept the initial proposal she gets? No—please allow her the opportunity to observe her surroundings.”

“I have consistently considered it a highly unwise friendship,” Mr. Knightley remarked after a moment, “even though I have held my opinions to myself; yet I now see that it will prove a very regrettable one for Harriet. You will inflate her with such notions of her own attractiveness, and of what she deserves, that, before long, no one in her circle will be sufficient for her. Vanity acting upon a fragile mind creates all manner of trouble. It is quite simple for a young woman to elevate her standards too far. Miss Harriet Smith might not see marriage proposals arrive so readily, even though she is a very lovely girl. Sensible men, regardless of what you might choose to claim, do not desire foolish wives. Men of status would not be eager to link themselves to a girl of such unknown origins—and most cautious men would fear the trouble and shame they might face when the secret of her birth is eventually disclosed. Let her wed Robert Martin, and she is secure, esteemed, and content for life; but if you urge her to anticipate a grand marriage, and instruct her to be content with nothing less than a man of importance and wealth, she might remain a boarder at Mrs. Goddard’s for the remainder of her days—or, at least, (for Harriet Smith is a girl who will eventually marry someone,) until she becomes desperate, and is happy to settle for the writing-master’s son.”

“We hold such opposing views on this matter, Mr. Knightley, that there is no point in debating it. We will only succeed in making one another more irritated. But regarding my allowing her to marry Robert Martin, it is out of the question; she has rejected him, and so firmly, I believe, as to stop any further attempts. She must accept the consequences of having turned him down, whatever they may be; and as for the rejection itself, I will not claim that I might not have swayed her slightly; but I promise you there was very little for me or anyone else to do. His appearance is so much to his disadvantage, and his behavior so poor, that if she were ever inclined to like him, she is not now. I can suppose that before she had encountered anyone superior, she might have put up with him. He was the brother of her acquaintances, and he worked to please her; and altogether, having met no one better (that must have been his greatest advantage) she might not, while she was at Abbey-Mill, have found him unpleasant. But the situation has changed now. She understands now what gentlemen are; and nothing but a gentleman in upbringing and conduct has any hope with Harriet.”

“Nonsense, absolute nonsense, as ever was spoken!” exclaimed Mr. Knightley.—“Robert Martin’s manners are supported by intelligence, honesty, and good-nature; and his character possesses more genuine refinement than Harriet Smith could grasp.”

Emma offered no reply, and attempted to appear pleasantly indifferent, but was actually feeling uneasy and wishing very much for him to depart."
}

{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_004",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“And is she, at seventeen, just starting out in life, just beginning to be known and admired because she didn’t accept the first proposal she got? No—please, let her have some time to look around.”

“I have always thought it a very foolish friendship,” Mr. Knightley said after a moment, “though I kept my opinions to myself; but I see now that it will be very unfortunate for Harriet. You will fill her head with such ideas about her own beauty and what she deserves that, before long, no one around her will be good enough. Vanity acting on a weak mind causes all sorts of trouble. It is so easy for a young woman to set her expectations too high. Miss Harriet Smith may not find marriage proposals coming in so quickly, even if she is a very pretty girl. Sensible men, whatever you might choose to say, do not want foolish wives. Men from established families would not be eager to connect themselves with a girl of such unknown origins—and most prudent men would fear the inconvenience and embarrassment they might face once the mystery of her parentage is finally revealed. Let her marry Robert Martin, and she is safe, respectable, and happy forever; but if you encourage her to aim for a high-status marriage and teach her to be satisfied with nothing less than a man of importance and great wealth, she might remain a boarder at Mrs. Goddard’s for the rest of her life—or, at least (since Harriet Smith is the type of girl who will marry someone eventually), until she grows desperate and is glad to settle for the old writing-master’s son.”

“We see this so differently, Mr. Knightley, that there is no point in debating it. We will only make each other angrier. But as for me ‘letting’ her marry Robert Martin, it is impossible; she has refused him, and so decisively that I think it must prevent any further attempts. She must live with the consequences of having refused him, whatever they may be; and as for the refusal itself, I won’t pretend that I might not have influenced her a little, but I assure you there was very little for me or anyone else to do. His appearance is so much against him, and his manner is so poor, that if she were ever inclined to favor him, she certainly isn’t now. I can imagine that before she had seen anyone superior, she might have tolerated him. He was the brother of her friends, and he tried hard to please her; and all things considered, having seen no one better (that must have been his greatest advantage), she might not have found him disagreeable while she was at Abbey-Mill. But the situation has changed now. She knows what gentlemen are like, and nothing but a man with the education and manners of a gentleman has any chance with Harriet.”

“Nonsense, absolute nonsense, as ever was spoken!” Mr. Knightley cried. “Robert Martin’s manners are defined by his sense, sincerity, and good humor; and his mind possesses more true refinement than Harriet Smith could ever understand.”

Emma did not answer and tried to look cheerfully unconcerned, but she was actually feeling uncomfortable and very much wanted him to leave."
}

Let her marry Robert Martin, and she is safe, respectable, and happy forever; but if you encourage her to aim for a high-status marriage and teach her to be satisfied with nothing less than a man of importance and great wealth, she might remain a boarder at Mrs. Goddard’s for the rest of her life—or, at least (since Harriet Smith is the type of girl who will marry someone eventually), until she grows desperate and is glad to settle for the old writing-master’s son.”

“We see this so differently, Mr. Knightley, that there is no point in debating it. We will only make each other angrier. But as for me ‘letting’ her marry Robert Martin, it is impossible; she has refused him, and so decisively that I think it must prevent any further attempts. She must live with the consequences of having refused him, whatever they may be; and as for the refusal itself, I won’t pretend that I might not have influenced her a little, but I assure you there was very little for me or anyone else to do. His appearance is so much against him, and his manner is so poor, that if she were ever inclined to favor him, she certainly isn’t now. I can imagine that before she had seen anyone superior, she might have tolerated him. He was the brother of her friends, and he tried hard to please her; and all things considered, having seen no one better (that must have been his greatest advantage), she might not have found him disagreeable while she was at Abbey-Mill. But the situation has changed now. She knows what gentlemen are like, and nothing but a man with the education and manners of a gentleman has any chance with Harriet.”

“Nonsense, absolute nonsense, as ever was spoken!” Mr. Knightley cried. “Robert Martin’s manners are defined by his sense, sincerity, and good humor; and his mind possesses more true refinement than Harriet Smith could ever understand.”

Emma did not answer and tried to look cheerfully unconcerned, but she was actually feeling uncomfortable and very much wanted him to leave."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_005",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“He is truly a bright lad. They are all exceptionally intelligent and possess such charming manners. They frequently gather by my seat, asking, ‘Grandfather, might I have a piece of string?’ Henry once requested a knife, though I informed him that knives are intended solely for grandfathers. I often feel their father treats them with excessive harshness.”

“He seems harsh to you,” Emma replied, “simply because you are so mild-mannered; yet, if you measured him against other fathers, you would not find him severe. He desires his sons to be vigorous and resilient; while he may speak sharply when they act poorly, he is a devoted parent—Mr. John Knightley is undoubtedly a loving father. The children adore him.”

“And then their uncle arrives and tosses them toward the ceiling in a truly terrifying fashion!”

“But they enjoy it, Father; they love it above all else. It brings them such delight that, unless their uncle insisted they take turns, the one who started would never yield to the other.”

“Well, I simply cannot fathom it.”

“That is true for all of us, Father. One portion of humanity cannot grasp the joys of the other.”

Later that morning, just as the young women were about to part to prepare for the customary four o’clock meal, the subject of this unique riddle reappeared. Harriet looked away, but Emma greeted him with her customary smile, her sharp gaze quickly detecting in his expression the awareness of having taken a risk—of having cast his lot—and she surmised he had arrived to observe the outcome. His stated purpose, however, was to inquire if Mr. Woodhouse’s gathering could proceed that evening without him, or if his presence at Hartfield was in any way required. If he were needed, all other plans would be set aside; otherwise, his friend Cole had spoken so insistently about him dining there—had made such a request—that he had tentatively agreed to attend.

Emma expressed her gratitude but insisted he must not disappoint his friend for their sake, as her father was certain to have his card game. He pressed the matter, she declined again, and he seemed ready to depart when she picked up the paper from the table and handed it back.

“Oh! Here is the riddle you were kind enough to leave with us; thank you for letting us see it. We admired it so greatly that I took the liberty of copying it into Miss Smith’s album. I trust your friend will not mind. Naturally, I only transcribed the initial eight lines.”

Mr. Elton clearly struggled for a response. He appeared somewhat uncertain and flustered, muttered something regarding an “honor,” glanced between Emma and Harriet, and then, noticing the open book on the table, picked it up to study it closely. Attempting to ease the uncomfortable silence, Emma said with a smile,

“Please convey my apologies to your friend; however, such a fine riddle should not be restricted to just a few. He can be certain of every woman’s praise when he writes with such chivalry.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_005",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“He is a very clever boy, truly. They are all remarkably bright, and they have so many charming habits. They will come and stand by my chair and ask, ‘Grandpapa, can you give me a piece of string?’ Once, Henry asked me for a knife, but I told him knives were only for grandpapas. I think their father is often too rough with them.”

“He seems rough to you,” Emma said, “because you are so very gentle yourself. But if you compared him to other fathers, you would not think him harsh. He wants his boys to be active and hardy, and if they misbehave, he might use a sharp word now and then. Still, he is an affectionate father—Mr. John Knightley is certainly an affectionate father. The children are all fond of him.”

“And then their uncle comes in and tosses them up to the ceiling in a very frightening way!”

“But they love it, Papa; there is nothing they enjoy more. It is such a thrill for them that if their uncle did not insist they take turns, whoever started would never let the other have a go.”

“Well, I cannot understand it.”

“That is true for all of us, Papa. One half of the world cannot understand the pleasures of the other.”

Later that morning, just as the girls were about to separate to prepare for the regular four o’clock dinner, the hero of this unique charade walked in again. Harriet turned away, but Emma greeted him with her usual smile. Her quick eye soon noticed in his expression the awareness of having made a move—of having taken a risk—and she imagined he had come to see how it might turn out. His stated reason, however, was to ask whether Mr. Woodhouse’s party could go ahead that evening without him, or if his presence was in any way necessary at Hartfield. If it were, everything else would be set aside, but otherwise, his friend Cole had spoken so much about him dining there—had made such a point of it—that he had promised to attend, provided he was not needed.

Emma thanked him but could not allow him to disappoint his friend on their account; her father was certain to have his card game. He insisted again, she declined again, and he seemed about to take his leave when she picked up the paper from the table and returned it.

“Oh! Here is the charade you were kind enough to leave with us; thank you for letting us see it. We admired it so much that I have ventured to copy it into Miss Smith’s collection. I hope your friend will not mind. Of course, I have not transcribed anything beyond the first eight lines.”

Mr. Elton clearly did not know quite what to say. He looked rather doubtful and confused, muttered something about “honor,” glanced at Emma and Harriet, and then, seeing the book open on the table, picked it up and examined it very closely. To smooth over the awkward moment, Emma said with a smile,

“You must offer my apologies to your friend, but such a good charade should not be kept to just one or two people. He can be sure of every woman’s approval while he writes with such gallantry.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_006",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Father is merely expressing his own sorrow.”

“It is greatly to the credit of both,” came the gracious response.

“And do you see her, sir, with any regularity?” Isabella inquired in the mournful voice that perfectly matched her father’s mood.

Mr. Woodhouse paused. “Not nearly as often, my dear, as I would like.”

“Oh! Father, we have only failed to see them on a single day since their wedding. Every day but one, either morning or evening, we have encountered Mr. or Mrs. Weston, and usually both, whether at Randalls or here—and as you might imagine, Isabella, most often here. They are extremely generous with their visits. Mr. Weston is truly as thoughtful as she is. Father, if you talk in such a gloomy fashion, you will provide Isabella with an incorrect impression of our situation. Everyone must realize that Miss Taylor is missed, but everyone should also be certain that Mr. and Mrs. Weston truly prevent us from missing her to the degree we initially feared—which is the absolute truth.”

“Exactly as it ought to be,” remarked Mr. John Knightley, “and just as I trusted it was based on your correspondence. Her desire to show you consideration was never in doubt, and his status as a man with leisure and a social nature makes it all simple. I have consistently told you, my dear, that I never believed the transition would be as significant for Hartfield as you feared; and now that you have Emma’s report, I trust you will be content.”

“Well, certainly,” said Mr. Woodhouse—“yes, indeed—I cannot dispute that Mrs. Weston, poor Mrs. Weston, does visit us quite frequently—but then—she is always compelled to leave again.”

“It would be quite difficult for Mr. Weston if she did not, Father. You entirely overlook poor Mr. Weston.”

“I truly believe,” John Knightley added pleasantly, “that Mr. Weston has some small right to her. You and I, Emma, will dare to defend the poor husband. I, being a husband, and you not being a wife, the husband’s perspective may very well strike us with similar weight. As for Isabella, she has been married long enough to recognize the benefit of setting all the Mr. Westons aside whenever she can.”

“Me, my dear?” his wife exclaimed, catching only a portion of the conversation. “Are you speaking of me? I am certain no one should be, or can be, a stronger supporter of marriage than I am; and if it were not for the sadness of her departing Hartfield, I would only have thought of Miss Taylor as the luckiest woman alive; and as for disregarding Mr. Weston, that wonderful Mr. Weston, I think there is nothing he does not merit. I believe he is one of the most good-natured men who ever lived. Aside from you and your brother, I know no one his equal in temperament. I shall never forget his flying Henry’s kite for him on that very blustery day last Easter—and ever since his special kindness last September a year ago in sending that note, at midnight, specifically to reassure me that there was no scarlet fever at Cobham, I have been certain there could not be a more compassionate heart or a better man in existence.”"
}

Mr. Woodhouse hesitated. “Not nearly as often, my dear, as I would like.”

“Oh! Dad, we have only missed seeing them for one full day since they were married. Every single day except for that one, we have seen either Mr. or Mrs. Weston—usually both—either at Randalls or here. And as you can imagine, Isabella, most often here. They are incredibly kind with their visits. Mr. Weston is truly as kind as she is. Dad, if you talk in that gloomy way, you will give Isabella the wrong impression of us all. Everyone knows Miss Taylor must be missed, but everyone should also be assured that Mr. and Mrs. Weston really prevent us from missing her as much as we expected—which is the absolute truth.”

“Just as it should be,” said Mr. John Knightley, “and just as I hoped it was from your letters. There was never any doubt about her desire to be attentive, and his being a man with free time and a social nature makes it all work out. I have always told you, my love, that I didn’t think the change would be as significant for Hartfield as you feared; and now that you have Emma’s report, I hope you will be satisfied.”

“Well, to be sure,” said Mr. Woodhouse, “yes, certainly—I cannot deny that Mrs. Weston, poor Mrs. Weston, does come to see us quite often—but then—she is always forced to leave again.”

“It would be very tough on Mr. Weston if she didn’t, Dad. You completely forget poor Mr. Weston.”

“I think, actually,” said John Knightley pleasantly, “that Mr. Weston has some small claim. You and I, Emma, will venture to stand up for the poor husband. Since I am a husband and you are not a wife, the man’s perspective might strike us with equal force. As for Isabella, she has been married long enough to see the convenience of setting all the Mr. Westons aside as much as she can.”

“Me, my love?” his wife cried, catching only part of what was said. “Are you talking about me? I am sure no one should be, or can be, a greater advocate for marriage than I am. If it weren’t for the misery of her leaving Hartfield, I would only think of Miss Taylor as the luckiest woman in the world. And as for slighting Mr. Weston, that excellent Mr. Weston, I think there is nothing he doesn’t deserve. I believe he is one of the best-tempered men who ever lived. Except for you and your brother, I don’t know his equal for temperament. I will never forget him flying Henry’s kite on that very windy day last Easter. And ever since his specific kindness last September a year ago, when he wrote that note at midnight just to assure me there was no scarlet fever at Cobham, I have been convinced there could not be a more sensitive heart or a better man in existence.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_007",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“You are acting correctly,” she remarked; “we shall convey your regrets to Mr. and Mrs. Weston.”

Yet, no sooner had she uttered these words than she observed her brother courteously proposing a place in his carriage, provided the weather was Mr. Elton’s sole concern, and Mr. Elton immediately embracing the proposal with eager readiness. The matter was settled; Mr. Elton was to accompany them, and his wide, attractive face had never displayed greater delight than at that instant; his grin had never been broader, nor his gaze more triumphant than when he turned his attention back to her.

“Well,” she mused, “this is truly bizarre! After I had managed his departure so effectively, to decide to join a social gathering and abandon an ailing Harriet! It is most peculiar! However, I suspect that many men, particularly those who are unmarried, possess such a tendency—such a craving for dining out—that a dinner invitation ranks so highly among their enjoyments, their occupations, their honors, and almost their obligations, that everything else is sacrificed for it. This must be the situation with Mr. Elton; he is undoubtedly a highly worthy, kind, and agreeable young man, and very much enamored with Harriet, yet he cannot decline an invitation; he feels compelled to dine out whenever he is requested. How odd love is! He can perceive intelligence in Harriet, yet he will not dine alone for her sake.”

Shortly thereafter, Mr. Elton departed, and she felt compelled to acknowledge that there was considerable feeling in the manner he mentioned Harriet upon leaving; in the inflection of his voice as he promised her that he would stop at Mrs. Goddard’s to inquire about her lovely friend, just before he readied himself for the joy of seeing her again, when he hoped to provide a more positive update; he sighed and smiled as he left, leaving her with a generally favorable impression.

Following a few moments of complete quiet between them, John Knightley commenced by saying,

“I have never encountered a man more determined to be charming than Mr. Elton. It is sheer exertion for him when women are involved. With men, he can be sensible and natural, but when he has women to impress, every muscle in his face is in motion.”

“Mr. Elton’s conduct is not flawless,” Emma answered; “but when there is a desire to please, one should disregard, and indeed one does disregard, a great deal. When a man exerts his best efforts with only limited abilities, he will surpass those with careless superiority. There is such genuine good nature and kindness in Mr. Elton that one cannot help but appreciate it.”

“Indeed,” Mr. John Knightley added after a moment, with a touch of irony, “he appears to have a great deal of good will toward you.”

“Me!” she responded with a look of surprise, “are you suggesting that I am the target of Mr. Elton’s affections?”

“Such a thought has occurred to me, I admit, Emma; and if it has never crossed your mind before, you might as well reflect upon it now.”

“Mr. Elton in love with me! What a notion!”

“I am not asserting that it is the case; but you would be wise to contemplate whether it is so or not, and to adjust your conduct in response. I believe your behavior toward him is encouraging.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_007",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“You are doing the right thing,” she said. “We will make your apologies to Mr. and Mrs. Weston.”

But she had barely spoken when she noticed her brother politely offering Mr. Elton a seat in his carriage, provided the weather was his only objection, and Mr. Elton immediately accepted the offer with prompt satisfaction. It was settled; Mr. Elton was going, and his broad, handsome face had never expressed more pleasure than at that moment. His smile had never been wider, nor his eyes more triumphant than when he looked at her next.

“Well,” she thought to herself, “this is very strange! After I had helped him get out of it so well, to choose to go out and leave Harriet behind, feeling ill! It is truly bizarre. But I suppose many men, especially bachelors, have such an inclination—such a passion for dining out—that a dinner invitation ranks so high among their pleasures, their activities, their status, and almost their duties, that everything else is pushed aside. This must be the case with Mr. Elton. He is undoubtedly a valuable, amiable, and pleasing young man, and very much in love with Harriet, but he simply cannot refuse an invitation; he must dine out wherever he is asked. What a strange thing love is! He can see Harriet’s quick wit, but he will not stay home alone for her sake.”

Shortly afterward, Mr. Elton left them, and she had to admit that there was a great deal of genuine feeling in the way he mentioned Harriet as he departed. The tone of his voice as he assured her he would stop at Mrs. Goddard’s for news of her friend—the very last thing he would do before preparing for the pleasure of meeting her again, when he hoped to have a better report—was sincere. He sighed and smiled as he left, leaving her with a generally favorable impression.

After a few minutes of complete silence between them, John Knightley began, “I have never in my life seen a man more determined to be agreeable than Mr. Elton. It is actual labor for him when ladies are involved. With men, he can be rational and natural, but when he has ladies to please, every muscle in his face works.”

“Mr. Elton’s manners are not perfect,” Emma replied, “but when someone has a genuine desire to please, one should overlook, and indeed does overlook, a great deal. When a man does his best with only moderate abilities, he will have the advantage over those who are superior but indifferent. There is such perfect good nature and goodwill in Mr. Elton that one cannot help but value it.”

“Yes,” Mr. John Knightley said presently, with a touch of sarcasm, “he seems to have a great deal of goodwill toward you.”

“Me!” she replied with a smile of astonishment. “Are you imagining that I am the object of Mr. Elton’s interest?”

“Such a thought has crossed my mind, I admit, Emma; and if it has never occurred to you before, you might as well consider it now.”

“Mr. Elton in love with me! What an idea!”

“I am not saying it is so, but you would do well to consider whether it is or not, and to adjust your behavior accordingly. I think your manner toward him is encouraging.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He had traveled past the entrance—a short distance down the Highbury road—where the snow was nowhere deeper than half an inch; in many spots, it barely dusted the earth. Only a few flakes were currently descending, but the clouds were breaking, suggesting the storm would soon cease. He had consulted the drivers, and they concurred that there was no cause for concern.

For Isabella, this news brought immense relief, and it was nearly as welcome to Emma regarding her father, who was rendered as comfortable as his anxious temperament permitted. Yet, the panic that had been stirred could not be calmed enough to provide him any peace while he remained at Randalls. He accepted that the journey home posed no immediate threat, but no amount of persuasion could convince him that remaining was secure. While the others offered various suggestions, Mr. Knightley and Emma reached a decision in a few short remarks: thus—

“Your father will not be at peace; why do you not depart?”

“I am prepared, provided the others are.”

“Shall I summon the servant?”

“Yes, please.”

And the bell was pulled, and the vehicles were requested. In a few more minutes, Emma hoped to see one vexing companion delivered to his own residence to regain his composure, and the other restore his spirits once this difficult visit concluded.

The carriage arrived: Mr. Woodhouse, always the primary concern during such events, was attentively assisted into his vehicle by Mr. Knightley and Mr. Weston. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_009",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Once he reached the age of reason, he should have exerted himself to cast aside any influence from them that was beneath him. He should have resisted their initial efforts to encourage him to disregard his father. Had he acted correctly from the start, there would be no complications at present.

“We are never going to see eye to eye regarding him,” Emma exclaimed; “but that is hardly surprising. I do not believe for a moment that he is a feeble-minded young man: I am certain he is not. Mr. Weston would not ignore foolishness, even in his own child; however, it is quite probable that he possesses a more accommodating, gentle, and submissive temperament than what you consider the ideal for a man. I expect that is the case; and while it might deprive him of certain benefits, it will surely grant him many others.”

“Indeed; the benefits of remaining inactive when he should be taking action, of pursuing a life of simple, lazy enjoyment, and of convincing himself that he is remarkably clever at inventing justifications for it. He can sit down and compose an elaborate, flowery letter, brimming with empty promises and lies, and convince himself that he has discovered the perfect way to maintain domestic harmony and ensure his father has no grounds for grievance. His correspondence repulses me.”

“Your perspective is unique. Everyone else seems quite content with them.”

“I doubt they satisfy Mrs. Weston. They could scarcely please a woman of her intelligence and sensitivity: occupying a maternal role, yet lacking the blind devotion of a mother. It is for her sake that he owes Randalls extra consideration, and she must feel his neglect all the more keenly. Had she been a woman of high social standing, I am sure he would have visited; and it would not have mattered whether he did or not. Do you believe your friend is oblivious to such matters? Do you imagine she does not frequently reflect on this herself? No, Emma, your charming young man is only charming in the French sense, not the English. He may be ‘amiable,’ possess polished manners, and be quite pleasant; yet he lacks any true English sensitivity toward the feelings of others: there is nothing genuinely virtuous about him.”

“You appear set on thinking the worst of him.”

“I? Not at all,” Mr. Knightley answered, sounding somewhat annoyed; “I have no desire to think poorly of him. I would be just as willing to recognize his good qualities as anyone else; but I hear of none, aside from those that are merely superficial; that he is tall, handsome, and possesses smooth, persuasive manners.”

“Well, if he has nothing else to offer, he will be a prize for Highbury. We do not frequently encounter handsome, well-mannered, and pleasant young men. We must not be too particular and demand every virtue as well. Can you not imagine, Mr. Knightley, the stir his arrival will cause? There will be only one topic of conversation throughout the parishes of Donwell and Highbury; only one focus—one object of interest; it will be entirely Mr. Frank Churchill; we shall think and talk of no one else.”

“You will forgive me if I am not quite so overwhelmed. If I find him capable of good conversation, I shall be pleased to know him; but if he is merely a talkative fop, he will not take up much of my time or attention.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_009",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Once he reached the age of reason, he should have stepped up and rejected any influence over him that was beneath his dignity. He should have resisted their initial attempts to make him disrespect his father. If he had started out the way he should have, there would be no trouble now.”

“We will never see eye to eye about him,” Emma exclaimed; “but that is nothing new. I do not think for a second that he is a weak young man; I am certain he is not. Mr. Weston would not be blind to foolishness, even in his own son, but it is very likely that he has a more yielding, agreeable, and mild personality than what fits your idea of a perfect man. I imagine he does; and while that might cost him some advantages, it will guarantee him many others.”

“Yes; all the advantages of staying put when he should be moving, living a life of pure, idle pleasure, and convincing himself that he is incredibly clever at finding excuses for it. He can sit down and write a fancy, elaborate letter, full of empty promises and lies, and persuade himself that he has found the best way in the world to keep the peace at home and prevent his father from having any reason to complain. His letters disgust me.”

“Your perspective is unique. They seem to satisfy everyone else.”

“I suspect they do not satisfy Mrs. Weston. They could hardly satisfy a woman of her good sense and sharp instincts, standing in a mother’s place but without a mother’s affection to blind her. It is because of her that he owes Randalls extra attention, and she must feel his absence twice as much. If she were a person of high status herself, I am sure he would have visited, and it would not have mattered whether he did or not. Can you really think your friend is oblivious to these kinds of considerations? Do you suppose she does not often tell herself all of this? No, Emma, your charming young man can only be charming in the French sense, not the English one. He may be very ‘amiable,’ have excellent manners, and be very pleasant, but he lacks any true English sensitivity toward the feelings of others; there is nothing genuinely kind about him.”

“You seem determined to think the worst of him.”

“Me? Not at all,” Mr. Knightley replied, looking annoyed; “I do not want to think the worst of him. I would be just as ready to acknowledge his merits as anyone else, but I hear of none, except for his physical traits; that he is tall, good-looking, and has smooth, persuasive manners.”

“Well, if he has nothing else to offer, he will be a treasure in Highbury. We do not often see handsome young men who are well-bred and agreeable. We must not be too picky and demand every single virtue as well. Can you not imagine, Mr. Knightley, what a sensation his arrival will cause? There will be only one topic of conversation throughout the parishes of Donwell and Highbury; only one interest—one object of curiosity; it will be all about Mr. Frank Churchill; we will think and speak of no one else.”

“You will have to excuse me for not being so overwhelmed. If I find him easy to talk to, I will be happy to get to know him, but if he is just a chattering show-off, he will not take up much of my time or thoughts.”"
}

“We will never agree about him,” Emma exclaimed, “but that is not surprising. I do not believe he is a weak young man; I am certain he is not. Mr. Weston would not be blind to foolishness, even in his own son, but his son likely has a more yielding, agreeable, and mild personality than what you consider perfect for a man. I suspect he does; and while that might cost him some advantages, it will secure him many others.”

“Yes, the advantages of sitting still when he should be active, living a life of idle pleasure, and imagining he is clever at finding excuses for it. He can sit down and write a fancy, elaborate letter, full of empty promises and lies, and convince himself that he has found the perfect way to keep the peace at home and prevent his father from having any reason to complain. His letters disgust me.”

“Your feelings are unusual. They seem to satisfy everyone else.”

“I suspect they do not satisfy Mrs. Weston. They can hardly satisfy a woman of her good sense and quick feelings, as she stands in a mother’s place without a mother’s affection to blind her. It is for her sake that he owes extra attention to Randalls, and she must feel his neglect twice as much. If she were a person of high status, I am sure he would have visited, and it would not have mattered whether he did or not. Do you think your friend is unaware of these considerations? Do you suppose she does not often say all this to herself? No, Emma, your charming young man is only charming in the French sense, not the English one. He may be ‘amiable,’ have good manners, and be very agreeable, but he lacks any true English sensitivity toward the feelings of others. There is nothing genuinely kind about him.”

“You seem determined to think poorly of him.”

“Me? Not at all,” Mr. Knightley replied, looking annoyed. “I do not want to think poorly of him. I would be just as ready to acknowledge his merits as anyone else, but I hear of none, except for his physical traits; that he is well-built and good-looking, with smooth, persuasive manners.”

“Well, if he has nothing else to offer, he will be a treasure in Highbury. We do not often see fine, well-bred, and agreeable young men. We must not be too picky and demand every virtue as well. Cannot you imagine, Mr. Knightley, what a sensation his arrival will cause? There will be only one topic throughout the parishes of Donwell and Highbury; only one interest and one object of curiosity. It will be all about Mr. Frank Churchill; we will think and speak of no one else.”

“You will have to excuse me for not being so overwhelmed. If I find him capable of good conversation, I will be glad to know him, but if he is just a chattering show-off, he will not take up much of my time or thoughts.”"
}

Emma eventually felt somewhat pleased that such an encounter had occurred. It had proven useful in softening the initial impact without leaving any cause for concern. As Harriet resided now, the Martins could not reach her without actively seeking her out, a task for which they had previously lacked either the nerve or the humility; since she had rejected the brother, the sisters had not visited Mrs. Goddard’s, and a full year might elapse without them crossing paths, let alone needing to converse.

CHAPTER IV

Human nature is so favorably inclined toward those in compelling circumstances that a young person who either weds or passes away is certain to be spoken of with kindness.

Less than a week had elapsed since Miss Hawkins was first spoken of in Highbury before she was somehow found to possess every virtue of appearance and character; she was deemed beautiful, graceful, exceptionally talented, and entirely charming. When Mr. Elton himself returned to celebrate his fortunate prospects and spread word of her virtues, he had little left to do but reveal her first name and mention which composer’s music she favored.

Mr. Elton returned a very joyful man. He had departed feeling rejected and humiliated—thwarted in a very confident expectation following a series of what he perceived as clear encouragement—and having not only lost the ideal lady but finding himself lowered to the status of an unsuitable one. He had left deeply insulted; he returned betrothed to another—someone naturally as superior to the first as, in such situations, what is acquired always is to what is forfeited. He returned cheerful and content, energetic and occupied, indifferent to Miss Woodhouse and dismissive of Miss Smith.

The delightful Augusta Hawkins, besides possessing the standard benefits of flawless beauty and merit, held an independent fortune of as many thousands as would always be termed ten; a detail of some prestige as well as practical utility. The narrative was impressive; he had not settled for less—he had secured a woman with 10,000 pounds or thereabouts. Furthermore, he had secured her with such pleasing speed; the initial hour of their meeting had been followed so quickly by marked attention. The account he provided to Mrs. Cole regarding the development of the romance was so triumphant—the progression so swift, from their chance meeting to the dinner at Mr. Green’s and the gathering at Mrs. Brown’s—with smiles and blushes gaining significance, and awareness and excitement liberally sprinkled throughout. The lady had been so easily won, so sweetly inclined, and in short, to use a very clear expression, so very eager to accept him, that both his vanity and his sense of prudence were entirely satisfied."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_012",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He reasoned like a youth determined to dance, and Emma felt some surprise that the Weston nature should so clearly triumph over the customs of the Churchills. He appeared to possess all the vitality, high spirits, and sociable nature of his father, showing none of the haughtiness or aloofness associated with Enscombe. Indeed, he perhaps lacked a sufficient sense of pride; his disregard for social distinctions leaned toward a lack of refinement. He could not, however, grasp the potential harm in his casual attitude. It was merely an overflow of exuberant energy.

Eventually, he was convinced to depart from the front of the Crown, and as they were nearly opposite the residence where the Bates family stayed, Emma remembered his planned call from the previous day and inquired if he had completed it.

“Yes, certainly,” he answered. “I was about to bring it up. It was a productive visit; I saw all three ladies and felt quite grateful for your advance warning. Had the talkative aunt caught me off guard, it would have been my undoing. As it happened, I was merely tricked into staying for an excessive amount of time. Ten minutes might have sufficed, or perhaps been appropriate, and I had promised my father I would be home before him, but there was no opportunity to leave, no break in the conversation. To my complete shock, when he finally located me there, I realized I had been sitting with them for nearly forty-five minutes. The dear woman had not allowed me any chance to get away sooner.”

“And how did Miss Fairfax appear to you?”

“Poorly, very poorly—that is, if a young lady is ever permitted to look unwell. But that phrasing is hardly acceptable, is it? Ladies are never supposed to look ill. In all seriousness, Miss Fairfax is naturally so pale that she almost always seems in poor health. A most regrettable lack of color.”

Emma disagreed, launching into a spirited defense of Miss Fairfax’s skin tone. “It was certainly never vibrant, but I would not call it sickly; there is a softness and refinement in her skin that lends a unique grace to her features.” He listened with proper respect, admitting he had heard others express the same view, yet he had to confess that for him, nothing could compensate for the absence of a healthy glow. Where features were plain, a lovely complexion provided beauty, and where they were attractive, the result was—fortunately, he did not need to try to explain the effect.

“Well,” Emma remarked, “there is no accounting for taste. At the very least, you admire her, apart from her complexion.”

He laughed and shook his head. “I cannot view Miss Fairfax apart from her complexion.”

“Did you see her frequently at Weymouth? Were you often in the same social circles?”

Just then, they neared Ford’s, and he quickly cried out, “Ah! This must be the very shop that everyone visits daily, as my father tells me. He claims he comes to Highbury himself six days out of seven and always has errands at Ford’s.”"
}

He seemed to possess all the energy, high spirits, and social nature of his father, with none of the pride or reserve associated with Enscombe. In fact, he had perhaps too little pride; his indifference to social standing bordered on a lack of refinement. He could not, however, judge the potential harm in his casual attitude. It was merely an outburst of high spirits.

At last, he was persuaded to move on from the front of the Crown, and as they were now nearly facing the house where the Bateses lived, Emma remembered his planned visit from the day before and asked if he had gone.

“Yes, oh! yes,” he replied. “I was just about to mention it. A very successful visit: I saw all three ladies and felt very grateful for your warning. If the talkative aunt had caught me completely by surprise, it would have been the death of me. As it was, I was only tricked into staying for an unreasonably long visit. Ten minutes would have been enough, perhaps all that was appropriate, and I had told my father I would certainly be home before him—but there was no way to leave, no pause; and to my utter astonishment, I found, when he finally joined me there after failing to find me anywhere else, that I had actually been sitting with them for nearly forty-five minutes. The good lady hadn't given me a chance to escape sooner.”

“And how did Miss Fairfax look to you?”

“Ill, very ill—that is, if a young lady is ever allowed to look ill. But the phrase is hardly acceptable, is it? Ladies can never look ill. And, in all seriousness, Miss Fairfax is naturally so pale that she almost always appears to be in poor health. A most regrettable lack of color.”

Emma would not agree with this and began a spirited defense of Miss Fairfax’s complexion. “It was certainly never brilliant, but I wouldn't call it sickly as a rule; there is a softness and delicacy to her skin that gives her face a unique elegance.” He listened with proper respect, acknowledging that he had heard many people say the same, but he still had to admit that, for him, nothing could make up for the lack of a healthy glow. Where features were average, a fine complexion made them beautiful, and where they were already good, the effect was—fortunately, he did not need to try to describe what the effect was.

“Well,” said Emma, “there is no arguing about taste. At least you admire her, apart from her complexion.”

He shook his head and laughed. “I cannot separate Miss Fairfax from her complexion.”

“Did you see her often at Weymouth? Were you often in the same social circles?”

At that moment, they were approaching Ford’s, and he exclaimed quickly, “Ha! This must be the very shop that everyone visits every single day, as my father tells me. He says he comes to Highbury himself six days out of seven and always has business at Ford’s.”"
}

In fact, he had almost too little pride; his indifference to social rank bordered on a lack of refinement. He could not judge the harm in his casual attitude, however; it was merely an expression of his lively spirit.

Finally, he was persuaded to move on from the front of the Crown Inn. As they were now nearly in front of the house where the Bates family lived, Emma remembered his planned visit from the day before and asked if he had gone.

“Yes, oh! yes,” he replied. “I was just going to mention it. It was a very successful visit. I saw all three ladies and felt very grateful for your warning. If the talkative aunt had caught me by surprise, it would have been the death of me. As it was, I was tricked into staying for an unreasonably long visit. Ten minutes would have been enough, perhaps all that was proper, and I had told my father I would certainly be home before him. But there was no way to leave, no pause in the conversation. To my total surprise, when he finally found me there, I realized I had been sitting with them for nearly forty-five minutes. The good lady had not given me any chance to escape sooner.”

“And how did Miss Fairfax look to you?”

“Ill, very ill—that is, if a young lady is ever allowed to look ill. But that expression is hardly acceptable, is it? Ladies can never look ill. And, seriously, Miss Fairfax is naturally so pale that she almost always appears to be in poor health. A most unfortunate lack of color.”

Emma would not agree and began a warm defense of Miss Fairfax’s complexion. “It was certainly never brilliant, but I would not call it sickly. There is a softness and delicacy in her skin that gives a unique elegance to her face.” He listened with proper respect and acknowledged that he had heard many people say the same, but he still had to admit that, for him, nothing could make up for the lack of a healthy glow. When features were plain, a good complexion made them beautiful, and when they were already good, the effect was—fortunately, he did not need to describe what the effect was.

“Well,” said Emma, “there is no arguing about taste. At least you admire her, apart from her complexion.”

He shook his head and laughed. “I cannot separate Miss Fairfax from her complexion.”

“Did you see her often at Weymouth? Were you often in the same social circles?”

At that moment, they were approaching Ford’s shop, and he exclaimed quickly, “Ha! This must be the very shop that everyone visits every day of their lives, as my father tells me. He says he comes to Highbury himself six days out of seven and always has business at Ford’s.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_013",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "It was only yesterday that I mentioned to Mr. Cole how embarrassed I felt gazing at our new grand piano in the parlor, given that I cannot distinguish one note from another, and our young daughters, who are merely starting out, might never master it. Meanwhile, poor Jane Fairfax, who is a skilled musician, lacks any sort of instrument—not even the most modest, worn-out spinet—to occupy her time. I shared this with Mr. Cole just yesterday, and he fully concurred; he simply loves music so dearly that he could not resist buying it, hoping some of our kind neighbors might occasionally utilize it better than we can. That is truly why the instrument was acquired, or else I am certain we should feel quite ashamed of it. We are very hopeful that Miss Woodhouse might be persuaded to play it this evening.

Miss Woodhouse offered the expected polite response, and realizing no further information could be coaxed from Mrs. Cole, she turned toward Frank Churchill.

“Why are you grinning?” she asked.

“Well, why are you?”

“Me? I imagine I am smiling out of delight that Colonel Campbell is so wealthy and generous. It is a magnificent gift.”

“Indeed.”

“I find it somewhat strange that it was not presented sooner.”

“Perhaps Miss Fairfax has never visited here for such an extended period before.”

“Or that he did not allow her to use their own instrument, which must be sitting locked away in London, unused by anyone.”

“That is a grand piano, and he might have considered it too bulky for Mrs. Bates’s residence.”

“You may claim whatever you like, but your expression reveals that your opinions on this matter mirror my own.”

“I am not certain. I suspect you are crediting me with more perception than I possess. I smile because you do, and I will likely harbor whatever doubts you hold; yet, at the moment, I see no reason for skepticism. If it is not Colonel Campbell, who else could it be?”

“What is your opinion of Mrs. Dixon?”

“Mrs. Dixon! Quite right. I had not considered her. She would be as aware as her father of how welcome such a gift would be, and perhaps the manner of it—the secrecy and the surprise—is more characteristic of a young woman’s plan than an older man’s. I am certain it is Mrs. Dixon. I mentioned that your theories would direct mine.”

“If that is the case, you must broaden your suspicions to include Mr. Dixon as well.”

“Mr. Dixon. Very well. Yes, I see immediately that it must be a combined gift from Mr. and Mrs. Dixon. We were discussing the other day, you recall, how much he admires her musical talent.”

“Yes, and what you shared with me regarding that topic reinforced a suspicion I had already formed. I do not intend to cast doubt on the honorable motives of Mr. Dixon or Miss Fairfax, but I cannot help but suspect that, after proposing to her friend, he unfortunately fell in love with her, or perhaps he realized she had developed a slight attachment to him.”"
}

He is just so fond of music that he couldn't help but treat himself to the purchase, hoping that some of our good neighbors might be kind enough to put it to better use than we can. That is the real reason the instrument was bought; otherwise, I am sure we ought to be ashamed of it. We are very hopeful that Miss Woodhouse might be persuaded to try it this evening.

Miss Woodhouse offered the appropriate polite response, and realizing there was nothing more to be gleaned from Mrs. Cole’s conversation, she turned to Frank Churchill.

“Why are you smiling?” she asked.

“Well, why are you?”

“Me? I suppose I am smiling because I am pleased that Colonel Campbell is so wealthy and generous. It is a lovely gift.”

“Very.”

“I am a bit surprised it was never given before.”

“Perhaps Miss Fairfax has never stayed here this long before.”

“Or that he didn't let her use their own instrument, which must be sitting locked up in London, untouched by anyone.”

“That is a grand piano, and he might have thought it too large for Mrs. Bates’s house.”

“You can say whatever you like, but your expression shows that your thoughts on this matter are very similar to mine.”

“I don't know. I think you are giving me more credit for insight than I deserve. I smile because you smile, and I will likely suspect whatever you suspect; but right now, I don't see what there is to question. If it isn't Colonel Campbell, who could it be?”

“What do you think about Mrs. Dixon?”

“Mrs. Dixon! That is very true. I hadn't thought of Mrs. Dixon. It is Mrs. Dixon, I am sure. I told you that your suspicions would guide mine.”

“If that is the case, you must broaden your suspicions to include Mr. Dixon as well.”

“Yes, and what you told me about that confirmed an idea I had already formed. I do not mean to question the good intentions of either Mr. Dixon or Miss Fairfax, but I cannot help suspecting that, after proposing to her friend, he either had the misfortune of falling in love with her, or he became aware of a little attachment on her part.”"

“Very.”

  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "He did not appear immediately. No; he was chatting with Mrs. Cole, looking completely unconcerned, while someone else asked Jane to dance, and he continued his conversation with Mrs. Cole.

Emma was no longer worried about Henry; his interest remained secure, and she began the dance with genuine spirit and enjoyment. Only five couples could be gathered, but the rarity and spontaneity of the event made it delightful, and she found herself well-matched with her partner. They were a couple worth watching.

Unfortunately, they were only allowed two dances. It was getting late, and Miss Bates was anxious to return home for her mother’s sake. After a few attempts to be allowed to start again, they were forced to thank Mrs. Weston, act disappointed, and finish.

“Perhaps it is for the best,” Frank Churchill said as he escorted Emma to her carriage. “I would have had to ask Miss Fairfax, and her listless dancing would not have suited me after yours.”

CHAPTER IX

Emma did not regret her decision to lower herself by attending the Coles’ party. The visit provided her with many pleasant memories the following day, and whatever she might have lost in terms of dignified seclusion was surely repaid by the splendor of her popularity. She must have delighted the Coles—worthy people who deserved to be made happy—and left a reputation behind that would not soon fade.

Perfect happiness, even in memory, is rare, and there were two points on which she was not entirely at ease. She doubted whether she had violated the duty of one woman to another by revealing her suspicions about Jane Fairfax’s feelings to Frank Churchill. It was hardly right, but the idea had been so strong that it slipped out, and his acceptance of everything she said was such a compliment to her insight that it was difficult for her to be certain she should have kept quiet.

The other source of regret also concerned Jane Fairfax, and there she had no doubts. She genuinely and unequivocally regretted the inferiority of her own playing and singing. She felt deep remorse over the laziness of her childhood and sat down to practice vigorously for an hour and a half.

She was then interrupted by Harriet’s arrival, and if Harriet’s praise could have satisfied her, she might have been comforted quickly.

“Oh! If only I could play as well as you and Miss Fairfax!”

“Don’t group us together, Harriet. My playing is no more like hers than a lamp is like sunshine.”

“Oh, dear—I think you play better than she does. I think you play just as well as she does. I am sure I would much rather listen to you. Everyone last night said how well you played.”

“Those who knew anything about music must have felt the difference. The truth is, Harriet, that my playing is just good enough to be praised, but Jane Fairfax’s is far superior.”

“Well, I will always think that you play just as well as she does, or that if there is any difference, nobody would ever notice. Mr. Cole mentioned how much taste you had; and Mr.”"
}

{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_015",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Oh! truly wonderful; I must say as much, for I imagine Miss Woodhouse and Mr. Frank Churchill are catching every word. And (elevating his tone further) I see no reason why Miss Fairfax should be excluded. I believe Miss Fairfax is an excellent dancer; and Mrs. Weston is, without question, the finest country-dance musician in England. If your companions possess any sense of gratitude, they will speak quite loudly of you and me in turn; however, I cannot wait to hear it.”

“Oh! Mr. Knightley, just a second; something of importance—I am so distressed!—Jane and I are both so troubled regarding the apples!”

“What is the trouble now?”

“To think of you sending us your entire supply of apples. You mentioned having a large quantity, and now you have none remaining. We are truly so distressed! Mrs. Hodges has every right to be annoyed. William Larkins spoke of it here. You really should not have done it, you truly should not. Ah! he has departed. He cannot endure being thanked. Yet I imagined he might linger now, and it would have been a shame not to have brought up.... Well, (re-entering the room,) I have failed. Mr. Knightley cannot remain. He is traveling to Kingston. He inquired if he could assist with anything....”

“Yes,” Jane replied, “we overheard his generous offers, we heard everything.”

“Oh! yes, my dear, I suppose you might have, as the door was ajar, and the window was open, and Mr. Knightley spoke with a loud voice. You must have heard everything, certainly. ‘Can I do anything for you at Kingston?’ he asked; so I simply brought up.... Oh! Miss Woodhouse, must you leave?—You seem to have only just arrived—how very kind of you.”

Emma realized it was truly time to return home; the call had already been lengthy; and upon checking their watches, they saw so much of the morning had vanished that Mrs. Weston and her friend, while departing as well, could only manage to accompany the two young ladies as far as the Hartfield gates before heading toward Randalls.

CHAPTER XI

It is perhaps possible to exist entirely without dancing. There have been cases of young individuals spending many, many months in a row without attending a ball of any kind, and no significant harm coming to their physical or mental health;—yet once a start is made—once the joys of swift movement have been experienced, however briefly—it must be a very dull group that does not desire more.

Frank Churchill had danced once in Highbury, and desired to dance again; and the final half-hour of an evening that Mr. Woodhouse was convinced to spend with his daughter at Randalls was occupied by the two young people in planning for this purpose. Frank initiated the concept; and his was the greatest enthusiasm in following it; for the lady was the better judge of the obstacles, and the most concerned for convenience and propriety.
