parts.text:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_015",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Oh! truly wonderful; I must say as much, for I imagine Miss Woodhouse and Mr. Frank Churchill are catching every word. And (elevating his tone further) I see no reason why Miss Fairfax should be excluded. I believe Miss Fairfax is an excellent dancer; and Mrs. Weston is, without question, the finest country-dance musician in England. If your companions possess any sense of gratitude, they will speak quite loudly of you and me in turn; however, I cannot wait to hear it.”

“Oh! Mr. Knightley, just a second; something of importance—I am so distressed!—Jane and I are both so troubled regarding the apples!”

“What is the trouble now?”

“To think of you sending us your entire supply of apples. You mentioned having a large quantity, and now you have none remaining. We are truly so distressed! Mrs. Hodges has every right to be annoyed. William Larkins spoke of it here. You really should not have done it, you truly should not. Ah! he has departed. He cannot endure being thanked. Yet I imagined he might linger now, and it would have been a shame not to have brought up.... Well, (re-entering the room,) I have failed. Mr. Knightley cannot remain. He is traveling to Kingston. He inquired if he could assist with anything....”

“Yes,” Jane replied, “we overheard his generous offers, we heard everything.”

“Oh! yes, my dear, I suppose you might have, as the door was ajar, and the window was open, and Mr. Knightley spoke with a loud voice. You must have heard everything, certainly. ‘Can I do anything for you at Kingston?’ he asked; so I simply brought up.... Oh! Miss Woodhouse, must you leave?—You seem to have only just arrived—how very kind of you.”

Emma realized it was truly time to return home; the call had already been lengthy; and upon checking their watches, they saw so much of the morning had vanished that Mrs. Weston and her friend, while departing as well, could only manage to accompany the two young ladies as far as the Hartfield gates before heading toward Randalls.

}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_016",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Everyone was so happy, and she and her partner were the happiest of all! “I told you it would be this way,” was her only consolation.

Her father’s perspective was entirely different. He was primarily concerned with Mrs. Churchill’s illness and wanted to know about her medical treatment. As for the ball, he found it terrible that dear Emma was disappointed, but he felt they would all be safer at home.

Emma was ready for her visitor well before he arrived, but if that suggested he lacked impatience, his sorrowful expression and complete lack of energy when he finally appeared made up for it. He found the prospect of leaving almost too difficult to discuss. His low spirits were obvious. He sat lost in thought for the first few minutes, and when he finally roused himself, it was only to say, “Of all the terrible things, saying goodbye is the worst.”

“But you will come back,” Emma said. “This won’t be your only visit to Randalls.”

“Ah,” he said, shaking his head, “the uncertainty of when I might return! I will try with all my might! It will be the focus of all my thoughts and efforts. If my aunt and uncle go to the city this spring—though I’m afraid they didn’t go last spring—I fear that tradition is gone forever.”

“Our poor ball must be completely called off.”

“Ah, that ball! Why did we wait for anything? Why didn’t we just grab the opportunity for fun? How often is happiness ruined by preparation—foolish preparation! You told us it would happen. Oh, Miss Woodhouse, why are you always so right?”

“Truly, I am very sorry to be right in this case. I would much rather have been happy than wise.”

“If I can return, we are still going to have our ball. My father is counting on it. Don’t forget your promise.”

Emma gave him a gracious look.

“What a fortnight it has been!” he continued. “Every day was more precious and delightful than the last! Every day made me less capable of enduring any other place. How happy are those who can stay in Highbury!”

“Since you are giving us such high praise now,” Emma said with a laugh, “I’ll dare to ask if you didn’t arrive feeling a bit doubtful at first? Don’t we exceed your expectations? I’m sure we do. I’m certain you didn’t expect to like us very much. You wouldn’t have taken so long to visit if you had held a positive view of Highbury.”

He laughed with a sense of self-awareness, and although he denied the feeling, Emma was convinced it had been true.

“And you really must leave this very morning?”

“Yes, my father is meeting me here. We will walk back together, and I must leave immediately. I’m almost afraid he will arrive at any moment.”

“Not even five minutes to spare for your friends Miss Fairfax and Miss Bates? How unfortunate! Miss Bates’s powerful, argumentative mind might have strengthened your own.”

“Actually, I have already stopped by there. Passing the door, I thought it was the right thing to do. I went in for three minutes, but I was held up because Miss Bates was out. She wasn’t there, and I felt I couldn’t leave until she returned.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_017",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "No one is more attached to their own home than I am. I was practically famous for it at Maple Grove. Selina often remarked when departing for Bristol, ‘I simply cannot persuade this girl to leave the house. I am forced to travel alone, even though I detest sitting in the barouche-landau by myself; yet I believe Augusta, if left to her own devices, would never venture past the park fence.’ She said this frequently; nevertheless, I do not support total isolation. On the contrary, I believe it is quite harmful for individuals to cut themselves off from society entirely; it is far better to engage with the world to a reasonable extent, neither overindulging nor withdrawing too much. I fully grasp your circumstances, however, Miss Woodhouse—(glancing toward Mr. Woodhouse), your father’s health must be a significant hindrance. Why does he not attempt a visit to Bath? He really ought to. Allow me to suggest Bath to you. I am certain it would prove beneficial for Mr. Woodhouse.”

“My father attempted it on several occasions in the past, but found no relief; and Mr. Perry, whose reputation I assume you are familiar with, does not believe it would be any more effective at this time.”

“Oh! that is a great shame; for I promise you, Miss Woodhouse, when the waters are effective, the relief they provide is truly remarkable. During my time in Bath, I witnessed such cases! Furthermore, it is such a lively location that it would surely improve Mr. Woodhouse’s spirits, which I hear are sometimes quite low. As for the benefits to you, I suspect I need not elaborate. The advantages of Bath for young people are widely recognized. It would be a delightful introduction for you, having lived such a quiet life; and I could immediately arrange for you to meet some of the finest people there. A brief note from me would provide you with a circle of acquaintances; and my close friend, Mrs. Partridge, with whom I have always stayed while in Bath, would be delighted to assist you and would be the perfect person to accompany you into public settings.”

Emma could barely endure this without being rude. The thought of owing Mrs. Elton for an introduction—of entering society under the protection of one of Mrs. Elton’s friends—likely some common, flashy widow who barely scraped by with the help of a lodger!—the status of Miss Woodhouse of Hartfield would indeed be diminished!

She managed to suppress the rebukes she might have offered, merely thanking Mrs. Elton with reserve; “but a trip to Bath was entirely impossible; and she was not entirely certain that the location would be any more suitable for her than it was for her father.” Then, to avoid further offense and irritation, she shifted the topic immediately.

“I need not inquire if you are musical, Mrs. Elton. In these matters, a lady’s reputation usually arrives before her; and Highbury has long been aware that you are an accomplished performer.”"
}

Selina often remarked when departing for Bristol, ‘I simply cannot persuade this girl to leave the house. I am forced to travel alone, even though I detest sitting in the barouche-landau by myself; yet I believe Augusta, if left to her own devices, would never venture past the park fence.’ She said this frequently; nevertheless, I do not support total isolation. On the contrary, I believe it is quite harmful for individuals to cut themselves off from society entirely; it is far better to engage with the world to a reasonable extent, neither overindulging nor withdrawing too much. I fully grasp your circumstances, however, Miss Woodhouse—(glancing toward Mr. Woodhouse), your father’s health must be a significant hindrance. Why does he not attempt a visit to Bath? He really ought to. Allow me to suggest Bath to you. I am certain it would prove beneficial for Mr. Woodhouse.”

“My father attempted it on several occasions in the past, but found no relief; and Mr. Perry, whose reputation I assume you are familiar with, does not believe it would be any more effective at this time.”

“Oh! that is a great shame; for I promise you, Miss Woodhouse, when the waters are effective, the relief they provide is truly remarkable. During my time in Bath, I witnessed such cases! Furthermore, it is such a lively location that it would surely improve Mr. Woodhouse’s spirits, which I hear are sometimes quite low. As for the benefits to you, I suspect I need not elaborate. The advantages of Bath for young people are widely recognized. It would be a delightful introduction for you, having lived such a quiet life; and I could immediately arrange for you to meet some of the finest people there. A brief note from me would provide you with a circle of acquaintances; and my close friend, Mrs. Partridge, with whom I have always stayed while in Bath, would be delighted to assist you and would be the perfect person to accompany you into public settings.”

Emma could barely endure this without being rude. The thought of owing Mrs. Elton for an introduction—of entering society under the protection of one of Mrs. Elton’s friends—likely some common, flashy widow who barely scraped by with the help of a lodger!—the status of Miss Woodhouse of Hartfield would indeed be diminished!

She managed to suppress the rebukes she might have offered, merely thanking Mrs. Elton with reserve; “but a trip to Bath was entirely impossible; and she was not entirely certain that the location would be any more suitable for her than it was for her father.” Then, to avoid further offense and irritation, she shifted the topic immediately.

“I need not inquire if you are musical, Mrs. Elton. In these matters, a lady’s reputation usually arrives before her; and Highbury has long been aware that you are an accomplished performer.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_018",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“The depth of your regard might catch you unawares at some point.”

Mr. Knightley was occupied with the bottom buttons of his heavy leather gaiters, and whether from the physical effort or some other reason, his face flushed as he replied,

“Oh! Is that your position? You are quite late to the news. Mr. Cole suggested as much to me a month and a half ago.”

He paused. Emma felt Mrs. Weston squeeze her foot, and she was uncertain how to interpret the situation. Shortly, he continued—

“That will never occur, I promise you. Miss Fairfax, I suspect, would decline me if I proposed—and I am certain I shall never ask her.”

Emma squeezed her friend’s hand back firmly; she was sufficiently satisfied to remark,

“You lack vanity, Mr. Knightley. I will grant you that.”

He appeared barely to notice her; he was pensive—and in a manner that indicated his displeasure, he added soon after,

“So you have decided that I ought to wed Jane Fairfax?”

“Certainly not. You have reprimanded me too often for matchmaking for me to dare take such a liberty with you. My previous comment meant nothing. One makes such remarks, naturally, without any serious intent. Oh! No, I truly have no desire for you to marry Jane Fairfax or anyone else. You would not visit and sit with us in this relaxed manner if you were a married man.”

Mr. Knightley grew pensive once more. His reflection concluded with, “No, Emma, I do not believe my admiration for her will ever catch me off guard.—I have never considered her in that light, I assure you.” And shortly after, “Jane Fairfax is a very lovely young lady—but even Jane Fairfax is not flawless. She possesses a defect. She lacks the candid disposition a man would desire in a spouse.”

Emma could not help but be glad to learn she had a flaw. “Well,” she said, “and I assume you quickly quieted Mr. Cole?”

“Indeed, very quickly. He offered a subtle suggestion; I informed him he was in error; he apologized and dropped the subject. Cole has no desire to appear more intelligent or clever than his peers.”

“In that regard, how different from dear Mrs. Elton, who aspires to be wiser and wittier than everyone else! I wonder how she refers to the Coles—what she names them! How can she find a title for them sufficiently steeped in common vulgarity? She addresses you as Knightley—what could she possibly find for Mr. Cole? And so, I am not to be shocked that Jane Fairfax accepts her kindnesses and agrees to spend time with her. Mrs. Weston, your reasoning carries the most weight with me. I can far more easily understand the allure of escaping Miss Bates than I can accept that Miss Fairfax’s intellect could triumph over Mrs. Elton. I do not believe Mrs. Elton would ever admit to being inferior in thought, word, or action; nor do I think she is governed by anything beyond her own limited sense of etiquette. I cannot conceive that she will not constantly offend her guest with patronizing praise, encouragement, and offers of assistance; or that she will not endlessly describe her grand plans, from securing her a lasting position to including her in those wonderful excursions in the barouche-landau.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_018",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“The depth of your admiration might catch you off guard one of these days.”

Mr. Knightley was busy fastening the lower buttons of his thick leather gaiters, and either the effort of pulling them together, or some other reason, caused his face to flush as he replied,

“Oh! Is that where you are? You are terribly behind the times. Mr. Cole gave me a hint about that six weeks ago.”

He paused. Emma felt Mrs. Weston press her foot, and she did not know what to think. In a moment, he continued—

“That will never happen, however, I can assure you. Miss Fairfax, I suspect, would not have me if I were to ask her—and I am quite certain I shall never ask her.”

Emma returned her friend’s pressure with interest and was pleased enough to exclaim,

“You are not vain, Mr. Knightley. I will give you that.”

He seemed barely to hear her; he was pensive—and in a way that showed he was not pleased, he said shortly afterward,

“So you have decided that I should marry Jane Fairfax?”

“No, indeed I have not. You have scolded me too much for matchmaking for me to dare to take such a liberty with you. What I said just now meant nothing. One says those sorts of things, naturally, without any serious intent. Oh! No, upon my word, I have not the slightest wish for you to marry Jane Fairfax or Jane anybody. You would not come in and sit with us in this comfortable way if you were married.”

Mr. Knightley was thoughtful again. The result of his reflection was, “No, Emma, I do not think the extent of my admiration for her will ever catch me by surprise. I never had a thought of her in that way, I assure you.” And soon afterward, “Jane Fairfax is a very charming young woman—but not even Jane Fairfax is perfect. She has a fault. She lacks the open temperament that a man would desire in a wife.”

Emma could not help but rejoice to hear that she had a fault. “Well,” she said, “and I suppose you quickly silenced Mr. Cole?”

“Yes, very quickly. He gave me a quiet hint; I told him he was mistaken; he apologized and said no more. Cole does not feel the need to be wiser or wittier than his neighbors.”

“In that respect, how unlike dear Mrs. Elton, who wants to be wiser and wittier than the entire world! I wonder how she speaks of the Coles—what she calls them! How can she find a label for them deep enough in familiar vulgarity? She calls you ‘Knightley’—what can she possibly do for Mr. Cole? And so I am not to be surprised that Jane Fairfax accepts her civilities and agrees to spend time with her. Mrs. Weston, your argument carries the most weight with me. I can much more easily understand the temptation of getting away from Miss Bates than I can believe in the triumph of Miss Fairfax’s mind over Mrs. Elton. I have no faith in Mrs. Elton acknowledging herself as inferior in thought, word, or deed, or in her being under any restraint beyond her own meager standards of good manners. I cannot imagine that she will not constantly insult her visitor with praise, encouragement, and offers of service; that she will not constantly detail her magnificent intentions, from securing her a permanent position to including her in those delightful exploring parties that are to take place in the barouche-landau.”"
}

“Yes, very quickly. He gave me a quiet hint; I told him he was mistaken; he apologized and said no more. Cole does not try to be wiser or wittier than his neighbors.”

“In that way, he is quite unlike dear Mrs. Elton, who wants to be wiser and wittier than everyone else! I wonder how she speaks of the Coles—what she calls them! How can she find a name for them vulgar enough? She calls you ‘Knightley’—what could she possibly call Mr. Cole? And so, I am not to be surprised that Jane Fairfax accepts her kindness and agrees to spend time with her. Mrs. Weston, your argument carries the most weight with me. I can understand the temptation of getting away from Miss Bates much more easily than I can believe in Miss Fairfax’s ability to endure Mrs. Elton. I have no faith that Mrs. Elton will ever admit she is inferior in thought, word, or deed, or that she will follow any rules other than her own poor sense of manners. I cannot imagine she will not constantly insult her visitor with patronizing praise, encouragement, and offers of help, or that she will not keep detailing her grand plans, from finding her a permanent job to including her in those delightful exploring parties in her carriage.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_021",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "A young beggar approached them, causing Miss Bickerton to shriek in terror. She shouted for Harriet to join her, then scrambled up a steep embankment, vaulted a low hedge, and hurried back toward Highbury via a shortcut. Poor Harriet, however, was unable to keep pace. Having suffered from severe leg cramps after dancing, her initial effort to climb the slope triggered a recurrence that left her completely immobilized, forcing her to stay behind in a state of extreme alarm.

It is uncertain how the vagrants might have acted had the young women shown more bravery, but such an obvious opportunity for harassment proved impossible to ignore. Harriet was quickly beset by six children, led by a robust woman and a large boy, all of whom were noisy and insolent in their demeanor, if not explicitly in their speech. Growing increasingly fearful, she promised them funds, produced her purse, and handed over a shilling, pleading with them to take no more and to do her no harm. She managed to begin walking away, albeit slowly, but her visible panic and the sight of her purse were too enticing; the entire group pursued and encircled her, clamoring for additional money.

Frank Churchill discovered her in this predicament, with Harriet shaking and pleading while the group remained loud and aggressive. By a stroke of immense luck, his departure from Highbury had been postponed, placing him at the scene during this vital moment. The fine weather had encouraged him to travel on foot, intending for his horses to meet him on a different path a mile or two past the village. Furthermore, having borrowed scissors from Miss Bates the previous evening and neglected to return them, he had been forced to stop at her residence for a brief visit. Consequently, he was behind schedule, and because he was walking, the group did not notice him until he was nearly upon them. The fear that the woman and boy had inflicted upon Harriet was suddenly visited upon them. He left them thoroughly intimidated, while Harriet, clutching him tightly and barely capable of speech, possessed just enough stamina to reach Hartfield before her nerves finally collapsed. It was his decision to bring her there; he had considered no other destination.

This comprised the entirety of the account, as related by him and confirmed by Harriet once she regained her composure and voice. He could not linger beyond ensuring her well-being, as his previous delays left him no time to spare. Emma promised to notify Mrs. Goddard of her safety and to alert Mr. Knightley regarding the presence of such individuals in the area, and he departed, accompanied by all the thankful blessings she could offer on behalf of her friend and herself.

An adventure of this nature—a handsome young man and a beautiful young woman brought together under such conditions—could hardly fail to spark certain notions in even the most indifferent heart or logical mind. At least, that was Emma’s assessment. Could a linguist, a grammarian, or even a mathematician have observed what she had, witnessed their arrival together, and listened to their tale without sensing that fate had conspired to make them uniquely significant to one another?"
}

In this helpless and terrified state, she was forced to stay where she was.

It is unclear how the group of travelers might have acted if the young women had shown more courage, but such an invitation for an attack was impossible to resist. Harriet was soon surrounded by half a dozen children, led by a large woman and a tall boy. They were noisy and rude in their appearance, if not explicitly in their words. Growing increasingly frightened, Harriet promised them money. She took out her purse, gave them a shilling, and begged them not to ask for more or treat her badly. She then managed to walk away, though only slowly. However, her obvious terror and her purse were too tempting, and she was followed—or rather surrounded—by the entire group, who demanded more money.

Frank Churchill found her in this situation, with her trembling and pleading while they were loud and aggressive. By a stroke of great luck, his departure from Highbury had been delayed, bringing him to her aid at this critical moment. The pleasant morning had encouraged him to walk ahead and have his horses meet him on a different road a mile or two past Highbury. Because he had borrowed a pair of scissors from Miss Bates the night before and forgotten to return them, he had stopped at her house and gone inside for a few minutes. This made him later than he had planned. Since he was on foot, the group did not see him until he was almost upon them. The fear that the woman and the boy had been inflicting on Harriet was then turned back on them. He left them completely terrified. model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_022",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Mrs. Perry felt quite eager for him to own a carriage, and she visited my mother one morning in high spirits, believing she had finally succeeded. Jane, do you recall grandmother relaying that to us upon our return? I cannot quite place where we had been walking—perhaps to Randalls; yes, I believe it was Randalls. Mrs. Perry always held my mother in high regard—though I suppose everyone does—and she shared this with her privately; she did not mind us knowing, naturally, but it was meant to go no further. Since that day, I have never spoken of it to anyone, to my knowledge. Still, I cannot swear I never let a hint slip, as I know I occasionally blurt things out without thinking. I am a talker, as you know; I am quite a talker, and sometimes I have let something escape that I should have kept quiet. I am not like Jane; I wish I were. I can guarantee she never revealed the slightest secret in her life. Where is she? Oh, right behind us. I remember Mrs. Perry’s visit perfectly. What an unusual dream, indeed!

They were walking into the hall. Mr. Knightley’s gaze had moved to Jane even before Miss Bates’s. From Frank Churchill’s expression, where he thought he detected hidden or dismissed embarrassment, he had instinctively looked toward hers; but she was trailing behind, occupied with her shawl. Mr. Weston had entered. The other two men paused at the doorway to allow her to walk through. Mr. Knightley suspected Frank Churchill of trying to catch her attention—he appeared to be watching her closely—but if so, it was to no avail, as Jane walked between them into the hall without glancing at either man.

There was no opportunity for further commentary or clarification. The dream had to be accepted, and Mr. Knightley had to take his place with the others around the large, modern, round table that Emma had brought into Hartfield. Only Emma could have managed to install it there and convince her father to utilize it rather than the small Pembroke table that had held two of his daily meals for four decades. Tea was served pleasantly, and no one seemed eager to depart.

“Miss Woodhouse,” Frank Churchill remarked after inspecting a table behind him that he could reach from his chair, “have your nephews removed their alphabet box? It used to be kept here. Where is it? This is a rather dreary evening that feels more like winter than summer. We found great entertainment with those letters one morning. I wish to challenge you again.”

Emma liked the idea, and once she produced the box, the table was soon covered in letters, which no one seemed as inclined to use as the two of them. They were quickly constructing words for one another, or for anyone else who might be stumped. The silent nature of the game made it especially suitable for Mr. Woodhouse, who had often been bothered by the more lively activities Mr. Weston occasionally brought in. He now sat contentedly, occupied with lamenting the absence of the “poor little boys” with gentle sadness, or fondly noting, as he picked up any stray letter, how beautifully Emma had written it."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_022",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Mrs. Perry was very eager for him to have a carriage, and she came to see my mother in high spirits one morning because she thought she had convinced him. Jane, don’t you remember grandmother telling us about it when we got home? I forget where we had been walking—very likely to Randalls; yes, I think it was to Randalls. Mrs. Perry was always especially fond of my mother—in fact, I don’t know who isn’t—and she had mentioned it to her in confidence. She had no objection to her telling us, of course, but it was not to go any further, and from that day to this, I have never mentioned it to a soul that I know of. At the same time, I won’t swear that I never dropped a hint, because I know I sometimes blurt things out before I realize it. I am a talker, you know; I am quite a talker, and now and then I have let something slip that I shouldn’t have. I am not like Jane; I wish I were. I can guarantee that she never betrayed the slightest thing in the world. Where is she? Oh, just behind us. I remember Mrs. Perry’s visit perfectly. What an extraordinary dream, indeed!

They were entering the hall. Mr. Knightley’s eyes had looked toward Jane even before Miss Bates’s did. From Frank Churchill’s face, where he thought he saw confusion being suppressed or laughed off, he had involuntarily turned to hers, but she was indeed behind and too busy with her shawl. Mr. Weston had walked in. The two other gentlemen waited at the door to let her pass. Mr. Knightley suspected Frank Churchill of being determined to catch her eye—he seemed to be watching her intently—but it was in vain if that was his goal; Jane passed between them into the hall and looked at neither.

There was no time for further remarks or explanations. The dream had to be accepted, and Mr. Knightley had to take his seat with the rest around the large, modern circular table that Emma had introduced at Hartfield. No one but Emma could have had the influence to put it there and persuade her father to use it instead of the small Pembroke table, on which two of his daily meals had been crowded for forty years. Tea passed pleasantly, and nobody seemed in a hurry to leave.

“Miss Woodhouse,” Frank Churchill said, after examining a table behind him that he could reach from his seat, “have your nephews taken away their alphabets—their box of letters? It used to stand here. Where is it? This is a rather dull-looking evening that ought to be treated more like winter than summer. We had great fun with those letters one morning. I want to puzzle you again.”

Emma was pleased with the idea, and after she produced the box, the table was quickly scattered with letters that no one seemed as eager to use as the two of them. They were rapidly forming words for each other, or for anyone else who wanted to be puzzled. The quiet nature of the game made it particularly suitable for Mr. Woodhouse, who had often been distressed by the more lively games Mr. Weston had occasionally introduced. He now sat happily occupied, lamenting with tender melancholy the departure of the “poor little boys,” or fondly pointing out, as he picked up any stray letter near him, how beautifully Emma had written it."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_023",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "She met them by the wall, discovering that they were occupied with conversation rather than the scenery. He was explaining farming techniques to Harriet, and Emma caught a smile that appeared to convey, “These matters are my personal business. I am entitled to discuss such topics without being accused of bringing up Robert Martin.” She did not harbor such suspicions. That matter was long settled; Robert Martin had likely stopped thinking of Harriet. They strolled together for a while. The shade provided a welcome relief, and Emma considered it the most agreeable portion of the day.

Next, they moved inside the house for a meal. They were all seated and occupied, yet Frank Churchill remained absent. Mrs. Weston searched for him repeatedly without success. His father refused to admit any anxiety, dismissing her concerns with a laugh, though she could not stop wishing he would sell his black mare. He had spoken of his arrival with unusual confidence, claiming his aunt’s improved health left him no doubt he could visit. However, as others were quick to point out, Mrs. Churchill’s condition was prone to sudden shifts that could thwart her nephew’s most logical plans. Mrs. Weston was eventually convinced to accept, or at least claim, that a fresh illness of Mrs. Churchill must have kept him away. Emma observed Harriet during this discussion; she conducted herself admirably, showing no signs of distress.

The light meal concluded, and the group prepared to venture out again to view the remaining sights, specifically the ancient Abbey fish-ponds. They might reach the clover field, scheduled for harvesting the following day, or at the very least, enjoy the sensation of getting warm and cooling down again. Mr. Woodhouse, having already completed his brief walk in the garden’s highest section, where he believed no river dampness could reach, refused to move further. His daughter decided to stay with him so that Mr. Weston could encourage his wife to seek the exercise and change of scenery her mood seemed to require.

Mr. Knightley had done everything possible to entertain Mr. Woodhouse. Volumes of prints, cases of medals, cameos, corals, shells, and every other family treasure from his cabinets had been arranged for his elderly friend to pass the morning, and the gesture had succeeded perfectly. Mr. Woodhouse had been thoroughly entertained. Mrs. Weston had displayed them all to him, and now he insisted on showing them all to Emma. He resembled a child only in his complete lack of appreciation for the items, as he remained slow, steady, and systematic. Before this second viewing commenced, however, Emma stepped into the hall to observe the house’s entrance and layout for a few moments. She had barely arrived when Jane Fairfax appeared, hurrying in from the garden with the expression of someone fleeing. Surprised to encounter Miss Woodhouse so unexpectedly, she started, yet Miss Woodhouse was the very person she had been seeking."
}

Mrs. Weston was finally persuaded to believe, or at least to say, that some health crisis must have prevented his arrival. Emma glanced at Harriet while this was being discussed; she handled it well and showed no emotion.

The cold meal finished, the group prepared to head out again to see the old Abbey fish-ponds, perhaps walk as far as the clover field that was to be harvested the next day, or at least enjoy the experience of getting hot and cooling down again. Mr. Woodhouse, who had already taken his short walk in the highest part of the gardens where he felt safe from river dampness, moved no further. His daughter decided to stay with him so that Mr. Weston could convince his wife to go out for the exercise and change of pace her spirits seemed to require.

Mr. Knightley had done everything possible to entertain Mr. Woodhouse. Books of engravings, drawers of medals, cameos, corals, shells, and every other family collection in his cabinets had been set out for his old friend to pass the morning, and the gesture had worked perfectly. Mr. Woodhouse had been thoroughly entertained. Mrs. Weston had shown them all to him, and now he wanted to show them all to Emma—fortunate in having no resemblance to a child other than a complete lack of interest in what he was looking at, for he was slow, steady, and methodical. Before this second viewing began, however, Emma walked into the hall for a few moments of quiet observation of the house’s entrance and layout. She had barely arrived when Jane Fairfax appeared, hurrying in from the garden with a look of escape. Not expecting to run into Miss Woodhouse so quickly, she started at first, but Miss Woodhouse was the very person she had been looking for."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_024",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Entrusting the instruction to her appeared to suggest it.

“Now, ma’am,” Jane remarked to her aunt, “shall we go find Mrs. Elton?”

“If you like, my dear. With all my heart. I am perfectly prepared. I was prepared to have departed with her, but this will serve just as well. We shall quickly catch up to her. There she is—no, that is someone else. That is one of the women from the Irish carriage group, not at all resembling her.—Well, I declare—”

They departed, trailed shortly thereafter by Mr. Knightley. Mr. Weston, his son, Emma, and Harriet remained, and the young man’s mood now climbed to a level nearly disagreeable. Even Emma eventually grew weary of the praise and gaiety, wishing instead to be strolling calmly with any of the others, or sitting nearly solitary, and entirely ignored, in peaceful contemplation of the lovely scenery below her. The sight of the attendants searching for them to announce the carriages was a welcome relief; and even the commotion of gathering and getting ready to leave, and the eagerness of Mrs. Elton to have her carriage arrive first, were patiently tolerated, in anticipation of the peaceful ride home that would conclude the highly dubious pleasures of this day of enjoyment. She hoped never to be lured into another such plan, made up of so many mismatched individuals, again.

While expecting the carriage, she discovered Mr. Knightley at her elbow. He glanced about, as if to ensure no one was nearby, and then remarked,

“Emma, I must speak to you once more as I have been accustomed to do: a right perhaps merely tolerated rather than permitted, yet I must still exercise it. I cannot watch you behaving incorrectly without a protest. How could you be so heartless toward Miss Bates? How could you be so rude in your humor toward a woman of her standing, years, and circumstances?—Emma, I had not imagined it possible.”

Emma remembered, flushed, felt regret, but attempted to dismiss it with a laugh.

“Why, how could I avoid saying what I did?—No one could have avoided it. It was not that terrible. I suspect she did not grasp my meaning.”

“I can confirm she did. She understood your entire intent. She has spoken of it since. I wish you could have heard how she spoke of it—with such fairness and kindness. I wish you could have heard her praising your patience, in being capable of showing her such kindness, as she was constantly receiving from you and your father, when her company must be so tiresome.”

“Oh!” Emma exclaimed, “I am aware there is not a finer person in existence: but you must concede, that what is virtuous and what is absurd are most unhappily mixed in her.”

“They are mixed,” he replied, “I concede; and, were she wealthy, I could excuse much for the occasional dominance of the absurd over the virtuous. Were she a woman of means, I would permit every harmless folly to pass, I would not argue with you for any freedoms of behavior. Were she your peer in status—but, Emma, reflect on how far this is from being the truth. She is indigent; she has fallen from the luxuries she was accustomed to; and, if she survives to old age, must likely fall further.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_024",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Leaving the education to her seemed to imply it.

“Now, ma’am,” Jane said to her aunt, “shall we join Mrs. Elton?”

“If you like, my dear. With all my heart. I am quite ready. I was ready to go with her, but this will do just as well. We shall soon catch up to her. There she is—no, that’s someone else. That’s one of the ladies from the Irish car party, not at all like her.—Well, I declare—”

They walked off, followed half a minute later by Mr. Knightley. Mr. Weston, his son, Emma, and Harriet remained, and the young man’s spirits rose to a level that was almost unpleasant. Even Emma eventually grew tired of the flattery and merriment, wishing instead to be walking quietly with any of the others, or sitting almost alone and completely ignored, peacefully observing the beautiful views below her. The sight of the servants looking for them to announce the carriages was a welcome one; even the chaos of gathering their things and preparing to leave, along with Mrs. Elton’s insistence on having her carriage first, were endured gladly in anticipation of the quiet drive home that would conclude the questionable enjoyments of this day. She hoped never to be tricked into another outing like this one, composed of so many mismatched people.

While waiting for the carriage, she found Mr. Knightley at her side. He looked around to ensure no one was nearby, then said,

“Emma, I must speak to you once more as I have in the past: a privilege that is perhaps tolerated rather than permitted, but I must still exercise it. I cannot watch you acting wrongly without speaking up. How could you be so unfeeling toward Miss Bates? How could you be so insolent with your wit toward a woman of her character, age, and situation?—Emma, I did not think it possible.”

Emma remembered, blushed, and felt sorry, but tried to laugh it off.

“Well, how could I help saying what I did?—Nobody could have helped it. It wasn’t that bad. I dare say she didn’t understand me.”

“I assure you she did. She understood exactly what you meant. She has spoken of it since. I wish you could have heard how she talked about it—with such candor and generosity. I wish you could have heard her praising your patience in being able to show her the attentions she was always receiving from you and your father, when her company must be so tiresome.”

“Oh!” Emma cried, “I know there isn’t a better person in the world: but you must admit that what is good and what is ridiculous are unfortunately blended in her.”

“They are blended,” he said, “I acknowledge that; and if she were prosperous, I could forgive the occasional dominance of the ridiculous over the good. If she were a woman of means, I would leave every harmless absurdity to take its course, and I wouldn’t argue with you about any liberties you took. If she were your equal in status—but, Emma, consider how far that is from the truth. She is poor; she has fallen from the comforts she was born into; and if she lives to old age, she will likely fall further.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_026",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "If only the revelation could mirror that situation!—But, regrettably, that was impossible.

“Well, Miss Woodhouse!” Harriet exclaimed, rushing into the chamber—“is this not the most peculiar news imaginable?”

“To what news do you refer?” Emma asked, unable to discern from her expression or tone if Harriet had actually been tipped off.

“Regarding Jane Fairfax. Have you ever heard anything so bizarre? Oh!—you need not hesitate to admit it to me, for Mr. Weston informed me himself. I encountered him just now. He mentioned it was meant to be a major secret; therefore, I would not dream of discussing it with anyone but you, yet he claimed you were aware of it.”

“What did Mr. Weston disclose to you?” Emma inquired, still bewildered.

“Oh! he explained everything; that Jane Fairfax and Mr. Frank Churchill are to wed, and that they have been secretly betrothed for quite some time. How truly strange!”

It was indeed so strange; Harriet’s conduct was so remarkably odd that Emma struggled to interpret it. Her personality seemed entirely transformed. She appeared to exhibit no distress, disappointment, or particular anxiety regarding the news. Emma stared at her, utterly speechless.

“Did you have any inkling,” Harriet asked, “that he was in love with her?—You, perhaps, might have.—You (flushing as she spoke) who can perceive everyone’s inner thoughts; but no one else—”

“Honestly,” Emma remarked, “I am beginning to question if I possess such a gift. Can you truly ask me, Harriet, if I suspected him of being attached to another woman while I was—implicitly, if not explicitly—urging you to indulge your own emotions?—I never had the faintest suspicion, until this past hour, that Mr. Frank Churchill held any affection for Jane Fairfax. You may be certain that had I known, I would have warned you.”

“Me!” Harriet exclaimed, blushing and surprised. “Why would you warn me?—You cannot think I have feelings for Mr. Frank Churchill.”

“I am thrilled to hear you speak so firmly on the matter,” Emma replied, smiling; “but you do not intend to deny that there was a period—and not long ago—when you led me to believe that you did care for him?”

“Him!—never, never. Dear Miss Woodhouse, how could you so misinterpret me?” she said, turning away in distress.

“Harriet!” Emma cried, after a brief silence—“What do you mean?—Good Heavens! what do you mean?—Misinterpret you!—Am I to conclude then?—”

She could not utter another syllable. Her voice failed her, and she sat down, waiting in great alarm for Harriet to respond.

Harriet, who stood some distance away with her back turned, did not reply at once; and when she finally spoke, her tone was nearly as shaken as Emma’s.

“I would not have believed it possible,” she began, “that you could have misunderstood me! I know we agreed never to mention his name—but considering how vastly superior he is to everyone else, I would not have thought it possible that I could be taken to mean anyone else. Mr. Frank Churchill, indeed! I cannot imagine who would ever notice him when in the presence of the other.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_027",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“A predictable result of the trouble she had brought upon herself,” she remarked, “was that it rendered her irrational. The awareness of her own wrongdoing left her vulnerable to endless anxieties, making her touchy and short-tempered to an extent that must have been—that certainly was—difficult for him to endure. ‘I failed to make the necessary allowances,’ she admitted, ‘for his temperament and his mood—his wonderful spirits, and that cheerfulness and playful nature which, in any other situation, I am certain would have remained as consistently charming to me as they were in the beginning.’ She proceeded to talk about you, and the immense kindness you demonstrated during her sickness; then, with a flush that revealed the underlying connection, she asked me, whenever I had the chance, to thank you—I could not thank you enough—for every desire and effort you made to assist her. She realized that you had never been properly thanked by her personally.”

“If I were not certain she is happy now,” Emma stated gravely, “which, despite any minor reservations from her meticulous conscience, she must be, I could not endure these expressions of gratitude; for, oh! Mrs. Weston, if one were to tally the harm and the good I have caused Miss Fairfax!—Well (stopping herself, and attempting a lighter tone), all of this must be put aside. You are very generous to share these compelling details. They present her in the best possible light. I am certain she is very virtuous—I hope she will be very happy. It is appropriate that the wealth should be on his side, for I believe the true merit belongs entirely to her.”

Such a final remark could not go unchallenged by Mrs. Weston. She held a high opinion of Frank in nearly every regard; and, furthermore, she cared for him deeply, so her defense was passionate. She spoke with considerable logic, and at least as much affection—but she had too many points to make for Emma to follow; her thoughts soon drifted to Brunswick Square or Donwell; she stopped trying to pay attention; and when Mrs. Weston concluded with, “We have not yet received the letter we are so eager for, you know, but I trust it will arrive shortly,” she had to hesitate before replying, and eventually had to respond vaguely, before she could even recall which letter they were so anxious to receive.

“Are you feeling well, my Emma?” was Mrs. Weston’s final inquiry.

“Oh! perfectly. I am always well, as you know. Make sure to inform me about the letter as soon as you can.”

Mrs. Weston’s news provided Emma with further material for uncomfortable contemplation, by deepening her respect and sympathy, as well as her awareness of her previous unfairness toward Miss Fairfax. She felt sharp regret for not having pursued a deeper friendship with her, and felt ashamed of the jealous impulses that had surely played some part. Had she acted upon Mr. Knightley’s known desires, by showing Miss Fairfax the consideration she fully deserved; had she attempted to understand her better; had she done her share to foster a bond; had she sought a friend in her rather than in Harriet Smith; she would, in all likelihood, have been saved from every distress that burdened her now."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_027",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“One natural consequence of the trouble she had brought upon herself,” she said, “was that it made her unreasonable. The awareness of having acted wrongly exposed her to endless anxieties, making her irritable and touchy to a degree that must have been—that certainly was—difficult for him to endure. ‘I did not make the allowances,’ she said, ‘that I should have made for his temperament and spirits—his delightful spirits, and that gaiety, that playful disposition, which, under any other circumstances, I am certain would have been as constantly charming to me as they were at first.’ She then began to speak of you, and of the great kindness you showed her during her illness; and with a blush that revealed how it was all connected, she asked me, whenever I had the opportunity, to thank you—I could not thank you enough—for every wish and every effort to help her. She was aware that you had never received any proper acknowledgment from her personally.”

“If I did not know her to be happy now,” Emma said seriously, “which, despite every little reservation from her scrupulous conscience, she must be, I could not bear these thanks; for, oh! Mrs. Weston, if there were a ledger drawn up of the harm and the good I have done Miss Fairfax!—Well (checking herself, and trying to sound more lively), this should all be forgotten. You are very kind to bring me these interesting details. They show her in the best possible light. I am sure she is very good—I hope she will be very happy. It is fitting that the fortune should be on his side, for I think the merit will be entirely on hers.”

Such a conclusion could not go unanswered by Mrs. Weston. She thought well of Frank in almost every respect; and, what was more, she loved him very much, so her defense was earnest. She spoke with a great deal of reason, and at least equal affection—but she had too much to say for Emma’s attention; it had soon drifted to Brunswick Square or to Donwell; she forgot to try to listen; and when Mrs. Weston finished with, “We have not yet had the letter we are so anxious for, you know, but I hope it will soon arrive,” she had to pause before she answered, and was finally forced to reply at random, before she could even recall which letter it was that they were so anxious for.

“Are you well, my Emma?” was Mrs. Weston’s parting question.

“Oh! perfectly. I am always well, you know. Be sure to give me news of the letter as soon as possible.”

Mrs. Weston’s news provided Emma with more food for unpleasant reflection, by increasing her esteem and compassion, and her sense of past injustice toward Miss Fairfax. She bitterly regretted not having sought a closer acquaintance with her, and blushed for the envious feelings which had certainly been, in some measure, the cause. Had she followed Mr. Knightley’s known wishes, in paying that attention to Miss Fairfax which was in every way her due; had she tried to know her better; had she done her part toward intimacy; had she endeavored to find a friend there instead of in Harriet Smith; she would, in all probability, have been spared every pain that pressed on her now."
}

“For when a lady’s in the case, 
“You know all other things give place.” 

Now I say, my dear, in our case, instead of lady, read—mum! a word to the wise.—I am in a wonderful mood, am I not? But I want to reassure you regarding Mrs. S.—My explanation, you see, has completely calmed her.” 

And later, when Emma simply turned her head to observe Mrs. Bates’s knitting, she added in a low whisper, 

“I mentioned no names, you will notice.—Oh! no; I am as cautious as a diplomat. I handled it perfectly.” 

Emma could not be mistaken. It was an obvious display, repeated at every opportunity. Once they had all chatted for a while about the weather and Mrs. Weston, she was suddenly addressed with, 

“Do you not think, Miss Woodhouse, that our cheeky little friend here has recovered beautifully?—Do you not think her recovery reflects great credit upon Perry?—(here she gave a very significant side-glance at Jane.) model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_030",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He hesitated briefly, smiling once more while keeping his gaze upon her. “Does nothing come to mind? Do you not recall? Harriet Smith.”

Her face reddened at the mention, and she experienced a vague sense of apprehension, though she could not define its source.

“Have you received word from her today?” he asked. “I suspect you have, and are fully informed.”

“No, I have not; I am entirely ignorant; please inform me.”

“I see you are braced for the worst—and it is indeed quite bad. Harriet Smith is to marry Robert Martin.”

Emma recoiled, appearing far from prepared, and while her wide eyes signaled, “No, this cannot be!” her mouth remained shut.

“It is true,” Mr. Knightley persisted; “I heard it from Robert Martin himself. He departed from me less than thirty minutes ago.”

She continued to stare at him in profound astonishment.

“I see you are as displeased as I anticipated. I wish we shared the same view. Yet, in time, we shall. Time will surely lead one of us to alter our perspective; until then, we need not discuss it further.”

“You misunderstand me, you entirely misunderstand,” she answered, forcing herself to speak. “It is not that such news would distress me now, but I find it incredible. It seems impossible! You cannot be suggesting that Harriet Smith has agreed to marry Robert Martin. You cannot mean he has even proposed to her again—not yet. You only mean he intends to.”

“I mean that he has already done so,” Mr. Knightley replied with a smile of firm conviction, “and has been accepted.”

“Good heavens!” she exclaimed. “Well!” Then, turning to her sewing basket to hide her face and conceal the intense joy and amusement she knew must be visible, she added, “Well, tell me everything; explain this to me. How, where, when? I must know it all. I have never been more astonished—but I promise you, it does not make me unhappy. How could this possibly have happened?”

“It is a straightforward tale. He traveled to town on errands three days ago, and I requested that he carry some documents I needed to send to John. He brought these papers to John’s chambers, and was invited to join their group that evening at Astley’s. They were taking the two eldest boys to the theater. The party consisted of our brother and sister, Henry, John, and Miss Smith. My friend Robert could not refuse. They collected him on their way; they all enjoyed themselves immensely; and my brother invited him to dine with them the following day—which he did—and during that visit, as I understand it, he found a moment to speak with Harriet; he certainly did not speak in vain. By accepting him, she has made him as happy as he is worthy of being. He returned on yesterday’s coach and visited me this morning right after breakfast to share his news, first regarding my business, then his own. That is all I can tell you of the how, where, and when. Your friend Harriet will provide a much more detailed account when you see her.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_EMMA_030",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "He paused for a moment, smiling again, his eyes fixed on her face. “Does nothing come to mind? Don’t you remember? Harriet Smith.”

Her cheeks flushed at the name, and she felt a sense of dread, though she did not know why.

“Have you heard from her yourself this morning?” he asked. “I believe you have, and that you know the whole story.”

“No, I haven’t; I know nothing; please tell me.”

“I see you are prepared for the worst—and it is very bad. Harriet Smith is marrying Robert Martin.”

Emma started, which did not look like being prepared, and her wide-eyed gaze seemed to say, “No, this is impossible!” but she kept her lips sealed.

“It is true, indeed,” Mr. Knightley continued. “I heard it from Robert Martin himself. He left me less than half an hour ago.”

She continued to look at him with profound astonishment.

“You are as unhappy about it as I feared, my Emma. I wish we saw eye to eye, but in time we will. Time will certainly make one of us change our perspective, and in the meantime, we don’t need to discuss it much.”

“You misunderstand me, you completely misunderstand me,” she replied, forcing herself to speak. “It isn’t that such news would make me unhappy now, but I cannot believe it. It seems impossible! You cannot mean that Harriet Smith has accepted Robert Martin. You cannot mean that he has even proposed to her again—yet. You only mean that he intends to.”

“I mean that he has already done it,” Mr. Knightley answered with a smile but firm conviction, “and has been accepted.”

“Good heavens!” she cried. “Well!” Then, reaching for her workbasket as an excuse to lean down and hide the intense feelings of delight and amusement she knew were showing on her face, she added, “Well, tell me everything now; make me understand. How, where, when? Let me know it all. I have never been more surprised—but I assure you, it does not make me unhappy. How has this been possible?”

“It is a very simple story. He went to town on business three days ago, and I asked him to take charge of some papers I needed to send to John. He delivered them to John at his office, and John invited him to join their group that evening at Astley’s. They were taking the two eldest boys. The party consisted of my brother and sister, Henry, John, and Miss Smith. My friend Robert could not resist. They picked him up on their way; they all had a wonderful time, and my brother invited him to dinner the next day. He went, and during that visit, as I understand it, he found an opportunity to speak to Harriet, and he certainly did not speak in vain. By accepting him, she made him as happy as he deserves to be. He returned on yesterday’s coach and came to see me this morning right after breakfast to report on his progress—first regarding my business, then his own. That is all I can tell you of the how, where, and when. Your friend Harriet will provide a much longer account when you see her.”"
}

  "rewritten_text": "He paused for a moment, smiling again as he looked directly at her. “Does nothing come to mind? Do you not remember? Harriet Smith.”

Her cheeks flushed at the name, and she felt a sudden sense of alarm, though she did not know why.

“Have you heard from her yourself this morning?” he asked. “I believe you have, and that you know the whole story.”

“No, I have not; I know nothing. Please, tell me.”

“I see you are prepared for the worst—and it is very bad. Harriet Smith is marrying Robert Martin.”

Emma jumped, which did not look like she was prepared at all. Her eyes stared in disbelief, clearly saying, “No, this is impossible!” but she kept her lips pressed together.

“It is true,” Mr. Knightley continued. “I heard it from Robert Martin himself. He left me less than half an hour ago.”

She continued to look at him with complete astonishment.

“I see you are as unhappy about this as I feared,” he said. “I wish we agreed, but in time we will. Time will surely make one of us change our mind, and until then, we do not need to discuss it much.”

“You misunderstand me, you completely misunderstand me,” she replied, forcing herself to speak. “It is not that this news makes me unhappy, but I cannot believe it. It seems impossible! You cannot mean that Harriet Smith has accepted Robert Martin. You cannot mean that he has even proposed to her again yet. You only mean that he intends to.”

“I mean that he has already done it,” Mr. Knightley answered with a smile but firm certainty, “and that she has accepted him.”

“Good God!” she cried. “Well!” Then, reaching for her workbasket to hide her face and conceal the intense feelings of delight and amusement she knew were showing, she added, “Well, tell me everything now. Make this make sense to me. How, where, when? Let me know it all. I have never been more surprised, but I assure you, it does not make me unhappy. How has this been possible?”

“It is a very simple story. He went to town on business three days ago, and I asked him to deliver some papers to John. He took them to John’s office, and John invited him to join their group that evening at Astley’s. They were taking the two oldest boys to the theater. The group included my brother and sister, Henry, John, and Miss Smith. My friend Robert could not resist. They picked him up on their way, they all had a wonderful time, and my brother invited him to dinner the next day. During that visit, as I understand it, he found a chance to speak to Harriet, and he certainly did not speak in vain. By accepting him, she made him as happy as he deserves to be. He returned on the coach yesterday and came to see me this morning right after breakfast to report on his progress—first regarding my business, and then his own. That is all I can tell you about the how, where, and when. Your friend Harriet will have a much longer story to tell when you see her.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_002",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Certainly not.”

“To trek three, four, or five miles, or whatever the distance, through mud well above her ankles, and entirely by herself! What could she possibly intend by such behavior? To me, it demonstrates a detestable sort of arrogant self-reliance, a provincial disregard for proper conduct.”

“It demonstrates a devotion to her sister that is truly charming,” Bingley remarked.

“I fear, Mr. Darcy,” Miss Bingley whispered, “that this escapade has somewhat diminished your appreciation for her beautiful eyes.”

“Not in the least,” he answered: “the physical exertion only made them more brilliant.” A brief silence ensued, and Mrs. Hurst resumed the conversation:

“I have a great fondness for Jane Bennet—she is truly a lovely girl—and I sincerely hope she finds a good match. Yet, given such parents and such inferior relatives, I worry there is little hope for it.”

“I believe I heard you mention that their uncle is a lawyer in Meryton?”

“Indeed; and they possess another who resides somewhere in the vicinity of Cheapside.”

“That is marvelous,” her sister chimed in; and they both erupted in laughter.

“Even if they had enough uncles to populate all of Cheapside,” Bingley exclaimed, “it would not render them even slightly less charming.”

“Nevertheless, it must significantly reduce their prospects of marrying men of any social standing,” Darcy countered.

Bingley offered no reply to this, but his sisters voiced their full agreement, continuing to mock their dear friend’s common family for some time.

Returning to their displays of affection, they visited her room after leaving the dining area and remained until called for coffee. She remained quite ill, and Elizabeth refused to leave her side until late in the evening, when she felt relieved to see her sleeping, deciding it was more appropriate than enjoyable to head downstairs. Upon entering the drawing-room, she discovered the group playing loo and was promptly asked to join; however, suspecting the stakes were high, she refused, citing her sister as the reason and stating she would entertain herself with a book for the brief time she could remain. Mr. Hurst stared at her in disbelief.

“You choose reading over cards?” he asked; “that is quite unusual.”

“Miss Eliza Bennet,” Miss Bingley stated, “looks down on cards. She is an avid reader and finds no enjoyment in anything else.”

“I merit neither such commendation nor such criticism,” Elizabeth replied; “I am not an avid reader, and I find pleasure in many activities.”

“I am certain you find pleasure in caring for your sister,” Bingley said; “and I trust that joy will soon grow when you see her fully recovered.”

Elizabeth thanked him sincerely and moved toward a table where several volumes were placed. He instantly volunteered to retrieve others for her from his library.

“I only wish my library were more extensive for your sake and my own reputation; but I am a lazy man; and although I do not own many, I possess more than I have ever opened.”

Elizabeth promised him that she was perfectly satisfied with the books available in the room.

“I am amazed,” Miss Bingley remarked, “that my father left such a meager library.”"
}

Upon entering the drawing room, she found the group playing loo and was immediately asked to join; however, suspecting the stakes were high, she declined. Using her sister as an excuse, she said she would entertain herself with a book for the short time she could stay. Mr. Hurst looked at her in disbelief.

“Do you prefer reading to cards?” he asked. “That is quite unusual.”

“Miss Eliza Bennet,” Miss Bingley said, “looks down on cards. She is a serious reader and finds no pleasure in anything else.”

“I deserve neither that praise nor that criticism,” Elizabeth replied. “I am not a serious reader, and I find pleasure in many things.”

“I am sure you find pleasure in nursing your sister,” Bingley said, “and I hope that pleasure will soon increase when you see her fully recovered.”

Elizabeth thanked him sincerely and walked toward a table where a few books were lying. He immediately offered to bring her others from his library.

“I wish my collection were larger, for your sake and my own reputation, but I am a lazy man. Although I don’t have many, I have more than I have ever actually read.”

Elizabeth assured him that she was perfectly happy with the books already in the room.

“I am amazed,” Miss Bingley said, “that my father left such a small collection of books.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_003",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I am not specifically referring to the scenario you imagined regarding Mr. Bingley. It might be better to wait until such an event actually happens before we debate the wisdom of his conduct. However, in typical situations between friends, if one person asks the other to alter a decision of little consequence, would you consider it wrong for that individual to agree without requiring a lengthy debate?”

“Would it not be prudent, before we continue this topic, to define with greater accuracy the level of significance attached to this request, as well as the closeness of the relationship between the individuals involved?”

“Certainly,” Bingley exclaimed; “let us have every detail, including their relative height and build, as that will carry more influence in the discussion, Miss Bennet, than you might realize. I promise you that if Darcy were not such a large, tall man compared to me, I would not show him nearly as much respect. I must say I know of nothing more intimidating than Darcy at certain times and in certain settings; particularly at his own residence, and on a Sunday evening, when he has no tasks to occupy him.”

Mr. Darcy offered a smile; yet Elizabeth felt she could detect that he was somewhat insulted, so she suppressed her laughter. Miss Bingley reacted with indignation to the slight he had suffered, scolding her brother for speaking such foolishness.

“I understand your intent, Bingley,” his friend remarked. “You are averse to debate and wish to end this one.”

“Perhaps I do. Arguments feel too much like quarrels. If you and Miss Bennet would postpone yours until I have left the room, I would be very grateful; then you may speak of me however you please.”

“What you request,” Elizabeth replied, “is no hardship for me; and Mr. Darcy would be much better off finishing his correspondence.”

Mr. Darcy followed her suggestion and completed his letter.

Once that task was finished, he asked Miss Bingley and Elizabeth to favor them with some music. Miss Bingley went eagerly to the piano, and after a courteous invitation for Elizabeth to play first, which the other declined just as politely and with more conviction, she took her seat.

Mrs. Hurst performed with her sister; and while they were occupied, Elizabeth could not help noticing, as she browsed through some music books resting on the instrument, how often Mr. Darcy’s gaze rested upon her. She found it difficult to believe she could be a subject of admiration for such a prominent man, yet the idea that he watched her because he disapproved of her seemed even more peculiar. She eventually concluded, however, that she caught his attention because there was something about her more flawed and objectionable, by his standards of conduct, than in anyone else in the room. This thought did not distress her. She held him in too little regard to be concerned with his approval.

Following a few Italian melodies, Miss Bingley changed the mood with a spirited Scotch tune; and shortly thereafter Mr. Darcy, approaching Elizabeth, asked her,--

“Do you not feel a strong urge, Miss Bennet, to take advantage of such a chance to dance a reel?”"
}

“By all means,” Bingley exclaimed; “let’s hear all the details, not forgetting their relative height and size, because that will carry more weight in the argument, Miss Bennet, than you might realize. I honestly don't know a more intimidating sight than Darcy on certain occasions and in certain places; especially at his own home on a Sunday evening when he has nothing to do.”

Mr. Darcy smiled, but Elizabeth thought she could tell he was slightly offended, so she suppressed her laugh. Miss Bingley angrily resented the insult he had received, scolding her brother for talking such nonsense.

“I see what you’re doing, Bingley,” his friend said. “You dislike arguing and want to shut this down.”

“Perhaps I do. Arguments are too much like fights. If you and Miss Bennet will postpone yours until I’ve left the room, I would be very grateful; then you can say whatever you like about me.”

She found it hard to believe she could be an object of admiration to such an important man, yet it seemed even stranger that he would look at her because he disliked her. She could only conclude, finally, that she caught his attention because there was something about her more flawed and objectionable, by his standards, than in anyone else present. This thought didn't bother her. She liked him too little to care about his approval.

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_004",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Elizabeth, however, remained firmly determined against prolonging their visit, and she did not anticipate being invited to stay. Worried that they might be viewed as overstaying their welcome, she pressed Jane to request Mr. Bingley’s carriage at once. Eventually, they agreed to bring up their initial plan to depart Netherfield that morning and make the request.

This announcement prompted many expressions of regret. Enough was said about wanting them to remain until the following day to influence Jane, and their departure was postponed until the next morning. Miss Bingley then regretted suggesting the delay, as her envy and resentment toward one sister far outweighed her fondness for the other.

The host learned with genuine sadness that they were leaving so soon, and he repeatedly attempted to convince Miss Bennet that it would be unsafe for her to travel, as she had not fully recovered. Yet, Jane remained resolute when she believed she was acting correctly.

For Mr. Darcy, this was pleasant news; Elizabeth had been at Netherfield long enough. She drew his attention more than he cared to admit, and Miss Bingley was being rude to her while pestering him more than usual. He wisely decided to be extremely cautious so that no hint of admiration would slip out—nothing that might encourage her to think she could affect his happiness. He realized that if such a notion had been planted, his conduct during this final day would be crucial in either fostering or destroying it. Committed to his plan, he barely spoke ten words to her throughout Saturday. Even when they were left alone for thirty minutes, he remained dutifully focused on his book, refusing to even glance in her direction.

On Sunday, following the morning service, the departure—which was pleasing to nearly everyone—finally occurred. Miss Bingley’s politeness toward Elizabeth grew quite rapidly at the end, as did her warmth toward Jane. Upon parting, she assured Jane of how delighted she would be to see her at either Longbourn or Netherfield, hugged her affectionately, and even shook hands with Elizabeth. Elizabeth said her goodbyes to the group in high spirits.

They did not receive a warm welcome from their mother upon returning home. Mrs. Bennet was surprised by their arrival, felt they had been quite inconsiderate to cause such trouble, and was certain Jane would catch a cold again. Conversely, their father, though brief in his remarks, was truly happy to have them back; he had missed their presence in the family. The evening discussions, when the family gathered, had lacked much of their usual energy and intelligence while Jane and Elizabeth were away.

They discovered Mary, as was her habit, immersed in the study of music theory and human nature, and they were forced to admire new excerpts and listen to fresh observations on worn-out moral themes. Catherine and Lydia had different news to share. Much had occurred and been discussed regarding the regiment since the previous Wednesday; several officers had recently dined with their uncle, a soldier had been whipped, and there was even a rumor that Colonel Forster was planning to marry."
}

On Sunday, after the morning service, the separation—which was agreeable to almost everyone—took place. Miss Bingley’s politeness toward Elizabeth increased rapidly at the end, as did her affection for Jane. When they parted, after assuring Jane of the pleasure it would always give her to see her at Longbourn or Netherfield and embracing her tenderly, she even shook hands with Elizabeth. Elizabeth said goodbye to the whole group in high spirits.

They were not welcomed home very warmly by their mother. Mrs. Bennet was surprised by their return, thought they were wrong to cause so much trouble, and was certain Jane would catch a cold again. But their father, though brief in his expressions of pleasure, was genuinely glad to see them; he had felt their importance in the family circle. The evening conversation, when they were all together, had lost much of its energy and almost all its sense during the absence of Jane and Elizabeth.

They found Mary, as usual, deep in the study of music theory and human nature, and had to admire some new excerpts and listen to some new observations on worn-out morality. Catherine and Lydia had different news for them. Much had happened and been said in the regiment since the previous Wednesday; several officers had recently dined with their uncle, a private had been flogged, and there was even a rumor that Colonel Forster was going to get married."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_005",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "His intentions remained unchanged upon observing them. Miss Bennet’s beautiful countenance validated his perspective and reinforced his rigid ideas regarding the privileges of the eldest; consequently, for the initial evening, she remained his decided preference. The following morning, however, brought a shift; a fifteen-minute private discussion with Mrs. Bennet prior to breakfast, which started with his parsonage and progressed toward his desire to find a mistress for it at Longbourn, prompted her, amidst gracious smiles and broad encouragement, to warn him against the very Jane he had selected. Regarding her younger girls, she could not speak with certainty, but she was unaware of any existing attachments; however, she felt obligated to suggest that her eldest daughter would likely be betrothed quite soon.

Mr. Collins merely needed to shift his focus from Jane to Elizabeth—a transition accomplished quickly while Mrs. Bennet tended the fire. Elizabeth, being next in both age and attractiveness to Jane, naturally took her place.

Mrs. Bennet carefully noted this suggestion, hoping she might soon see two of her daughters wed; the man she had despised mentioning the previous day was now held in high regard.

Lydia’s plan to walk to Meryton remained in place: every sister except Mary decided to accompany her. Mr. Collins was to join them at the urging of Mr. Bennet, who was desperate to escape him and reclaim his library. Mr. Collins had pursued him there after breakfast, where he remained, supposedly occupied with a massive volume, but actually speaking to Mr. Bennet almost incessantly about his residence and grounds at Hunsford. Such behavior greatly unsettled Mr. Bennet. He had always relied on his library for peace and quiet; although he had warned Elizabeth that he expected to encounter foolishness and vanity elsewhere in the house, he was accustomed to being undisturbed there. His politeness, therefore, was swift in suggesting that Mr. Collins accompany his daughters on their stroll. Mr. Collins, being far more suited to walking than reading, was delighted to shut his heavy book and depart.

Their journey to Meryton was filled with his grandiose trivialities and his cousins' polite agreements. Once they arrived, he could no longer hold the interest of the younger girls. Their gazes immediately scanned the street for the officers, and only a particularly stylish bonnet or a fresh muslin in a shop display could distract them.

However, the ladies were soon captivated by a stranger of refined appearance, walking with an officer on the opposite side of the road. The officer was Mr. Denny, whose arrival from London Lydia had intended to verify, and he offered a bow as they crossed paths. Everyone was impressed by the newcomer’s demeanor and curious about his identity. Kitty and Lydia, resolved to discover who he was, crossed the street under the guise of needing to visit a shop, and they fortunately reached the sidewalk just as the two men turned and arrived at the same location."
}
