model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_006",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I admit that I require company. Having suffered disappointments, my mood cannot endure isolation. I require both occupation and social interaction. Though a military career was not my original path, current circumstances have rendered it suitable. I was raised for the clergy and should have held a highly lucrative position by now, had it pleased the gentleman we recently discussed.

Indeed!

Yes, the late Mr. Darcy promised me the next vacancy of the finest benefice in his power to bestow. As my godfather, he was deeply fond of me. I cannot adequately describe his generosity. He intended to secure my future and believed he had succeeded; however, when the position became available, it was bestowed upon someone else.

Good heavens! Elizabeth exclaimed. But how could that happen? How could his wishes be ignored? Why did you not pursue legal action?

The wording of the bequest contained a technicality that left me no legal recourse. A man of integrity would not have questioned the intent, yet Mr. Darcy chose to doubt it, or perhaps viewed it as a mere suggestion, claiming I had forfeited my right through recklessness or poor judgment—or any excuse he could invent. It is a fact that the position opened two years ago, just as I reached the age to qualify, and was given to another. It is equally certain that I have done nothing to warrant such a loss. I possess a passionate, impulsive nature and may have occasionally expressed my views of him, and to him, with too much candor. I recall nothing more severe. Ultimately, we are simply incompatible, and he harbors a hatred for me.

This is truly appalling! He merits public shame.

Eventually, he will face it, but not by my hand. Out of respect for his father, I cannot bring myself to defy or denounce him.

Elizabeth admired these sentiments, finding him even more attractive as he spoke.

But what, she asked after a moment, could have been his reason? What drove him to such cruelty?

A deep, fixed animosity toward me—a feeling I must partly ascribe to jealousy. Had his father been less fond of me, his son might have tolerated me more easily. I believe his father’s unusual affection for me bothered him from a young age. He lacked the temperament to handle our rivalry or the favoritism I frequently received.

I never imagined Mr. Darcy was this wicked. While I have always disliked him, I did not think him this depraved. I assumed he merely looked down on others, but I never suspected him of such spiteful vengeance, such unfairness, and such cruelty.

After a brief pause, she added, I do recall him bragging at Netherfield about his unforgiving nature and his inability to let go of a grudge. His character must be truly terrible.

I dare not speak further on the matter, Wickham answered, for I can scarcely be fair to him."
}

I dare not speak further on the matter, Wickham answered, for I can scarcely be fair to him."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_006",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "I admit that I need company. I have been a disappointed man, and my spirits cannot handle being alone. I must have work and social interaction. A military life was not my original plan, but circumstances have made it a suitable choice now. I was raised to enter the church, and I should have held a very valuable position by now if it had pleased the gentleman we were just discussing.

“Indeed!”

“Yes, the late Mr. Darcy left me the right to the best church position he had to offer. He was my godfather and was extremely fond of me. I cannot fully express his kindness. He intended to provide for me generously and believed he had done so, but when the position became available, it was given to someone else.”

“Good heavens!” Elizabeth cried. “But how could that happen? How could his will be ignored? Why did you not seek legal help?”

“The terms of the gift were written in such a vague way that I had no hope of winning in court. An honorable man would not have doubted his intentions, but Mr. Darcy chose to doubt them, or at least treat them as a mere suggestion. He claimed I had lost my right to it through extravagance, recklessness, or some other excuse. It is certain that the position became vacant two years ago, exactly when I was old enough to hold it, and it was given to another man. It is equally certain that I have done nothing to deserve losing it. I have a warm, impulsive temper, and I may have sometimes spoken my opinion of him, and to him, too freely. I can recall nothing worse. The truth is that we are very different types of men, and he hates me.”

“This is shocking! He deserves to be publicly disgraced.”

“Someday he will be, but it will not be by me. As long as I remember his father, I can never defy or expose him.”

Elizabeth respected him for these feelings and thought he looked even handsomer as he expressed them.

“But what,” she asked after a pause, “could have been his motive? What could have led him to act so cruelly?”

“A complete, determined dislike of me—a dislike I must attribute partly to jealousy. If the late Mr. Darcy had liked me less, his son might have tolerated me better. I believe his father’s unusual attachment to me irritated him from an early age. He did not have the temperament to handle the competition between us or the preference his father often showed me.”

“I had not thought Mr. Darcy was this bad. Although I have never liked him, I did not think so poorly of him. I assumed he looked down on people in general, but I did not suspect him of such malicious revenge, injustice, and inhumanity.”

After reflecting for a few minutes, she added, “I do remember him boasting one day at Netherfield about his unforgiving nature and his inability to let go of a grudge. His personality must be dreadful.”

“I will not trust myself to speak on the subject,” Wickham replied. “I can hardly be fair to him.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Mrs. Bennet and Kitty departed, and once they had exited, Mr. Collins commenced: 

“Pray believe, my dear Miss Elizabeth, that your reserve, rather than hindering you, only enhances your other virtues. You would appear less charming to me had you not shown this slight reluctance; yet, permit me to confirm that I have your esteemed mother’s consent for this proposal. You can scarcely question the intent of my speech, regardless of how your inherent sensitivity might prompt you to feign ignorance; my conduct has been too obvious to be misunderstood. From the moment I arrived at this residence, I chose you as the partner for my future. However, before I am carried away by my emotions regarding this matter, it is perhaps prudent for me to outline my motives for matrimony—and, furthermore, for traveling to Hertfordshire with the specific intention of finding a bride, as I indeed did.”

The notion of Mr. Collins, with his characteristic grave composure, being carried away by his emotions, brought Elizabeth so close to laughter that she could not utilize the brief silence he provided to interrupt him further, and he proceeded: 

“My justifications for marriage are, firstly, that I believe it is proper for every clergyman in comfortable circumstances, such as myself, to provide a matrimonial example within his parish; secondly, that I am certain it will contribute significantly to my own contentment; and, thirdly, which I perhaps should have stated sooner, that it is the specific counsel and suggestion of the distinguished lady whom I have the privilege of naming my patroness. On two occasions she has deigned to offer me her views—unsolicited, at that—on this topic; and it was just the Saturday evening before I departed Hunsford, between our rounds of quadrille, while Mrs. Jenkinson was adjusting Miss De Bourgh’s footstool, that she remarked, ‘Mr. Collins, you must marry. A clergyman in your position ought to marry. Select wisely, select a gentlewoman for my sake and for your own; let her be an industrious, practical woman, not raised in luxury, but capable of managing a modest income effectively. This is my advice. Locate such a woman as soon as possible, bring her to Hunsford, and I shall call upon her.’ Allow me, incidentally, to remark, my fair cousin, that I do not count the attention and benevolence of Lady Catherine de Bourgh as the least of the benefits I have to provide. You will discover her demeanor to be beyond anything I might articulate; and your intelligence and spirit, I believe, will be pleasing to her, particularly when moderated by the quietude and deference that her social standing will naturally inspire. So much for my general desire for marriage; it remains to explain why my intentions were turned toward Longbourn rather than my own vicinity, where I assure you there are many delightful young ladies. The truth is that, being destined to inherit this property upon the passing of your respected father—who, nevertheless, may live for many years yet—I could not feel content without deciding to select a wife from among his children, so that the deprivation to them might be minimized when that sorrowful occasion arrives—which, as I have already noted, may not occur for quite some time.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_008",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Mrs. Bennet and Kitty walked away, and once they had left, Mr. Collins began:

“Believe me, my dear Miss Elizabeth, your modesty, far from hurting you, only adds to your other perfections. You would have been less appealing to me had you not shown this slight reluctance; but please be assured that I have your mother’s permission for this proposal. You can hardly doubt the purpose of my speech, no matter how your natural reserve leads you to pretend otherwise; my intentions have been too obvious to be misunderstood. Almost as soon as I arrived, I chose you as my future life partner. But before I get carried away by my feelings on this subject, perhaps I should explain my reasons for marrying—and, furthermore, for coming to Hertfordshire with the specific plan of choosing a wife.”

The thought of Mr. Collins, with his solemn composure, being carried away by his feelings, made Elizabeth so close to laughing that she could not use the short pause he provided to try and stop him, and he continued:

“My reasons for marrying are, first, that I think it is right for every clergyman in a comfortable financial position, like myself, to set an example of marriage in his parish; second, that I am convinced it will greatly increase my happiness; and third, which I perhaps should have mentioned sooner, that it is the specific advice and recommendation of the noble lady whom I have the honor of calling my patroness. Twice has she condescended to give me her opinion—unasked, too!—on this matter. It was just the Saturday night before I left Hunsford, between our card games while Mrs. Jenkinson was adjusting Miss De Bourgh’s footstool, that she said, ‘Mr. Collins, you must marry. A clergyman like you must marry. Choose properly; choose a gentlewoman for my sake and your own. Let her be an active, useful sort of person, not raised with expensive tastes, but able to make a small income go a long way. This is my advice. Find such a woman as soon as you can, bring her to Hunsford, and I will visit her.’ Allow me to note, my fair cousin, that I do not consider the attention and kindness of Lady Catherine de Bourgh to be the least of the advantages I have to offer. You will find her manners beyond anything I can describe; and your wit and liveliness, I think, will be acceptable to her, especially when tempered with the silence and respect that her rank will naturally inspire. So much for my general intention to marry; it remains to be explained why I looked toward Longbourn instead of my own neighborhood, where I assure you there are many lovely young women. But the fact is, since I am to inherit this estate after the death of your father—who may, however, live many years longer—I could not be satisfied without choosing a wife from among his daughters, so that the loss to them might be as small as possible when that sad event occurs, which, as I have said, may not happen for several years.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_009",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "My brother already holds her in high regard; he will now have constant chances to see her in a very familiar setting; her family desires the union just as much as his own; and I do not believe a sister’s bias blinds me when I say Charles is well-suited to win any woman’s heart. With all these factors supporting a romance, and nothing to hinder it, am I mistaken, my dearest Jane, in fostering the hope of an outcome that will ensure the joy of so many?’ What is your opinion of this passage, my dear Lizzy?” Jane asked as she concluded it. “Is it not plain enough? Does it not explicitly state that Caroline neither anticipates nor desires me to become her sister; that she is entirely certain of her brother’s lack of interest; and that if she guesses my true feelings for him, she intends (most benevolently!) to warn me? Can there be any other interpretation?”

“Yes, there can; for my view is entirely opposite. Will you listen to it?”

“Most gladly.”

“I will give it to you briefly. Miss Bingley perceives that her brother is enamored with you and desires him to wed Miss Darcy. She accompanies him to the city hoping to detain him there, and attempts to convince you that he has no regard for you.”

Jane moved her head in denial.

“Truly, Jane, you ought to trust me. No one who has witnessed you together can question his devotion; Miss Bingley, I am certain, cannot: she is not so foolish. Had she observed even half as much affection from Mr. Darcy toward herself, she would have already purchased her bridal attire. But the reality is this: we are not wealthy or distinguished enough for them; and she is the more eager to secure Miss Darcy for her brother, thinking that once one marriage has occurred, she might find it easier to arrange a second; there is certainly some cleverness in that, and I suspect it would work if Miss de Bourgh were not in the picture. But, my dearest Jane, you cannot honestly suppose that, because Miss Bingley claims her brother greatly admires Miss Darcy, he is in the slightest measure less aware of your worth than when he parted from you on Tuesday; or that she could convince him that, rather than being in love with you, he is deeply enamored with her friend.”

“If we shared the same view of Miss Bingley,” Jane answered, “your interpretation might put me entirely at ease. But I know the premise is unfair. Caroline is incapable of intentionally misleading anyone; and all I can hope for here is that she is mistaken herself.”

“That is well. You could not have proposed a more comforting thought, since you will not accept mine: by all means, believe her to be mistaken. You have now fulfilled your obligation to her, and must worry no more.”

“But, my dear sister, can I be content, even assuming the best, in marrying a man whose sisters and friends all desire him to wed someone else?”

“You must choose for yourself,” Elizabeth said; “and if, after careful reflection, you conclude that the distress of displeasing his two sisters outweighs the joy of being his wife, I suggest, by all means, that you reject him.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_010",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Darcy’s residence, and spoke with great enthusiasm regarding the latter’s intentions for fresh furnishings.

Elizabeth, whom Jane promptly informed of these primary details, listened with quiet fury. Her spirit was torn between sympathy for her sibling and bitterness toward the rest of the party. She gave no credence to Caroline’s claim that her brother harbored an affection for Miss Darcy. She remained as certain as ever that he truly cared for Jane; and despite her previous inclination to admire him, she could not contemplate his pliant nature and lack of firm resolve—which now rendered him a puppet to his manipulative companions and caused him to abandon his own joy for their whimsical demands—without feeling wrath and nearly disdain. If only his own contentment were at stake, he might have been permitted to trifle with it as he pleased; yet she believed he must realize that her sister’s welfare was also at risk. In short, it was a matter that invited endless, fruitless contemplation. She could focus on nothing else; yet, whether Bingley’s affection had genuinely faded or was merely stifled by his associates’ meddling, and whether he had perceived Jane’s feelings or remained oblivious, her sister’s plight stayed unchanged and her tranquility equally damaged, regardless of how these factors might alter her own judgment of him.

Several days elapsed before Jane found the strength to discuss her emotions with Elizabeth; eventually, once Mrs. Bennet departed, leaving them alone following a more prolonged period of agitation than usual regarding Netherfield and its proprietor, she could not refrain from remarking,

“Oh, if only my dear mother possessed more self-control! She cannot fathom the distress her constant remarks about him cause me. But I shall not complain. It cannot endure. He will be forgotten, and we shall return to our former state.”

Elizabeth gazed at her sister with skeptical concern, yet remained silent.

“You are doubtful,” Jane exclaimed, blushing faintly; “truly, you have no cause. He may remain in my thoughts as the kindest man I have known, but that is the extent of it. I possess neither hopes nor fears, and I have no reason to blame him. Thank heaven I am spared that agony. With a little time, therefore—I shall certainly endeavor to move past this—”

With a firmer tone, she quickly added, “I find immediate solace in the fact that this was merely a mistake of my own imagination, and that it has caused no injury to anyone save myself.”

“My dear Jane,” Elizabeth cried, “you are too virtuous. Your kindness and lack of selfishness are truly saintly; I am at a loss for words. I feel as though I have never appreciated you enough, or loved you as you merit.”

Miss Bennet quickly rejected any suggestion of exceptional character, attributing the praise instead to her sister’s deep devotion.

“No,” Elizabeth insisted, “that is not equitable. You desire to view everyone as honorable, and you are pained if I criticize anyone. I only wish to view you as flawless, and you resist it. Do not fear that I shall go to extremes, or infringe upon your habit of universal benevolence.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_010",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Darcy’s house, and spoke with excitement about some of the latter’s plans for new furniture.

Elizabeth, to whom Jane quickly shared the gist of all this, listened in silent indignation. Her heart was torn between concern for her sister and resentment toward everyone else. She did not believe Caroline’s claim that her brother was interested in Miss Darcy. She never doubted that he was truly fond of Jane; and as much as she had always liked him, she could not think without anger, or even contempt, of that easygoing nature and lack of resolve that now made him a puppet for his manipulative friends, leading him to sacrifice his own happiness to satisfy their whims. If his own happiness were the only thing at stake, he could have played with it however he liked; but her sister’s happiness was also involved, which she felt he must realize. In short, it was a subject she would dwell on for a long time, though it would be to no avail. She could think of nothing else; yet, whether Bingley’s feelings had truly faded or were being suppressed by his friends’ interference, whether he had noticed Jane’s affection or it had escaped his attention—whatever the case, while her opinion of him would be significantly affected by the truth, her sister’s situation remained unchanged, and her peace of mind was equally damaged.

A day or two passed before Jane had the courage to discuss her feelings with Elizabeth; but finally, after Mrs. Bennet left them alone following a longer-than-usual rant about Netherfield and its master, she could not help saying,

“Oh, I wish my dear mother had more self-control! She has no idea of the pain she causes me with her constant comments about him. But I will not complain. It cannot last long. He will be forgotten, and we will all be as we were before.”

Elizabeth looked at her sister with skeptical concern but said nothing.

“You doubt me,” Jane cried, blushing slightly; “truly, you have no reason to. He may remain in my memory as the most amiable man I have ever known, but that is all. I have nothing to hope or fear, and nothing to blame him for. Thank God I am spared that pain. With a little time, therefore, I will certainly try to move on.”

With a firmer voice, she added, “I have this immediate comfort: that it was nothing more than a mistake of the imagination on my part, and that it has harmed no one but myself.”

“My dear Jane,” Elizabeth exclaimed, “you are too good. Your sweetness and selflessness are truly angelic; I don’t know what to say to you. I feel as if I have never done you justice or loved you as you deserve.”

Miss Bennet eagerly denied having any extraordinary merit and redirected the praise toward her sister’s warm affection.

“No,” said Elizabeth, “that isn’t fair. You want to believe everyone is respectable, and you are hurt if I speak ill of anyone. I only want to think you are perfect, and you fight against it. Do not fear that I will go too far or infringe upon your privilege of universal goodwill.”"
}

She could focus on nothing else; and yet, whether Bingley’s feelings had truly faded or were being suppressed by his friends’ interference, or whether he had noticed Jane’s affection or remained oblivious, her sister’s situation remained unchanged, and her peace of mind was equally damaged.

A day or two passed before Jane felt brave enough to discuss her feelings with Elizabeth. Finally, after Mrs. Bennet left them alone following a longer-than-usual rant about Netherfield and its master, Jane could not help saying,

“I wish my dear mother had more self-control! She has no idea how much pain she causes me with her constant comments about him. But I will not complain. It cannot last long. He will be forgotten, and we will all be as we were before.”

Elizabeth looked at her sister with doubtful concern but said nothing.

“You doubt me,” Jane cried, blushing slightly. “Indeed, you have no reason to. He may remain in my memory as the most amiable man I have ever known, but that is all. I have nothing to hope or fear, and nothing to blame him for. Thank God I am spared that pain. With a little time, I will certainly try to move on.”

With a stronger voice, she added, “I find comfort in knowing that this was merely a mistake of my own imagination, and that it has harmed no one but myself.”

“My dear Jane,” Elizabeth exclaimed, “you are too good. Your sweetness and selflessness are truly angelic; I do not know what to say to you. I feel as if I have never done you justice or loved you as you deserve.”

Miss Bennet eagerly denied having any extraordinary merit and redirected the praise toward her sister’s warm affection.

“No,” said Elizabeth, “that is not fair. You want to believe everyone is respectable and are hurt if I speak ill of anyone. I only want to believe you are perfect, and you resist it. Do not fear that I will go too far or interfere with your habit of thinking well of everyone.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_013",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Darcy. His gaze had been frequently directed toward them with an air of inquiry; and that her Ladyship, after some time, experienced the same sentiment, was more plainly evident, for she did not hesitate to demand,--

“What are you discussing, Fitzwilliam? What is the subject? What are you relating to Miss Bennet? I must be informed.”

“We were discussing music, madam,” he answered, when he could no longer evade the question.

“Music! Then by all means speak up. It is my favorite topic. I must participate if you are on the subject of music. I imagine few individuals in England possess a greater genuine appreciation for music than I do, or a superior innate taste. Had I ever received instruction, I would have become quite accomplished. Anne would have been as well, had her constitution permitted her to study. I am certain she would have played beautifully. How is Georgiana progressing, Darcy?”

Mr. Darcy spoke with warm commendation of his sister’s skill.

“I am pleased to receive such a favorable report,” Lady Catherine remarked; “and please inform her from me that she cannot hope to master it unless she practices diligently.”

“I assure you, madam,” he replied, “that she requires no such counsel. She practices quite regularly.”

“That is excellent. One cannot practice too much; and when I next correspond with her, I shall insist that she not neglect it under any circumstances. I frequently advise young ladies that no musical mastery is achieved without persistent practice. I have mentioned to Miss Bennet on several occasions that she will never perform with true skill unless she practices more; and although Mrs. Collins lacks an instrument, she is quite welcome, as I have frequently assured her, to visit Rosings daily and use the pianoforte in Mrs. Jenkinson’s quarters. She would not be a nuisance to anyone, you understand, in that section of the house.”

Mr. Darcy appeared somewhat embarrassed by his aunt’s lack of manners and remained silent.

Once coffee was finished, Colonel Fitzwilliam reminded Elizabeth of her pledge to play for him; she immediately seated herself at the piano. He pulled a chair close to her. Lady Catherine attended to half a song before resuming her conversation with her other nephew; eventually, the latter moved away from her, and walking with his customary composure toward the instrument, positioned himself to obtain a clear view of the musician’s face. Elizabeth observed his actions, and at the first suitable interval, turned to him with a playful smile, remarking,--

“You intend to intimidate me, Mr. Darcy, by approaching with such solemnity to listen. But I shall not be intimidated, even if your sister does play so expertly. I possess a stubborn nature that refuses to be frightened by the whims of others. My resolve only strengthens whenever someone attempts to overawe me.”

“I will not claim you are in error,” he answered, “because you could not truly believe I harbor any intention of alarming you; and I have known you long enough to understand that you take great delight in occasionally asserting views that you do not actually hold.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_013",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Darcy. His eyes had been repeatedly drawn toward them with a look of curiosity; and that her Ladyship, after a while, shared the feeling was more openly acknowledged, for she did not hesitate to call out,

“What are you saying, Fitzwilliam? What are you talking about? What are you telling Miss Bennet? Let me hear what it is.”

“We were talking about music, madam,” he said, when he could no longer avoid a reply.

“Music! Then please speak up. It is my favorite subject. I must be included in the conversation if you are discussing music. I suppose there are few people in England who enjoy music more than I do, or who have a better natural taste. If I had ever learned, I would have been highly skilled. And so would Anne, if her health had allowed her to focus on it. I am confident she would have played beautifully. How is Georgiana doing, Darcy?”

Mr. Darcy spoke with affectionate praise of his sister’s skill.

“I am very glad to hear such a good report of her,” said Lady Catherine; “and please tell her from me that she cannot expect to excel if she does not practice a great deal.”

“I assure you, madam,” he replied, “that she does not need such advice. She practices very consistently.”

“So much the better. You cannot do too much of it; and when I next write to her, I shall instruct her not to neglect it under any circumstances. I often tell young ladies that no excellence in music is acquired without constant practice. I have told Miss Bennet several times that she will never play really well unless she practices more; and although Mrs. Collins has no instrument, she is very welcome, as I have often told her, to come to Rosings every day and play on the piano in Mrs. Jenkinson’s room. She would not be in anyone’s way, you know, in that part of the house.”

Mr. Darcy looked a little embarrassed by his aunt’s rudeness and made no answer.

When coffee was finished, Colonel Fitzwilliam reminded Elizabeth that she had promised to play for him, and she sat down at the instrument immediately. He pulled a chair near her. Lady Catherine listened to half a song and then talked, as before, to her other nephew, until he walked away from her and, moving with his usual deliberation toward the piano, positioned himself to have a full view of the performer’s face. Elizabeth saw what he was doing, and at the first convenient pause, she turned to him with a playful smile and said,

“You intend to frighten me, Mr. Darcy, by coming over in such a formal manner to hear me. But I will not be alarmed, even if your sister does play so well. There is a stubbornness in me that cannot bear to be intimidated by the will of others. My courage always rises whenever someone tries to intimidate me.”

“I will not say that you are mistaken,” he replied, “because you could not really believe that I have any intention of alarming you; and I have known you long enough to understand that you take great pleasure in occasionally professing opinions which, in fact, are not your own.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_014",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He spoke very little, and she did not exert herself to converse or attend to him; yet, during their third meeting, she noticed he posed several strange, disjointed inquiries regarding her enjoyment of Hunsford, her preference for lonely strolls, and her thoughts on the domestic contentment of Mr. and Mrs. Collins. Furthermore, when discussing Rosings and her lack of familiarity with the estate, he appeared to assume that her next visit to Kent would involve staying there. His remarks suggested as much. Was he considering Colonel Fitzwilliam? She surmised that if his comments held any significance, they must refer to a potential connection in that direction. This thought troubled her slightly, and she felt relieved to reach the gate in the fence across from the Parsonage.

One day, while walking and absorbed in reading Jane’s latest letter—reflecting on sections that indicated Jane’s low spirits—she looked up to find Colonel Fitzwilliam approaching, rather than the usual surprise of Mr. Darcy. Quickly stowing the letter and summoning a smile, she remarked,

“I was unaware that you ever strolled in this direction.”

“I have been circling the park,” he answered, “as is my annual custom, and I planned to finish by stopping at the Parsonage. Do you intend to walk much further?”

“No, I was just about to turn back.”

She turned as she said this, and they walked toward the Parsonage in company.

“Are you definitely departing Kent on Saturday?” she asked.

“Yes—provided Darcy does not postpone it once more. I am subject to his schedule. He manages our affairs exactly as he sees fit.”

“And if he cannot satisfy himself with the plan, he at least takes great satisfaction in his ability to decide. I cannot think of anyone who appears to relish the freedom to act as he pleases more than Mr. Darcy.”

“He certainly enjoys having his own way,” Colonel Fitzwilliam responded. “But we all do. The difference is that he possesses greater resources to achieve it than most, being wealthy while others are not. I speak from experience. A younger son, you realize, must become accustomed to sacrifice and reliance on others.”

“In my view, an earl’s younger son can understand very little of either. Tell me truly, what have you ever experienced of sacrifice or dependence? When have you been stopped by a lack of funds from traveling where you wished or obtaining anything you desired?”

“Those are pointed questions—and perhaps I cannot claim to have faced many such difficulties. Yet, in more significant matters, I may suffer due to a lack of wealth. Younger sons are not free to marry whom they choose.”

“Unless they choose women of wealth, which I suspect they frequently do.”

“Our expensive lifestyles render us too reliant on others, and few in my social standing can manage to marry without considering financial matters.”

“Is this,” Elizabeth wondered, “directed at me?” She flushed at the notion, but regaining her composure, she replied with a playful air, “And tell me, what is the typical market value of an earl’s younger son?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_014",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "He never said much, and she did not bother to talk or listen closely; however, it struck her during their third meeting that he was asking some strange, disjointed questions—about whether she enjoyed being at Hunsford, her love for solitary walks, and her opinion of Mr. and Mrs. Collins’s happiness. Furthermore, when discussing Rosings, and noting her lack of familiarity with the house, he seemed to expect that whenever she returned to Kent, she would be staying there as well. His words seemed to imply it. Could he be thinking of Colonel Fitzwilliam? She assumed that if he meant anything at all, he must be hinting at what might develop in that direction. It unsettled her slightly, and she was quite relieved to reach the gate in the fence opposite the Parsonage.

One day, while walking, she was busy re-reading Jane’s latest letter and focusing on certain passages that proved Jane had not been in high spirits, when, instead of being surprised by Mr. Darcy again, she looked up to see Colonel Fitzwilliam approaching her. Putting the letter away immediately and forcing a smile, she said,

“I didn’t know you ever walked this way.”

“I have been touring the park,” he replied, “as I generally do every year, and I intended to finish by calling at the Parsonage. Are you going much further?”

“No, I was just about to turn back.”

She turned accordingly, and they walked toward the Parsonage together.

“Are you definitely leaving Kent on Saturday?” she asked.

“Yes—if Darcy doesn’t postpone it again. But I am at his disposal. He arranges things exactly as he pleases.”

“And if he isn’t able to please himself in the arrangement, he at least takes great pleasure in the power of choice. I don’t know anyone who seems to enjoy the power of doing what he likes more than Mr. Darcy.”

“He likes to have his own way, certainly,” Colonel Fitzwilliam replied. “But we all do. It’s just that he has better means of getting it than many others, because he is rich and they are poor. I speak from experience. A younger son, you know, must get used to self-denial and dependence.”

“In my opinion, an earl’s younger son can know very little of either. Now, seriously, what have you ever known of self-denial and dependence? When have you ever been prevented by a lack of money from going wherever you chose or getting anything you wanted?”

“Those are pointed questions—and perhaps I cannot say that I have experienced many hardships of that nature. But in more important matters, I may suffer from a lack of money. Younger sons cannot marry whoever they like.”

“Unless they like women of fortune, which I think they very often do.”

“Our expensive habits make us too dependent, and there are not many in my social standing who can afford to marry without paying some attention to money.”

“Is this,” Elizabeth wondered, “meant for me?” She flushed at the thought, but recovering herself, she said in a lively tone, “And pray, what is the usual price of an earl’s younger son?”"
}

Could he be thinking of Colonel Fitzwilliam? She assumed that if he meant anything, he was hinting at a potential match there. This bothered her slightly, and she was relieved to reach the gate across from the Parsonage.

One day, while walking, she was busy rereading Jane’s latest letter and focusing on parts that showed Jane had not been in good spirits. Instead of being surprised by Mr. Darcy again, she looked up to see Colonel Fitzwilliam approaching. She quickly put the letter away, forced a smile, and said, “I did not know you ever walked this way.”

“I have been touring the park,” he replied, “as I usually do every year, and I intended to finish by stopping at the Parsonage. Are you going much further?”

“No, I was just about to turn back.”

She turned around, and they walked toward the Parsonage together.

“Are you definitely leaving Kent on Saturday?” she asked.

“Yes, if Darcy does not postpone it again. I am at his disposal. He arranges everything just as he likes.”

“And if he cannot please himself with the arrangements, he at least enjoys the power of choosing. I do not know anyone who seems to enjoy doing what he likes more than Mr. Darcy.”

“He likes to have his own way,” Colonel Fitzwilliam replied. “But we all do. It is just that he has better means to get it than others because he is rich, while many are poor. I speak from experience. A younger son, you know, must get used to self-denial and dependence.”

“In my opinion, an earl’s younger son can know very little of either. Tell me seriously, what have you ever known of self-denial and dependence? When have you been stopped by a lack of money from going where you wanted or buying anything you desired?”

“Those are direct questions, and perhaps I cannot say I have faced many hardships of that kind. But in more important matters, I may suffer from a lack of money. Younger sons cannot always marry whom they like.”

“Unless they like women of fortune, which I think they very often do.”

“Our expensive habits make us too dependent, and there are not many in my social position who can afford to marry without considering money.”

“Is this meant for me?” Elizabeth wondered, blushing at the thought. Recovering, she said in a lively tone, “And pray, what is the usual price of an earl’s younger son?”"
}

She realized that, proud and cold as his manners were, she had never—in all the time they had known each other, a period that had recently brought them together and given her some insight into his habits—seen anything that proved him to be unprincipled or unjust, or anything that suggested he was irreligious or immoral. She acknowledged that he was respected and valued by his own circle, that even Wickham had credited him with being a good brother, and that she had often heard him speak so affectionately of his sister that he clearly possessed some kind and gentle feelings. If his actions had truly been what Wickham claimed, such a blatant violation of right would have been impossible to hide from the world, and a friendship between someone capable of such things and a man as kind as Mr. Bingley would be impossible to understand.

She felt absolutely ashamed of herself. She could not think of either Darcy or Wickham without realizing that she had been blind, biased, prejudiced, and ridiculous.

“How despicably I have acted!” she cried. “I, who have prided myself on my discernment! I, who have valued myself on my intelligence! I, who have often looked down on my sister’s generous fairness and fed my own vanity with useless or groundless suspicion. How humiliating this discovery is! Yet, how deserved this humiliation is! If I had been in love, I could not have been more miserably blind. But vanity, not love, has been my folly. Pleased by the attention of one man and offended by the neglect of the other from the very start, I have encouraged my own biases and ignorance, pushing reason aside whenever they were involved. Until this moment, I never truly knew myself.”

Neither could she deny the accuracy of his description of Jane. When she reached the part of the letter where her family was mentioned, in tones of such mortifying yet deserved reproach, her sense of shame was intense."

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_017",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“If you understood,” Elizabeth remarked, “the significant harm to us all resulting from the public attention drawn by Lydia’s reckless and unwise behavior—harm that has, in fact, already occurred—I am certain you would view this matter differently.”

“Already occurred!” Mr. Bennet echoed. “What! Has she scared off any of your suitors? Poor little Lizzy! But do not feel discouraged. Any delicate young men unable to tolerate a bit of silliness are not worth mourning. Tell me, show me the names of the pathetic men who have been deterred by Lydia’s foolishness.”

“Truly, you are in error. I have no such personal grievances to address. I am speaking of broad, general consequences, not specific ones. Our standing and reputation in society must inevitably suffer from the wild instability, the boldness, and the complete lack of self-control that define Lydia’s nature. Forgive me, but I must be candid. If you, my dear father, will not exert yourself to restrain her excessive energy and show her that her current habits should not be her life’s focus, she will soon be past the point of improvement. Her personality will be set; by sixteen, she will be the most resolute flirt to ever bring ridicule upon herself and her family—a flirt, furthermore, of the most vulgar and trivial kind, possessing no charm beyond her youth and decent looks, and, due to her vacant and uneducated mind, entirely incapable of avoiding the widespread scorn her desperate need for attention will provoke. Kitty is also caught in this danger. She follows wherever Lydia goes. Vain, uneducated, lazy, and completely unrestrained! Oh, my dear father, can you truly believe they will not be criticized and looked down upon wherever they go, and that their sisters will not frequently share in that shame?”

Mr. Bennet perceived that she was deeply invested in the matter; he took her hand kindly and replied,

“Do not distress yourself, my dear. Wherever you and Jane are known, you will be held in high regard; you will not appear any less impressive for having two—or perhaps three—very foolish sisters. We will have no tranquility at Longbourn if Lydia does not travel to Brighton. Let her go, then. Colonel Forster is a prudent man who will prevent her from getting into any genuine trouble; fortunately, she is too poor to be a target for anyone. In Brighton, she will hold even less significance as a mere flirt than she has here. The officers will discover women more deserving of their attention. Let us trust, therefore, that her time there might teach her how unimportant she truly is. Regardless, she cannot become much worse without giving us cause to confine her for the remainder of her days.”

Elizabeth had to accept this response, though her own perspective remained unchanged, and she departed feeling let down and saddened. Nevertheless, it was not in her character to worsen her troubles by fixating on them. She felt satisfied that she had fulfilled her obligation, and worrying over inevitable problems or magnifying them through anxiety was not in her nature."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_019",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Mr. Gardiner, despite rarely finding the opportunity, possessed a great love for fishing. He became so preoccupied with observing the trout surfacing in the stream and discussing them with the attendant that his progress remained quite sluggish. As they strolled along in this leisurely fashion, they were startled once more—Elizabeth’s surprise matching her initial reaction—by the sight of Mr. Darcy drawing near. Because this section of the path lacked the heavy cover found on the opposite side, they spotted him well before they converged. Although stunned, Elizabeth felt better equipped for an encounter than previously and determined to maintain a composed demeanor should he truly intend to approach. For a brief interval, she hoped he might veer onto a different trail. This expectation held while a bend in the path hid him from sight, but once he rounded the corner, he stood directly before them. A single look confirmed that his recent courteous manner remained intact. Attempting to mirror his civility, she began to praise the scenery as they met, yet she faltered after uttering only “delightful” and “charming.” Unwelcome thoughts intruded, and she feared that her compliments regarding Pemberley might be interpreted in a troublesome light. Her face flushed, and she fell silent.

Mrs. Gardiner lingered slightly behind, and when Elizabeth paused, Darcy requested the honor of an introduction to her companions. This gesture of politeness caught her entirely off guard; she could barely hide a smile at the irony of him seeking the acquaintance of the very individuals whose social standing had once offended his pride when he proposed to her. “How shocked he will be,” she mused, “once he discovers their identity! He clearly mistakes them for people of high society.”

The introduction proceeded at once. As she identified their kinship, she cast a furtive glance at him to gauge his reaction, half-expecting him to flee from such embarrassing company. It was obvious that the connection startled him, yet he endured the revelation with composure. Far from departing, he turned to accompany them and began speaking with Mr. Gardiner. Elizabeth felt a sense of satisfaction and triumph. It was reassuring that he should realize she possessed relatives for whom she felt no shame. She paid close attention to their exchange, taking pride in every remark her uncle made that demonstrated his intellect, refined taste, or gentlemanly conduct.

The discussion soon shifted to fishing, and Elizabeth heard Mr. Darcy cordially invite her uncle to fish there whenever he pleased during his stay in the area. He offered to provide the necessary equipment and indicated the specific areas of the water where the best catches were found. Mrs. Gardiner, walking arm in arm with Elizabeth, exchanged a look of amazement with her. Elizabeth remained silent, yet she felt deeply pleased, knowing the gesture was intended for her sake. Nevertheless, her bewilderment remained profound, and she kept asking herself, “Why has he changed so much?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_020",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Elizabeth quickly realized that Miss Bingley was keeping a sharp eye on her, ensuring that any remark she made, particularly to Miss Darcy, drew her notice. While this scrutiny would not have deterred her from attempting to converse with the younger woman, the awkward seating arrangement made it difficult; regardless, she felt relieved to avoid the burden of conversation, as her mind was occupied with her own reflections. She anticipated the arrival of the gentlemen at any second, feeling a mixture of hope and dread regarding the potential appearance of the master of the house, though she could not decide which emotion held more sway. After fifteen minutes of silence from Miss Bingley, Elizabeth was startled when she was asked, in a frigid tone, about the well-being of her relatives. She replied with matching brevity and detachment, and the conversation ceased.

The visit’s next development occurred when attendants brought in cold meats, cakes, and an assortment of seasonal fruits, though this only happened after Mrs. Annesley had exchanged several meaningful glances and smiles with Miss Darcy to prompt her duties. The entire group now had a task; even if they could not all converse, they could all partake in the refreshments, and the elegant displays of grapes, nectarines, and peaches soon drew them toward the table.

During this activity, Elizabeth had a clear chance to determine whether she truly hoped for or feared Mr. Darcy’s arrival based on her reaction to his entry; yet, despite having felt certain of her desire just moments earlier, she immediately wished he had not come.

He had spent some time with Mr. Gardiner and several other gentlemen from the estate by the river, departing only upon discovering that the ladies intended to call on Georgiana that morning. As soon as he arrived, Elizabeth made a sensible decision to remain calm and composed—a resolution that felt vital, though perhaps difficult to maintain, as she noticed the group’s suspicions were piqued, with nearly every eye tracking his conduct upon entering. Miss Bingley’s face displayed the most intense, curious scrutiny, despite the smiles she wore whenever she addressed him; her jealousy had not yet rendered her reckless, and she continued to pursue him. Upon her brother’s arrival, Miss Darcy made a greater effort to speak, and Elizabeth observed his eagerness for his sister and herself to become better acquainted, as he encouraged every conversational opening. Miss Bingley noticed this as well and, driven by rash irritation, seized the first chance to remark with mocking politeness:

“Tell me, Miss Eliza, have the ----shire militia left Meryton? Their departure must be a significant blow to your family.”

She did not dare utter Wickham’s name while Darcy was present, but Elizabeth immediately understood that he was the true subject of her thoughts. The memories associated with him caused her a brief moment of pain, but she gathered her resolve to deflect the spiteful comment and soon replied with a reasonably composed air."
}

“Tell me, Miss Eliza, have the ----shire militia left Meryton? Their departure must be a significant blow to your family.”

She did not dare utter Wickham’s name while Darcy was present, but Elizabeth immediately understood that he was the true subject of her thoughts. The memories associated with him caused her a brief moment of pain, but she gathered her resolve to deflect the spiteful comment and soon replied with a reasonably composed air."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_021",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "F.; yet nobody can fault them. Our misery, my dear Lizzy, is profound. My parents assume the worst, but I cannot think so poorly of him. Various factors might make a secret marriage in the city preferable to their original scheme; and even if he could harbor such intentions toward a girl with Lydia’s family, which seems unlikely, can I believe her so utterly ruined? Impossible! I am saddened, however, that Colonel F. does not seem to count on their wedding: he gestured negatively when I voiced my optimism, remarking that he feared W. was not a man of honor. My mother is truly unwell and remains in her chamber. It would be better if she could rouse herself, but that is unlikely; and as for my father, I have never seen him so devastated. Poor Kitty is blamed for hiding their romance, but since it was told in confidence, it is hardly surprising. I am genuinely relieved, dearest Lizzy, that you have been saved from witnessing these painful events; but now that the initial blow has passed, shall I admit that I yearn for your arrival? I am not so self-centered, however, as to insist if it is troublesome. Farewell! I pick up my pen again to do exactly what I just said I would not; but the situation is such that I must urgently implore you all to arrive here immediately. I know my dear uncle and aunt so well that I do not hesitate to ask, though I have a further favor to request of the former. My father is departing for London with Colonel Forster at once to attempt to locate her. What he intends to do, I have no idea; but his extreme agitation will prevent him from acting in the most prudent manner, and Colonel Forster must return to Brighton by tomorrow evening. In such a crisis, my uncle’s counsel and help would mean everything; he will instantly grasp my feelings, and I trust in his kindness.”

“Oh! where, where is my uncle?” Elizabeth shouted, springing from her chair as she concluded the letter, desperate to pursue him without wasting a single precious moment; yet as she approached the door, it was opened by a servant, and Mr. Darcy stood there. Her pallid complexion and frantic demeanor caused him to recoil, and before he could gather his composure to speak, she, whose thoughts were entirely consumed by Lydia’s plight, quickly cried out, “I apologize, but I must depart. I must locate Mr. Gardiner this instant regarding a matter that cannot wait; I have not a second to spare.”

“Good heavens! what has happened?” he exclaimed, with more genuine concern than decorum; then, regaining his composure, “I will not hold you back for a moment; but let me, or let the servant, go after Mr. and Mrs. Gardiner. You are too unwell; you cannot go yourself.”

Elizabeth wavered; but her legs shook beneath her, and she realized how little would be achieved by her trying to follow them. Summoning the servant back, therefore, she instructed him, though in a voice so breathless that she was nearly impossible to understand, to bring his master and mistress home at once."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_021",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Forster; yet no one can blame them. Our distress, my dear Lizzy, is immense. My parents assume the worst, but I cannot think so poorly of him. Many factors might make a private marriage in the city more practical than their original plan; and even if he could harbor such intentions toward a young woman with Lydia’s family connections—which seems unlikely—can I really believe she has lost all sense of propriety? Impossible! I am saddened, however, that Colonel Forster does not seem to count on their marriage; he shook his head when I shared my hopes, saying he feared Wickham was not a man to be trusted. My poor mother is genuinely ill and remains in her room. It would be better if she could pull herself together, but that is not to be expected; and as for my father, I have never seen him so devastated. Poor Kitty is being blamed for hiding their relationship, but since it was told to her in confidence, it is hardly surprising. I am truly glad, dearest Lizzy, that you have been spared some of these painful scenes; but now that the initial shock has passed, must I admit that I long for your return? I am not so selfish, however, as to insist if it is inconvenient. Goodbye! I am picking up my pen again to do exactly what I just said I would not; but the situation is such that I must urgently beg you all to come here as soon as possible. I know my dear uncle and aunt so well that I am not afraid to ask, though I have an additional favor to request of my uncle. My father is leaving for London with Colonel Forster immediately to try to find her. I have no idea what he intends to do, but his extreme distress will prevent him from acting in the most sensible or safe way, and Colonel Forster must return to Brighton by tomorrow evening. In such a crisis, my uncle’s advice and help would mean everything; he will immediately understand what I am feeling, and I rely on his kindness.”

“Oh! Where, where is my uncle?” Elizabeth cried, jumping from her seat as she finished the letter, eager to follow him without wasting a single precious moment. But as she reached the door, a servant opened it, and Mr. Darcy appeared. Her pale face and frantic manner made him start, and before he could recover enough to speak, she—her mind entirely consumed by Lydia’s situation—hastily exclaimed, “I beg your pardon, but I must leave you. I must find Mr. Gardiner this instant regarding a matter that cannot be delayed; I do not have a second to lose.”

“Good God! What is the matter?” he cried, with more genuine concern than social grace. Then, collecting himself, he added, “I will not hold you for a minute; but let me, or let the servant, go after Mr. and Mrs. Gardiner. You are not well enough; you cannot go yourself.”

Elizabeth hesitated, but her knees were shaking, and she realized how little would be gained by her trying to chase after them. Calling the servant back, she instructed him—though her voice was so breathless she was almost impossible to understand—to bring his master and mistress home immediately."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_022",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Elizabeth jumped out; and after giving each of them a quick kiss, hurried into the entryway, where Jane, who came running downstairs from their mother’s room, immediately met her.

Elizabeth, as she affectionately hugged her, while tears filled the eyes of both, lost no time in asking whether anything had been heard of the runaways.

“Not yet,” replied Jane. “But now that my dear uncle has arrived, I hope everything will be fine.”

“Is my father in town?”

“Yes, he went on Tuesday, just as I wrote to tell you.”

“And have you heard from him often?”

“We have heard only once. He wrote me a few lines on Wednesday to say that he had arrived safely and to give me his contact information, which I specifically asked him to do. He simply added that he would not write again until he had something important to report.”

“And my mother—how is she? How are you all?”

“My mother is doing reasonably well, I believe, though her nerves are badly shaken. She is upstairs and will be very glad to see you all. She is not leaving her dressing-room yet. Mary and Kitty, thank goodness, are quite well.”

“But you—how are you?” cried Elizabeth. “You look pale. You must have gone through so much!”

Her sister, however, assured her that she was perfectly fine; and their conversation, which had been happening while Mr. and Mrs. Gardiner were occupied with their children, was now interrupted by the arrival of the whole group. Jane ran to her uncle and aunt, welcoming and thanking them both with a mix of smiles and tears.

When they were all in the living room, the questions that Elizabeth had already asked were naturally repeated by the others, and they soon discovered that Jane had no news to share. The optimistic hope for a good outcome, however, which her kind heart suggested, had not yet left her; she still expected that it would all end well and that every morning would bring a letter, either from Lydia or her father, to explain their actions and perhaps announce the marriage.

Mrs. Bennet, to whose room they all went after a few minutes of talking together, received them exactly as one might expect: with tears and loud expressions of regret, attacks against the villainous behavior of Wickham, and complaints about her own suffering and mistreatment; blaming everyone except the person whose poor judgment and indulgence were primarily responsible for her daughter’s mistakes.

“If I had been able,” she said, “to get my way and go to Brighton with my whole family, this would not have happened; but poor dear Lydia had nobody to look after her. Why did the Forsters ever let her out of their sight? I am sure there was some major negligence on their part, because she is not the type of girl to do such a thing if she had been properly supervised. I always thought they were very unfit to be in charge of her, but I was overruled, as I always am. Poor, dear child! And now Mr. Bennet has gone away, and I know he will fight Wickham wherever he finds him, and then he will be killed, and what will become of us all? The Collinses will kick us out before his body is even cold, and if you are not kind to us, brother, I do not know what we shall do.”"

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_023",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Money! My uncle!” Jane exclaimed, “what are you suggesting, sir?”

“I mean that no sane individual would wed Lydia for such a meager incentive as one hundred pounds annually while I live, and fifty after my death.”

“That is quite correct,” Elizabeth remarked; “though it had not struck me until now. His obligations must be settled, with funds remaining besides! Oh, this must be my uncle’s work! That noble, kind man; I fear he has burdened himself. A minor amount could not possibly cover all this.”

“Indeed,” her father replied. “Wickham would be a fool to accept her for a penny under ten thousand pounds: I would regret thinking so poorly of him, right at the start of our connection.”

“Ten thousand pounds! Heaven help us! How could half that amount ever be reimbursed?”

Mr. Bennet offered no reply; and both women, lost in reflection, remained quiet until they arrived home. Their father then retreated to his study to write, while the sisters entered the breakfast parlor.

“And they are truly to be wed!” Elizabeth cried, once they were alone. “How bizarre this is! And for this we are expected to be grateful. That they must marry, despite their slim prospects for joy and his miserable reputation, we are compelled to celebrate! Oh, Lydia!”

“I console myself by believing,” Jane answered, “that he surely would not wed Lydia unless he felt a genuine affection for her. Although our dear uncle has assisted in settling his affairs, I cannot imagine that ten thousand pounds, or anything approaching it, has been provided. He has his own children, and may have more. How could he possibly part with five thousand pounds?”

“If we ever discover the extent of Wickham’s debts,” Elizabeth said, “and the sum settled upon our sister, we shall know precisely what Mr. Gardiner has contributed, since Wickham possesses nothing of his own. The generosity of my aunt and uncle can never be repaid. Their decision to take her in, offering her their personal guidance and support, is a sacrifice for her benefit that years of thankfulness could not adequately honor. By now, she is surely with them! If such benevolence does not make her feel wretched now, she will never merit happiness! What a reunion it will be when she first encounters my aunt!”

“We must try to put all that has occurred behind us,” Jane suggested: “I hope and believe they will find happiness yet. His agreement to marry her is evidence, I choose to think, that he has adopted a better perspective. Their shared feelings will ground them; and I hope they will settle down so peacefully, and live in such a sensible fashion, that their previous folly may eventually be overlooked.”

“Their behavior has been such,” Elizabeth countered, “that neither you, nor I, nor anyone else, could ever overlook it. It is pointless to discuss it.”

It then struck the sisters that their mother was likely entirely unaware of these developments. They proceeded to the library, therefore, and inquired whether their father wished for them to inform her. He was busy writing, and, without looking up, calmly answered,--

“Do as you like.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_023",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Money! My uncle!” Jane exclaimed. “What are you talking about, sir?”

“I mean that no man in his right mind would marry Lydia for such a small incentive as a hundred a year while I’m alive, and fifty after I’m gone.”

“That’s very true,” Elizabeth said, “though it hadn’t occurred to me before. His debts to be paid off, and money still left over! Oh, it must be my uncle’s doing! Generous, good man, I’m afraid he has put himself in a difficult position. A small amount couldn’t cover all this.”

“No,” her father said. “Wickham would be a fool to take her for a penny less than ten thousand pounds; I’d be sorry to think so poorly of him, right at the start of our relationship.”

“Ten thousand pounds! Heaven forbid! How could half that amount ever be repaid?”

Mr. Bennet didn’t answer, and each of them, lost in thought, remained silent until they reached the house. Their father then went to the library to write, and the girls walked into the breakfast room.

“And they are actually getting married!” Elizabeth cried, as soon as they were alone. “How strange this is! And we are supposed to be thankful for this. That they should marry, despite the slim chance of happiness and his wretched character, we are forced to rejoice! Oh, Lydia!”

“I comfort myself by thinking,” Jane replied, “that he certainly wouldn’t marry Lydia if he didn’t have a genuine affection for her. Although our kind uncle has done something to help clear his debts, I cannot believe that ten thousand pounds, or anything close to it, has been provided. He has children of his own, and may have more. How could he spare five thousand pounds?”

“If we are ever able to find out what Wickham’s debts were,” Elizabeth said, “and how much is settled on our sister from his side, we will know exactly what Mr. Gardiner has done for them, because Wickham doesn’t have a cent of his own. The kindness of my uncle and aunt can never be repaid. Their taking her in and providing her with their personal protection and support is such a sacrifice for her benefit that years of gratitude could never be enough. By now she is actually with them! If such goodness doesn’t make her miserable now, she will never deserve to be happy! What a meeting it will be for her when she first sees my aunt!”

“We must try to forget everything that has happened on both sides,” Jane said. “I hope and trust they will be happy yet. His agreeing to marry her is proof, I want to believe, that he has come to his senses. Their mutual affection will steady them, and I flatter myself that they will settle down so quietly and live in such a sensible manner that, in time, their past recklessness will be forgotten.”

“Their behavior has been such,” Elizabeth replied, “that neither you, nor I, nor anyone else can ever forget it. It is useless to talk about it.”

It then occurred to the girls that their mother was likely completely unaware of what had happened. They went to the library, therefore, and asked their father if he would like them to tell her. He was writing, and without looking up, he replied coolly,

“Just as you please.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_024",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“They shall never be permitted to enter one house in this vicinity. I refuse to sanction the recklessness of either party by welcoming them to Longbourn.”

This statement sparked a lengthy argument, yet Mr. Bennet remained resolute. A subsequent disagreement arose when Mrs. Bennet discovered, to her shock and dismay, that her husband would not contribute a single guinea toward his daughter’s wedding attire. He insisted that he would offer no gesture of kindness regarding the event. Mrs. Bennet struggled to grasp this. That his indignation could reach such an extreme of unforgiving resentment—denying his daughter a necessity that made her marriage appear legitimate—surpassed anything she deemed plausible. She felt the humiliation of her daughter’s lack of new garments at the ceremony far more acutely than any shame regarding her running off to live with Wickham for two weeks beforehand.

Elizabeth now deeply regretted that, in a moment of agitation, she had revealed their anxieties about her sister to Mr. Darcy. Since the marriage would soon provide a suitable conclusion to the elopement, they might have hoped to hide its scandalous start from anyone not present at the time.

She did not worry that he would spread the news. There were few individuals whose discretion she would have trusted more; yet, there was no one whose awareness of her sister’s moral failure would have embarrassed her more deeply. This was not due to any fear of personal repercussions, as a vast, unbridgeable distance already existed between them. Even if Lydia’s marriage had been arranged under the most respectable circumstances, it was inconceivable that Mr. Darcy would unite himself with a family that, among other faults, would now be linked by the closest ties to the man he so rightly despised.

She could not be surprised that he would recoil from such an association. Any desire to win her affection, which she had felt certain he possessed while in Derbyshire, could not reasonably survive such a disaster. She felt humbled and sorrowful, regretting things she could barely define. She grew possessive of his good opinion once she could no longer expect to gain from it. She longed for news of him whenever the slightest opportunity arose. She became certain she could have found happiness with him just as it became unlikely they would ever meet again.

How he would triumph, she frequently mused, if he knew that the offer she had haughtily rejected only four months prior would now be accepted with joy and gratitude! She did not doubt he was as noble as any man, yet, being human, he must surely feel a sense of victory.

She began to realize that he was precisely the individual whose character and abilities would have best complemented her own. His intellect and temperament, though distinct from hers, would have fulfilled all her desires. It was a partnership that would have benefited them both: her relaxed and spirited nature might have tempered his mind and refined his manners, while she would have gained significantly from his wisdom, insight, and worldly experience."
}

  "rewritten_text": "“They shall never be permitted to enter one house in this vicinity. I refuse to sanction the recklessness of either party by welcoming them to Longbourn.”

This statement sparked a lengthy argument, yet Mr. Bennet remained resolute. A subsequent disagreement arose when Mrs. Bennet discovered, to her shock and dismay, that her husband would not contribute a single guinea toward his daughter’s wedding attire. He insisted that he would offer no gesture of kindness regarding the event. Mrs. Bennet struggled to grasp this. That his indignation could reach such an extreme of unforgiving resentment—denying his daughter a necessity that made her marriage appear legitimate—surpassed anything she deemed plausible. She felt the humiliation of her daughter’s lack of new garments at the ceremony far more acutely than any shame regarding her running off to live with Wickham for two weeks beforehand.

Elizabeth now deeply regretted that, in a moment of agitation, she had revealed their anxieties about her sister to Mr. Darcy. Since the marriage would soon provide a suitable conclusion to the elopement, they might have hoped to hide its scandalous start from anyone not present at the time.

She did not worry that he would spread the news. There were few individuals whose discretion she would have trusted more; yet, there was no one whose awareness of her sister’s moral failure would have embarrassed her more deeply. This was not due to any fear of personal repercussions, as a vast, unbridgeable distance already existed between them. Even if Lydia’s marriage had been arranged under the most respectable circumstances, it was inconceivable that Mr. Darcy would unite himself with a family that, among other faults, would now be linked by the closest ties to the man he so rightly despised.

She could not be surprised that he would recoil from such an association. Any desire to win her affection, which she had felt certain he possessed while in Derbyshire, could not reasonably survive such a disaster. She felt humbled and sorrowful, regretting things she could barely define. She grew possessive of his good opinion once she could no longer expect to gain from it. She longed for news of him whenever the slightest opportunity arose. She became certain she could have found happiness with him just as it became unlikely they would ever meet again.

How he would triumph, she frequently mused, if he knew that the offer she had haughtily rejected only four months prior would now be accepted with joy and gratitude! She did not doubt he was as noble as any man, yet, being human, he must surely feel a sense of victory.

She began to realize that he was precisely the individual whose character and abilities would have best complemented her own. His intellect and temperament, though distinct from hers, would have fulfilled all her desires. It was a partnership that would have benefited them both: her relaxed and spirited nature might have tempered his mind and refined his manners, while she would have gained significantly from his wisdom, insight, and worldly experience."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_026",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The time for Lydia and her husband to leave arrived quickly, and Mrs. Bennet had to accept a parting that would probably last at least a year, as her husband refused to support her plan for them all to relocate to Newcastle.

“Oh, my dearest Lydia,” she lamented, “when will we see each other again?”

“Goodness, I have no idea. Perhaps not for two or three years.”

“You must write to me frequently, my dear.”

“I will write as much as I am able. But you know that married women rarely have much time for correspondence. My sisters can write to me; they will have plenty of leisure.”

Mr. Wickham’s farewells were far warmer than his wife’s. He offered a handsome smile and spoke many charming pleasantries.

“He is as fine a man,” Mr. Bennet remarked once they had departed, “as I have ever encountered. He grins, and simpers, and flirts with us all. I am immensely proud of him. I challenge even Sir William Lucas to present a more impressive son-in-law.”

Mrs. Bennet remained quite melancholy for several days following her daughter’s departure.

“I frequently reflect,” she stated, “that there is nothing more painful than separating from one’s friends. One feels so abandoned without them.”

“You see, mother, this is the inevitable result of a daughter marrying,” Elizabeth observed. “It should make you feel more content that your other four remain unmarried.”

“That is not the case at all. Lydia is not leaving me because she is married, but simply because her husband’s military unit is stationed so far away. Had they been closer, she would not have departed so soon.”

However, the listless state this event caused was soon lifted, and her mind was once again stirred by anticipation due to a piece of news that began to spread. The housekeeper at Netherfield had been instructed to prepare for her master’s return, as he was arriving in a day or two to hunt there for several weeks. Mrs. Bennet was in a state of nervous excitement. She glanced at Jane, smiling and shaking her head by turns.

“Well, well, so Mr. Bingley is returning, sister,” she said to Mrs. Philips, who had brought the news. “Well, that is all for the best. Not that I am concerned, mind you. He means nothing to us, and I am certain I have no desire to see him again. Still, he is welcome to visit Netherfield if he chooses. And who can say what might occur? But that is of no consequence to us. You know, sister, we decided long ago never to speak of it. So, is it absolutely certain he is coming?”

“You can rely on it,” the other replied, “for Mrs. Nichols was in Meryton yesterday evening: I saw her walking by and went out specifically to confirm the truth; she assured me it was certain. He will arrive by Thursday at the latest, and likely on Wednesday. She told me she was heading to the butcher to order meat for Wednesday, and she has three pairs of ducks ready for slaughter.”

Miss Bennet could not hear of his arrival without flushing."
}

“Oh, my dear Lydia,” she cried, “when will we see each other again?”

“Goodness! I don’t know. Not for two or three years, perhaps.”

“Write to me very often, my dear.”

“As often as I can. But you know married women never have much time for writing. My sisters can write to me. They won’t have anything else to do.”

Mr. Wickham’s farewells were much more affectionate than his wife’s. He smiled, looked handsome, and said many charming things.

“He is as fine a fellow,” Mr. Bennet remarked as soon as they had left the house, “as I have ever seen. He simpers, and smirks, and flirts with us all. I am incredibly proud of him. I challenge even Sir William Lucas himself to produce a more valuable son-in-law.”

The loss of her daughter left Mrs. Bennet feeling very gloomy for several days.

“I often think,” she said, “that there is nothing worse than parting with one’s friends. One feels so lonely without them.”

“This is the result, you see, mother, of marrying off a daughter,” Elizabeth said. “It should make you more content that your other four are still single.”

“That is not the case at all. Lydia is not leaving me because she is married, but only because her husband’s regiment happens to be stationed so far away. If it had been closer, she would not have left so soon.”

However, the listless state this event had caused was soon relieved, and her mind was once again stirred by the excitement of hope, due to a piece of news that began to circulate. The housekeeper at Netherfield had received orders to prepare for the arrival of her master, who was coming down in a day or two to shoot there for several weeks. Mrs. Bennet was in a complete state of agitation. She looked at Jane, smiled, and shook her head by turns.

“Well, well, so Mr. Bingley is coming back, sister,” she said (for Mrs. Philips had first brought her the news). “Well, so much the better. Not that I care about it, though. He is nothing to us, you know, and I am sure I never want to see him again. But, regardless, he is very welcome to come to Netherfield if he likes. And who knows what might happen? But that is nothing to us. You know, sister, we agreed long ago never to mention a word about it. So, is it absolutely certain he is coming?”

“You can count on it,” the other replied, “for Mrs. Nichols was in Meryton last night. I saw her passing by and went out myself specifically to learn the truth of it, and she told me it was certainly true. He is arriving on Thursday at the latest, very likely on Wednesday. She was going to the butcher’s, she told me, specifically to order some meat for Wednesday, and she has three pairs of ducks ready to be slaughtered.”

Miss Bennet could not hear of his arrival without changing color."
}

He means nothing to us, and I am sure I never want to see him again. Still, he is very welcome to come to Netherfield if he likes. And who knows what might happen? But that is none of our business. You know, sister, we agreed long ago never to speak of it. So, is it absolutely certain he is coming?”

“You can count on it,” the other replied, “because Mrs. Nichols was in Meryton last night. I saw her passing by and went out specifically to learn the truth, and she confirmed it was certain. He is arriving on Thursday at the latest, and very likely on Wednesday. She told me she was going to the butcher’s to order meat for Wednesday, and she has three pairs of ducks ready to be prepared.”

Jane Bennet could not hear of his arrival without changing color."
}
