It merely stated, ‘Lately, George Wickham, Esq., to Miss Lydia Bennet,’ without mentioning her father, her residence, or any other details. My brother Gardiner drafted it, and I am surprised he managed such a clumsy job. Did you happen to notice it?”

Bingley answered that he had, and offered his congratulations. Elizabeth did not venture to look up, so she could not observe Mr. Darcy’s expression.

“It is certainly a wonderful thing to have a daughter married well,” her mother persisted; “yet, Mr. Bingley, it is quite difficult to have her removed from my side. They have traveled to Newcastle, which seems to be quite far north, and they are to remain there for an indefinite period. His regiment is stationed there; for I assume you have heard of his departure from the ----shire militia and his entry into the Regulars. Thank goodness he has some friends, even if he perhaps does not have as many as he merits.”

Elizabeth, recognizing this remark was aimed at Mr. Darcy, felt such agonizing embarrassment that she could barely remain in her chair. It forced her to speak, however, which nothing else had managed to do; she inquired of Bingley if he intended to remain in the neighborhood for long. He believed he would stay for a few weeks.

“Once you have finished hunting your own birds, Mr. Bingley,” her mother added, “I hope you will come here and shoot as many as you like on Mr. Bennet’s estate. I am certain he would be delighted to accommodate you and will preserve the finest coveys for your benefit.”

Elizabeth’s distress grew at such uncalled-for, meddlesome hospitality! She was convinced that if the same promising situation were to emerge now as had encouraged them a year ago, everything would be rushing toward the same frustrating outcome. In that moment, she felt that years of joy could not compensate Jane or herself for such moments of painful humiliation.

“My greatest desire,” she thought to herself, “is to never again be in the presence of either of them. Their company offers no delight that could justify such misery! May I never encounter either one of them again!”

However, the suffering for which years of happiness could provide no remedy was soon eased by noticing how much her sister’s beauty reignited the admiration of her former suitor. Upon his arrival, he had spoken to her sparingly, but with every passing moment, he seemed to grant her more of his focus. He found her just as lovely as he had the previous year; just as kind and sincere, though perhaps slightly less talkative. Jane was eager that no change in her should be noticed, and she truly believed she was conversing as much as ever; yet, her thoughts were so occupied that she was not always aware when she had fallen silent.

When the men stood to depart, Mrs. Bennet remembered her planned courtesy, and they were invited and agreed to dine at Longbourn in a few days."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_028",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Has my nephew proposed marriage to you?”

“Your Ladyship has already stated that such a thing is impossible.”

“It certainly should be, provided he remains in his right mind. However, your cunning and charms might have caused him, in a fit of madness, to disregard his duty to himself and his relatives. You may have ensnared him.”

“If I had, I would be the last person to admit it.”

“Miss Bennet, are you aware of my identity? I am not used to being spoken to in this manner. As one of his closest living relatives, I have a right to be informed of his most private affairs.”

“You have no right to know mine, and your current conduct will certainly not encourage me to be open with you.”

“Let me be perfectly clear. This union, which you are arrogant enough to desire, will never happen. Never. Mr. Darcy is betrothed to my daughter. What is your response to that?”

“Only this: if that is the case, you have no grounds to believe he would propose to me.”

Lady Catherine paused briefly before answering:

“Their engagement is of a unique nature. They have been destined for one another since they were infants. It was the cherished desire of both his mother and mine. We arranged this match while they were still in their cribs, and now, just as the sisters’ wishes are about to be fulfilled, is their marriage to be thwarted by a young woman of lower social standing, no significance, and no connection to the family? Do you have no respect for the desires of his family, or for his unspoken commitment to Miss de Bourgh? Have you no sense of decency or propriety? Have you not heard me state that he was intended for his cousin from his earliest days?”

“Yes, I had heard that previously. But how does that concern me? If there is no other obstacle to my marrying your nephew, I will not be deterred simply because his mother and aunt wanted him to marry Miss de Bourgh. You both did your part in planning the marriage, but its success relied on others. If Mr. Darcy is not bound to his cousin by either duty or desire, why shouldn't he choose someone else? And if I am that choice, why shouldn't I accept him?”

“Because honor, propriety, wisdom, and even self-interest forbid it. Yes, Miss Bennet, self-interest; do not expect to be acknowledged by his family or friends if you deliberately defy their wishes. You will be criticized, ignored, and looked down upon by everyone associated with him. Your marriage will be a scandal; your name will never be spoken by any of us.”

“Those are significant hardships,” Elizabeth answered. “But the wife of Mr. Darcy must possess such remarkable advantages that she would, on the whole, have no reason to complain.”

“You stubborn, willful girl! I am embarrassed for you! Is this how you show gratitude for the kindness I showed you last spring? Do you owe me nothing for that? Let us be seated. You must realize, Miss Bennet, that I arrived here with the firm intention of succeeding, and I will not be turned away from my goal.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_028",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Has he—has my nephew—asked you to marry him?”

“Your Ladyship has already stated that it is impossible.”

“It should be, and it must be, as long as he is in his right mind. But your tricks and seductions may have caused him, in a moment of weakness, to forget his duty to himself and his entire family. You may have manipulated him.”

“If I had, I would be the last person to admit it.”

“Miss Bennet, do you know who I am? I am not used to being spoken to like this. I am nearly his closest living relative, and I have a right to know all his most private affairs.”

“But you have no right to know mine, and this kind of behavior will never make me open up to you.”

“Let me be perfectly clear. This match, which you have the audacity to hope for, will never happen. No, never. Mr. Darcy is engaged to my daughter. Now, what do you have to say to that?”

“Only this: if that is true, you have no reason to think he would ever propose to me.”

Lady Catherine paused for a moment, then replied,

“Their engagement is of a unique nature. They have been intended for one another since they were infants. It was the deepest wish of his mother, as well as mine. We planned their union while they were still in their cradles. And now, just as the wishes of both sisters are about to be fulfilled, is their marriage to be ruined by a young woman of lower social standing, with no status in the world, and no connection to our family? Do you have no respect for the wishes of his family, or for his unspoken commitment to Miss de Bourgh? Have you lost all sense of propriety and decency? Haven't you heard me say that he was destined for his cousin from the time he was born?”

“Yes, and I had heard it before. But what does that have to do with me? If there is no other reason I shouldn't marry your nephew, I certainly won't be stopped just because his mother and aunt wanted him to marry Miss de Bourgh. You both did everything you could to arrange the marriage, but its success depended on others. If Mr. Darcy is not bound to his cousin by honor or desire, why shouldn't he make his own choice? And if I am that choice, why shouldn't I accept him?”

“Because honor, decorum, prudence—and even your own interests—forbid it. Yes, Miss Bennet, your interests; do not expect to be acknowledged by his family or friends if you deliberately act against everyone’s wishes. You will be criticized, ignored, and looked down upon by everyone connected to him. Your marriage will be a disgrace; your name will never even be spoken by any of us.”

“Those are serious misfortunes,” Elizabeth replied. “But the wife of Mr. Darcy would have such extraordinary sources of happiness attached to her position that, all things considered, she would have no reason to complain.”

“Stubborn, headstrong girl! I am ashamed of you! Is this how you show gratitude for the attention I gave you last spring? Do you owe me nothing for that? Let us sit down. You must understand, Miss Bennet, that I came here with the firm intention of getting my way, and I will not be talked out of it.”"
}

“Their engagement is of a special nature. They have been intended for each other since they were infants. It was the favorite wish of his mother, as well as mine. We planned their union while they were still in their cradles. Now, just as the wishes of both mothers are about to be fulfilled, is their marriage to be stopped by a young woman of lower social standing, with no importance in the world, and no connection to the family? Do you have no respect for the wishes of his family, or for his unspoken agreement with Miss de Bourgh? Have you lost all sense of propriety and decency? Have you not heard me say that he was destined for his cousin from his earliest days?”

“Yes, I have heard that before. But what does that have to do with me? If there is no other objection to my marrying your nephew, I will certainly not be stopped by the fact that his mother and aunt wanted him to marry Miss de Bourgh. You both did what you could to plan the marriage, but its success depended on others. If Mr. Darcy is not bound to his cousin by honor or by his own desires, why shouldn't he make a different choice? And if I am that choice, why shouldn't I accept him?”

“Because honor, social standards, and common sense—and even your own self-interest—forbid it. Yes, Miss Bennet, your interest; do not expect to be accepted by his family or friends if you deliberately act against everyone’s wishes. You will be criticized, ignored, and looked down upon by everyone connected to him. Your marriage will be a disgrace, and your name will never even be mentioned by any of us.”

“Those are serious misfortunes,” Elizabeth replied. “But the wife of Mr. Darcy would have such extraordinary sources of happiness in her life that she would, on the whole, have no reason to complain.”

“You stubborn, headstrong girl! I am ashamed of you! Is this your gratitude for the attention I showed you last spring? Do you owe me nothing for that? Let us sit down. You must understand, Miss Bennet, that I came here with the firm intention of getting what I want, and I will not be talked out of it.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_029",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“No, truly, I experienced nothing but astonishment.”

“Your astonishment could not exceed my own at being observed by you. My conscience whispered that I merited no exceptional courtesy, and I admit I did not anticipate obtaining more than I was entitled to.”

“My intention then,” Darcy answered, “was to demonstrate to you, through every kindness within my reach, that I was not so base as to harbor resentment for the past; and I hoped to secure your pardon, to diminish your poor opinion, by showing you that your criticisms had been heeded. How quickly other desires took root, I can scarcely say, but I suspect it was within half an hour of seeing you.”

He then spoke of Georgiana’s pleasure in her company, and of her dismay at its abrupt conclusion; this naturally brought them to the reason for that interruption, and she soon discovered that his decision to pursue her from Derbyshire in search of her sister had been reached before he left the inn, and that his solemnity and contemplation there had stemmed from nothing but the internal conflicts such an objective required.

She voiced her thanks once more, yet the topic was too distressing for either to linger upon.

Having strolled several miles at a relaxed pace, and being too preoccupied to notice their surroundings, they eventually discovered, upon checking their timepieces, that they needed to return home.

“What could have happened to Mr. Bingley and Jane?” was a query that prompted a conversation regarding their situation. Darcy was overjoyed by their betrothal; his companion had shared the news with him immediately.

“I must inquire if you were taken by surprise?” Elizabeth asked.

“Not in the least. When I departed, I sensed it would soon come to pass.”

“Which is to say, you had granted your consent. I suspected as much.” And although he protested the phrasing, she realized it had been essentially the truth.

“On the night before I left for London,” he explained, “I made an admission to him, which I believe I should have offered long before. I recounted everything that had transpired to render my previous meddling in his matters ridiculous and intrusive. His astonishment was profound. He had never harbored the slightest inkling. Furthermore, I told him that I believed I had been in error to assume, as I once did, that your sister was cold toward him; and since I could readily see that his devotion to her remained unchanged, I felt certain of their future happiness.”

Elizabeth could not suppress a smile at his casual way of guiding his friend.

“Did you speak from your own assessment,” she asked, “when you informed him that my sister loved him, or simply from what I told you last spring?”

“From the former. I had watched her closely during the two visits I recently paid her here; and I was certain of her feelings.”

“And your certainty, I imagine, brought him immediate assurance.”

“It did. Bingley is genuinely humble. His lack of confidence had kept him from trusting his own instincts in such a delicate matter, but his faith in mine made everything simple. I was forced to admit one detail, which for a time, and quite rightly, angered him. I could not bring myself to hide that your sister had been in the city for three months last winter, that I had been aware of it, and had deliberately withheld the information from him.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "AUSTEN_PRIDE_AND_PREJUDICE_030",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Yet I felt quite awkward.”

“As did I.”

“You could have conversed with me more during dinner.”

“A gentleman with fewer feelings might have.”

“How unfortunate that you possess such a logical defense, and that I am sensible enough to accept it! Still, I ponder how long you might have remained silent had you been solitary. I wonder when you would have addressed me had I not initiated the conversation! My decision to express gratitude for your assistance regarding Lydia certainly had a significant impact. Perhaps too much; for what remains of the moral lesson if our contentment arises from a broken vow, given that I should not have broached the topic? This is quite improper.”

“Do not trouble yourself. The moral remains entirely sound. Lady Catherine’s unwarranted attempts to divide us served only to dispel my remaining hesitations. My current joy does not stem from your impulsive need to show appreciation. I was not in a frame of mind to await your lead. My aunt’s report provided me with hope, and I resolved to ascertain the truth immediately.”

“Lady Catherine has proven immensely helpful, which should please her, as she delights in being useful. But tell me, what was your true objective in visiting Netherfield? Was it simply to ride toward Longbourn and feel flustered, or did you anticipate more significant results?”

“My genuine intention was to see you and determine, if possible, whether I might ever earn your affection. My stated purpose, or at least what I admitted to myself, was to observe if your sister still favored Bingley, and if so, to offer him the admission I have since provided.”

“Will you ever possess the nerve to inform Lady Catherine of what is to happen?”

“I am more likely to lack the time than the nerve, Elizabeth. Yet it must be accomplished; if you provide me with stationery, I shall attend to it at once.”

“If I did not have my own correspondence to finish, I might sit beside you and marvel at your steady penmanship, as another young lady once did. However, I have an aunt who must not be ignored any longer.”

Because she was reluctant to admit how greatly her closeness with Mr. Darcy had been exaggerated, Elizabeth had not yet replied to Mrs. Gardiner’s lengthy letter; yet now, possessing news she knew would be most pleasing, she felt nearly embarrassed that her aunt and uncle had already missed three days of joy, and she promptly began to write:

“My dear aunt, I should have thanked you sooner, as was proper, for your lengthy, kind, and thorough account; but, to be honest, I was too irritable to write. You imagined more than was actually the case. But now, imagine whatever you wish; let your fancy roam, indulge your imagination in every possible direction, and unless you believe me already wed, you cannot be far from the truth. You must write back very soon, and offer him much more praise than you did previously. I thank you again and again for avoiding the Lakes.”"
}

I don’t owe my current happiness to your eager desire to express gratitude. I wasn’t in the mood to wait for you to open the conversation. My aunt’s news had given me hope, and I was determined to know everything immediately.”

“Lady Catherine has been incredibly useful, which should make her happy, as she loves to be useful. But tell me, why did you come down to Netherfield? Was it just to ride to Longbourn and feel awkward, or did you intend for something more serious to happen?”

“My real purpose was to see you and to judge, if I could, whether I might ever hope to make you love me. My stated purpose—or what I told myself—was to see if your sister was still fond of Bingley, and if she were, to make the confession to him that I have since made.”

“Will you ever have the courage to announce to Lady Catherine what is about to happen to her?”

“I am more likely to lack time than courage, Elizabeth. But it must be done; and if you will give me a sheet of paper, it shall be done right away.”

“And if I didn’t have a letter to write myself, I might sit by you and admire the evenness of your handwriting, as another young lady once did. But I have an aunt, too, who must not be neglected any longer.”

Now, however, having news to share that she knew would be most welcome, she was almost ashamed to realize that her uncle and aunt had already lost three days of happiness, and she immediately wrote the following:

  "rewritten_text": "“But I was embarrassed.”

“And so was I.”

“You could have spoken to me more when you came to dinner.”

“A man who felt less might have.”

“How unfortunate that you have a logical answer, and that I am reasonable enough to accept it! But I wonder how long you would have continued if left to your own devices. I wonder when you would have spoken if I hadn’t asked you! My decision to thank you for your kindness to Lydia certainly had a major effect. Too much, I fear; for what happens to the moral of the story if our comfort comes from breaking a promise, since I shouldn’t have brought up the subject? This won’t do at all.”

“You needn’t worry. The moral will be perfectly sound. Lady Catherine’s unjustifiable attempts to separate us were the very things that removed all my doubts. I don’t owe my current happiness to your eager desire to express gratitude. I wasn’t in the mood to wait for you to open the conversation. My aunt’s news had given me hope, and I was determined to know everything immediately.”

“Lady Catherine has been incredibly useful, which should make her happy, as she loves to be useful. But tell me, why did you come down to Netherfield? Was it just to ride to Longbourn and feel awkward, or did you intend for something more serious to happen?”

“My real purpose was to see you and to judge, if I could, whether I might ever hope to make you love me. My stated purpose—or what I told myself—was to see if your sister was still fond of Bingley, and if she were, to make the confession to him that I have since made.”

“Will you ever have the courage to announce to Lady Catherine what is about to happen to her?”

“I am more likely to lack time than courage, Elizabeth. But it must be done; and if you will give me a sheet of paper, it shall be done right away.”

“And if I didn’t have a letter to write myself, I might sit by you and admire the evenness of your handwriting, as another young lady once did. But I have an aunt, too, who must not be neglected any longer.”

Because she was unwilling to admit how much her intimacy with Mr. Darcy had been exaggerated, Elizabeth had not yet answered Mrs. Gardiner’s long letter. You must write again very soon and praise him much more than you did in your last letter. I thank you again and again for not going to the Lakes.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_001",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Hurrying along, I had just traversed a trench I recognized as being close to the Battery and climbed the embankment beyond it, when I spotted a man sitting ahead. His back was turned, his arms were crossed, and he was nodding off, overcome by slumber. 

Thinking he might be more pleased if I surprised him with his morning meal, I approached quietly and tapped his shoulder. He sprang up immediately, and it was not the same individual, but a different man entirely!

Yet this fellow was also clad in rough grey, wore a heavy iron shackle on his leg, and was limping, raspy, and shivering—he possessed every trait of the first man, save for his features and the flat, wide-brimmed, low-crowned felt hat he wore. I took all this in instantly, as I had only a second to observe: he cursed at me, swung at me—a clumsy, feeble strike that missed and nearly toppled him over—and then he vanished into the fog, tripping twice as he fled, until he was out of sight.

"It's the young man!" I mused, my heart leaping as I recognized him. I suspect I would have felt a pang in my liver, too, had I known its location.

I reached the Battery shortly thereafter, and there stood the correct man—clutching himself and pacing back and forth, as if he had spent the entire night doing nothing else—awaiting my arrival. He was dreadfully cold, to be sure. I half anticipated seeing him collapse before me and perish from the bitter chill. His eyes appeared so ravenous that when I gave him the file and he set it upon the grass, it crossed my mind that he might have attempted to consume it had he not noticed my parcel. He did not invert me this time to reach my supplies, but allowed me to remain upright while I unpacked the bundle and cleared my pockets.

"What is inside the flask, boy?" he inquired.

"Brandy," I replied.

He was already shoveling mincemeat into his mouth in a peculiar fashion—more like someone stowing it away in a frantic rush than someone actually eating—but he paused to drink. He trembled so intensely the whole time that he struggled to keep the bottle's neck between his teeth without snapping it off.

"I believe you have the ague," I remarked.

"I share your assessment, lad," he answered.

"It is severe around here," I informed him. "You have been sleeping out on the marshes, and they are terribly prone to ague. And rheumatism as well."

"I shall finish my meal before they kill me," he declared. "I would do so even if I were to be hanged from that gallows over there immediately afterward. I will defeat the tremors that much, I promise you."

He was devouring mincemeat, meatbone, bread, cheese, and pork pie all at once, casting suspicious glances at the surrounding mist while he ate, and frequently pausing—even ceasing his chewing—to listen."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_001",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Moving along as quickly as I could, I had just crossed a ditch I knew was very close to the Battery, and had just scrambled up the mound on the other side, when I saw a man sitting in front of me. His back was turned, his arms were folded, and he was nodding forward, heavy with sleep.

I thought he would be more pleased if I surprised him with his breakfast, so I stepped forward quietly and touched him on the shoulder. He jumped up instantly, and it was not the same man, but someone else entirely!

Yet this man was also dressed in coarse grey, had a heavy iron shackle on his leg, was limping, hoarse, and cold, and was everything the other man was, except that his face was different and he wore a flat, broad-brimmed, low-crowned felt hat. I saw all this in an instant, for I only had a moment to look: he swore at me, took a swing at me—a weak, circular blow that missed and nearly knocked him over, causing him to stumble—and then he ran into the mist, tripping twice as he went, until I lost sight of him.

"It’s the young man!" I thought, feeling my heart race as I recognized him. I suspect I would have felt a pain in my liver, too, if I had known where it was located.

I reached the Battery soon after that, and there was the right man—hugging himself and limping back and forth, as if he had never stopped hugging and limping all night—waiting for me. He was incredibly cold, to be sure. I half expected to see him collapse in front of me and die from the extreme chill. His eyes looked so ravenous that when I handed him the file and he set it on the grass, it occurred to me he might have tried to eat it if he hadn't seen my bundle. He didn't turn me upside down this time to get at my things, but left me standing while I opened the bundle and emptied my pockets.

"What’s in the bottle, boy?" he asked.

"Brandy," I said.

He was already shoving mincemeat down his throat in a strange way—more like a man hiding it somewhere in a desperate hurry than a man eating—but he stopped to take some of the liquor. He shivered so violently the whole time that it was all he could do to keep the neck of the bottle between his teeth without biting it off.

"I think you have the chills," I said.

"I’m inclined to agree, boy," he replied.

"It’s bad around here," I told him. "You’ve been lying out on the marshes, and they’re terrible for chills. And rheumatism, too."

"I’ll eat my breakfast before they kill me," he said. "I’d do that even if I were going to be hanged on that gallows over there immediately afterward. I’ll beat the shivers that much, I bet you."

He was gobbling up mincemeat, meat bone, bread, cheese, and pork pie all at once, staring suspiciously at the mist all around us while he did so, and often pausing—even stopping his chewing—to listen."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_002",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The sergeant finished his drink once more and appeared entirely prepared for a refill. I observed that Mr. Pumblechook, in his role as host, seemed to overlook the fact that he had gifted the wine, instead taking the bottle from Mrs. Joe and claiming the praise for serving it with a burst of cheer. Even I received a portion. He was so generous with the wine that he requested the second bottle, distributing it with equal open-handedness once the first was empty.

As I observed them gathered around the forge, clearly relishing the moment, I reflected on what an excellent seasoning for a meal my runaway acquaintance on the marshes had become. They had not enjoyed themselves nearly as much before the event was enlivened by the thrill he provided. Now, as they waited eagerly for the capture of “the two villains,” and as the bellows seemed to howl for the fugitives, the flames to blaze for them, the smoke to race in pursuit, Joe to strike and ring for them, and all the dark shadows on the wall to tremble in threat as the fire flickered and the glowing embers fell and faded, the dim afternoon outside appeared to my sympathetic young imagination to have turned pale on their behalf, poor souls.

Eventually, Joe finished his task, and the clanging and roaring ceased. While putting on his coat, Joe gathered the nerve to suggest that some of us accompany the soldiers to see the outcome of the search. Mr. Pumblechook and Mr. Hubble refused, citing a desire for a pipe and the company of the ladies; however, Mr. Wopsle agreed to go if Joe would. Joe expressed his willingness and offered to bring me, provided Mrs. Joe consented. I am certain we never would have received permission to leave, had it not been for Mrs. Joe’s desire to learn every detail of how it concluded. As it happened, she only insisted, “If you return with the boy’s head shattered by a musket, do not expect me to reassemble it.”

The sergeant bid the ladies a courteous farewell and parted from Mr. Pumblechook as if they were brothers-in-arms, though I suspect he was not as keenly aware of that man’s virtues when things were dry as he was when something wet was available. His soldiers shouldered their muskets and formed ranks. Mr. Wopsle, Joe, and I were strictly ordered to remain behind them and to stay silent once we arrived at the marshes. When we were out in the biting air and marching steadily toward our objective, I whispered treasonously to Joe, “I hope, Joe, that we do not find them.” Joe whispered back, “I would pay a shilling if they had escaped, Pip.”

No stragglers from the village joined us, as the weather was frigid and ominous, the path bleak, the ground difficult, night was approaching, and the residents were enjoying their fires indoors while observing the holiday. A few faces peered from bright windows as we passed, but no one emerged. We went by the signpost and continued directly toward the churchyard. There, we were halted for a few moments by a gesture from the sergeant, while several of his men spread out among the tombstones and inspected the porch."
}

Mr. Pumblechook and Mr. Hubble refused, citing a desire for a pipe and the company of the ladies; however, Mr. Wopsle agreed to go if Joe would. Joe expressed his willingness and offered to bring me, provided Mrs. Joe consented. I am certain we never would have received permission to leave, had it not been for Mrs. Joe’s desire to learn every detail of how it concluded. As it happened, she only insisted, “If you return with the boy’s head shattered by a musket, do not expect me to reassemble it.”

The sergeant bid the ladies a courteous farewell and parted from Mr. Pumblechook as if they were brothers-in-arms, though I suspect he was not as keenly aware of that man’s virtues when things were dry as he was when something wet was available. His soldiers shouldered their muskets and formed ranks. Mr. Wopsle, Joe, and I were strictly ordered to remain behind them and to stay silent once we arrived at the marshes. When we were out in the biting air and marching steadily toward our objective, I whispered treasonously to Joe, “I hope, Joe, that we do not find them.” Joe whispered back, “I would pay a shilling if they had escaped, Pip.”

No stragglers from the village joined us, as the weather was frigid and ominous, the path bleak, the ground difficult, night was approaching, and the residents were enjoying their fires indoors while observing the holiday. A few faces peered from bright windows as we passed, but no one emerged. We went by the signpost and continued directly toward the churchyard. There, we were halted for a few moments by a gesture from the sergeant, while several of his men spread out among the tombstones and inspected the porch."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_003",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I bent toward Joe and, using my index finger to guide him, read the entire letter aloud. 

“Incredible!” Joe remarked once I concluded. “You truly are a learned fellow.” 

“How do you spell Gargery, Joe?” I inquired, adopting a tone of humble condescension. 

“I don’t spell it at all,” Joe replied. 

“But what if you were to try?” 

“It is not possible to suppose such a thing,” Joe insisted. “Even though I am quite fond of reading myself.” 

“Are you, Joe?” 

“Extremely. Give me,” Joe said, “a fine book or a quality newspaper, place me before a warm fire, and I desire nothing more. Goodness!” he added, after massaging his knees briefly, “when you reach a J and an O, and you declare, ‘Here, at last, is a J-O, Joe,’ how fascinating reading becomes!” 

I gathered from this that Joe’s schooling, much like steam power, was still in its early stages. Continuing the conversation, I asked, “Did you never attend school, Joe, back when you were my size?” 

“No, Pip.” 

“Why did you never go to school, Joe, when you were as little as I am?” 

“Well, Pip,” Joe said, grabbing the poker and preparing for his usual habit when deep in thought, which was to slowly stir the embers between the bottom grate bars; “I will explain. My father, Pip, had a weakness for drink, and when he was overcome by it, he would beat my mother quite mercilessly. It was nearly the only hammering he performed, aside from striking me. And he struck me with a vigor only matched by the intensity with which he avoided striking his anvil. You are listening and following along, Pip?” 

“Yes, Joe.” 

“As a result, my mother and I fled from my father on several occasions; then my mother would find work and say, ‘Joe,’ she would say, ‘now, God willing, you shall receive some education, my child,’ and she would enroll me in school. But my father was so kind-hearted that he could not bear to be apart from us. Consequently, he would arrive with a massive crowd and create such a disturbance at the doors of our lodgings that the people were forced to stop sheltering us and hand us back to him. Then he would bring us home and beat us. Which, you see, Pip,” Joe said, pausing his thoughtful raking of the fire to look at me, “was a hindrance to my education.” 

“Indeed, poor Joe!” 

“Though keep in mind, Pip,” Joe added, tapping the top bar with the poker in a measured way, “to give everyone their due and maintain fairness between man and man, my father was truly good-hearted, do you see?” 

I did not see, but I kept that to myself. 

“Anyway!” Joe continued, “someone has to keep the pot boiling, Pip, or the pot will not boil, don’t you understand?” 

I understood that, and told him so. 

“As a result, my father did not object to my working; so I entered my current trade, which would have been his as well, had he chosen to pursue it, and I labored quite hard, I promise you, Pip. Eventually, I was able to support him, and I cared for him until he passed away from a seizure. It was my intention to have inscribed on his headstone that, whatever his shortcomings, Remember reader he was truly good-hearted.”"
}

“Are you, Joe?”

“Quite. Give me,” Joe said, “a good book or a good newspaper, sit me down in front of a good fire, and I ask for nothing more. Lord!” he continued, after rubbing his knees for a moment, “when you finally reach a J and an O, and you say, ‘Here, at last, is a J-O, Joe,’ how interesting reading is!”

I gathered from this that Joe’s education, like steam power, was still in its infancy. Continuing the conversation, I asked, “Didn’t you ever go to school, Joe, when you were as small as I am?”

“No, Pip.”

“Why didn’t you ever go to school, Joe, when you were as small as I am?”

“Well, Pip,” Joe said, picking up the poker and settling into his usual habit when thoughtful—slowly raking the fire between the lower bars—“I’ll tell you. “Yes, Joe.”

“Certainly, poor Joe!”

I didn’t see it, but I didn’t say so.

“Well!” Joe continued, “someone has to keep the pot boiling, Pip, or the pot won’t boil, don’t you know?”

I understood that and said so.

“Consequently, my father didn’t object to me going to work; so I went to work at my current trade, which would have been his too if he had stuck to it, and I worked reasonably hard, I assure you, Pip. My father, Pip, was a heavy drinker, and when he was drunk, he beat my mother mercilessly. It was almost the only work he did, except for beating me. He hammered at me with a vigor only matched by the vigor with which he avoided his anvil. You are listening and understanding, Pip?”

“Yes, Joe.”

“Consequently, my mother and I ran away from my father several times. My mother would go out to work and say, ‘Joe, please God, you shall have some schooling, child,’ and she would send me to school. But my father was so good-hearted that he could not bear to be without us. He would arrive with a loud crowd and make such a scene at the doors of the houses where we stayed that the people were forced to turn us away and give us back to him. Then he took us home and beat us. Which, you see, Pip,” Joe said, pausing his raking to look at me, “was a drawback to my education.”

“Certainly, poor Joe!”

“Though mind you, Pip,” Joe added, tapping the poker against the top bar, “to be fair and maintain justice between man and man, my father was truly good-hearted, don’t you see?”

I did not see, but I did not say so.

“Well!” Joe continued, “someone must keep the pot boiling, Pip, or the pot will not boil, don’t you know?”

I understood that and told him so.

“Consequently, my father did not object to my going to work. So, I took up my current trade, which would have been his as well if he had stuck to it, and I worked quite hard, I assure you, Pip. In time, I was able to support him, and I kept him until he died from a seizure. It was my intention to have had written on his tombstone that, whatever his failings, the reader should remember he was good-hearted.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_004",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“You remain silent regarding her,” Miss Havisham observed while watching me. “She speaks quite harshly of you, yet you say nothing of her. What is your opinion of her?”

“I would rather not say,” I faltered.

“Whisper it in my ear,” Miss Havisham commanded, leaning toward me.

“I believe she is quite arrogant,” I murmured.

“Is there more?”

“I think she is very beautiful.”

“Anything else?”

“I find her very rude.” (At that moment, she regarded me with a look of intense dislike.)

“Anything else?”

“I think I would like to return home.”

“And never see her again, despite her beauty?”

“I am not certain I would not want to see her again, but I would prefer to go home now.”

“You will leave shortly,” Miss Havisham stated aloud. “Finish the game.”

Except for that one strange smile at the beginning, I felt certain Miss Havisham’s face was incapable of smiling. It had settled into a brooding, vigilant mask—likely from the time her surroundings became frozen in place—and it seemed as though nothing could ever alter it. Her posture was stooped, and her voice was low, possessing a lifeless quality; she appeared to have collapsed, both physically and spiritually, under the burden of some devastating misfortune.

I finished the game with Estella, and she left me with nothing. Once she had won every card, she tossed them onto the table as if she held them in contempt for having been taken from me.

“When shall you return?” Miss Havisham asked. “Let me consider.”

I started to mention that it was Wednesday, but she stopped me with that familiar, impatient flick of her right hand.

“Enough! I do not keep track of days or weeks. Return in six days. Do you understand?”

“Yes, ma’am.”

“Estella, lead him downstairs. Provide him with food, and let him wander the grounds while he eats. Go, Pip.”

I followed the candle down just as I had followed it up, and she placed it back where we had first seen it. Until she unlatched the side door, I had assumed, without really considering it, that it must be night. The sudden brightness of the day startled me, making it feel as though I had spent many hours in the dim light of that peculiar room.

“Wait here, boy,” Estella commanded before vanishing and shutting the door.

Left alone in the courtyard, I seized the chance to examine my rough hands and ordinary boots. I did not think highly of them. They had never bothered me previously, but now they seemed like vulgar traits. I resolved to ask Joe why he had taught me to refer to those playing cards as Jacks, when they should be called knaves. I wished Joe had been raised with more refinement, as I would have been as well.

She returned carrying bread, meat, and a small mug of beer. She set the mug upon the courtyard stones and handed me the food without glancing at me, with the same disdain one might show a disgraced dog."
}

{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_004",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“You say nothing about her,” Miss Havisham remarked to me, while watching. “She says many harsh things about you, but you say nothing about her. What is your opinion of her?”

“I would rather not say,” I stammered.

“Whisper it in my ear,” Miss Havisham said, leaning down.

“I think she is very proud,” I replied, in a whisper.

“Anything else?”

“I think she is very pretty.”

“Anything else?”

“I think she is very insulting.” (She was looking at me then with a look of intense dislike.)

“Anything else?”

“I think I would like to go home.”

“And never see her again, even though she is so pretty?”

“I am not sure that I wouldn’t like to see her again, but I would like to go home now.”

“You will go soon,” Miss Havisham said, aloud. “Finish the game.”

Except for that one strange smile at the beginning, I would have felt almost certain that Miss Havisham’s face was incapable of smiling. It had settled into a watchful and brooding expression—likely since everything around her had become frozen in time—and it looked as if nothing could ever lift it again. Her chest had slumped, causing her to stoop, and her voice had dropped, so that she spoke quietly and with a lifeless tone; altogether, she had the appearance of having collapsed, body and soul, inside and out, under the weight of a devastating blow.

I played the game to the end with Estella, and she cleaned me out. She threw the cards onto the table once she had won them all, as if she despised them for having been won from me.

“When shall I have you here again?” Miss Havisham asked. “Let me think.”

I was starting to remind her that today was Wednesday, when she stopped me with her previous impatient flick of her right hand’s fingers.

“There, there! I know nothing of days of the week; I know nothing of weeks of the year. Come back in six days. Do you hear?”

“Yes, ma’am.”

“Estella, take him downstairs. Let him have something to eat, and let him wander and look around while he eats. Go, Pip.”

I followed the candle down, just as I had followed it up, and she placed it where we had first found it. Until she opened the side door, I had assumed, without really thinking about it, that it must be nighttime. The rush of daylight completely confused me, making me feel as if I had been in the candlelight of that strange room for many hours.

“You are to wait here, boy,” Estella said; then she disappeared and closed the door.

I took the chance of being alone in the courtyard to look at my rough hands and my ordinary boots. My opinion of those features was not favorable. They had never bothered me before, but they bothered me now, as vulgar traits. I decided to ask Joe why he had ever taught me to call those picture cards Jacks, which should be called knaves. I wished Joe had been raised with more refinement, and then I would have been as well.

She returned with some bread, meat, and a small mug of beer. She set the mug down on the stone yard and handed me the bread and meat without looking at me, as insolently as if I were a dog in disgrace."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_005",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Well, miss?” I replied, nearly tripping over her and catching myself.

She stood watching me, and naturally, I stood watching her.

“Am I pretty?”

“Yes; I think you are very pretty.”

“Am I insulting?”

“Not as much as you were last time,” I said.

“Not as much?”

“No.”

She flared up at that last question and slapped my face with all the strength she had when I answered it.

“Well?” she said. “You little coarse monster, what do you think of me now?”

“I won’t tell you.”

“Because you’re going to tell on me upstairs. Is that it?”

“No,” I said, “that’s not it.”

“Why don’t you cry again, you little wretch?”

“Because I’ll never cry for you again,” I said. That was, I suppose, as dishonest a statement as was ever made; for I was crying for her on the inside even then, and I know full well the pain she caused me later on.

We continued upstairs after this incident; and, as we climbed, we encountered a gentleman feeling his way down.

“Who have we here?” the gentleman asked, stopping to look at me.

“A boy,” Estella said.

He was a burly man with an extremely dark complexion, an exceptionally large head, and a hand to match. He grabbed my chin in his large hand and tilted my face up to examine me by the candlelight. He was balding prematurely on top and had thick black eyebrows that refused to lie flat, standing up instead in stiff bristles. His eyes were set deep in his skull and were unpleasantly sharp and suspicious. He wore a large watch-chain and had dark, coarse stubble where his beard and whiskers would have been had he let them grow. He meant nothing to me, and I could have had no way of knowing then that he would ever become significant to me, but it happened that I had this chance to observe him closely.

“Local boy? Hey?” he asked.

“Yes, sir,” I said.

“How did you get here?”

“Miss Havisham sent for me, sir,” I explained.

“Well! Behave yourself. I have quite a lot of experience with boys, and you’re a bad bunch. Now listen!” he said, biting the side of his large forefinger as he frowned at me, “you behave yourself!”

With those words, he let me go—which I was relieved about, as his hand smelled of scented soap—and went on his way downstairs. I wondered if he might be a doctor; but no, I thought; he couldn’t be a doctor, or he would have a calmer and more persuasive manner. There wasn’t much time to ponder this, for we were soon in Miss Havisham’s room, where she and everything else remained exactly as I had left them. Estella left me standing by the door, and I waited there until Miss Havisham looked at me from her dressing-table.

“So!” she said, without appearing startled or surprised: “the days have passed, have they?”

“Yes, ma’am. Today is—”

“There, there, there!” she said with an impatient flick of her fingers. “I don’t want to know. Are you ready to play?”

I was forced to answer, feeling somewhat flustered, “I don’t think I am, ma’am.”"
}

He was a heavy-set man with a very dark complexion, an exceptionally large head, and a hand to match. He took my chin in his large hand and tilted my face up to examine me by the candlelight. He was balding early, and his bushy black eyebrows stood up in stiff bristles. His eyes were set deep in his head and looked unpleasantly sharp and suspicious. He wore a large watch-chain and had dark, coarse spots where his beard and whiskers would have been if he had let them grow. He meant nothing to me, and I could not have known then that he would ever be important, but I had this chance to observe him closely.

“A local boy? Hey?” he asked.

“Yes, sir,” I said.

“How did you get here?”

“Miss Havisham sent for me, sir,” I explained.

“Well! Behave yourself. I have a great deal of experience with boys, and you are a bad group. Now, listen!” he said, biting the side of his large forefinger as he frowned at me, “you behave yourself!”

With those words, he let me go—which I was glad of, as his hand smelled of scented soap—and went on his way downstairs. I wondered if he might be a doctor, but I decided no; he could not be a doctor, or he would have a quieter, more persuasive manner. There was little time to think about it, as we were soon in Miss Havisham’s room, where she and everything else remained exactly as I had left them. Estella left me standing near the door, and I waited there until Miss Havisham looked at me from her dressing table.

“So!” she said, without showing any surprise: “the days have passed, have they?”

“Yes, ma’am. Today is—”

“There, there, there!” she said, with an impatient wave of her fingers. “I do not want to know. Are you ready to play?”

I had to answer, feeling somewhat confused, “I don’t think I am, ma’am.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_006",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Then, he and my sister would pair off in such ridiculous speculations about Miss Havisham, and about what she would do with me and for me, that I used to want—quite painfully—to burst into spiteful tears, attack Pumblechook, and pummel him all over. In these dialogues, my sister spoke to me as if she were morally wrenching one of my teeth out at every reference; while Pumblechook himself, having appointed himself my patron, would sit supervising me with a critical eye, like the architect of my fortunes who thought himself engaged on a very unprofitable job.

In these discussions, Joe took no part. But he was often talked at while they were in progress, because Mrs. Joe noticed that he was not in favor of my being taken from the forge. I was fully old enough now to be apprenticed to Joe; and when Joe sat with the poker on his knees thoughtfully raking out the ashes between the lower bars, my sister would so clearly interpret that innocent action as opposition on his part that she would dive at him, take the poker out of his hands, shake him, and put it away. There was a most irritating end to every one of these debates. All in a moment, with nothing to lead up to it, my sister would stop herself in a yawn, and catching sight of me as if by accident, would swoop upon me with, “Come! That’s enough of you! You get along to bed; you’ve given enough trouble for one night, I hope!” As if I had begged them as a favor to bother my life out.

We went on in this way for a long time, and it seemed likely that we would continue to go on in this way for a long time, when one day Miss Havisham stopped short as she and I were walking, she leaning on my shoulder, and said with some displeasure, “You are growing tall, Pip!”

I thought it best to hint, through the medium of a thoughtful look, that this might be caused by circumstances over which I had no control.

She said no more at the time; but she presently stopped and looked at me again; and presently again; and after that, looked frowning and moody. On the next day of my attendance, when our usual exercise was over, and I had landed her at her dressing-table, she stopped me with a movement of her impatient fingers: “Tell me the name again of that blacksmith of yours.”

“Joe Gargery, ma’am.”

“Meaning the master you were to be apprenticed to?”

“Yes, Miss Havisham.”

“You had better be apprenticed at once. Would Gargery come here with you, and bring your indentures, do you think?”

I indicated that I had no doubt he would take it as an honor to be asked.

“Then let him come.”

“At any particular time, Miss Havisham?”

“There, there! I know nothing about times. Let him come soon, and come along with you.”

When I got home at night, and delivered this message for Joe, my sister “went on the rampage” to a more alarming degree than at any previous time. She asked me and Joe whether we supposed she was a doormat under our feet, and how we dared to treat her so, and what company we graciously thought she was fit for?"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_007",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He resided at a sluice-keeper’s dwelling out upon the marshes, and on weekdays he would shuffle from his solitary home, hands tucked into his pockets, with his midday meal knotted into a parcel about his neck, swinging against his back. On Sundays, he typically spent the entire day reclining upon the sluice-gates or leaning against haystacks and barns. He moved with a perpetual, slouching gait, his gaze fixed downward; whenever he was spoken to or otherwise compelled to lift his eyes, he would look up with a mixture of resentment and confusion, as if his only recurring thought was that it was a strange and unfair circumstance that he should never be thinking at all.

This sullen laborer held no affection for me. During my early, timid years, he led me to believe that the Devil resided in a dark recess of the forge and that he was well-acquainted with the fiend; he further claimed that the fire required a living boy as fuel every seven years, and that I ought to view myself as that very material. Once I became Joe’s apprentice, Orlick likely grew more convinced that I would eventually replace him; regardless, his dislike for me only deepened. He never explicitly stated or performed any act of overt hostility; I simply observed that he consistently directed his sparks toward me, and whenever I sang Old Clem, he would intentionally sing out of rhythm.

Dolge Orlick was present and working the following day when I mentioned my half-holiday to Joe. He remained silent at first, as he and Joe were occupied with a piece of heated iron while I operated the bellows; however, after a short time, he leaned upon his hammer and remarked, “Now, master! Surely you aren’t going to show favoritism to just one of us. If young Pip is granted a half-holiday, grant the same to old Orlick.” I estimate he was roughly twenty-five, yet he frequently referred to himself as an elderly man.

“And what would you do with a half-holiday, should you receive one?” Joe inquired.

“What would I do with it! What will he do with it? I shall make as much use of it as he does,” Orlick replied.

“As for Pip, he is heading into town,” Joe explained.

“Well then, as for old Orlick, he is heading into town as well,” the man retorted. “Two people can go to town. It isn’t only one person who is permitted to go.”

“Do not lose your temper,” Joe cautioned.

“I shall if I choose,” Orlick grumbled. “Some people and their trips to town! Now, master! Come. No partiality in this workshop. Act like a man!”

Because the master refused to discuss the matter until the journeyman’s mood improved, Orlick lunged toward the furnace, extracted a glowing bar, and lunged at me as if to pierce my body with it. He swung it around my head, placed it upon the anvil, and hammered it—I imagined it was me, and the sparks were my spraying blood—and finally, once he had hammered himself into a sweat and the iron had cooled, he leaned on his hammer once more and said, “Now, master!”

“Are you feeling better now?” Joe asked.

“Yes! I am fine,” the surly Orlick answered.

“Then, since you generally apply yourself to your labor as well as any man,” Joe stated, “let it be a half-holiday for everyone.”

My sister, who had been standing quietly in the yard within earshot—she was a relentless spy and eavesdropper—immediately peered in through one of the windows."
}

When I became Joe’s apprentice, Orlick likely grew suspicious that I would replace him; regardless, he liked me even less. It wasn’t that he ever said or did anything openly hostile; I only noticed that he always directed his sparks toward me, and whenever I sang 'Old Clem,' he would intentionally sing out of time.

Dolge Orlick was working and present the next day when I reminded Joe about my half-holiday. He said nothing at that moment, as he and Joe were busy with a piece of hot iron and I was working the bellows, but eventually he spoke, leaning on his hammer:

“Now, boss! Surely you aren’t going to favor only one of us. If young Pip gets a half-holiday, do the same for old Orlick.” I suppose he was about twenty-five, but he usually referred to himself as an old man.

“As for Pip, he’s going into town,” Joe said.

“Well then, as for old Orlick, he’s going into town,” the man retorted. “Two can go into town. It isn’t only one person who can go into town.”

“Don’t lose your temper,” Joe said.

“I’ll lose it if I want to,” Orlick growled. “Some people and their trips to town! Now, boss! Come on. No favoritism in this shop. Be a man!”

When Joe refused to discuss the matter until the journeyman was in a better mood, Orlick lunged at the furnace, pulled out a red-hot bar, and swung it at me as if he were going to run it through my body. He whisked it around my head, laid it on the anvil, and hammered it—as if it were me, I thought, and the sparks were my spurting blood. Finally, once he had hammered himself hot and the iron cold, he leaned on his hammer again.

“Now, boss!”

“Are you all right now?” Joe demanded.

“Yeah, I’m all right,” gruff old Orlick said.

“Then, since you generally stick to your work as well as most men,” Joe said, “let it be a half-holiday for everyone.”

My sister had been standing silently in the yard, within earshot—she was a shameless spy and eavesdropper—and she instantly looked in through one of the windows."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Biddy,” I stated, after swearing her to silence, “my ambition is to become a gentleman.”

“Oh, I wouldn’t, if I were in your place,” she replied. “I don’t believe it would turn out well.”

“Biddy,” I said, with a touch of sternness, “I have specific motives for desiring to be a gentleman.”

“You are the best judge, Pip; but don’t you feel that you are more content as you are?”

“Biddy,” I cried, with impatience, “I am not content at all as I am. I loathe my trade and my existence. I have never grown accustomed to either since my apprenticeship began. Don’t be ridiculous.”

“Was I being ridiculous?” Biddy asked, calmly lifting her brows; “I regret that; it was not my intention. I only wish for your success and your happiness.”

“Well, then, realize once and for all that I never will or can be happy—or anything but wretched—there, Biddy!—unless I can live a life entirely unlike the one I lead at present.”

“That is a shame!” Biddy remarked, moving her head with a mournful expression.

Now, I had also frequently considered it a shame, so that, in the strange sort of internal conflict I was constantly waging, I felt nearly ready to weep from frustration and misery when Biddy voiced her opinion, which mirrored my own. I informed her she was correct, and I acknowledged it was deeply regrettable, yet it remained unavoidable.

“If I could have been content,” I told Biddy, tearing at the short grass nearby, much as I had once plucked my emotions from my hair and kicked them toward the brewery wall,—“if I could have been content and felt even half the affection for the forge that I felt when I was a child, I know it would have been far better for me. You and I and Joe would have lacked for nothing then, and Joe and I might have become partners once my time was served, and I might even have matured to court you, and we might have rested on this very bank on a pleasant Sunday, as entirely different people. I would have been suitable for you; wouldn’t I, Biddy?”

Biddy sighed as she watched the vessels passing by, and replied, “Yes; I am not overly fastidious.” It hardly felt like a compliment, yet I understood her intentions were kind.

“Instead of that,” I said, pulling up more grass and gnawing on a blade or two, “look at how I am living. Discontented, and uneasy, and—what would it matter to me, being rough and ordinary, if no one had ever told me so!”

Biddy shifted her gaze suddenly toward me, and studied me with far more focus than she had given the passing ships.

“That was neither a very honest nor a very courteous remark,” she observed, turning her eyes back to the ships. “Who told you that?”

I was rattled, for I had spoken impulsively without fully considering my path. It could not be avoided now, however, and I replied, “The lovely young lady at Miss Havisham’s, and she is more beautiful than anyone has ever been, and I admire her intensely, and I wish to be a gentleman for her sake.”"
}

{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Biddy,” I stated, after swearing her to silence, “my ambition is to become a gentleman.”

“Oh, I wouldn’t, if I were in your place,” she replied. “I don’t believe it would turn out well.”

“Biddy,” I said, with a touch of sternness, “I have specific motives for desiring to be a gentleman.”

“You are the best judge, Pip; but don’t you feel that you are more content as you are?”

“Biddy,” I cried, with impatience, “I am not content at all as I am. I loathe my trade and my existence. I have never grown accustomed to either since my apprenticeship began. Don’t be ridiculous.”

“Was I being ridiculous?” Biddy asked, calmly lifting her brows; “I regret that; it was not my intention. I only wish for your success and your happiness.”

“Well, then, realize once and for all that I never will or can be happy—or anything but wretched—there, Biddy!—unless I can live a life entirely unlike the one I lead at present.”

“That is a shame!” Biddy remarked, moving her head with a mournful expression.

Now, I had also frequently considered it a shame, so that, in the strange sort of internal conflict I was constantly waging, I felt nearly ready to weep from frustration and misery when Biddy voiced her opinion, which mirrored my own. I informed her she was correct, and I acknowledged it was deeply regrettable, yet it remained unavoidable.

“If I could have been content,” I told Biddy, tearing at the short grass nearby, much as I had once plucked my emotions from my hair and kicked them toward the brewery wall,—“if I could have been content and felt even half the affection for the forge that I felt when I was a child, I know it would have been far better for me. You and I and Joe would have lacked for nothing then, and Joe and I might have become partners once my time was served, and I might even have matured to court you, and we might have rested on this very bank on a pleasant Sunday, as entirely different people. I would have been suitable for you; wouldn’t I, Biddy?”

Biddy sighed as she watched the vessels passing by, and replied, “Yes; I am not overly fastidious.” It hardly felt like a compliment, yet I understood her intentions were kind.

“Instead of that,” I said, pulling up more grass and gnawing on a blade or two, “look at how I am living. Discontented, and uneasy, and—what would it matter to me, being rough and ordinary, if no one had ever told me so!”

Biddy shifted her gaze suddenly toward me, and studied me with far more focus than she had given the passing ships.

“That was neither a very honest nor a very courteous remark,” she observed, turning her eyes back to the ships. “Who told you that?”

I was rattled, for I had spoken impulsively without fully considering my path. It could not be avoided now, however, and I replied, “The lovely young lady at Miss Havisham’s, and she is more beautiful than anyone has ever been, and I admire her intensely, and I wish to be a gentleman for her sake.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_009",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Excellent. It would be best to test him at his own residence. The path will be cleared for you, and you may meet his son first, who resides in London. When do you intend to arrive in London?”

I replied (casting a look at Joe, who remained standing there, perfectly still), that I imagined I could depart immediately.

“To begin with,” Mr. Jaggers stated, “you ought to acquire fresh attire for your arrival, and it should not be laboring garments. Let us say this day week. You will require funds. Shall I provide you with twenty guineas?”

He drew out a lengthy purse with absolute composure, tallied the coins upon the table, and slid them toward me. This was the initial moment he had removed his leg from the chair. He sat straddling the seat once he had moved the money, swinging his purse while observing Joe.

“Well, Joseph Gargery? You appear stunned?”

“I am!” Joe declared, in a very firm tone.

“It was agreed that you desired nothing for your own benefit, keep in mind?”

“It were agreed,” Joe replied. “And it are agreed. And it ever will be the same.”

“But what,” Mr. Jaggers inquired, swinging his purse,—“what if my instructions included offering you a gift, by way of compensation?”

“Compensation for what?” Joe asked.

“For the forfeiture of his labor.”

Joe placed his hand upon my shoulder with a woman’s gentleness. I have frequently thought of him since as resembling a steam-hammer capable of crushing a man or tapping an eggshell, in his blend of power and tenderness. “Pip is most welcome,” Joe said, “to depart freely with his services, toward honor and fortune, in ways no words can express. But if you imagine that money can compensate me for the loss of the little child—who arrived at the forge—and was always the best of friends!—”

Oh dear, kind Joe, whom I was so prepared to abandon and so ungrateful toward, I behold you once more, with your powerful blacksmith’s arm covering your eyes, your broad chest rising and falling, and your voice fading away. Oh dear, kind, loyal, tender Joe, I sense the affectionate tremor of your hand upon my arm, as solemnly today as if it were the rustle of an angel’s wing!

Yet I urged Joe to be heartened at the time. I was consumed by the complexities of my future prospects and could not revisit the paths we had walked together. I implored Joe to find comfort, for (as he noted) we had always been the best of friends, and (as I noted) we always would be. Joe wiped his eyes with his free wrist, as if he intended to gouge himself, but uttered nothing further.

Mr. Jaggers had observed this, like a man who identified Joe as the village simpleton and me as his guardian. When it concluded, he remarked, balancing the purse he had stopped swinging in his hand:—

“Now, Joseph Gargery, I caution you that this is your final opportunity. No half-measures with me. If you intend to accept a gift that I am authorized to offer, speak plainly, and it shall be yours. If, conversely, you intend to say—” Here, to his profound astonishment, he was interrupted by Joe suddenly circling him with every indication of a fierce pugilistic intent."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_009",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Good. You should try meeting him at his own home. The way will be cleared for you, and you can meet his son first, who is currently in London. When will you be able to come to London?”

I replied (glancing at Joe, who stood watching, motionless), that I supposed I could come immediately.

“First,” Mr. Jaggers said, “you should get some new clothes for the trip, and they shouldn't be work clothes. Let’s say a week from today. You will need some money. Shall I leave you twenty guineas?”

He pulled out a long purse with total composure, counted the coins onto the table, and slid them toward me. This was the first time he had moved his leg from the chair. He sat astride the chair after pushing the money over, swinging his purse and eyeing Joe.

“Well, Joseph Gargery? You look stunned.”

“I am!” Joe said, very firmly.

“It was understood that you wanted nothing for yourself, remember?”

“It was understood,” Joe said. “And it is understood. And it always will be.”

“But what,” Mr. Jaggers asked, swinging his purse, “what if my instructions were to give you a gift as compensation?”

“Compensation for what?” Joe demanded.

“For the loss of his services.”

Joe placed his hand on my shoulder with a woman’s gentleness. I have often thought of him since as being like a steam hammer that can crush a man or gently tap an eggshell, in the way he combines strength with tenderness. “Pip is more than welcome,” Joe said, “to go off and pursue his honor and fortune, more than words can say. But if you think money can compensate me for the loss of the little child who came to the forge—and we were always the best of friends!—”

Oh, dear, good Joe, whom I was so ready to leave and so ungrateful toward, I see you again, with your muscular blacksmith’s arm covering your eyes, your broad chest heaving, and your voice fading away. Oh, dear, good, faithful, tender Joe, I feel the loving tremble of your hand on my arm as solemnly today as if it were the rustle of an angel’s wing!

But I encouraged Joe at the time. I was lost in the maze of my future prospects and could not look back at the paths we had walked together. I begged Joe to be comforted, for (as he said) we had always been the best of friends, and (as I said) we always would be. Joe wiped his eyes with his free wrist, as if he were trying to gouge them out, but said nothing more.

Mr. Jaggers had watched this as if he recognized Joe as the village idiot and me as his keeper. When it was over, he said, weighing the purse he had stopped swinging in his hand:

“Now, Joseph Gargery, I warn you that this is your last chance. No half-measures with me. If you intend to accept a gift that I am authorized to give you, speak up, and you shall have it. If, on the contrary, you mean to say—” Here, to his great surprise, he was interrupted by Joe suddenly circling him with every sign of a fierce, pugilistic intent."
}

He calmly pulled out a long purse, counted the coins onto the table, and pushed them toward me. This was the first time he had moved his leg from the chair. He sat sideways on the chair after pushing the money over, swinging his purse and watching Joe.

“Well, Joseph Gargery? You look stunned.”

“I am!” Joe said firmly.

“Remember, it was understood that you wanted nothing for yourself?”

“It was understood,” Joe replied. “And it is understood. And it always will be.”

“But what,” Mr. Jaggers asked, swinging his purse, “if my instructions were to give you a gift as compensation?”

“Compensation for what?” Joe demanded.

“For the loss of his services.”

Joe placed his hand on my shoulder with a woman’s gentleness. I have often thought of him since as being like a steam-hammer that can crush a man or gently tap an eggshell, such is his mix of strength and kindness. “Pip is more than welcome,” Joe said, “to go off to honor and fortune, more than I can say. But if you think money can compensate me for the loss of the little child who came to the forge—and we were always the best of friends!—”

Oh, dear, good Joe, whom I was so ready to leave and so ungrateful toward, I see you again, with your muscular blacksmith’s arm covering your eyes, your broad chest heaving, and your voice fading away. Oh, dear, good, faithful, tender Joe, I feel the loving tremble of your hand on my arm as clearly today as if it were the rustle of an angel’s wing!

However, I encouraged Joe at the time. I was lost in the excitement of my future wealth and could not look back at the path we had walked together. I begged Joe to be comforted, for as he said, we had always been the best of friends, and as I said, we always would be. Joe wiped his eyes with his free wrist, as if he were trying to gouge them out, but he said nothing more.

Mr. Jaggers had watched this as if he saw Joe as the village fool and me as his keeper. When it ended, he weighed the purse in his hand and said:

“Now, Joseph Gargery, I warn you that this is your last chance. No half-measures with me. If you intend to take the gift I am authorized to give you, speak up, and you shall have it. If, on the other hand, you mean to say—” Here, to his great surprise, he was interrupted by Joe, who suddenly moved toward him with every sign of a violent, fighting intent."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_010",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“And yet I cannot,” Mr. Pumblechook remarked, rising once more, “behold one—and likewise toast one—without again expressing—May I—may I—?”

I granted him permission, and he shook my hand again, drained his glass, and inverted it. I followed suit; had I stood on my head before consuming the wine, it could not have rushed to my brain more swiftly.

Mr. Pumblechook served me the liver wing and the finest portion of tongue (avoiding those obscure pork remnants now), while neglecting his own plate almost entirely. “Oh! poultry, poultry! You little imagined,” Mr. Pumblechook declared, addressing the bird on the platter, “when you were a fledgling, what awaited you. You little imagined you would serve as sustenance under this modest roof for one who—call it a frailty, if you choose,” Mr. Pumblechook said, rising again, “but may I? may I—?”

It became redundant to formally grant him permission, so he proceeded immediately. How he managed this repeatedly without stabbing himself with my knife, I cannot fathom.

“And your sister,” he continued, after eating steadily for a moment, “who had the distinction of raising you by hand! It is a melancholy image to consider that she is no longer capable of fully grasping that distinction. May—”

Seeing he was about to approach me again, I interrupted him.

“We shall drink to her health,” I stated.

“Ah!” Mr. Pumblechook exclaimed, reclining in his chair, utterly limp with admiration, “that is how you recognize them, sir!” (I have no idea who 'Sir' was, but it certainly was not me, and no one else was present); “that is how you recognize the noble-hearted, sir! Always forgiving and always gracious. It might,” the obsequious Pumblechook said, setting down his full glass hastily and rising again, “to an ordinary person, seem repetitive—but may I—?”

Once finished, he returned to his seat and toasted my sister. “Let us never ignore her temperamental flaws,” Mr. Pumblechook noted, “but we must hope her intentions were good.”

Around this time, I noticed his face growing ruddy; as for me, I felt entirely like a face, saturated with wine and stinging.

I informed Mr. Pumblechook that I wanted my new attire delivered to his residence, and he was thrilled that I had singled him out. I explained my desire to evade notice in the village, and he praised the idea effusively. He suggested that no one but himself was deserving of my trust, and—in short, might he? Then he asked me affectionately if I recalled our childhood arithmetic lessons, and how we had traveled together to have me apprenticed, and, essentially, how he had always been my preferred companion and chosen friend? Had I consumed ten times the amount of wine, I would have known he had never occupied that position in my life, and I would have inwardly rejected the notion. Nevertheless, I recall feeling certain that I had misjudged him, and that he was a sensible, pragmatic, kind-hearted, excellent man."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_010",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“And yet I cannot,” said Mr. Pumblechook, standing up once more, “see before me one—and likewise drink to one—without again expressing—May I—may I—?”

I told him he could, and he shook my hand again, drained his glass, and turned it upside down. I did the same; and if I had turned myself upside down before drinking, the wine could not have gone more directly to my head.

Mr. Pumblechook served me the liver wing and the best slice of tongue (none of those obscure cuts of pork now), while taking almost no care of himself. “Ah! Poultry, poultry! You little thought,” Mr. Pumblechook said, addressing the bird on the platter, “when you were a young fledgling, what was in store for you. You little thought you were to be refreshment beneath this humble roof for one who—Call it a weakness, if you like,” said Mr. Pumblechook, rising again, “but may I? May I—?”

It became unnecessary to repeat the permission, so he simply did it. How he managed to do it so often without stabbing himself with my knife, I have no idea.

“And your sister,” he continued, after eating steadily for a moment, “who had the honor of raising you by hand! It is a sad picture to reflect that she is no longer capable of fully understanding the honor. May—”

I saw he was about to approach me again, so I stopped him.

“We will drink her health,” I said.

“Ah!” cried Mr. Pumblechook, leaning back in his chair, completely limp with admiration, “that is how you know them, sir!” (I did not know who ‘sir’ was, but it certainly was not me, and there was no third person present); “that is how you know the noble-minded, sir! Ever forgiving and ever affable. It might,” said the servile Pumblechook, setting down his untasted glass in a hurry and standing up again, “to a common person, appear repetitive—but may I—?”

When he had finished, he sat back down and drank to my sister. “Let us never be blind,” said Mr. Pumblechook, “to her temperamental faults, but one hopes she meant well.”

Around this time, I began to notice his face was getting flushed; as for myself, I felt entirely lightheaded, soaked in wine and stinging.

I mentioned to Mr. Pumblechook that I wanted my new clothes sent to his house, and he was ecstatic that I had chosen him. I explained my reason for wanting to avoid being seen in the village, and he praised the idea to the skies. He hinted that no one but himself was worthy of my confidence, and—in short, might he? Then he asked me tenderly if I remembered our childhood math lessons, and how we had gone together to have me bound as an apprentice, and, in effect, how he had always been my favorite companion and chosen friend? If I had consumed ten times as much wine as I had, I would still have known that he had never stood in that relationship to me, and in my heart of hearts, I would have rejected the notion. Yet, despite that, I remember feeling convinced that I had been much mistaken about him, and that he was a sensible, practical, good-hearted, and excellent fellow."
}

“And your sister,” he continued, after eating steadily for a moment, “who had the honor of raising you by hand! It is a sad picture to reflect that she is no longer capable of fully understanding that honor. May—”

“We will drink her health,” I said.
