model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_011",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "I then discovered that Wemmick was the clerk in the adjacent office. Another clerk was summoned from upstairs to cover his duties while he was away, and I joined him on the street after shaking hands with my guardian. We encountered a new group of people waiting outside, but Wemmick cleared a path through them by stating calmly yet firmly, “I’m telling you, it’s pointless; he won’t speak to any of you.” We soon moved past them and walked along side by side.

Chapter XXI.

As we walked, I looked at Mr. Wemmick to see what he looked like in the daylight. I found him to be a dry man, somewhat short, with a square, wooden face that looked as if it had been crudely carved with a dull chisel. There were marks on his face that might have been dimples if his features were softer and the tool had been sharper, but as it was, they were merely dents. The chisel had made three or four attempts at decoration around his nose but had given up without trying to smooth them out. I assumed he was a bachelor based on the frayed state of his shirt cuffs, and he seemed to have experienced many losses, as he wore at least four mourning rings, along with a brooch depicting a lady and a weeping willow by a tomb with an urn. I also noticed several rings and seals hanging from his watch chain, as if he were burdened with mementos of deceased friends. He had bright, small, keen, black eyes and thin, wide, mottled lips. He had possessed them, I estimated, for forty to fifty years.

“So, you’ve never been to London before?” Mr. Wemmick asked me.

“No,” I replied.

“I was new here once, too,” Mr. Wemmick said. “Strange to think about now!”

“Are you very familiar with it now?”

“Well, yes,” Mr. Wemmick said. “I know how it works.”

“Is it a very wicked place?” I asked, more to make conversation than to gain information.

“You can get cheated, robbed, and murdered in London. But there are plenty of people anywhere who will do that to you.”

“If there is bad blood between you and them,” I said, trying to soften the statement.

“Oh, I don’t know about bad blood,” Mr. Wemmick replied. “There isn’t much bad blood involved. They’ll do it if there’s anything to be gained.”

“That makes it worse.”

“You think so?” Mr. Wemmick returned. “I’d say it’s about the same.”

He wore his hat pushed back on his head and looked straight ahead, walking in a self-contained manner as if nothing in the streets required his attention. His mouth was shaped like a post-office slot, giving him the mechanical appearance of smiling. We had reached the top of Holborn Hill before I realized it was merely a mechanical expression and that he wasn’t smiling at all.

“Do you know where Mr. Matthew Pocket lives?” I asked Mr. Wemmick.

“Yes,” he said, nodding in that direction. “In Hammersmith, west of London.”

“Is that far?”"
}

I also noticed several rings and seals hanging from his watch chain, as if he were burdened with memories of deceased friends. He had small, sharp, black, glittering eyes and thin, wide, mottled lips. I believed he had been between forty and fifty years old for quite some time.

“So you have never been to London before?” Mr. Wemmick asked me.

“No,” I replied.

“I was new here once,” Mr. Wemmick said. “Strange to think of that now!”

“Are you well acquainted with it now?”

“Well, yes,” said Mr. Wemmick. “I know how things work here.”

“Is it a very wicked place?” I asked, mostly just to keep the conversation going.

“You can get cheated, robbed, and murdered in London. But there are plenty of people anywhere who will do that to you.”

“If there is bad blood between you and them,” I said, trying to soften his statement.

“Oh, I don’t know about bad blood,” Mr. Wemmick replied. “There isn’t much bad blood involved. They will do it if there is anything to be gained from it.”

“That makes it worse.”

“You think so?” Mr. Wemmick answered. “I would say it is about the same.”

He wore his hat on the back of his head and looked straight ahead, walking in a self-contained way as if nothing in the streets caught his attention. His mouth was shaped like a post-office slot, giving him the mechanical appearance of smiling. We had reached the top of Holborn Hill before I realized it was just a mechanical look and that he was not actually smiling at all.

“Do you know where Mr. Matthew Pocket lives?” I asked Mr. Wemmick.

“Yes,” he said, nodding in that direction. “At Hammersmith, west of London.”

“Is that far?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_012",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“And also to Ceylon, particularly for elephant ivory.”

“You must require a great number of vessels,” I remarked.

“An entire armada,” he answered.

Feeling quite overwhelmed by the scale of these dealings, I inquired where the ships he currently insured were primarily sailing.

“I haven’t started insuring quite yet,” he responded. “I am currently surveying the landscape.”

In some way, that activity felt more appropriate for Barnard’s Inn. I replied (with a tone of certainty), “Ah!”

“Indeed. I am employed in a counting-house, and I am surveying the landscape.”

“Does a counting-house provide a good income?” I questioned.

“To—are you asking if it is profitable for the young man working there?” he countered.

“Yes; for you.”

“Well, n-no; not for me.” He stated this with the demeanor of someone meticulously calculating and balancing figures. “Not directly lucrative. That is to say, it provides me no salary, and I must—support myself.”

This certainly did not appear to be a profitable arrangement, and I gestured with my head to suggest that it would be challenging to accumulate significant savings from such an income.

“But the point is,” Herbert Pocket explained, “that you survey the landscape. That is the essential thing. You are situated in a counting-house, you see, and you survey the landscape.”

It seemed a strange notion to me that one could not be elsewhere and still survey the landscape, but I quietly yielded to his expertise.

“Then the moment arrives,” Herbert continued, “when you identify your opportunity. You seize it, you pounce upon it, you generate your capital, and then you have arrived! Once you have established your capital, you simply need to put it to work.”

This was very similar to his approach during our fight in the garden; very similar indeed. His way of enduring his poverty also matched his way of enduring that loss. It appeared to me that he accepted all of life’s setbacks with the same attitude he had shown toward my blows back then. It was clear he possessed only the most basic necessities, as everything I commented upon turned out to have been provided on my behalf from the coffee-house or another source.

Yet, having already secured his fortune in his imagination, he remained so modest about it that I felt genuinely appreciative of his lack of arrogance. It was a charming quality added to his naturally agreeable personality, and we got along wonderfully. That evening we strolled through the streets and attended the theater at a reduced rate; the following day we visited Westminster Abbey for services, and later walked through the Parks. I found myself wondering who provided the horseshoes for all the animals there, and wished that Joe were the one doing it.

By a reasonable estimate, it had been many months that Sunday since I had departed from Joe and Biddy. The distance separating me from them seemed to have expanded, and our marshes felt incredibly far away. That I could have been at our local church in my old Sunday attire just the previous week seemed a collection of impossibilities—geographical, social, solar, and lunar. Nevertheless, in the London streets, so packed with people and brightly illuminated in the evening twilight, there were somber reminders of guilt for having pushed the humble kitchen at home so far into the past; and in the middle of the night, the footsteps of some incompetent porter wandering around Barnard’s Inn, pretending to keep watch, echoed hollowly within my heart."
}

{

  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“And to Ceylon, especially for elephant tusks.”

“You’ll need quite a few ships,” I said.

“An entire fleet,” he replied.

Completely overwhelmed by the scale of these deals, I asked him where the ships he currently insured were trading.

“I haven’t started insuring yet,” he answered. “I’m just looking around.”

Somehow, that pursuit seemed more appropriate for Barnard’s Inn. I said, with a tone of conviction, “Ah!”

“Yes. I’m working in an office, and I’m looking around.”

“Is an office job profitable?” I asked.

“Do you mean for the young guy working there?” he asked in return.

“Yes; for you.”

“Well, no; not for me.” He said this with the air of someone carefully calculating a balance sheet. “Not directly profitable. That is, it doesn’t pay me anything, and I have to support myself.”

This certainly didn’t look profitable, and I shook my head to imply that it would be difficult to build up any significant savings from such an income.

“But the thing is,” Herbert Pocket said, “that you look around. That’s the main thing. You’re in an office, you see, and you look around.”

It struck me as a strange suggestion that you couldn’t be outside of an office and still look around, but I silently deferred to his experience.

“Then the time comes,” Herbert said, “when you see your opportunity. You go in, you seize it, you make your fortune, and then you’re set! Once you’ve made your capital, you have nothing to do but invest it.”

This was very similar to how he handled that fight in the garden; very similar. His way of dealing with his poverty also matched how he handled that defeat. It seemed to me that he took all of life’s setbacks now with the same attitude he had shown toward my punches back then. It was clear he had nothing around him but the barest necessities, as everything I commented on turned out to have been brought in for my benefit from a coffee shop or elsewhere.

Yet, having already made his fortune in his own mind, he was so modest about it that I felt grateful he wasn’t arrogant. It was a pleasant addition to his naturally easygoing personality, and we got along famously. In the evening, we went for a walk through the streets and caught a half-price show at the theater; the next day we attended church at Westminster Abbey, and in the afternoon we walked in the parks. I wondered who shod all the horses there and wished Joe did.

By a moderate estimate, it had been many months since that Sunday I left Joe and Biddy. The distance between them and me felt vast, and our marshes seemed miles away. That I could have been at our old church in my old church clothes just last Sunday felt like a combination of geographical, social, and cosmic impossibilities. Yet, in the London streets, so crowded with people and brightly lit in the evening dusk, there were depressing hints of guilt for having pushed the poor old kitchen at home so far away; and in the dead of night, the footsteps of some incompetent night watchman wandering around Barnard’s Inn, pretending to keep guard, echoed hollowly in my heart."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_013",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Then I asked if there were many clerks, to which he replied,

“We don’t really do clerks, because there is only one Jaggers, and people won’t accept him secondhand. There are only four of us. Would you like to see them? You are one of us, so to speak.”

I accepted the offer. After Mr. Wemmick had dropped all the biscuits into the post and paid me my money from a cash-box in a safe—the key to which he kept somewhere down his back, pulling it from his coat collar like an iron pigtail—we went upstairs. The house was dark and shabby, and the greasy shoulders that had left marks in Mr. Jaggers’s room seemed to have been shuffling up and down the staircase for years. On the front floor, a clerk who looked like a cross between a pub owner and a rat-catcher—a large, pale, puffed-up man—was busy with three or four shabby-looking people, whom he treated as unceremoniously as everyone seemed to be treated if they contributed to Mr. Jaggers’s coffers. “Gathering evidence,” Mr. Wemmick said as we left, “for the Old Bailey.” In the room above that, a small, flabby, terrier-like clerk with dangling hair (it seemed he had forgotten to get a haircut since he was a puppy) was similarly occupied with a man with weak eyes, whom Mr. Wemmick introduced to me as a smelter who kept his pot constantly boiling and could melt anything I pleased; he was sweating profusely, as if he had been practicing his craft on himself. In a back room, a high-shouldered man with a toothache wrapped in dirty flannel, dressed in old black clothes that looked as if they had been waxed, was hunched over his work, making clean copies of the other two men’s notes for Mr. Jaggers’s own use.

This was the entire staff. When we went back downstairs, Wemmick led me into my guardian’s room and said, “You have already seen this.”

“Tell me,” I said, as the two hideous casts with their twitching leers caught my eye again, “whose likenesses are those?”

“These?” Wemmick said, climbing onto a chair and blowing the dust off the horrible heads before bringing them down. “These are two famous ones. Renowned clients of ours who brought us a world of credit. This guy (why, you must have come down in the night and been peeping into the inkstand to get this blot on your eyebrow, you old rascal!) murdered his master, and considering he wasn’t trained in legal evidence, he didn’t plan it badly.”

“Is it a good likeness?” I asked, recoiling from the brute as Wemmick spat on his eyebrow and rubbed it with his sleeve.

“Like him? It is him, you know. The cast was made in Newgate immediately after he was taken down. You had a particular fondness for me, didn’t you, Old Artful?” Wemmick said. He then explained this affectionate remark by touching his brooch, which depicted a lady and a weeping willow at a tomb with an urn on it, saying, “Had it made for me, specifically!”

“Is the lady anyone in particular?” I asked.

“No,” Wemmick replied. “Just his little game. (You liked your bit of fun, didn’t you?)"
}

He treated them as rudely as everyone else who contributed to Mr. Jaggers’s wealth. “Gathering evidence,” Mr. Wemmick said as we left, “for the Old Bailey.” In the room above that, a small, flabby clerk with dangling hair (it seemed he had never been given a proper haircut) was working with a man who had weak eyes. Mr. Wemmick introduced him to me as a smelter who kept his pot boiling and could melt anything I liked; the man was sweating profusely, as if he had been practicing his trade on himself. In a back room, a man with hunched shoulders and a face wrapped in dirty flannel, dressed in old black clothes that looked as if they had been waxed, was busy making clean copies of the other two men’s notes for Mr. Jaggers’s use.

That was the entire staff. When we went back downstairs, Wemmick led me into my guardian’s room and said, “You have already seen this.”

“Tell me,” I said, as the two hateful casts with their twitching leers caught my eye again, “who are those?”

“These?” said Wemmick, standing on a chair and blowing the dust off the horrible heads before bringing them down. “These are two famous ones. They were our clients and brought us a great deal of credit. This fellow (why, you must have been peeping into the inkstand at night to get that blot on your eyebrow, you old rascal!) murdered his master, and considering he wasn’t trained in legal matters, he didn’t plan it badly.”

“Is it a good likeness?” I asked, pulling back from the brute as Wemmick spat on the cast’s eyebrow and rubbed it with his sleeve.

“Like him? It is him. The cast was made in Newgate prison right after he was taken down. You were quite fond of me, weren’t you, Old Artful?” Wemmick said. He then explained this affectionate comment by touching his brooch, which showed a lady and a weeping willow at a tomb with an urn on it, saying, “I had it made for me, specifically!”

“Is the lady someone in particular?” I asked.

“No,” Wemmick replied. “Just his little joke. (You liked your little joke, didn’t you?)"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_014",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": ""Who is the Spider?"

"The spider?" I repeated.

"That blotchy, awkward, sullen individual."

"That is Bentley Drummle," I answered; "the one with the refined features is Startop."

Ignoring the mention of the one with the refined features, he replied, "Bentley Drummle is his name, is it? I find that fellow’s appearance interesting."

He promptly started a conversation with Drummle, entirely unbothered by his blunt, reserved responses, and seemingly encouraged by them to extract more talk from him. As I watched the pair, the housekeeper stepped between us, carrying the initial course for our meal.

She appeared to be roughly forty, though I might have underestimated her age. She was somewhat tall, possessed a supple, agile frame, was remarkably pale, and had large, washed-out eyes along with a mass of flowing hair. I cannot determine if a heart condition caused her lips to remain parted as if she were breathless, or gave her countenance such a strange, startled, and agitated look; however, I recall having attended a performance of Macbeth a few nights prior, and her face reminded me of those disturbed by scorching vapors, much like the visages I had witnessed emerging from the Witches’ caldron.

She placed the dish down, tapped my guardian lightly on the arm to signal that dinner was served, and disappeared. We sat at the circular table, with my guardian keeping Drummle on one side and Startop on the other. The housekeeper had provided a magnificent fish dish, followed by a joint of equally excellent mutton, and then a bird of similar quality. Sauces, wines, and every necessary accompaniment were provided by our host from his dumb-waiter; once they had been passed around the table, he consistently returned them to their place. In the same manner, he provided fresh plates, knives, and forks for every course, dropping the used ones into two baskets positioned on the floor beside his chair. No servant other than the housekeeper was present. She placed every dish; and I consistently perceived in her features a face emerging from the caldron. Many years later, I created a haunting portrait of that woman by having a face, which shared no natural similarity to hers other than the streaming hair, pass behind a bowl of burning spirits in a darkened room.

Prompted to pay close attention to the housekeeper, both by her remarkable appearance and by Wemmick’s earlier remarks, I noticed that whenever she was present, she kept her gaze fixed on my guardian, and she would withdraw her hands from any dish she placed before him with hesitation, as if she feared he might summon her back and desired him to speak while she was near, should he have anything to communicate. I imagined I could perceive in his behavior an awareness of this, and an intention to keep her in a state of constant uncertainty.

The dinner proceeded cheerfully, and although my guardian seemed to follow the topics rather than initiate them, I understood that he was skillfully drawing the most vulnerable aspects of our characters out of us."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_015",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "One man was taller and heavier than his companion, and by the strange logic of the world—whether regarding convicts or free men—he had been assigned the smaller set of garments. His limbs resembled oversized pincushions, and his clothing made him look ridiculous; yet, I recognized his partially shut eye immediately. There stood the individual I had encountered on the bench at the Three Jolly Bargemen on a Saturday evening, the one who had leveled me with his unseen firearm.

It was evident that he did not recognize me at all. He glanced my way, assessed my watch-chain, spat casually, and remarked something to the other prisoner. They chuckled, turned with the metallic rattle of their connecting shackle, and shifted their attention elsewhere. The large numerals on their backs, resembling house numbers; their rough, mangy, and awkward appearance, like beasts; their shackled legs, wrapped apologetically in handkerchiefs; and the way the crowd observed them while maintaining a distance, rendered them—as Herbert had noted—a truly repulsive and debased sight.

However, that was not the extent of the trouble. It turned out that a family moving from London had reserved the entire rear of the coach, leaving no room for the two captives except on the front seat behind the driver. Consequently, a hot-tempered gentleman occupying the fourth spot on that bench erupted in fury, declaring it a violation of his agreement to be seated with such criminal types, calling it toxic, ruinous, disgraceful, and scandalous, among other things. By then, the coach was prepared, the driver was eager to depart, and we were all getting ready to board, while the prisoners approached with their guard—carrying that distinct scent of bread-poultice, baize, hemp, and hearthstone that follows those in custody.

“Do not be so upset, sir,” the guard urged the irate traveler; “I will sit beside you myself. I will place them on the edge of the bench. They will not bother you, sir. You need not even acknowledge their presence.”

“And do not blame me,” the convict I knew grumbled. “I have no desire to go. I am perfectly willing to remain here. As far as I am concerned, anyone is free to take my seat.”

“Or mine,” the other added harshly. “I would not have bothered any of you, had it been my choice.” They both laughed, started cracking nuts, and spat the shells about—an action I believe I would have enjoyed myself, had I been in their position and so scorned.

Eventually, it was decided that the angry man had no alternative but to accept his unwanted companions or stay behind. He took his seat, continuing to grumble, the guard sat beside him, the convicts pulled themselves up as best they could, and the man I had recognized sat directly behind me, his breath hitting the hair on my head."
}

His limbs looked like oversized pincushions, and his outfit made him look ridiculous; yet, I recognized his half-closed eye instantly. There stood the man I had seen on the bench at the Three Jolly Bargemen on a Saturday night, the one who had brought me down with his invisible gun!

It was clear he did not recognize me at all. He glanced my way, checked out my watch-chain, spat casually, and said something to the other convict. They laughed, turned around with a clink of their shackles, and looked elsewhere. The large numbers on their backs, like house addresses; their rough, mangy, awkward appearance, as if they were beasts; their shackled legs, apologetically wrapped in pocket-handkerchiefs; and the way everyone stared at them while keeping their distance—all of this made them, as Herbert had said, a truly unpleasant and degraded sight.

But that wasn't the worst of it. It turned out that a family moving from London had booked the entire back of the coach, leaving no room for the two prisoners except on the front seat behind the driver. A hot-tempered gentleman, who had taken the fourth seat there, flew into a rage, claiming it was a breach of contract to force him to sit with such villainous company. He called it poisonous, harmful, disgraceful, and shameful, among other things. By then, the coach was ready, the driver was impatient, and we were all getting on board. The prisoners arrived with their guard, bringing that strange scent of bread-poultice, felt, rope, and hearthstone that always follows convicts.

“Don’t take it so hard, sir,” the guard pleaded with the angry passenger. “I’ll sit next to you myself. I’ll put them on the outside. They won’t bother you, sir. You won’t even know they’re there.”

“And don’t blame me,” grumbled the convict I had recognized. “I don’t want to go. I’m perfectly happy to stay behind. As far as I’m concerned, anyone is welcome to my seat.”

“Or mine,” the other added gruffly. “I wouldn’t have bothered any of you if I’d had my way.” Then they both laughed, started cracking nuts, and spat the shells around—something I think I would have done myself if I were in their position and treated with such contempt.

Eventually, it was decided that the angry gentleman had no choice but to accept his company or stay behind. He took his seat, still complaining, the guard sat next to him, and the convicts hauled themselves up as best they could. The convict I had recognized sat directly behind me, his breath hitting the back of my head."
}

A hot-tempered gentleman, who had taken the fourth seat there, flew into a rage. He claimed it was a breach of contract to force him to sit with such villainous company, calling it poisonous, harmful, shameful, and much more. The coach was ready, the driver was impatient, and we were all preparing to board. The prisoners arrived with their guard, bringing that strange smell of bread-poultice, wool, rope, and stone that always follows convicts.

“Don’t take it so hard, sir,” the guard pleaded with the angry passenger. “I’ll sit next to you myself. I’ll put them on the outside of the row. They won’t bother you, sir. You won’t even know they’re there.”

“And don’t blame me,” the convict I recognized growled. “I don’t want to go. I’m perfectly happy to stay behind. As far as I’m concerned, anyone is welcome to my seat.”

“Or mine,” the other added gruffly. “I wouldn’t have bothered any of you if I’d had my way.” Then they both laughed and began cracking nuts, spitting the shells around—something I think I would have liked to do myself if I were in their position and treated with such contempt.

Eventually, it was decided that the angry gentleman had no choice but to accept his company or stay behind. He took his seat while continuing to complain, the guard sat next to him, and the convicts pulled themselves up as best they could. The convict I had recognized sat directly behind me, his breath hitting the back of my head."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_016",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "You arrived here with your devotion and your luggage in tow. Told me? You have been telling me constantly. When you recounted your history, you stated clearly that your worship of her began at your first meeting, when you were quite young.”

“Very well, then,” I replied, finding this perspective fresh and agreeable, “I have never ceased to worship her. She has returned, a truly stunning and refined woman. I saw her yesterday. If I loved her before, I love her twice as much now.”

“It is fortunate for you, Handel,” Herbert remarked, “that you have been chosen and destined for her. Without crossing into restricted topics, we can certainly agree that this is true. Do you have any sense of Estella’s feelings regarding this worship?”

I gave a somber shake of my head. “Oh! She is worlds apart from me,” I said.

“Be patient, my dear Handel: there is plenty of time. But is there more you wish to say?”

“I am embarrassed to admit it,” I answered, “yet it is no worse to speak it than to harbor the thought. You label me a lucky man. Naturally, I am. I was a blacksmith’s apprentice only yesterday; what am I to call myself today?”

“Call yourself a good fellow, if you need a label,” Herbert replied, grinning and patting my hand—“a good fellow, possessing a strange blend of impulsiveness and doubt, courage and shyness, action and fantasy.”

I paused briefly to reflect on whether my personality truly contained such a mixture. On balance, I did not really identify with his assessment, but decided it was not worth arguing.

“When I ask what I should call myself today, Herbert,” I continued, “I am hinting at my internal concerns. You claim I am lucky. I realize I have done nothing to elevate my station, and that only Fortune has lifted me; that is indeed great luck. And yet, when I contemplate Estella—”

(“And when do you not, really?” Herbert interjected, gazing at the fire; I found this gesture kind and understanding.)

“—Then, my dear Herbert, I cannot express how vulnerable and insecure I feel, and how susceptible I am to countless risks. While avoiding restricted topics, as you did, I can still say that my entire future rests upon the loyalty of one individual. At best, how vague and frustrating it is to have such an unclear understanding of what those prospects are!” By saying this, I unburdened myself of thoughts that had always been present, though certainly most intense since yesterday.

“Now, Handel,” Herbert answered, in his cheerful, optimistic manner, “it strikes me that in the gloom of romance, we are inspecting our gift-horse’s teeth with a magnifying lens. Furthermore, it seems we are so focused on this inspection that we ignore one of the animal’s finest qualities. Did you not mention that your guardian, Mr. Jaggers, informed you at the start that your prospects were not limited to expectations alone? And even if he had not—though that is a significant ‘if,’ I admit—could you possibly believe that of all the men in London, Mr. Jaggers would be the one to mislead you?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_016",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "You brought your devotion and your suitcase here together. Told me! Why, you have been telling me all day long. When you shared your own story, you stated clearly that you began adoring her the very first time you saw her, when you were still quite young.”

“Very well, then,” I said, finding this to be a new and not unwelcome perspective, “I have never stopped adoring her. And she has returned, a most beautiful and elegant creature. I saw her yesterday. And if I adored her before, I now adore her twice as much.”

“Lucky for you, then, Handel,” Herbert said, “that you are chosen for her and destined for her. Without crossing any forbidden lines, we can certainly agree between ourselves that this is a fact. Do you have any idea yet what Estella’s thoughts are on the subject of your adoration?”

I shook my head gloomily. “Oh! She is thousands of miles away from me,” I said.

“Patience, my dear Handel: there is time enough, plenty of time. But you have something more to say?”

“I am ashamed to say it,” I replied, “and yet it is no worse to say it than to think it. You call me a lucky guy. Of course, I am. I was a blacksmith’s apprentice just yesterday; what should I call myself today?”

“Call yourself a good guy, if you want a label,” Herbert replied, smiling and patting my hand—“a good guy, with impulsiveness and hesitation, boldness and shyness, action and dreaming, all strangely mixed together.”

I paused for a moment to consider whether this mixture really existed in my character. On the whole, I did not recognize the description, but I decided it was not worth arguing about.

“When I ask what I am to call myself today, Herbert,” I continued, “I am expressing what is on my mind. You say I am lucky. I know I have done nothing to improve my own life, and that fortune alone has elevated me; that is being very lucky. And yet, when I think of Estella—”

(“And when don’t you, really?” Herbert added, staring into the fire; which I thought was kind and sympathetic of him.)

“—Then, my dear Herbert, I cannot tell you how dependent and uncertain I feel, and how vulnerable I am to hundreds of risks. Avoiding forbidden topics, as you did just now, I can still say that all my expectations depend on the constancy of one person. And at best, how vague and unsatisfactory it is to only know so little about what those expectations actually are!” In saying this, I unburdened my mind of what had always been there, more or less, though certainly more so since yesterday.

“Now, Handel,” Herbert replied, in his cheerful, hopeful way, “it seems to me that in the despair of being in love, we are examining our gift horse’s mouth with a magnifying glass. Furthermore, it seems to me that by focusing so intently on that examination, we completely overlook one of the animal’s best features. Didn’t you tell me that your guardian, Mr. Jaggers, told you from the start that you were not just endowed with expectations? And even if he hadn’t told you so—though that is a very big ‘if,’ I admit—could you believe that of all the men in London, Mr. Jaggers would be the one to mislead you?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_017",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Avoid pointless efforts. Why bother? Now, who is next?”

In this manner, we strolled through Wemmick’s yard until he pivoted toward me and remarked, “Observe the individual I am about to greet.” I would have noted him regardless of the warning, as he had not yet shaken hands with anyone.

Immediately after he spoke, a stout, erect man—whom I can still visualize as I write—wearing a faded olive frock-coat, his ruddy face marked by a strange paleness and his eyes drifting aimlessly when he attempted to focus, approached the bars. He touched his hat, which possessed a greasy, slick texture reminiscent of chilled broth, in a salute that was part military, part mocking.

“Greetings, Colonel!” Wemmick called out. “How are you, Colonel?”

“I am well, Mr. Wemmick.”

“We did everything possible, but the proof was simply too overwhelming, Colonel.”

“Yes, it was too much, sir—but I do not mind.”

“Indeed,” Wemmick replied calmly, “you do not mind.” He then turned to me and added, “This man served the King. He was a regular soldier who purchased his release.”

I replied, “Is that so?” The man’s gaze met mine, then drifted above my head, scanned the area around me, and finally, he wiped his hand across his mouth and chuckled.

“I expect to be finished here by Monday, sir,” he told Wemmick.

“Possibly,” my companion answered, “but one never knows.”

“I am pleased to have the opportunity to say farewell, Mr. Wemmick,” the man stated, reaching his hand through the iron bars.

“Thank you,” Wemmick said, grasping his hand. “The same to you, Colonel.”

“If the items I possessed when arrested had been genuine, Mr. Wemmick,” the man said, reluctant to release his grip, “I would have requested that you wear another ring as a token of my appreciation for your help.”

“I will take the intention as the act,” Wemmick replied. “By the way, I heard you were quite the pigeon enthusiast.” The man glanced toward the sky. “I am told you kept an exceptional breed of tumblers. Could you arrange for a friend to bring me a pair, provided you have no further need for them?”

“It shall be done, sir.”

“Very well,” Wemmick said, “they will be well looked after. Good afternoon, Colonel. Farewell!” They shook hands once more, and as we moved away, Wemmick remarked to me, “He is a counterfeiter, and a highly skilled one. The judge’s report is submitted today, and he will certainly be hanged on Monday. Still, you see, pigeons remain portable property regardless.” With that, he glanced back, nodded at the lifeless plant, and scanned the yard as he exited, as if contemplating which other pot would best fill the space.

Upon leaving the prison via the lodge, I realized that my guardian’s significant status was recognized by the jailers just as much as by their captives. “Well, Mr. Wemmick,” the turnkey said, keeping us between the two heavy, spiked gates and carefully locking one before opening the next, “what is Mr. Jaggers planning to do regarding that riverside murder?”"
}

{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_017",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Don’t bother with pointless gestures. Why would you? Now, who’s next?”

We walked through Wemmick’s greenhouse until he turned to me and said, “Pay attention to the man I’m about to shake hands with.” I would have done so anyway, as he hadn’t shaken hands with anyone yet.

Almost immediately, a stout, upright man (whom I can still picture as I write this) in a worn olive-colored frock coat, with a strange pallor over his ruddy complexion and eyes that darted around whenever he tried to focus, approached the bars. He touched his hat—which had a greasy, slick surface like cold broth—in a half-serious, half-joking military salute.

“Colonel, to you!” Wemmick said. “How are you, Colonel?”

“I’m alright, Mr. Wemmick.”

“Everything possible was done, but the evidence was just too strong against you, Colonel.”

“Yes, it was too strong, sir—but I don’t care.”

“No, no,” Wemmick said coolly, “you don’t care.” Then, turning to me, he added, “This man served the King. He was a regular soldier and bought his discharge.”

I said, “Really?” The man’s eyes looked at me, then over my head, then all around me, before he wiped his hand across his lips and laughed.

“I think I’ll be out of here on Monday, sir,” he told Wemmick.

“Perhaps,” my friend replied, “but there’s no way to know for sure.”

“I’m glad to have the chance to say goodbye, Mr. Wemmick,” the man said, reaching his hand through the bars.

“Thank you,” Wemmick said, shaking his hand. “Same to you, Colonel.”

“If what I had on me when I was arrested had been real, Mr. Wemmick,” the man said, reluctant to let go, “I would have asked you to wear another ring as a token of my appreciation for your help.”

“I’ll take the thought for the deed,” Wemmick said. “By the way, I heard you were quite the pigeon enthusiast.” The man looked up at the sky. “I’m told you had a remarkable breed of tumblers. Could you ask a friend to bring me a pair, if you have no further use for them?”

“It will be done, sir.”

“Alright,” Wemmick said, “they’ll be well looked after. Good afternoon, Colonel. Goodbye!” They shook hands again, and as we walked away, Wemmick said to me, “He’s a counterfeiter, a very skilled one. The Recorder’s report is being filed today, and he’s certain to be executed on Monday. Still, you see, a pair of pigeons is portable property regardless.” With that, he looked back, nodded at the dead plant, and scanned the area as we left the yard, as if considering what other pot would fit best in its place.

As we exited the prison through the gatehouse, I noticed that my guardian’s importance was recognized by the guards just as much as by the prisoners. “Well, Mr. Wemmick,” said the guard who kept us between the two heavy, spiked gates, carefully locking one before unlocking the other, “what is Mr. Jaggers going to do about that waterfront murder?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_018",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Whenever he sensed his situation was particularly dire and that he absolutely needed to secure an opportunity, he would visit the Exchange during peak hours, pacing back and forth in a somber, rhythmic pattern among the gathered titans of industry. “Because,” Herbert explained to me upon returning home for dinner after one of those particular outings, “I have realized, Handel, that an opportunity will not seek you out; you must seek it out yourself—which is what I have been doing.”

Had we not been so fond of each other, I suspect we would have grown to despise one another every single morning. During those periods of remorse, I loathed our rooms beyond words and could not stand the sight of the Avenger’s uniform, which seemed more costly and less profitable at that hour than at any other time of the day. As our debts mounted, breakfast turned into an increasingly empty ritual, and on one occasion, after receiving a letter threatening legal action “not entirely unrelated,” as my local newspaper might phrase it, “to jewelry,” I went so far as to grab the Avenger by his blue collar and lift him off the ground—leaving him suspended in the air like a booted Cupid—simply for daring to assume we desired a bread roll.

At certain intervals—or rather, uncertain ones, as they relied entirely on our moods—I would remark to Herbert, as if it were a brilliant revelation:

“My dear Herbert, we are faring poorly.”

“My dear Handel,” Herbert would reply with complete sincerity, “if you can believe it, those exact words were just on my tongue, by some odd coincidence.”

“In that case, Herbert,” I would answer, “let us examine our finances.”

We always gained a deep sense of gratification from scheduling a time for this task. I always felt this was true business, the proper way to face the situation, the way to seize the enemy by the throat. And I am certain Herbert felt the same.

We would order a somewhat special dinner, accompanied by a bottle of something equally out of the ordinary, so that our spirits might be bolstered for the task and we could rise to the occasion. Once dinner was finished, we brought out a bundle of pens, a generous amount of ink, and a substantial display of writing and blotting paper. There was, after all, something very reassuring about having an abundance of stationery.

I would then take a sheet of paper and write at the top, in a tidy script, the title, “Memorandum of Pip’s debts,” carefully adding Barnard’s Inn and the date. Herbert would likewise take a sheet and write with similar precision, “Memorandum of Herbert’s debts.”

Each of us would then consult a disorganized pile of documents at our sides, which had been tossed into drawers, worn thin in our pockets, partially scorched while lighting candles, wedged for weeks into the mirror frame, and otherwise ruined. The scratching of our pens provided us with immense relief, to the point where I sometimes found it hard to differentiate between this instructive business activity and the actual act of settling our accounts. In terms of their virtuous quality, the two actions seemed nearly identical.

After we had been writing for a short while, I would inquire how Herbert was progressing."
}

  "rewritten_text": "Whenever he felt his situation was particularly dire and that he absolutely had to find an opportunity, he would head to the Stock Exchange during peak hours, pacing back and forth in a gloomy, rhythmic pattern among the gathered titans of industry. “Because,” Herbert told me when he returned home for dinner after one of those specific outings, “I’ve realized, Handel, that an opportunity won’t just come to you; you have to go out and find it—so that’s what I’ve been doing.”

If we hadn’t been so close, I think we would have genuinely hated each other every single morning. I loathed our apartment during those periods of regret and couldn’t stand the sight of the Avenger’s uniform, which looked more expensive and less productive then than at any other time of the day. As our debts grew, breakfast became an increasingly empty ritual. On one occasion, after receiving a letter threatening legal action—which, as my local newspaper might put it, was “not entirely unrelated” to jewelry—I went so far as to grab the Avenger by his blue collar and shake him off his feet until he was dangling in the air like a booted Cupid, simply for daring to assume we wanted a bread roll.

At certain times—or rather, uncertain times, since they depended entirely on our moods—I would say to Herbert, as if it were a groundbreaking discovery:

“My dear Herbert, we are doing quite poorly.”

“My dear Handel,” Herbert would reply with complete sincerity, “if you can believe it, those exact words were on my lips, by some strange coincidence.”

“Then, Herbert,” I would respond, “let’s examine our finances.”

We always felt a deep sense of satisfaction in setting a time for this task. I always believed this was real business, the right way to face the problem, the way to grab the enemy by the throat. And I know Herbert felt the same.

We would order something special for dinner, along with a bottle of something equally out of the ordinary, so that our minds would be fortified for the task and we could perform at our best. Once dinner was finished, we brought out a bundle of pens, plenty of ink, and a generous supply of writing and blotting paper. There was something very comforting about having an abundance of stationery.

I would then take a sheet of paper and write at the top in a neat hand, “Memorandum of Pip’s debts,” carefully adding Barnard’s Inn and the date. Herbert would also take a sheet of paper and write with similar formality, “Memorandum of Herbert’s debts.”

Each of us would then refer to a messy pile of papers at his side, which had been tossed into drawers, worn into holes in our pockets, half-burned while lighting candles, stuck into the mirror for weeks, or otherwise damaged. The sound of our pens scratching away refreshed us so much that I sometimes found it difficult to distinguish between this productive business activity and actually paying the money. In terms of moral value, the two things seemed about the same.

After we had been writing for a while, I would ask Herbert how he was getting on."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_019",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I concluded by resting my hand on his shoulder and remarking, “I cannot avoid confiding in you, even though I realize it must be a burden; yet that is your own doing, for having ever led me to this place.”

Wemmick remained quiet for a brief moment, then responded with a slight jolt, “Well, you see, Mr. Pip, I must mention one thing. This is remarkably kind of you.”

“Then promise you will assist me in being good,” I urged.

“Goodness,” Wemmick answered, moving his head, “that is not my line of work.”

“And this is not your place of business,” I countered.

“You are correct,” he replied. “You have struck the mark. Mr. Pip, I shall put on my thinking cap, and I believe everything you desire can be accomplished gradually. Skiffins—that is her brother—is an accountant and agent. I will contact him and begin the task for you.”

“I offer you ten thousand thanks.”

“Quite the opposite,” he stated, “I thank you, for although we are acting strictly in a private and personal capacity, it is worth noting that there are Newgate cobwebs present, and this helps to clear them away.”

Following a bit more discussion along these lines, we went back into the Castle, where we discovered Miss Skiffins getting tea ready. The significant task of preparing the toast was assigned to the Aged, and that fine old gentleman was so focused on the job that he appeared to be in risk of melting his own eyes. It was no mere symbolic meal we were about to consume, but a hearty reality. The Aged assembled such a mountain of buttered toast that I could barely spot him behind it as it sizzled on an iron rack attached to the top bar; meanwhile, Miss Skiffins prepared such a large pot of tea that the pig in the yard grew quite agitated, repeatedly signaling his wish to join in the feast.

The flag had been lowered and the cannon discharged at the precise hour, and I felt as securely isolated from the remainder of Walworth as if the moat were thirty feet in width and depth. Nothing interrupted the peace of the Castle, save for the occasional swinging open of the doors for John and Miss Skiffins, which were prone to a spasmodic defect that made me feel sympathetically uneasy until I grew accustomed to it. I gathered from the orderly way Miss Skiffins handled things that she prepared tea there every Sunday evening; and I suspected that a traditional brooch she wore, depicting the silhouette of an unappealing woman with a very straight nose and a crescent moon, was an item of portable property gifted to her by Wemmick.

We consumed the entire supply of toast and drank a corresponding amount of tea, and it was pleasant to observe how warm and oily we all became afterward. The Aged, in particular, might have been mistaken for a clean, elderly chief of a primitive tribe, freshly anointed. After a brief interval of rest, Miss Skiffins—since the young servant had apparently returned to her family for the Sunday afternoon—cleaned the tea service in a light, ladylike, amateur fashion that did not compromise any of us. Then, she replaced her gloves, we gathered around the hearth, and Wemmick commanded, “Now, Aged Parent, read us the newspaper.”"
}

{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_019",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "I concluded by resting my hand on his shoulder and remarking, “I cannot avoid confiding in you, even though I realize it must be a burden; yet that is your own doing, for having ever led me to this place.”

Wemmick remained quiet for a brief moment, then responded with a slight jolt, “Well, you see, Mr. Pip, I must mention one thing. This is remarkably kind of you.”

“Then promise you will assist me in being good,” I urged.

“Goodness,” Wemmick answered, moving his head, “that is not my line of work.”

“And this is not your place of business,” I countered.

“You are correct,” he replied. “You have struck the mark. Mr. Pip, I shall put on my thinking cap, and I believe everything you desire can be accomplished gradually. Skiffins—that is her brother—is an accountant and agent. I will contact him and begin the task for you.”

“I offer you ten thousand thanks.”

“Quite the opposite,” he stated, “I thank you, for although we are acting strictly in a private and personal capacity, it is worth noting that there are Newgate cobwebs present, and this helps to clear them away.”

Following a bit more discussion along these lines, we went back into the Castle, where we discovered Miss Skiffins getting tea ready. The significant task of preparing the toast was assigned to the Aged, and that fine old gentleman was so focused on the job that he appeared to be in risk of melting his own eyes. It was no mere symbolic meal we were about to consume, but a hearty reality. The Aged assembled such a mountain of buttered toast that I could barely spot him behind it as it sizzled on an iron rack attached to the top bar; meanwhile, Miss Skiffins prepared such a large pot of tea that the pig in the yard grew quite agitated, repeatedly signaling his wish to join in the feast.

The flag had been lowered and the cannon discharged at the precise hour, and I felt as securely isolated from the remainder of Walworth as if the moat were thirty feet in width and depth. Nothing interrupted the peace of the Castle, save for the occasional swinging open of the doors for John and Miss Skiffins, which were prone to a spasmodic defect that made me feel sympathetically uneasy until I grew accustomed to it. I gathered from the orderly way Miss Skiffins handled things that she prepared tea there every Sunday evening; and I suspected that a traditional brooch she wore, depicting the silhouette of an unappealing woman with a very straight nose and a crescent moon, was an item of portable property gifted to her by Wemmick.

We consumed the entire supply of toast and drank a corresponding amount of tea, and it was pleasant to observe how warm and oily we all became afterward. The Aged, in particular, might have been mistaken for a clean, elderly chief of a primitive tribe, freshly anointed. After a brief interval of rest, Miss Skiffins—since the young servant had apparently returned to her family for the Sunday afternoon—cleaned the tea service in a light, ladylike, amateur fashion that did not compromise any of us. Then, she replaced her gloves, we gathered around the hearth, and Wemmick commanded, “Now, Aged Parent, read us the newspaper.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_020",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The wind had blown with such intensity that lead had been ripped from the roofs of tall city buildings, while in rural areas, trees were uprooted and windmill sails were swept away. Furthermore, grim reports of fatalities and shipwrecks arrived from the coast. These fierce gales were accompanied by heavy downpours, and the day that had just ended as I began my reading was the most severe of all.

Since that period, the Temple has undergone changes, making it less isolated and less vulnerable to the river than it was then. We resided in the uppermost part of the final building, and that night, the wind surging up the river rattled our home as if it were being struck by artillery fire or crashing waves. As the rain lashed against the windows, I looked up at them as they swayed and imagined I was inside a lighthouse battered by a storm. At times, smoke billowed down the chimney as if it were unable to withstand the night outside. When I opened the doors to peer down the stairwell, the lamps were extinguished. Shielding my eyes to look through the dark windows—for opening them against such a gale was impossible—I observed that the court lamps had gone out, the lights on the bridges and banks were flickering, and the coal fires on river barges were being scattered by the wind like glowing embers in the rain.

I read with my watch on the table, intending to finish at eleven. As I closed the volume, Saint Paul’s and the various city church clocks—some ahead, some in unison, some behind—chimed the hour. The wind distorted the sound, and as I listened to it being torn apart, I heard footsteps on the stairs.

It is irrelevant what anxious impulse caused me to jump and dreadfully associate the sound with my late sister’s footsteps. The feeling vanished quickly, and I listened again, hearing the person stumble as they climbed. Recalling that the stairwell lights were out, I grabbed my reading lamp and walked to the landing. Whoever was below had paused upon seeing my light, and everything fell silent.

“Is someone down there?” I asked, peering over the edge.

“Yes,” a voice replied from the darkness below.

“Which floor are you looking for?”

“The top. Mr. Pip.”

“That is me. Is something wrong?”

“Nothing is wrong,” the voice answered, and the man continued upward.

I held my lamp over the railing, and he gradually entered the glow. Because it was a shaded lamp meant for reading, its beam was very narrow; he was illuminated for only a brief moment before stepping back into the shadows. In that instant, I caught sight of a face I did not recognize, gazing up at me with an inexplicable expression of being moved and delighted to see me."
}

Grim reports of shipwrecks and fatalities had arrived from the coast. Violent downpours had accompanied these gales, and the day that had just ended, as I sat down to read, had been the most severe of all.

Renovations have changed that section of the Temple since then, so it no longer feels as isolated or as exposed to the river as it once did. We lived on the top floor of the final building, and that night, the wind rushing up the river shook the structure like cannon fire or crashing waves. When the rain lashed against the windows, I looked up at them as they rattled and thought I could easily imagine myself in a storm-battered lighthouse. Occasionally, smoke billowed down the chimney as if it could not bear to venture out into such a night. When I opened the doors to look down the stairwell, the lamps were blown out. Shielding my eyes to peer through the dark windows—opening them even slightly was impossible against such wind and rain—I saw that the courtyard lamps were extinguished, the lights on the bridges and the shore were flickering, and the coal fires on river barges were being swept away like glowing embers in the rain.

I read with my watch on the table, intending to finish my book at eleven o’clock. As I closed it, Saint Paul’s and all the various church clocks across the City—some ahead, some in unison, some trailing—struck the hour. The wind strangely distorted the sound, and as I listened, wondering how the gale tore at the chimes, I heard a footstep on the stairs.

It does not matter what nervous impulse made me jump and fearfully associate it with the footsteps of my deceased sister. The feeling passed instantly, and I listened again, hearing the person stumble as they climbed. Remembering that the stairwell lights were out, I grabbed my reading lamp and walked to the top of the stairs. Whoever was below had stopped upon seeing my light, for everything went quiet.

“Is someone down there?” I called out, looking down.

“Yes,” a voice replied from the darkness below.

“Which floor are you looking for?”

“The top. Mr. Pip.”

“That is my name. Is something wrong?”

“Nothing is wrong,” the voice answered. The man continued upward.

I stood holding my lamp over the stair railing, and he slowly stepped into its glow. It was a shaded lamp meant for reading, so its circle of light was very narrow; he was illuminated for only a brief moment before stepping back into the shadows. In that instant, I saw a face I did not recognize, looking up at me with an inexplicable expression of being moved and delighted by the sight of me."
}

“That is my name. Is something wrong?”

  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_021",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "And what follows, once that is finished? Here I am. Returning now would be just as detrimental as remaining—perhaps even more so. Furthermore, Pip, I am here because I have intended to do right by you for many years. Regarding what I am willing to risk, I am an aged creature now, having survived all sorts of snares since I first left the nest, and I am not frightened to land on a scarecrow. If Death is concealed within it, so be it; let him emerge, and I will confront him, and only then will I acknowledge him, not before. Now, let me examine my gentleman once more."

He grasped my hands again and inspected me with a sense of proud ownership, puffing on his pipe with immense satisfaction the entire time.

It seemed to me that my best course was to find him some private quarters nearby, which he could occupy once Herbert arrived, whom I anticipated in a few days. It was obvious that I must share the secret with Herbert, even setting aside the immense relief I would feel by unburdening myself to him. However, Mr. Provis—the name I decided to use for him—did not agree, withholding his approval of Herbert’s involvement until he could see him and judge his appearance for himself. "And even then, dear boy," he remarked, drawing a worn, small black Testament with a clasp from his pocket, "we shall have him swear an oath."

To claim that my frightening benefactor traveled the globe with this small volume solely to administer oaths in urgent situations would be to assert something I never fully confirmed; yet, I can say I never witnessed him utilize it for any other purpose. The book itself looked as though it had been pilfered from a courtroom, and perhaps his awareness of its history, coupled with his own background in such matters, led him to trust in its efficacy as a kind of legal incantation. When he produced it on this first occasion, I remembered how he had forced me to vow loyalty in the graveyard years before, and how he had mentioned the previous night that he habitually swore to his own intentions while in isolation.

Since he was currently attired in a sailor’s outfit, which made him appear as if he had parrots and cigars to sell, I proceeded to discuss what clothing he ought to adopt. He held a peculiar conviction regarding the effectiveness of "shorts" as a disguise, and had mentally designed an outfit for himself that would have rendered him a cross between a clergyman and a dentist. I managed with great effort to persuade him to adopt the attire of a successful farmer; we also agreed that he should crop his hair short and use a bit of powder. Finally, since the laundress and her niece had not yet spotted him, he was to remain hidden from their sight until his transformation was complete.

Deciding on these safety measures might seem straightforward, but in my bewildered, if not entirely frantic, condition, the process took so long that I did not venture out to implement them until mid-afternoon."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_021",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "So, what happens once that is finished? Here I am. To turn back now would be just as bad as staying put—or worse. Besides, Pip, I am here because I have intended to do right by you for years and years. As for what I dare, I am an old bird now, having braved all kinds of traps since I first learned to fly, and I am not afraid to land on a scarecrow. If death is hidden inside it, so be it; let it come out, and I will face it, and only then will I believe in it, not before. Now, let me have another look at my gentleman.”

Once more, he took me by both hands and examined me with an air of admiring ownership, smoking with great satisfaction the entire time.

It seemed to me that I could do nothing better than secure him some quiet lodging nearby, which he could occupy when Herbert returned, whom I expected in two or three days. It was clear to me that the secret had to be shared with Herbert as a matter of absolute necessity, even if I ignored the immense relief I would feel by confiding in him. However, this was by no means clear to Mr. Provis (I decided to call him by that name), who withheld his consent to Herbert’s involvement until he could see him and form a favorable opinion of his appearance. “And even then, dear boy,” he said, pulling a greasy little black Testament with a clasp from his pocket, “we will have him swear an oath.”

To claim that my terrifying patron carried this little black book around the world solely to swear people in during emergencies would be to state something I never quite confirmed; but I can say that I never saw him use it for anything else. The book itself looked as though it had been stolen from a courtroom, and perhaps his knowledge of its history, combined with his own experiences in that regard, gave him a reliance on its power as a kind of legal spell or charm. On this first occasion of him producing it, I recalled how he had made me swear loyalty in the churchyard long ago, and how he had described himself last night as always swearing to his resolutions while in solitude.

As he was currently dressed in a seafaring outfit, in which he looked as if he had parrots and cigars to sell, I next discussed with him what he should wear. He held an extraordinary belief in the effectiveness of knee-breeches as a disguise, and had mentally designed an outfit for himself that would have made him look like a cross between a dean and a dentist. It was with considerable difficulty that I convinced him to adopt a style more like a prosperous farmer’s; we arranged that he should cut his hair short and wear a little powder. Finally, since he had not yet been seen by the laundress or her niece, he was to stay out of their sight until he had changed his clothes.

It might seem like a simple matter to decide on these precautions, but in my dazed, if not distracted, state, it took so long that I did not get out to carry them out until two or three in the afternoon."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_022",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“There was another man associated with Compeyson named Arthur—not his given name, but his surname. He suffered from a wasting disease and appeared like a mere ghost. He and Compeyson had previously swindled a wealthy woman out of a large sum of money, but Compeyson gambled his share away so recklessly he would have squandered the king’s own revenue. Consequently, Arthur was dying in poverty, plagued by terrifying delusions, while Compeyson’s wife—whom Compeyson frequently abused—tried to show him kindness, though Compeyson himself felt compassion for no one.

“I could have learned a lesson from Arthur, but I failed to do so, and I will not claim I was particularly scrupulous—for what would be the point of that, my dear boy and friend? Thus, I started working with Compeyson, becoming a mere instrument in his grasp. Arthur resided in the upper level of Compeyson’s home near Brentford, where Compeyson kept a meticulous record of his expenses for room and board, expecting him to repay the debt if he ever recovered. However, Arthur soon closed that account. During my second or third visit, he rushed into Compeyson’s parlor late at night wearing only a flannel robe, his hair damp with perspiration. He told Compeyson’s wife, ‘Sally, she is truly upstairs with me now, and I cannot make her leave. She is dressed entirely in white, with white flowers in her hair; she is terribly insane, carrying a burial shroud over her arm, and she insists she will drape it over me at five o’clock in the morning.’

“Compeyson replied, ‘You fool, don’t you realize she is a living person? How could she possibly be up there without entering through the door or window and climbing the stairs?’

“‘I do not know how she arrived,’ Arthur answered, trembling violently from his delirium, ‘but she is standing in the corner at the foot of my bed, completely mad. And where her heart is broken—which you caused!—there are droplets of blood.’

“Compeyson spoke with feigned toughness, but he was inherently a coward. ‘Go upstairs with this babbling invalid,’ he told his wife, ‘and Magwitch, assist her, will you?’ Yet, he never approached the room himself.

“Compeyson’s wife and I helped him back to bed, where he raved in a terrifying manner. ‘Look at her!’ he shouted. ‘She is shaking the shroud at me! Can’t you see her? Look at her eyes! Isn’t it horrific to see her so deranged?’ Then he cried, ‘She is going to put it on me, and then I am finished! Take it away from her, take it away!’ He grabbed onto us, continuing to speak to her and respond to her presence until I almost believed I could see her myself.

“Compeyson’s wife, accustomed to his condition, gave him some spirits to ease his terrors, and eventually he grew calm. ‘Oh, she has left! Has her attendant come to collect her?’ he asked. ‘Yes,’ Compeyson’s wife replied. ‘Did you tell him to lock and bolt her in?’ ‘Yes.’ ‘And to remove that hideous object from her?’ ‘Yes, yes, everything is fine.’ ‘You are a kind soul,’ he said, ‘do not abandon me, whatever you do, and thank you!’"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_022",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“There was another man with Compeyson named Arthur—not his first name, but his surname. He was suffering from a wasting disease and looked like a ghost. He and Compeyson had been involved in a shady scheme with a wealthy woman years earlier, and they had made a fortune from it. However, Compeyson gambled and bet so recklessly that he would have squandered the king’s own taxes. So, Arthur was dying, penniless and tormented by hallucinations, while Compeyson’s wife—whom Compeyson frequently abused—tried to show him mercy when she could, though Compeyson himself had no pity for anyone.

“I could have taken warning from Arthur, but I didn’t; and I won’t pretend I was particularly choosy—what would be the point of that, my dear boy and comrade? So I started working with Compeyson, and I was a pathetic tool in his hands. Arthur lived at the top of Compeyson’s house near Brentford, and Compeyson kept a meticulous record of his debts for room and board, in case he ever recovered enough to pay them off. But Arthur soon settled the account. The second or third time I ever saw him, he came tearing down into Compeyson’s parlor late at night, wearing only a flannel robe, his hair soaked in sweat. He said to Compeyson’s wife, ‘Sally, she really is upstairs with me now, and I can’t get rid of her. She’s dressed all in white,’ he said, ‘with white flowers in her hair. She’s furious, she’s carrying a burial shroud over her arm, and she says she’s going to put it on me at five in the morning.’

“Compeyson replied, ‘You fool, don’t you realize she’s a living person? How could she be up there without coming through the door, the window, or up the stairs?’

“‘I don’t know how she’s there,’ Arthur said, shivering violently from his delirium, ‘but she’s standing in the corner at the foot of my bed, looking absolutely deranged. And over where her heart is broken—you broke it!—there are drops of blood.’

“Compeyson spoke boldly, but he was always a coward. ‘Go upstairs with this babbling sick man,’ he told his wife, ‘and Magwitch, give her a hand, will you?’ But he never went near him himself.

“Compeyson’s wife and I helped him back to bed, and he raved horribly. ‘Look at her!’ he screamed. ‘She’s shaking the shroud at me! Don’t you see her? Look at her eyes! Isn’t it terrifying to see her so mad?’ Next, he cried, ‘She’s going to put it on me, and then I’m finished! Take it away from her, take it away!’ He grabbed onto us, continuing to talk to her and answer her, until I almost believed I saw her myself.

“Compeyson’s wife, being used to his condition, gave him some liquor to calm his tremors, and eventually he quieted down. ‘Oh, she’s gone! Has her keeper come for her?’ he asked. ‘Yes,’ Compeyson’s wife replied. ‘Did you tell him to lock her up and bar the door?’ ‘Yes.’ ‘And to take that hideous thing away from her?’ ‘Yes, yes, it’s all taken care of.’ ‘You’re a good soul,’ he said, ‘don’t leave me, whatever you do, and thank you!’"
}

  "rewritten_text": "Among that small number, there might be someone who cherishes you just as deeply, even if he has not loved you for as long as I have. Choose him, and I can endure it more easily, for your own sake!”

My sincerity stirred a sense of wonder in her that appeared as if it might have been softened by pity, had she been able to make me at all comprehensible to her own thoughts.

“I am going,” she repeated, in a softer tone, “to marry him. The arrangements for my wedding are underway, and I shall be wed shortly. Why do you unfairly bring up the name of my adoptive mother? This is my own decision.”

“Is it your own decision, Estella, to throw yourself away on such a brute?”

“On whom should I throw myself away?” she replied, with a smile. “Should I throw myself away on the man who would most quickly realize—if people truly feel such things—that I brought him nothing? There! It is settled. I shall fare well enough, and so will my husband. Regarding your claim that I was led into this disastrous choice, Miss Havisham would have preferred that I wait and not marry yet; however, I am weary of the life I have lived, which holds very little appeal for me, and I am quite ready to alter it. Say no more. We shall never see eye to eye.”

“Such a base brute, such a dull brute!” I pleaded, in desperation.

“Do not worry about me being a blessing to him,” Estella remarked; “I shall not be that. Come! Here is my hand. Do we part on these terms, you idealistic boy—or man?”

“O Estella!” I replied, as my painful tears dropped quickly onto her hand, despite my efforts to hold them back; “even if I stayed in England and could hold my head high like the others, how could I bear to see you as Drummle’s wife?”

“Nonsense,” she answered,—“nonsense. This will be forgotten in no time.”

“Never, Estella!”

“You will banish me from your thoughts within a week.”

“Banish you from my thoughts! You are woven into my existence, a part of my very self. You have been in every sentence I have ever read since I first arrived here, the unrefined, common boy whose fragile heart you wounded even then. You have been in every view I have ever beheld since—on the river, on the sails of the vessels, on the marshes, in the clouds, in the light, in the shadows, in the wind, in the forests, in the sea, in the city streets. You have been the personification of every elegant notion my mind has ever encountered. The stones that form the most solid London structures are no more substantial, or more impossible to dislodge, than your presence and influence have been to me, there and everywhere, and will always be. Estella, until the final hour of my life, you cannot help but remain a part of my nature, part of the little good within me, and part of the evil. Yet, in this parting, I link you only with the good; and I will loyally hold you to that forever, for you must have done me far more good than harm, no matter how sharp my current distress may be. O God bless you, God forgive you!”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_024",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "On my initial trip past Mill Pond Bank, Herbert and I were rowing; both on the way there and back, we observed the eastern blind being lowered. Herbert visited at least three times weekly, yet he never returned with any distressing news. Nevertheless, I remained aware of the danger and could not shake the feeling that I was under surveillance. Once such a thought takes root, it becomes persistent; it would be difficult to count the number of innocent people I wrongly suspected of spying on me.

In essence, I was perpetually anxious for the reckless man in concealment. Herbert occasionally remarked that he enjoyed standing by our windows after nightfall, watching the ebbing tide and imagining it flowing toward Clara. Conversely, I dreaded that the water was carrying toward Magwitch, and that any dark shape upon the current might be his hunters, moving rapidly, quietly, and inevitably to capture him.

Chapter XLVII.

Several weeks elapsed without any developments. We awaited word from Wemmick, but he remained silent. Had I only known him within the confines of Little Britain, rather than enjoying the intimacy of his Castle, I might have questioned his loyalty; however, knowing him as I did, I never doubted him for an instant.

My financial situation began to look bleak, and I faced pressure from several creditors. I personally started to experience a shortage of ready cash, which I addressed by selling off some jewelry I could spare. Yet, I had firmly resolved that it would be a cruel deception to accept further funds from my benefactor while my own intentions and future remained so uncertain. Consequently, I had Herbert return the unopened pocket-book to him for safekeeping, and I felt a sense of contentment—whether genuine or misguided, I cannot say—in having refrained from benefiting from his wealth since he revealed his identity.

As time progressed, a heavy conviction took hold of me that Estella had wed. Terrified of confirming what I already believed, I shunned the newspapers and requested that Herbert, who knew the details of our final meeting, never mention her name. Why did I cling to this final, tattered remnant of a hope that had long since been torn away? Why do you, the reader, engage in your own similar contradictions, as you did last year, last month, or even last week?

I led a miserable existence, and its primary worry, which loomed over every other concern like a massive peak above a mountain range, never left my thoughts. Still, no fresh threats emerged. Regardless of how often I bolted from my bed, gripped by the sudden terror that he had been found, or how I sat in dread listening for Herbert’s return at night, fearing his footsteps might be faster than usual and carry dire tidings—despite all this and more, life continued its monotonous cycle."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_024",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The first time I passed Mill Pond Bank, Herbert and I were rowing, and both on the way there and back, we saw the blind on the east window lowered. Herbert visited at least three times a week, and he never brought me any alarming news. Still, I knew there was reason to worry, and I could not shake the feeling that I was being watched. Once that idea takes root, it is haunting; it would be difficult to count how many innocent people I suspected of spying on me.

In short, I was constantly anxious for the reckless man in hiding. Herbert sometimes mentioned that he enjoyed standing at our windows after dark, watching the tide go out, and imagining it flowing toward Clara. But I thought with dread that it was flowing toward Magwitch, and that any dark shape on the water might be his pursuers, moving quickly, silently, and surely to capture him.

Chapter XLVII.

Several weeks passed without change. We waited for word from Wemmick, but he gave no sign. If I had only known him in Little Britain and had never enjoyed the privilege of being on familiar terms at his home, I might have doubted him; but knowing him as I did, I never doubted him for a moment.

My financial situation began to look grim, and I was pressured by more than one creditor. I even began to feel the lack of ready cash in my own pocket, which I relieved by selling some jewelry I could spare. However, I had firmly decided that it would be a heartless fraud to take more money from my benefactor while my thoughts and plans remained so uncertain. Therefore, I had sent him the unopened pocketbook via Herbert for safekeeping, and I felt a sense of satisfaction—whether genuine or misguided, I hardly know—in not having profited from his generosity since he revealed his identity.

As time went on, a heavy conviction settled on me that Estella was married. Afraid to have it confirmed, even though I was nearly certain, I avoided the newspapers and begged Herbert, to whom I had confided the details of our last meeting, never to speak of her to me. Why did I cling to this last miserable shred of hope, which had already been torn to pieces and scattered to the winds? Why did you, who are reading this, commit a similar inconsistency yourself last year, last month, or last week?

It was an unhappy life I led, and its primary anxiety, towering over all others like a high mountain above a range, never left my sight. Still, no new cause for fear arose. No matter how often I started from my bed, terrified that he had been discovered, or how often I sat listening in dread for Herbert’s return at night, fearing his footsteps might be faster than usual and carry bad news—despite all that and much more, the daily routine continued."
}
