Herbert visited at least three times a week, and he never brought me any alarming news. Still, I knew there was reason to worry, and I could not shake the feeling that I was being watched. Once that idea takes hold, it is haunting; it would be difficult to count how many innocent people I suspected of spying on me.

In short, I was constantly afraid for the reckless man in hiding. Herbert had sometimes mentioned that he found it pleasant to stand at our windows after dark, watching the tide go out and thinking that it was flowing toward Clara. But I thought with dread that it was flowing toward Magwitch, and that any dark shape on the water might be his pursuers, moving quickly, silently, and surely to capture him.

Chapter XLVII.

Several weeks passed without any change. We waited for word from Wemmick, but he gave no sign. If I had only known him in his office at Little Britain and had never experienced the privilege of visiting him at his home, I might have doubted him; but knowing him as I did, I never doubted him for a moment.

My financial situation began to look bleak, and I was pressured for payment by several creditors. I even began to feel the lack of ready cash in my own pocket, which I relieved by selling some jewelry I could spare. However, I had firmly decided that it would be dishonest to take more money from my benefactor while my own plans and thoughts remained so uncertain. Therefore, I had asked Herbert to return the unopened pocket-book to him for safekeeping, and I felt a sense of satisfaction—whether genuine or misguided, I am not sure—in not having profited from his generosity since he revealed his identity to me.

As time went on, a heavy feeling settled over me that Estella was married. Afraid to have it confirmed, even though I was almost certain, I avoided the newspapers and asked Herbert, to whom I had confided the details of our last meeting, never to speak of her to me. Why did I cling to this last, miserable shred of hope? Why did you, who are reading this, commit a similar inconsistency in your own life last year, last month, or last week?

It was an unhappy life I lived, and one primary anxiety, towering over all others like a high mountain above a range, never left my mind. Still, no new cause for fear arose. No matter how often I started from my bed in terror that he had been discovered, or how often I sat listening with dread for Herbert’s return at night, fearing his steps might be faster than usual and carry bad news—despite all that and much more, life continued on as before."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_025",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "You claim these are fingernail marks, and you propose the theory that she killed her infant. You must embrace every implication of that claim. For all we can tell, she might have killed the child, and the infant, while grasping at her, could have clawed her hands. What follows? You are not prosecuting her for the murder of her child; why aren't you? Regarding this matter, if you insist on these scratches, we argue that, for all we know, your explanation might be valid, provided we assume for the sake of argument that you did not fabricate them? "To conclude, sir," Wemmick remarked, "Mr. Jaggers completely overwhelmed the jury, and they surrendered."

"Has she remained in his employ ever since?"

"Indeed; and more than that," Wemmick replied, "she entered his service right after she was acquitted, already as subdued as she is today. She has since learned various tasks related to her work, but she was broken from the start."

"Do you recall the gender of the child?"

"It was said to be a girl."

"Do you have anything else to tell me tonight?"

"Nothing. I received your note and burned it. Nothing."

We shared a friendly farewell, and I returned home, burdened with fresh subjects for reflection, yet finding no respite from the previous ones.

Chapter XLIX.

Placing Miss Havisham’s letter in my pocket, so it might act as my proof for returning to Satis House so soon, in case her unpredictable nature caused her to show surprise at my arrival, I traveled down by coach the following day. However, I stepped off at the Halfway House, ate breakfast there, and finished the journey on foot; for I wanted to enter the town discreetly via the less-traveled paths, and depart in the same fashion.

The finest daylight had faded by the time I walked through the quiet, resonant courtyards behind the High Street. The ruined corners where the ancient monks once kept their dining halls and gardens, and where the sturdy walls were now utilized for modest sheds and stables, were nearly as hushed as the monks in their tombs. The cathedral bells possessed a more mournful and distant tone to me as I hurried along, shunning notice, than they ever had previously; likewise, the swell of the antique organ reached me like a funeral dirge; and the rooks, as they circled the grey tower and swayed in the leafless, tall trees of the priory garden, appeared to signal to me that the location had altered, and that Estella had departed from it forever.

An older woman, whom I had previously encountered as one of the staff residing in the secondary building across the rear courtyard, unlatched the gate. The illuminated candle sat in the dim hallway inside, just as in the past, and I picked it up and climbed the stairs by myself. Miss Havisham was not in her private chamber, but was in the larger room across the landing. Peering through the doorway, after knocking without success, I observed her seated on the hearth in a tattered chair, positioned near to, and absorbed in the contemplation of, the dying fire.

Following my usual custom, I entered and stood leaning against the old mantelpiece, where she might notice me once she lifted her gaze."
}

"Do you recall the sex of the child?"

"It was said to have been a girl."

"Do you have anything else to tell me tonight?"

"Nothing. I received your letter and destroyed it. Nothing."

Chapter XLIX.

Putting Miss Havisham’s note in my pocket so it could serve as my proof for returning to Satis House so soon, in case her unpredictable nature led her to express surprise at seeing me, I traveled down by coach the next day. However, I got off at the Halfway House, ate breakfast there, and walked the remaining distance; I wanted to enter the town quietly using the less-traveled paths and leave the same way.

The best light of the day had faded by the time I walked through the quiet, echoing courtyards behind the High Street. The ruined corners where the old monks once had their dining halls and gardens, and where the sturdy walls were now used for humble sheds and stables, were nearly as silent as the monks in their graves. The cathedral chimes sounded sadder and more distant to me as I hurried along, avoiding notice, than they ever had before; the swell of the old organ reached my ears like funeral music, and the rooks, as they hovered around the grey tower and swayed in the bare, high trees of the priory garden, seemed to call out that the place had changed and that Estella was gone from it forever.

An elderly woman, whom I had seen before as one of the servants living in the secondary house across the back courtyard, opened the gate. The lit candle stood in the dark hallway inside, just as it used to, and I picked it up and climbed the staircase alone. Miss Havisham was not in her own room, but was in the larger room across the landing. Looking into the door after knocking without success, I saw her sitting on the hearth in a tattered chair, positioned right in front of, and lost in the contemplation of, the ashy fire.

As this task proceeded, I observed Wemmick writing, while Mr. Jaggers, balancing and rocking upon his gleaming boots, watched me. “I regret, Pip,” he remarked after signing the document and watching me pocket it, “that we do nothing for you.”

“Miss Havisham kindly inquired,” I replied, “whether she might assist me, and I declined.”

“One ought to understand one’s own affairs,” Mr. Jaggers stated. I noticed Wemmick’s mouth shape the phrase “portable property.”

“Had I been in your position, I would not have refused her,” Mr. Jaggers added; “but every individual should best comprehend his own business.”

“Every person’s business,” Wemmick remarked, with a hint of reproach toward me, “is portable property.”

Believing the moment arrived to address the subject weighing on me, I turned to Mr. Jaggers:—

“I did request something of Miss Havisham, sir. I asked for details regarding her adopted daughter, and she shared everything she knew.”

“Indeed?” Mr. Jaggers said, leaning down to inspect his footwear before standing erect. “Hah! I doubt I would have acted similarly, had I been Miss Havisham. But she ought to best understand her own affairs.”

“I possess more knowledge regarding the background of Miss Havisham’s adopted child than she does, sir. I am acquainted with her mother.”

Mr. Jaggers regarded me with curiosity, echoing, “Mother?”

“I have encountered her mother within the last three days.”

“Indeed?” Mr. Jaggers said.

“And so have you, sir. You have seen her even more recently.”

“Indeed?” Mr. Jaggers said.

“Perhaps I am better informed about Estella’s history than you are,” I continued. “I know her father as well.”

A subtle pause in Mr. Jaggers’s demeanor—he was too composed to alter his behavior, yet he could not prevent a momentary, indefinable stillness—convinced me he was unaware of her father’s identity. I had strongly suspected this based on Provis’s narrative (as relayed by Herbert) regarding his desire for anonymity; I connected this to the fact that he only became Mr. Jaggers’s client four years later, when he had no motive to reveal his identity. I had been uncertain of Mr. Jaggers’s ignorance previously, but I was certain now.

“So! You are acquainted with the young lady’s father, Pip?” Mr. Jaggers asked.

“Yes,” I answered, “and his name is Provis—arriving from New South Wales.”

Even Mr. Jaggers flinched at those words. It was the most minute start a man could exhibit, carefully suppressed and instantly controlled, yet he did flinch, though he masked it by drawing out his handkerchief. I cannot say how Wemmick reacted to the news; I feared looking at him then, lest Mr."
}

“Miss Havisham was kind enough to ask me,” I replied, “whether she could do anything for me, and I told her no.”

“Everyone should know their own business,” Mr. Jaggers said. I saw Wemmick’s lips form the words “portable property.”

“I would not have told her no if I were you,” Mr. Jaggers added, “but every man ought to know his own business best.”

“Every man’s business,” Wemmick said, sounding rather reproachful toward me, “is portable property.”

Thinking the time had come to pursue the topic I cared about, I turned to Mr. Jaggers and said, “I did ask something of Miss Havisham, however, sir. I asked her for information regarding her adopted daughter, and she gave me everything she knew.”

“Did she?” Mr. Jaggers asked, leaning forward to look at his boots before straightening up. “Hah! I don’t think I would have done that if I were Miss Havisham. But she ought to know her own business best.”

“I know more about the history of Miss Havisham’s adopted child than Miss Havisham herself does, sir. I know her mother.”

Mr. Jaggers looked at me inquisitively and repeated, “Mother?”

“I have seen her mother within the last three days.”

“Yes?” said Mr. Jaggers.

“And so have you, sir. You have seen her even more recently.”

“Yes?” said Mr. Jaggers.

“Perhaps I know more about Estella’s history than even you do,” I said. “I know her father, too.”

A certain pause in Mr. Jaggers’s demeanor—he was too composed to change his manner, but he could not prevent it from coming to an indefinably attentive stop—assured me that he did not know who her father was. I had strongly suspected this from Provis’s account (as Herbert had relayed it) of having kept himself hidden; I connected this to the fact that he did not become Mr. Jaggers’s client until four years later, when he would have had no reason to claim his identity. I could not be certain of Mr. Jaggers’s ignorance before, but I was sure of it now.

“So! You know the young lady’s father, Pip?” Mr. Jaggers asked.

“Yes,” I replied, “and his name is Provis—from New South Wales.”

Even Mr. Jaggers started when I said those words. It was the slightest start a man could make, carefully repressed and quickly checked, but he did flinch, though he masked it by pulling out his handkerchief. “Yes?” said Mr. Jaggers.

“And so have you, sir. You have seen her even more recently.”

“Yes?” said Mr. Jaggers.

“Perhaps I know more about Estella’s history than even you do,” I said. “I know her father, too.”

A certain pause in Mr. Jaggers’s demeanor—he was too composed to change his manner, but he could not prevent it from coming to an indefinably attentive stop—assured me that he did not know who her father was. I had strongly suspected this from Provis’s account (as Herbert had relayed it) of having kept himself hidden; I connected this to the fact that he did not become Mr. Jaggers’s client until four years later, when he would have had no reason to claim his identity. I could not be certain of Mr. Jaggers’s ignorance before, but I was sure of it now.

“So! You know the young lady’s father, Pip?” Mr. Jaggers asked.

“Yes,” I replied, “and his name is Provis—from New South Wales.”

Even Mr. Jaggers started when I said those words. It was the slightest start a man could make, carefully repressed and quickly checked, but he did flinch, though he masked it by pulling out his handkerchief. I cannot say how Wemmick reacted to the announcement, as I was afraid to look at him just then, for fear that Mr. Jaggers might notice."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_027",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Following a period of unconsciousness, I discovered myself lying free upon the floor in the same location, resting my head upon someone’s lap. My gaze was locked onto the ladder leaning against the wall the moment I regained my senses, and through this, I realized I remained exactly where I had lost my awareness. 

Initially too listless to turn and identify my benefactor, I simply stared at the ladder until a face obscured my view. It was the face of Trabb’s boy! 

“I believe he is recovering,” Trabb’s boy remarked in a serious tone, “but he certainly looks pale!” 

Upon hearing this, the person holding me leaned over, and I recognized my rescuer as— 

“Herbert! Good heavens!” 

“Easy,” Herbert cautioned. “Gently, Handel. Do not be so impulsive.” 

“And our old friend, Startop!” I exclaimed as he also leaned down toward me. 

“Keep in mind the task he is helping us with,” Herbert reminded me, “and remain composed.” 

This mention caused me to leap up, though I collapsed again due to the agony in my arm. “The time has not passed, has it, Herbert? What night is this? How long have I been here?” I was gripped by a powerful, unsettling fear that I had been lying there for a long time—a day and a night, perhaps two days and nights, or even longer. 

“The time has not passed. It is still Monday night.” 

“Thank God!” 

“And you have all day Tuesday to recover,” Herbert added. “But you cannot help but groan, my dear Handel. What injury have you sustained? Are you able to stand?” 

“Yes, yes,” I replied, “I can walk. My only injury is this throbbing arm.” 

They uncovered it and did what they could. It was severely swollen and inflamed, and I could barely tolerate them touching it. Nevertheless, they tore their handkerchiefs into strips to create new bandages and carefully reset it in the sling until we could reach the town to acquire a cooling ointment. Shortly after, we locked the door of the dark, vacant sluice-house and walked through the quarry on our return journey. Trabb’s boy—now a grown young man—led the way with a lantern, which provided the light I had noticed entering the doorway. However, the moon had climbed two hours higher since I last viewed the sky, and the night, despite the rain, was significantly brighter. The white mist from the kiln drifted away as we passed, and just as I had offered a prayer earlier, I now offered a prayer of gratitude. 

I begged Herbert to explain how he had arrived to save me—a request he initially refused, insisting I stay quiet—but I eventually learned that I had dropped the open letter in our rooms in my haste. He had found it shortly after my departure while returning home with Startop, whom he had encountered in the street while traveling to find me. The letter’s contents unsettled him, particularly because they contradicted the hurried note I had left behind for him."
}

Too indifferent at first to even look around and see who was supporting me, I lay staring at the ladder until a face appeared between me and it. It was Trabb’s boy!

“I think he’s okay,” Trabb’s boy said in a serious voice, “but man, is he pale!”

At these words, the face of the person supporting me leaned over to look at mine, and I saw that it was—

“Herbert! Good heavens!”

“Easy,” Herbert said. “Gently, Handel. Don’t get too worked up.”

“And our old friend, Startop!” I cried, as he also leaned over me.

“Remember what he’s going to help us with,” Herbert said, “and stay calm.”

That mention made me jump up, though I collapsed again from the pain in my arm. “The time hasn’t passed, has it, Herbert? What night is it? How long have I been here?” I had a strange, intense fear that I had been lying there for a long time—a day and a night, or perhaps two days and nights, or even longer.

“The time hasn’t passed. It’s still Monday night.”

“Thank God!”

“And you have all day tomorrow, Tuesday, to rest,” Herbert said. “But you can’t help groaning, my dear Handel. What injury do you have? Can you stand?”

“Yes, yes,” I said, “I can walk. I have no injury except for this throbbing arm.”

They uncovered it and did what they could. It was severely swollen and inflamed, and I could barely stand to have it touched. Still, they tore up their handkerchiefs to make fresh bandages and carefully put it back in the sling until we could reach town and get some cooling lotion for it. Before long, we had shut the door of the dark, empty sluice-house and were walking through the quarry on our way back. Trabb’s boy—now a grown young man—walked ahead of us with a lantern, which was the light I had seen enter the doorway. The moon was a good two hours higher than when I had last seen the sky, and the night, though rainy, was much brighter. The white steam from the kiln drifted away from us as we passed, and just as I had offered a prayer before, I now offered a prayer of thanks.

I begged Herbert to tell me how he had come to my rescue—which he had initially refused to do, insisting I stay quiet—and I learned that I had dropped the open letter in our rooms in my haste. He had found it shortly after I left, when he returned home with Startop, whom he had met in the street on his way to find me. The tone of the letter made him uneasy, especially because it contradicted the hurried note I had left for him."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_028",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Getting up quietly, as my ward was soundly slumbering, I gazed through the window. It overlooked the path where we had pulled our vessel ashore, and, once my vision adjusted to the dim, overcast moonlight, I noticed a pair of men examining it. They walked beneath the window, ignoring everything else, and bypassed the landing area, which I could see was deserted, instead heading across the marsh toward the Nore.

My initial instinct was to wake Herbert and point out the departing men. However, realizing before reaching his room—which was situated at the rear of the building next to mine—that he and Startop had endured a more strenuous day than I and were exhausted, I refrained. Returning to my window, I watched the two figures traversing the marsh. In that gloom, though, they soon vanished, and, feeling quite chilled, I lay down to ponder the situation and drifted off once more.

We rose early. While the four of us paced about before our morning meal, I decided it was proper to describe what I had witnessed. Once more, our charge appeared the least concerned of the group. He remarked calmly that the men were likely from the Custom House and had no interest in us. I attempted to convince myself of this, as it was certainly plausible. Nevertheless, I suggested that he and I depart together toward a distant landmark we could spot, and that the boat should collect us there, or as close as possible, around midday. Deeming this a wise safety measure, he and I departed shortly after breakfast, leaving the tavern without a word.

He puffed on his pipe as we traveled, occasionally pausing to pat my shoulder. One might have assumed I was the one in peril rather than him, and that he was the one offering comfort. We exchanged very few words. As we neared the location, I asked him to stay in a protected spot while I scouted ahead, since that was the direction the men had traveled during the night. He agreed, and I proceeded by myself. There was no vessel off the point, nor any pulled up nearby, nor any indication that the men had departed from there. Of course, the tide was high, and any tracks might have been submerged.

When he peered from his hiding spot in the distance and noticed me signaling with my hat to approach, he met me, and we waited there; sometimes reclining on the embankment, bundled in our jackets, and sometimes pacing to keep warm, until we spotted our boat approaching. We boarded without difficulty and rowed into the steamer's path. By then, it was ten minutes before one, and we began watching for its smoke.

Yet, it was half-past one before the smoke appeared, and shortly thereafter we observed the smoke of a second steamer trailing it. As they approached at maximum speed, we prepared our two bags and took the moment to bid farewell to Herbert and Startop."
}

However, before reaching his room, which was at the back of the house next to mine, I remembered that he and Startop had worked harder than I had that day and were exhausted, so I decided against it. Returning to my window, I watched the two men moving across the marsh. In that light, however, I soon lost sight of them, and feeling quite cold, I lay down to think about the situation and fell back asleep.

We were up early. As the four of us paced back and forth before breakfast, I felt it was right to describe what I had seen. Once again, the man we were protecting was the least worried of the group. He said calmly that the men likely worked for the Customs office and had no interest in us. I tried to convince myself that was the case—which, in truth, was very possible. Still, I suggested that he and I walk to a distant point we could see, and that the boat should pick us up there, or as close as possible, around noon. Since this seemed like a sensible precaution, we left shortly after breakfast without saying anything at the tavern.

He smoked his pipe as we walked, occasionally stopping to pat me on the shoulder. One might have thought I was the one in danger, not him, and that he was the one offering reassurance. We spoke very little. As we neared the point, I asked him to stay in a sheltered spot while I went ahead to scout, since that was the direction the men had taken during the night. He agreed, and I went on alone. There was no boat off the point, nor any boat pulled up nearby, nor were there any signs that the men had left from there. Of course, the tide was high, so there might have been footprints hidden underwater.

When he looked out from his hiding spot in the distance and saw me wave my hat for him to join me, he came over, and we waited there. We spent the time lying on the bank wrapped in our coats or moving around to stay warm, until we saw our boat approaching. We boarded easily and rowed out into the steamer's path. By then, it was ten minutes to one, and we began watching for its smoke.

However, it was half-past one before we spotted the smoke, and shortly after, we saw the smoke of another steamer behind it. As they were approaching at full speed, we prepared our two bags and took the chance to say goodbye to Herbert and Startop."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_029",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "With a final, weak exertion that only succeeded because I surrendered to it and helped him, he lifted my hand to his mouth. Afterward, he softly allowed it to rest upon his chest once more, covering it with his own hands. The calm gaze directed at the pale ceiling returned, then faded, and his head sank peacefully onto his chest.

Recalling what we had studied together, I remembered the two men who entered the Temple to pray, and I realized there were no finer words I could utter at his bedside than, “O Lord, be merciful to him a sinner!”

Chapter LVII.

Finding myself entirely alone, I announced my plan to vacate the Temple chambers as soon as my lease could be legally terminated, intending to sublet them in the interim. I immediately posted notices in the windows; I was burdened by debt, possessed almost no funds, and had begun to feel genuine panic regarding my financial situation. In truth, I should have been alarmed, had I possessed the vigor and focus to grasp any reality beyond the fact that I was becoming quite sick. The recent strain had allowed me to delay my illness, but not to avoid it; I understood it was overtaking me now, though I knew little else and felt indifferent even to that.

For a couple of days, I remained on the sofa or the floor—wherever I happened to collapse—suffering from a heavy head, aching limbs, and a total lack of purpose or strength. Then came a night that seemed to last an eternity, filled with dread and terror; when I attempted to rise in bed the next morning to reflect upon it, I discovered I was unable to do so.

Whether I had truly been down in Garden Court in the middle of the night, searching for a boat I imagined was there; whether I had awakened on the stairs several times in a panic, unaware of how I had left my bed; whether I had found myself igniting the lamp, convinced he was ascending the stairs and that the flames had been extinguished; whether I had been tormented by the frantic speech, laughter, and moaning of someone, and had half-suspected those noises were my own; whether there had been a sealed iron furnace in a shadowed corner of the room, and a voice had repeatedly cried out that Miss Havisham was burning inside—these were matters I attempted to resolve and organize as I lay in bed that morning. Yet, the haze of a limekiln drifted between me and these thoughts, confusing them all, and it was through that mist that I finally perceived two men observing me.

“What do you want?” I inquired, startled; “I do not recognize you.”

“Well, sir,” one of them replied, leaning forward to touch my shoulder, “this is a situation you will likely resolve quickly, I imagine, but you are under arrest.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_GREAT_EXPECTATIONS_030",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Yet, it felt all the more agreeable to shift my focus toward Biddy and Joe, whose immense patience appeared even more luminous than previously, especially when set against that shameless imposter. I approached them at a leisurely pace, as my body felt frail, yet I experienced a growing sense of comfort as I neared them, feeling as though I were putting vanity and deceit further and further into the past.

The June climate was delightful. The heavens were azure, larks ascended high above the verdant grain, and I perceived the entire rural landscape as far more lovely and serene than I had ever previously considered it. Numerous pleasant visions of the existence I would pursue there, and of the positive transformation my personality would undergo once I possessed a guiding influence by my side—someone whose uncomplicated belief and transparent domestic sagacity I had verified—charmed my journey. These thoughts stirred a gentle sentiment within me; for my spirit was mellowed by my homecoming, and such a transformation had occurred that I felt akin to a traveler trudging home unshod from a far-off journey, whose travels had spanned many years.

I had never viewed the schoolhouse where Biddy served as mistress; however, the small, winding path I took to enter the village, chosen for its tranquility, led me past it. I felt let down to discover that it was a day of rest; no pupils were present, and Biddy’s residence was locked. A hopeful idea of observing her, occupied with her routine tasks before she noticed me, had occupied my thoughts but was now thwarted.

However, the forge was only a brief distance away, and I walked toward it beneath the fragrant green linden trees, straining to hear the ringing of Joe’s mallet. Long after I should have detected it, and long after I had imagined I heard it only to realize it was merely a delusion, everything remained silent. The lindens were present, as were the white hawthorns and the chestnut trees, and their foliage rustled in unison whenever I paused to attend; yet, the metallic ring of Joe’s mallet was absent from the midsummer breeze.

Almost dreading, without understanding the cause, to catch sight of the forge, I finally beheld it and noted that it was shuttered. No glow of embers, no brilliant spray of sparks, no rumbling of the bellows; everything was sealed and quiet.

Yet the dwelling was not abandoned, and the primary parlor appeared to be occupied, as white draperies swayed in its casement, and the window stood open, adorned with blossoms. I crept quietly toward it, intending to glance over the flowers, when Joe and Biddy appeared before me, linked arm in arm.

Initially, Biddy let out a shout, as if she believed I were a ghost, but a second later she was within my arms. I sobbed to behold her, and she sobbed to behold me; I, because she appeared so vibrant and delightful; she, because I appeared so haggard and pale.

“But dear Biddy, how elegant you look!”

“Indeed, dear Pip.”

“And Joe, how elegant you look!”

“Indeed, dear old Pip, old friend.”

I gazed at both of them, shifting from one to the other, and then—

“It is my wedding day!” Biddy exclaimed, in a surge of joy, “and I am wedded to Joe!”

They had guided me into the kitchen, and I had rested my head upon the weathered pine table."
}

I crept quietly toward it, intending to glance over the flowers, when Joe and Biddy appeared before me, linked arm in arm.

Initially, Biddy let out a shout, as if she believed I were a ghost, but a second later she was within my arms. I sobbed to behold her, and she sobbed to behold me; I, because she appeared so vibrant and delightful; she, because I appeared so haggard and pale.

“But dear Biddy, how elegant you look!”

“Indeed, dear Pip.”

“And Joe, how elegant you look!”

“Indeed, dear old Pip, old friend.”

I gazed at both of them, shifting from one to the other, and then—

“It is my wedding day!” Biddy exclaimed, in a surge of joy, “and I am wedded to Joe!”

They had guided me into the kitchen, and I had rested my head upon the weathered pine table."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_001",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Pshaw! Rubbish!” remarked the man wearing the white waistcoat. “He would be a bargain even without a fee. Accept him, you foolish man! He is the perfect lad for your needs. He requires a beating occasionally; it will benefit him. Furthermore, his maintenance will not be costly, as he has certainly not been overfed since his birth. Ha! ha! ha!”

Mr. Gamfield glanced slyly at the individuals seated around the table, and, noticing their amusement, slowly began to grin himself. The agreement was reached. Mr. Bumble was immediately directed to take Oliver Twist and his legal papers to the magistrate that same afternoon for official authorization.

Following this decision, young Oliver, to his immense surprise, was freed from his confinement and told to put on a clean shirt. He had barely finished this rare physical exertion when Mr. Bumble personally delivered a bowl of porridge and the special holiday ration of two and a quarter ounces of bread. Faced with this extraordinary display, Oliver started to weep quite mournfully, assuming, not without reason, that the board must have resolved to slaughter him for some practical end, or they would never have started fattening him up in such a manner.

“Do not redden your eyes, Oliver, but consume your meal and show gratitude,” Mr. Bumble stated with grandiloquent gravity. “You are about to become an apprentice, Oliver.”

“An apprentice, sir!” the boy replied, shaking.

“Indeed, Oliver,” Mr. Bumble continued. “Those benevolent gentlemen who act as parents to you when you possess none of your own are going to apprentice you, provide you with a start in life, and turn you into a man, despite the cost to the parish being three pounds ten! Three pounds ten, Oliver! Seventy shillings—one hundred and forty sixpences! And all for a wicked orphan whom no one can love.”

As Mr. Bumble stopped to catch his breath after delivering this speech in a solemn tone, tears streamed down the unfortunate boy’s cheeks, and he sobbed intensely.

“Come now,” Mr. Bumble said, with slightly less arrogance, as it pleased him to witness the impact his rhetoric had made. “Come, Oliver! Dry your eyes with your jacket sleeves and do not weep into your porridge; that is a very silly thing to do, Oliver.” It was indeed foolish, as the dish already contained quite enough water.

While traveling to the magistrate, Mr. Bumble told Oliver that his only task was to appear very cheerful and to state, when the gentleman inquired if he wished to be apprenticed, that he would like it very much. Oliver agreed to follow both instructions, especially since Mr. Bumble offered a subtle warning that if he failed in either regard, there was no predicting what might happen to him. Upon reaching the office, he was locked in a small room alone and told by Mr. Bumble to remain there until he returned to collect him.

The boy stayed there for thirty minutes with a racing heart. At the end of that time, Mr. Bumble poked his head inside, lacking his cocked hat, and announced: “Now, Oliver, my dear, come to the gentleman.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_001",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": ""Nonsense!" said the gentleman in the white waistcoat. "He would be a bargain even without a fee. Take him, you fool! He is exactly the boy you need. He needs a beating now and then; it will do him good. His food won't cost much, either, since he hasn't been overfed since he was born. Ha! ha! ha!"

Mr. Gamfield glanced slyly at the faces around the table, and seeing them all smiling, he eventually began to smile himself. The deal was struck. Mr. Bumble was immediately instructed to take Oliver Twist and his legal papers to the magistrate that very afternoon for signature and approval.

Following this decision, little Oliver, to his great surprise, was released from confinement and told to put on a clean shirt. He had barely managed this unusual task when Mr. Bumble brought him, with his own hands, a bowl of gruel and a holiday ration of two and a quarter ounces of bread. At this incredible sight, Oliver began to cry pitifully, thinking—not unreasonably—that the board must have decided to kill him for some practical purpose, or they never would have started fattening him up like that.

"Don't make your eyes red, Oliver, just eat your food and be grateful," Mr. Bumble said with impressive pomposity. "You are going to be made an apprentice, Oliver."

"An apprentice, sir?" the child asked, trembling.

"Yes, Oliver," said Mr. Bumble. "The kind and blessed gentlemen who act as parents to you when you have none of your own are going to apprentice you, set you up in life, and make a man of you, even though it costs the parish three pounds ten! Three pounds ten, Oliver! Seventy shillings! One hundred and forty sixpences! And all for a naughty orphan that nobody can love."

As Mr. Bumble paused to catch his breath after delivering this speech in a stern voice, tears rolled down the poor child's face, and he sobbed bitterly.

"Come now," said Mr. Bumble, sounding slightly less pompous, as it was gratifying to see the effect his eloquence had produced. "Come, Oliver! Wipe your eyes with your jacket cuffs and don't cry into your gruel; that is a very foolish thing to do, Oliver." It certainly was, as there was already plenty of water in it.

On their way to the magistrate, Mr. Bumble instructed Oliver that all he had to do was look very happy and say, when the gentleman asked if he wanted to be apprenticed, that he would like it very much. Oliver promised to obey both instructions, especially since Mr. Bumble hinted that if he failed in either, there was no telling what might be done to him. When they arrived at the office, he was locked in a small room by himself and told by Mr. Bumble to stay there until he came back to get him.

The boy remained there, heart pounding, for half an hour. At the end of that time, Mr. Bumble poked his head in, without his cocked hat, and said loudly, "Now, Oliver, my dear, come to the gentleman.""
}

Mr. Gamfield looked slyly at the faces around the table, and seeing them all smiling, he began to smile as well. The deal was struck. Mr. Bumble was immediately told that Oliver Twist and his legal papers were to be taken to the magistrate for approval that very afternoon.

Following this decision, little Oliver was, to his great surprise, released from his confinement and told to put on a clean shirt. He had barely finished this unusual task when Mr. Bumble brought him a bowl of thin porridge and his holiday portion of two and a quarter ounces of bread. Seeing this, Oliver began to cry pitifully, thinking—not unreasonably—that the board must have decided to kill him for some purpose, or they would never have started fattening him up like that.

Seventy shillings—one hundred and forty sixpences! And all for a naughty orphan that nobody can love.”

As Mr. Bumble paused to catch his breath after this dramatic speech, tears rolled down the poor child’s face, and he sobbed bitterly.

“Come,” said Mr. Bumble, in a slightly less pompous tone, as he was pleased by the effect his words had on the boy. “Come, Oliver! Wipe your eyes with your jacket cuffs and don’t cry into your porridge; that is very foolish.” It certainly was, as the porridge was already watery enough.

On the way to the magistrate, Mr. Bumble told Oliver that he only needed to look very happy and say that he would like to be an apprentice when the gentleman asked him. When they arrived at the office, he was locked in a small room by himself and told by Mr. Bumble to stay there until he returned.

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_002",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The air felt corrupted by the scent of funeral caskets. The small alcove under the counter where his thin mattress was shoved resembled a burial vault.

These were not the only gloomy sensations weighing upon Oliver. He found himself solitary in an unfamiliar environment; and everyone understands how cold and abandoned even the strongest among us can feel in such circumstances. The lad possessed no companions to cherish, nor any to cherish him. No recent parting left a fresh sting in his memory; the lack of any beloved and familiar countenance pressed down upon his spirit.

Yet his heart remained burdened, nonetheless; and as he crawled into his cramped bed, he wished it were his coffin, and that he might be laid to rest in a peaceful, eternal slumber within the churchyard, with the long grass swaying softly above him, and the tolling of the ancient, resonant bell to lull him in his sleep.

Oliver was roused at dawn by a violent pounding against the shop-door: which, before he could scramble into his garments, was repeated with irritable force about twenty-five times. As he started to release the chain, the kicking ceased, and a voice spoke.

“Unlock the door, won’t you?” shouted the voice belonging to the feet that had struck the door.

“I am doing so immediately, sir,” Oliver answered, sliding back the chain and turning the lock.

“I assume you are the new lad, aren’t you?” the voice asked through the keyhole.

“Yes, sir,” Oliver responded.

“What is your age?” the voice demanded.

“Ten, sir,” Oliver replied.

“Then I shall thrash you once I enter,” the voice declared; “you wait and see if I don’t, that is all, you workhouse brat!” and having delivered this generous vow, the voice began to whistle.

Oliver had been exposed to the action indicated by that expressive term too frequently to harbor any doubt that the speaker, whoever he might be, would fulfill his promise with total integrity. With shaking fingers, he pulled back the bolts and threw open the door.

For a moment or two, Oliver peered up and down the street and across the way, convinced that the stranger who had spoken through the keyhole had stepped a short distance away to keep warm; for he saw no one except a large charity-boy perched on a post outside the building, devouring a piece of bread and butter, which he sliced into mouthfuls with a pocketknife and then ate with impressive skill.

“I apologize, sir,” Oliver said finally, observing that no other caller appeared; “did you knock?”

“I kicked,” the charity-boy answered.

“Were you in need of a coffin, sir?” Oliver asked, naively.

At this, the charity-boy glared with immense ferocity, warning that Oliver would require one soon enough if he dared to jest with his superiors in that manner.

“You have no idea who I am, I suppose, Workhouse?” the charity-boy continued, climbing down from the post with solemn dignity.

“No, sir,” Oliver replied.

“I am Mister Noah Claypole,” the charity-boy announced, “and you are my subordinate. Remove the shutters, you lazy young scoundrel!” With this, Mr."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_003",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Oh, you little wretch!” Charlotte screamed, grabbing Oliver with all her strength, which was roughly equal to that of a moderately fit man. “Oh, you little un-grate-ful, mur-de-rous, hor-rid villain!” With every syllable, Charlotte struck Oliver with all her might, punctuating each blow with a scream for the benefit of anyone listening.

Charlotte’s fist was heavy, but fearing it might not be enough to calm Oliver’s rage, Mrs. Sowerberry rushed into the kitchen to help hold him with one hand while scratching his face with the other. In this advantageous position, Noah stood up from the floor and began pummeling him from behind.

This level of violence could not last long. Once they were exhausted and could no longer beat him, they dragged the struggling, shouting, yet defiant Oliver into the dust-cellar and locked him inside. Once finished, Mrs. Sowerberry collapsed into a chair and burst into tears.

“Bless her, she’s fainting!” Charlotte said. “Noah, dear, get a glass of water. Hurry!”

“Oh! Charlotte,” Mrs. Sowerberry said, speaking as best she could while gasping for air and dripping with the cold water Noah had poured over her head and shoulders. “Oh! Charlotte, it’s a miracle we weren’t all murdered in our beds!”

“Yes, a miracle indeed, ma’am,” Charlotte replied. “I just hope this teaches the master not to bring home any more of these dreadful creatures, born to be murderers and thieves from the cradle. Poor Noah! He was nearly killed, ma’am, when I arrived.”

“Poor boy!” Mrs. Sowerberry said, looking sympathetically at the charity-boy.

Noah, whose top waistcoat button was roughly level with the top of Oliver’s head, rubbed his eyes with his wrists while receiving this sympathy, putting on a convincing display of tears and sniffing.

“What are we going to do!” Mrs. Sowerberry exclaimed. “Your master isn’t home; there isn’t a man in the house, and he’ll kick that door down in ten minutes.” Oliver’s powerful kicks against the wood made this outcome seem very likely.

“Oh dear, I don’t know, ma’am,” Charlotte said, “unless we call the police.”

“Or the military,” Mr. Claypole suggested.

“No, no,” Mrs. Sowerberry said, remembering Oliver’s old acquaintance. “Run to Mr. Bumble, Noah, and tell him to come here immediately without wasting a second; never mind your cap! Hurry! You can hold a knife to that black eye while you run. It will keep the swelling down.”

Noah didn’t stop to reply but took off at full speed. Passersby were astonished to see a charity-boy sprinting through the streets, hatless, holding a pocketknife to his eye.

CHAPTER VII.
OLIVER REMAINS DEFIANT

Noah Claypole ran through the streets at top speed, not stopping for breath until he reached the workhouse gate. After resting for a minute to gather a good supply of sobs and an impressive display of tears and terror, he knocked loudly at the gate. He presented such a miserable face to the elderly pauper who opened it that even the old man, who was accustomed to seeing miserable faces, recoiled in shock."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_004",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Goodness, how naive!” the youth cried. “A beak is a magistrate; and when you walk by a magistrate’s command, it is never a direct path, but always an ascent, never to return. Have you never been on the treadmill?”

“What mill?” Oliver asked.

“What mill! Why, the mill—the one that occupies so little space it operates inside a prison; and it functions better when people are short on funds than when they are flush, because then they cannot find laborers. But come,” the boy said; “you need food, and you shall have it. I am low on cash myself—only one shilling and a penny; but, as far as it goes, I will pay. Get on your feet. There! Now then! Move!”

Helping Oliver stand, the youth brought him to a nearby shop, where he bought enough prepared ham and a small loaf, or, as he called it, “a fourpenny loaf!” The ham was kept tidy and protected from dirt by the clever trick of hollowing out the bread and stuffing the meat inside. Carrying the bread, the youth walked into a small tavern and guided Oliver to a back room. There, a pot of beer was ordered by the mysterious boy; and Oliver, eating at his new companion’s suggestion, enjoyed a long, satisfying meal, while the strange boy watched him periodically with intense interest.

“Are you headed to London?” the boy asked once Oliver had finished.

“Yes.”

“Have you a place to stay?”

“No.”

“Any money?”

“No.”

The boy whistled and shoved his hands into his pockets as deep as his oversized sleeves allowed.

“Do you reside in London?” Oliver asked.

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_005",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Stop thief! Stop thief!” That cry possesses a strange power. The shopkeeper abandons his stall, the carter his cart; the butcher drops his tray, the baker his basket, the milkman his bucket, the messenger his packages, the student his marbles, the road-worker his tool, and the youngster his toy. They dash off in a chaotic rush, shouting and screaming, colliding with pedestrians at corners, startling dogs, and alarming chickens, while the noise reverberates through every street and square.

“Stop thief! Stop thief!” Hundreds of voices join the chorus, and the mob grows at every corner. They race forward, splashing through mire and clattering over the stones. Windows are flung open, residents rush out, and the throng surges onward. Even the spectators of a puppet show abandon the performance to join the chase, adding their voices to the clamor of “Stop thief! Stop thief!”

“Stop thief! Stop thief!” There exists a natural human instinct for the chase. One miserable, gasping boy, exhausted and terrified, with sweat pouring down his face, exerts every ounce of strength to outrun his pursuers. As they close the distance, they cheer his failing stamina. “Stop thief!” Yes, stop him for heaven’s sake, if only out of pity!

Caught at last! A well-aimed strike. He falls to the ground, and the crowd swarms around him, shoving to get a better view. “Move back!” “Give him room to breathe!” “Rubbish! He doesn’t deserve it.” “Where is the gentleman?” “He is approaching now.” “Clear a path for the gentleman!” “Is this the lad, sir?” “Yes.”

Oliver lay there, caked in dirt and bleeding from his mouth, gazing frantically at the sea of faces around him, just as the elderly man was pushed into the center by those who had caught the boy.

“Yes,” the gentleman remarked, “I fear this is the boy.”

“Fear!” the crowd whispered. “That is a rich one!”

“Poor lad,” the gentleman added, “he has injured himself.”

“I did that, sir,” a large, clumsy man said, stepping forward, “and I bruised my knuckle against his teeth. I was the one who stopped him, sir.”

He tipped his hat with a smirk, hoping for a reward, but the gentleman looked at him with distaste and glanced about nervously, as if considering flight himself. He might well have tried to escape, sparking another pursuit, had a police officer—typically the final person to reach such scenes—not pushed through the mob and grabbed Oliver by the neck.

“Get up,” the officer commanded harshly.

“It was not me, sir. Truly, it was two other boys,” Oliver pleaded, wringing his hands and scanning the crowd. “They are around here somewhere.”

“Oh no, they are not,” the officer replied. He intended the remark as sarcasm, yet it was accurate, for the Dodger and Charley Bates had already vanished down the nearest alleyway."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_005",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Stop thief! Stop thief!” There is a magic in the sound. The shopkeeper leaves his counter, and the driver his truck; the butcher drops his tray; the baker his basket; the milkman his pail; the delivery boy his packages; the schoolboy his marbles; the construction worker his pickaxe; the child his toy. Away they run, pell-mell, helter-skelter, slap-dash: tearing, yelling, screaming, knocking down pedestrians as they turn the corners, rousing the dogs, and startling the chickens: and streets, squares, and alleys echo with the sound.

“Stop thief! Stop thief!” The cry is taken up by a hundred voices, and the crowd grows at every turn. Away they fly, splashing through the mud, and clattering along the pavement: windows fly open, people run out, the mob surges forward, a whole audience abandons a street performance at the very peak of the show, and, joining the rushing throng, they swell the shout, and lend fresh energy to the cry, “Stop thief! Stop thief!”

“Stop thief! Stop thief!” There is a passion for hunting something deeply implanted in the human heart. One wretched, breathless child, panting with exhaustion; terror in his expression; agony in his eyes; large drops of sweat streaming down his face; strains every nerve to pull ahead of his pursuers; and as they follow his trail, gaining on him every instant, they greet his failing strength with joy. “Stop thief!” Yes, stop him for God’s sake, if only out of mercy!

Stopped at last! A well-placed blow. He is down on the pavement; and the crowd eagerly gathers around him: each new arrival jostling and struggling with the others to catch a glimpse. “Stand aside!” “Give him some air!” “Nonsense! He doesn’t deserve it.” “Where’s the gentleman?” “Here he is, coming down the street.” “Make room there for the gentleman!” “Is this the boy, sir?” “Yes.”

Oliver lay, covered in mud and dust, and bleeding from the mouth, looking wildly around at the mass of faces surrounding him, when the old gentleman was officiously dragged and pushed into the circle by the foremost of the pursuers.

“Yes,” said the gentleman, “I am afraid it is the boy.”

“Afraid!” murmured the crowd. “That’s a good one!”

“Poor fellow!” said the gentleman, “he has hurt himself.”

“I did that, sir,” said a large, clumsy fellow, stepping forward; “and I cut my knuckle pretty badly against his mouth. I stopped him, sir.”

The fellow touched his hat with a grin, expecting a reward for his efforts; but the old gentleman, eyeing him with an expression of dislike, looked anxiously around, as if he were considering running away himself: which it is very possible he might have attempted, and thus have triggered another chase, had not a police officer (who is generally the last person to arrive in such cases) at that moment made his way through the crowd and seized Oliver by the collar.

“Come, get up,” said the man, roughly.

“It wasn’t me, truly, sir. Truly, truly, it was two other boys,” said Oliver, clasping his hands passionately, and looking around. “They are here somewhere.”

“Oh no, they aren’t,” said the officer. He meant this to be sarcastic, but it was true as well; for the Dodger and Charley Bates had slipped away down the first convenient alley they came to."
}

}

parts.text:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_005",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "“Stop thief! Stop thief!” There is magic in that cry. The shopkeeper leaves his counter, the driver his wagon; the butcher drops his tray, the baker his basket, the milkman his pail, the errand-boy his packages, the schoolboy his marbles, the road-worker his pickaxe, and the child his toy. They all run pell-mell, helter-skelter, and slap-dash. They tear along, yelling and screaming, knocking down pedestrians at corners, waking up dogs, and startling chickens. Streets, squares, and courtyards echo with the noise.

“Stop thief! Stop thief!” A hundred voices take up the cry, and the crowd grows at every turn. They fly along, splashing through mud and clattering on the pavement. Windows fly open, people run out, and the mob surges forward. Even the audience watching a puppet show abandons the performance at its peak to join the rushing crowd, adding their voices to the shout: “Stop thief! Stop thief!”

“Stop thief! Stop thief!” There is a deep-seated human passion for hunting something. One miserable, breathless child, panting with exhaustion, terror in his expression, agony in his eyes, and sweat streaming down his face, strains every muscle to stay ahead of his pursuers. As they follow his trail and gain on him every second, they cheer his fading strength. “Stop thief!” Yes, stop him for God’s sake, if only out of mercy!

Stopped at last! A well-placed blow. He falls to the pavement, and the crowd eagerly gathers around him, each new arrival jostling and struggling to get a look. “Stand back!” “Give him some air!” “Nonsense! He doesn’t deserve it.” “Where is the gentleman?” “Here he comes down the street.” “Make room for the gentleman!” “Is this the boy, sir?” “Yes.”

Oliver lay covered in mud and dust, bleeding from the mouth, looking wildly at the sea of faces surrounding him, just as the old gentleman was pushed and pulled into the circle by the leaders of the chase.

“Yes,” the gentleman said, “I am afraid it is the boy.”

“Afraid!” the crowd muttered. “That’s a good one!”

“Poor fellow,” the gentleman said, “he has hurt himself.”

“I did that, sir,” said a large, clumsy fellow, stepping forward. “I cut my knuckle on his mouth. I stopped him, sir.”

The man touched his hat with a grin, expecting a reward. However, the old gentleman looked at him with disgust and glanced around anxiously, as if he were thinking of running away himself. He might have tried, which would have started another chase, had a police officer—who is usually the last person to arrive in such situations—not pushed through the crowd and grabbed Oliver by the collar.

“Come on, get up,” the officer said roughly.

“It wasn’t me, sir, truly. It was two other boys,” Oliver said, clasping his hands desperately and looking around. “They are here somewhere.”

“Oh no, they aren’t,” the officer said. He meant it sarcastically, but it was true; the Dodger and Charley Bates had slipped away down the first alley they found."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_006",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Slowly, he drifted into that profound, quiet slumber that only relief from recent agony can provide; that serene and tranquil repose from which it is distressing to be stirred. If this were death, who would wish to be awakened again to all the conflicts and chaos of existence; to all its present burdens, its future worries, and, above all, its exhausting memories of what has passed?

It had been broad daylight for hours when Oliver unsealed his eyes; he felt lighthearted and content. The turning point of his illness had been successfully navigated. He was once more a part of the living world.

Within three days, he was capable of sitting in an armchair, supported by cushions; and, as he remained too feeble to move about, Mrs. Bedwin had him transported downstairs to the small housekeeper’s room that was her domain. Once he was settled by the hearth, the kind elderly woman took her own seat; and, feeling quite overjoyed to see him so much improved, she immediately began to weep quite copiously.

“Pay no attention to me, my dear,” the old woman remarked; “I am simply having a good, thorough cry. There; it is finished now; and I feel perfectly fine.”

“You are extremely kind to me, ma’am,” Oliver replied.

“Well, do not concern yourself with that, my dear,” the old woman said; “that has no bearing on your broth; and it is high time you consumed it; for the physician says Mr. Brownlow might visit you this morning; and we must ensure you look your best, because the better you appear, the more delighted he will be.” With this, the old woman set about heating a basin of broth in a small saucepan—a concoction so potent, Oliver mused, that it could provide a substantial meal for three hundred and fifty paupers, if diluted to the standard strength, by the most conservative estimate.

“Do you enjoy paintings, dear?” the old woman asked, noticing that Oliver had fastened his gaze intently upon a portrait hanging on the wall directly across from his chair.

“I am not entirely certain, ma’am,” Oliver answered, never shifting his eyes from the canvas; “I have viewed so few that I can barely say. What a lovely, gentle face that lady possesses!”

“Oh!” the old woman exclaimed, “artists always render women more attractive than they truly are, or they would never secure any business, child. The individual who created the device for capturing likenesses should have realized that would never catch on; it is far too truthful. Far too truthful,” the old woman added, chuckling warmly at her own cleverness.

“Is—is that a portrait, ma’am?” Oliver inquired.

“Indeed,” the old woman said, glancing up briefly from the broth; “that is a painting.”

“Of whom, ma’am?” Oliver asked.

“Well, truly, my dear, I cannot say,” the old woman replied in a cheerful tone. “It is not a likeness of anyone you or I would recognize, I suspect. It seems to capture your interest, dear.”

“It is so beautiful,” Oliver responded.

“Surely you are not frightened by it?” the old woman asked, observing with great astonishment the expression of reverence with which the boy viewed the image.

“Oh no, no,” Oliver answered hastily; “but the eyes appear so mournful; and from where I am sitting, they seem to be staring directly at me. It makes my heart race,” Oliver whispered, “as if it were living and wished to speak to me, but was unable to.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_006",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "He gradually drifted into that deep, tranquil sleep that only relief from recent suffering can provide; that calm, peaceful rest from which it is painful to wake. If this were death, who would want to be roused again to all the struggles and turmoil of life; to all its present worries, its anxieties for the future, and, above all, its weary memories of the past?

It had been bright daylight for hours when Oliver opened his eyes; he felt cheerful and happy. The crisis of his illness had safely passed. He belonged to the world once more.

Within three days, he was able to sit in an armchair, well supported by pillows. Since he was still too weak to walk, Mrs. Bedwin had him carried downstairs into her own small housekeeper’s room. After settling him by the fireplace, the kind old lady sat down as well. Overjoyed to see him so much better, she immediately began to cry quite heavily.

“Don’t mind me, my dear,” the old lady said. “I’m just having a good, long cry. There, it’s all over now, and I feel perfectly fine.”

“You are very, very kind to me, ma’am,” Oliver said.

“Well, never mind that, my dear,” the old lady replied. “That has nothing to do with your broth, and it is high time you had it. The doctor says Mr. Brownlow may come in to see you this morning, and we must look our best, because the better we look, the more pleased he will be.” With that, the old lady began warming a basin of broth in a small saucepan—a broth so thick, Oliver thought, that if diluted to the standard strength, it could provide a full dinner for three hundred and fifty paupers, at the very least.

“Do you like pictures, dear?” the old lady asked, noticing that Oliver was staring intently at a portrait hanging on the wall directly across from his chair.

“I’m not quite sure, ma’am,” Oliver said, without taking his eyes off the canvas. “I have seen so few that I hardly know. What a beautiful, gentle face that lady has!”

“Ah!” the old lady said. “Painters always make ladies look prettier than they really are, or they wouldn’t get any business, child. The man who invented the machine for taking photographs should have known that would never catch on; it’s far too honest. Far too honest,” the old lady said, laughing heartily at her own cleverness.

“Is—is that a portrait, ma’am?” Oliver asked.

“Yes,” the old lady said, looking up for a moment from the broth. “That is a portrait.”

“Whose is it, ma’am?” Oliver asked.

“Well, really, my dear, I don’t know,” the old lady answered good-naturedly. “I expect it isn’t a likeness of anyone you or I know. It seems to catch your interest, dear.”

“It is so pretty,” Oliver replied.

“Surely you aren’t afraid of it?” the old lady asked, noticing with great surprise the look of awe with which the child regarded the painting.

“Oh no, no,” Oliver returned quickly. “But the eyes look so sorrowful, and from where I sit, they seem fixed on me. It makes my heart beat,” Oliver added in a low voice, “as if it were alive and wanted to speak to me, but couldn’t.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_007",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Nolly, darling?” Nancy whispered softly; “Nolly?”

The only occupant was a wretched, shoeless offender, arrested for flute-playing. His crime against the public order having been established, Mr. Fang had quite appropriately sentenced him to a month in the House of Correction, adding the witty observation that since he possessed such an abundance of breath, it would be better utilized on the treadmill than in a musical pipe. He remained silent, preoccupied with mourning his confiscated flute, now property of the county. Nancy moved to the adjacent cell and tapped.

“Well!” a weak, thin voice called out.

“Is there a young boy in there?” Nancy asked, accompanied by a preparatory sob.

“No,” the voice answered; “Heaven forbid.”

This was a sixty-five-year-old vagrant, headed to jail for failing to play the flute—or, more accurately, for street begging and lacking a trade. The next cell held another man, bound for the same prison for selling tin pots without a permit, thus earning a living in violation of Stamp-office regulations.

Since neither prisoner answered to the name Oliver or possessed information about him, Nancy approached the gruff official in the striped vest. With heart-rending cries and lamentations—made more convincing by her skillful handling of the door key and small basket—she demanded her beloved brother.

“I don’t have him, my dear,” the elderly man stated.

“Where is he?” Nancy shrieked, appearing distraught.

“The gentleman took him,” the officer answered.

“What gentleman! Oh, merciful heavens! Which gentleman?” Nancy cried.

Responding to her frantic inquiries, the old man told the distressed sister that Oliver had fallen ill in the courtroom and was released after a witness testified that a different boy, still at large, had committed the theft. The prosecutor had taken him, unconscious, to his own home; the only detail the informant could provide was that it was located somewhere in Pentonville, a name he had overheard given to the carriage driver.

Filled with terrible doubt and anxiety, the tormented woman stumbled to the gate, then switched her unsteady gait for a rapid sprint, returning to the Jew’s residence by the most winding and indirect path she could manage.

Bill Sikes, upon hearing the report of her mission, immediately whistled for his white dog, donned his hat, and departed in haste, skipping the formality of bidding the others farewell.

“We must discover his location, my dears; he must be recovered,” the Jew declared, visibly agitated. “Charley, do nothing but lurk about until you return with news of him! Nancy, my dear, I must have him found. I rely on you—on you and the Artful for everything! Wait, wait,” the Jew added, his hand trembling as he unlocked a drawer; “take this money, my dears. I shall close the shop this evening. You will know where to locate me!”"
}

Inside, there was only a miserable, shoeless criminal who had been arrested for playing the flute. His offense against society having been clearly proven, Mr. Fang had quite properly sentenced him to a month in the House of Correction, adding the fitting and amusing remark that since he had so much extra breath, it would be better spent on the treadmill than on a musical instrument. The man did not answer, as he was busy mourning the loss of his flute, which had been confiscated for the county’s use. Nancy moved on to the next cell and knocked.

“Well!” a faint, weak voice cried out.

“Is there a little boy in there?” Nancy asked, letting out a sob.

“No,” the voice replied. “God forbid.”

This was a sixty-five-year-old vagrant headed to prison for not playing the flute—or, in other words, for begging in the streets and having no way to earn a living. In the next cell was another man, headed to the same prison for selling tin saucepans without a license, thereby earning a living in defiance of the Stamp Office.

Since neither of these criminals answered to the name of Oliver or knew anything about him, Nancy went straight to the gruff officer in the striped waistcoat. With piteous wailing and lamentations—made more convincing by her skillful use of a street-door key and a small basket—she demanded to see her dear brother.

“I don’t have him, my dear,” the old man said.

“Where is he?” Nancy screamed, acting frantic.

“The gentleman took him,” the officer replied.

“What gentleman? Oh, heavens! What gentleman?” Nancy exclaimed.

In response to her frantic questions, the old man informed the deeply distressed sister that Oliver had fallen ill in the office and was released after a witness proved the robbery had been committed by another boy who was not in custody. The prosecutor had carried him away, unconscious, to his own home. All the officer knew was that it was somewhere in Pentonville, as he had heard that word mentioned in the instructions given to the coachman.

Filled with dread and uncertainty, the agonized young woman staggered to the gate. Then, switching her faltering walk for a swift run, she returned to the Jew’s house by the most indirect and complicated route she could find.

Bill Sikes had no sooner heard her account of the trip than he hastily called his white dog, put on his hat, and left immediately, without bothering with the formality of saying goodbye.

“We must know where he is, my dears; he must be found,” the Jew said, clearly agitated. “Charley, do nothing but lurk around until you bring back some news of him! Nancy, my dear, I must have him found. I am counting on you—on you and the Artful for everything! Wait, wait,” the Jew added, unlocking a drawer with a shaking hand. “Here is money, my dears. I am closing this shop tonight. You will know where to find me!”"

Inside, there was only a miserable, shoeless criminal who had been arrested for playing the flute. His offense against society having been clearly proven, Mr. Fang had quite properly sentenced him to a month in the House of Correction, adding the fitting and amusing remark that since he had so much extra breath, it would be better spent on the treadmill than on a musical instrument. The man did not answer, as he was busy mourning the loss of his flute, which had been confiscated for the county’s use. Nancy moved on to the next cell and knocked.

“Well!” a faint, weak voice cried out.

“Is there a little boy in there?” Nancy asked, letting out a sob.

“No,” the voice replied. “God forbid.”

This was a sixty-five-year-old vagrant headed to prison for not playing the flute—or, in other words, for begging in the streets and having no way to earn a living. In the next cell was another man, headed to the same prison for selling tin saucepans without a license, thereby earning a living in defiance of the Stamp Office.

Since neither of these criminals answered to the name of Oliver or knew anything about him, Nancy went straight to the gruff officer in the striped waistcoat. With piteous wailing and lamentations—made more convincing by her skillful use of a street-door key and a small basket—she demanded to see her dear brother.

“I don’t have him, my dear,” the old man said.

“Where is he?” Nancy screamed, acting frantic.

“The gentleman took him,” the officer replied.

“What gentleman? Oh, heavens! What gentleman?” Nancy exclaimed.

In response to her frantic questions, the old man informed the deeply distressed sister that Oliver had fallen ill in the office and was released after a witness proved the robbery had been committed by another boy who was not in custody. The prosecutor had carried him away, unconscious, to his own home. All the officer knew was that it was somewhere in Pentonville, as he had heard that word mentioned in the instructions given to the coachman.

Filled with dread and uncertainty, the agonized young woman staggered to the gate. Then, switching her faltering walk for a swift run, she returned to the Jew’s house by the most indirect and complicated route she could find.

Bill Sikes had no sooner heard her account of the trip than he hastily called his white dog, put on his hat, and left immediately, without bothering with the formality of saying goodbye.

“We must know where he is, my dears; he must be found,” the Jew said, clearly agitated. “Charley, do nothing but lurk around until you bring back some news of him! Nancy, my dear, I must have him found. I am counting on you—on you and the Artful for everything! Wait, wait,” the Jew added, unlocking a drawer with a shaking hand. “Here is money, my dears. I am closing this shop tonight. You will know where to find me!”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_008",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Such defiance only enraged Mr. Sikes further; he fell to his knees and began to attack the creature with great intensity. The animal leaped from side to side, snarling, snapping, and barking, while the man lunged, cursed, struck, and swore. The conflict was nearing a decisive moment for one or the other when the door swung open, and the dog bolted out, leaving Bill Sikes holding the clasp-knife and the poker.

An old proverb claims it takes two to make a fight. Finding himself deprived of the dog’s involvement, Mr. Sikes immediately directed his aggression toward the person who had just entered.

“Why the devil are you interfering between me and my dog?” Sikes demanded, gesturing aggressively.

“I was unaware, my dear, I was unaware,” Fagin answered meekly, for it was the Jew who had arrived.

“Unaware, you cowardly thief!” Sikes snarled. “Could you not hear the commotion?”

“Not a single sound, as I am a living man, Bill,” the Jew replied.

“Oh, certainly! You never hear anything, do you?” Sikes retorted with a harsh sneer. “Creeping in and out so that no one notices your arrival or departure! I wish you had been the dog, Fagin, just a moment ago.”

“Why?” the Jew asked, forcing a smile.

“Because the law, which protects the lives of men like you who lack even half the spirit of a mongrel, allows a man to kill a dog however he pleases,” Sikes answered, closing his knife with a meaningful expression; “that is why.”

The Jew rubbed his palms together and, seating himself at the table, pretended to find his friend’s joke amusing. Nevertheless, he was clearly quite uncomfortable.

“Keep grinning,” Sikes said, putting the poker aside and regarding him with brutal disdain; “keep grinning. You will never have the last laugh at me, unless it is from behind a nightcap. I hold the advantage over you, Fagin; and, damn me, I intend to keep it. There! If I go down, you go down; so look after me.”

“Well, well, my dear,” the Jew said, “I am fully aware of that; we—we—share a mutual interest, Bill—a mutual interest.”

“Humph,” Sikes grunted, as if he believed the interest favored the Jew more than himself. “Well, what is it you have to tell me?”

“Everything has passed safely through the melting-pot,” Fagin answered, “and this is your portion. It is somewhat more than it should be, my dear; but since I know you will do me a favor in return, and—”

“Cut the nonsense,” the robber interrupted impatiently. “Where is it? Hand it over!”

“Yes, yes, Bill; give me a moment, give me a moment,” the Jew replied soothingly. “Here it is! All secure!” As he spoke, he pulled an old cotton handkerchief from his chest, untied a large knot in the corner, and revealed a small brown-paper parcel. Sikes snatched it from him, opened it quickly, and began counting the gold coins inside.

“This is the entire amount, is it?” Sikes asked.

“The entire amount,” the Jew replied.

“You have not opened the package and swallowed one or two on your way here, have you?” Sikes asked with suspicion. “Do not put on a look of hurt innocence; you have done it plenty of times before. Ring the bell.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_008",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "This resistance only infuriated Mr. Sikes further; he dropped to his knees and began to attack the animal with renewed fury. The dog leaped from side to side, snapping, growling, and barking, while the man lunged, swore, struck out, and cursed. The struggle was reaching a critical point for one or the other when the door suddenly opened and the dog darted out, leaving Bill Sikes standing there with the poker and the clasp-knife in his hands.

There must always be two parties to a quarrel, as the old saying goes. Mr. Sikes, deprived of the dog’s participation, immediately transferred his aggression to the newcomer.

“What the hell are you doing, getting between me and my dog?” Sikes demanded with a fierce gesture.

“I didn’t know, my dear, I didn’t know,” Fagin replied humbly, for the Jew was the one who had entered.

“Didn’t know, you spineless thief!” Sikes growled. “Couldn’t you hear the noise?”

“Not a sound of it, as I’m a living man, Bill,” the Jew replied.

“Oh no! You never hear anything, do you?” Sikes retorted with a vicious sneer. “Sneaking in and out so that nobody hears how you come or go! I wish you had been the dog, Fagin, half a minute ago.”

“Why?” the Jew inquired with a forced smile.

“Because the government, which cares for the lives of men like you who don’t have half the guts of a stray cur, lets a man kill a dog however he likes,” Sikes replied, closing his knife with a meaningful look. “That’s why.”

The Jew rubbed his hands together and sat down at the table, pretending to laugh at his friend’s joke. He was clearly very uneasy, however.

“Go ahead and grin,” Sikes said, putting the poker away and surveying him with savage contempt. “Grin all you want. You’ll never have the last laugh on me, though, unless it’s behind my back. I’ve got the upper hand over you, Fagin, and damn me, I’m going to keep it. There! If I go down, you go down; so you’d better take care of me.”

“Well, well, my dear,” the Jew said, “I know all that; we—we—have a mutual interest, Bill—a mutual interest.”

“Humph,” Sikes grunted, as if he thought the interest favored the Jew more than himself. “Well, what do you have to say to me?”

“It’s all passed safely through the melting-pot,” Fagin replied, “and this is your share. It’s a bit more than it should be, my dear; but as I know you’ll do me a favor another time, and—”

“Cut the nonsense,” the robber interrupted impatiently. “Where is it? Hand it over!”

“Yes, yes, Bill; give me time, give me time,” the Jew replied soothingly. “Here it is! All safe!” As he spoke, he pulled an old cotton handkerchief from his chest, untied a large knot in one corner, and produced a small brown-paper packet. Sikes snatched it from him, hastily opened it, and began to count the sovereigns inside.

“This is all, is it?” Sikes asked.

“All,” the Jew replied.

“You haven’t opened the parcel and swallowed one or two on your way here, have you?” Sikes asked suspiciously. “Don’t look so offended by the question; you’ve done it plenty of times before. Ring the bell.”"
}
