model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_009",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Bumble, having swapped his cocked hat for a round one and wrapped himself in a blue overcoat with a cape, took his seat on the outside of the coach, accompanied by the paupers whose legal residence was in question; with them, in due time, he arrived in London.

He faced no difficulties on the journey other than those caused by the stubborn behavior of the two paupers, who insisted on shivering and complaining of the cold in a way that Mr. Bumble claimed made his own teeth chatter and left him feeling quite uncomfortable, despite his heavy coat.

Having settled these troublesome individuals for the night, Mr. Bumble sat down at the inn where the coach stopped and enjoyed a modest dinner of steak, oyster sauce, and porter. Placing a glass of hot gin and water on the mantelpiece, he pulled his chair toward the fire and, after various moral reflections on the common sin of discontent and complaining, settled down to read the newspaper.

The very first paragraph that caught Mr. Bumble’s eye was the following advertisement:

“FIVE GUINEAS REWARD

“Whereas a young boy named Oliver Twist ran away, or was lured away, last Thursday evening from his home in Pentonville and has not been heard from since. The above reward will be paid to anyone who provides information leading to the discovery of the said Oliver Twist, or who can shed any light on his background, in which the advertiser is, for many reasons, deeply interested.”

Then followed a full description of Oliver’s clothing, physical appearance, and disappearance, along with Mr. Brownlow’s full name and address.

Mr. Bumble opened his eyes wide, read the advertisement slowly and carefully three times, and in just over five minutes was on his way to Pentonville, having actually left his glass of hot gin and water untouched in his excitement.

“Is Mr. Brownlow at home?” Mr. Bumble asked the girl who opened the door.

To this inquiry, the girl gave the common but evasive reply, “I don’t know; where are you from?”

Mr. Bumble had barely mentioned Oliver’s name to explain his business when Mrs. Bedwin, who had been listening at the parlor door, hurried into the hallway, breathless.

“Come in, come in,” the old lady said. “I knew we would hear about him. Poor dear! I knew we would! I was certain of it. Bless his heart! I said so all along.”

Having said this, the kind old lady hurried back into the parlor and, sitting on a sofa, burst into tears. The girl, who was not quite as emotional, had run upstairs in the meantime and now returned with a request that Mr. Bumble follow her immediately, which he did.

He was shown into the small back study, where Mr. Brownlow and his friend Mr. Grimwig sat with decanters and glasses before them. The latter gentleman immediately exclaimed:

“A beadle. A parish beadle, or I’ll eat my hat.”

“Please don’t interrupt right now,” Mr. Brownlow said. “Take a seat, will you?”

Mr. Bumble sat down, completely bewildered by Mr. Grimwig’s strange manner. Mr. Brownlow moved the lamp to get a clear view of the beadle’s face and said, with a hint of impatience,

“Now, sir, you have come because you saw the advertisement?”"
}

parts.text:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_009",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Bumble, having swapped his cocked hat for a round one and wrapped himself in a blue overcoat with a cape, took his seat on the outside of the coach, accompanied by the paupers whose legal residence was in question; with them, in due time, he arrived in London.

He faced no difficulties on the journey other than those caused by the stubborn behavior of the two paupers, who insisted on shivering and complaining of the cold in a way that Mr. Bumble claimed made his own teeth chatter and left him feeling quite uncomfortable, despite his heavy coat.

Having settled these troublesome individuals for the night, Mr. Bumble sat down at the inn where the coach stopped and enjoyed a modest dinner of steak, oyster sauce, and porter. Placing a glass of hot gin and water on the mantelpiece, he pulled his chair toward the fire and, after various moral reflections on the common sin of discontent and complaining, settled down to read the newspaper.

The very first paragraph that caught Mr. Bumble’s eye was the following advertisement:

“FIVE GUINEAS REWARD

“Whereas a young boy named Oliver Twist ran away, or was lured away, last Thursday evening from his home in Pentonville and has not been heard from since. The above reward will be paid to anyone who provides information leading to the discovery of the said Oliver Twist, or who can shed any light on his background, in which the advertiser is, for many reasons, deeply interested.”

Then followed a full description of Oliver’s clothing, physical appearance, and disappearance, along with Mr. Brownlow’s full name and address.

Mr. Bumble opened his eyes wide, read the advertisement slowly and carefully three times, and in just over five minutes was on his way to Pentonville, having actually left his glass of hot gin and water untouched in his excitement.

“Is Mr. Brownlow at home?” Mr. Bumble asked the girl who opened the door.

To this inquiry, the girl gave the common but evasive reply, “I don’t know; where are you from?”

Mr. Bumble had barely mentioned Oliver’s name to explain his business when Mrs. Bedwin, who had been listening at the parlor door, hurried into the hallway, breathless.

“Come in, come in,” the old lady said. “I knew we would hear about him. Poor dear! I knew we would! I was certain of it. Bless his heart! I said so all along.”

Having said this, the kind old lady hurried back into the parlor and, sitting on a sofa, burst into tears. The girl, who was not quite as emotional, had run upstairs in the meantime and now returned with a request that Mr. Bumble follow her immediately, which he did.

He was shown into the small back study, where Mr. Brownlow and his friend Mr. Grimwig sat with decanters and glasses before them. The latter gentleman immediately exclaimed:

“A beadle. A parish beadle, or I’ll eat my hat.”

“Please don’t interrupt right now,” Mr. Brownlow said. “Take a seat, will you?”

Mr. Bumble sat down, completely bewildered by Mr. Grimwig’s strange manner. Mr. Brownlow moved the lamp to get a clear view of the beadle’s face and said, with a hint of impatience,

“Now, sir, you have come because you saw the advertisement?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_010",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Which one is that, Bill?” the Jew inquired with keen interest.

“Well,” Sikes murmured, “when you traverse the lawn—”

“Yes?” the Jew responded, leaning forward, his eyes nearly bulging from their sockets.

“Hmph!” Sikes grunted, breaking off as the young woman, barely turning her head, glanced sharply around and gestured briefly toward the Jew’s countenance. “Forget which part it is. I realize you cannot manage this without me, but it is wise to exercise caution when conducting business with you.”

“As you wish, my dear, as you wish,” the Jew answered. “Is no assistance required beyond yours and Toby’s?”

“None,” Sikes stated, “except for a centre-bit and a lad. We both possess the tool; you must provide the boy.”

“A boy!” the Jew cried. “Ah! So it involves a panel, does it?”

“Never mind what it involves!” Sikes retorted. “I require a boy, and he must be small. Good heavens!” Mr. Sikes remarked, lost in thought, “if only I had that lad of Ned’s, the chimney-sweep! He kept him stunted intentionally, hiring him out by the task. But the father got transported; then the Juvenile Delinquent Society stepped in, removed the boy from a trade where he was making a living, instructed him in reading and writing, and eventually apprenticed him. And so it continues,” Sikes added, his anger mounting as he recalled his grievances, “they persist; and if they possessed sufficient funds—which, fortunately, they do not—we would not have half a dozen boys remaining in the entire profession within a year or two.”

“We certainly would not,” the Jew agreed, having been deep in thought during this monologue and only hearing the final remark. “Bill!”

“What is it now?” Sikes asked.

The Jew gestured toward Nancy, who remained staring into the flames, signaling that he wanted her sent out of the room. Sikes shrugged his shoulders with annoyance, seemingly finding the measure redundant, yet he obeyed by asking Miss Nancy to bring him a pitcher of ale.

“You do not want any ale,” Nancy stated, crossing her arms and remaining seated quite calmly.

“I tell you that I do!” Sikes insisted.

“Rubbish,” the girl replied coolly. “Continue, Fagin. I am aware of what he intends to say, Bill; he need not worry about me.”

The Jew remained hesitant. Sikes looked between them, somewhat surprised.

“Surely you do not distrust the girl, Fagin?” he finally asked. “You have known her long enough to rely on her, or the Devil is in it. She is not one to talk. Are you, Nancy?”

“I should think not!” the young woman replied, pulling her chair to the table and resting her elbows upon it.

“No, no, my dear, I know you are not,” the Jew said; “but—” and once more the old man stopped.

“But what?” Sikes demanded.

“I was uncertain if she might perhaps be feeling unwell, you understand, my dear, as she was the other evening,” the Jew answered.

At this admission, Miss Nancy erupted into a boisterous laugh; then, downing a glass of brandy, she shook her head defiantly and exclaimed several times, “Keep the game going!” “Never say die!” and similar phrases."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_010",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Which one is that, Bill?” the Jew asked eagerly.

“Well,” Sikes whispered, “as you cross the lawn—”

“Yes?” said the Jew, leaning forward, his eyes nearly popping out of his head.

“Ugh!” Sikes grunted, stopping abruptly as the girl, barely moving her head, glanced around suddenly and pointed briefly at the Jew’s face. “Never mind which part it is. I know you can’t do it without me, but it’s best to be cautious when dealing with you.”

“As you like, my dear, as you like,” the Jew replied. “Is no other help needed besides yours and Toby’s?”

“None,” Sikes said, “except for a drill and a boy. We both have the first; you have to find us the second.”

“A boy!” the Jew exclaimed. “Oh! So it’s a panel, is it?”

“Never mind what it is!” Sikes replied. “I need a boy, and he can’t be a big one. Lord!” Mr. Sikes said reflectively, “if I only had that young boy of Ned’s, the chimney sweep! He kept him small on purpose and rented him out by the job. But the father got arrested, and then the Juvenile Delinquent Society came along and took the boy away from a trade where he was earning money, taught him to read and write, and eventually made an apprentice of him. And that’s how they go on,” Sikes said, his anger rising at the memory of his grievances, “that’s how they go on; and if they had enough money—which, thank goodness, they don’t—we wouldn’t have half a dozen boys left in the whole trade within a year or two.”

“We certainly wouldn’t,” agreed the Jew, who had been thinking during this speech and had only caught the final sentence. “Bill!”

“What now?” Sikes asked.

The Jew nodded toward Nancy, who was still staring at the fire, and signaled that he wanted her told to leave the room. Sikes shrugged his shoulders impatiently, as if he thought the precaution unnecessary, but complied by asking Miss Nancy to fetch him a jug of beer.

“You don’t want any beer,” Nancy said, folding her arms and remaining in her seat quite calmly.

“I’m telling you I do!” Sikes replied.

“Nonsense,” the girl rejoined coolly. “Go on, Fagin. I know what he’s going to say, Bill; he doesn’t need to worry about me.”

The Jew still hesitated. Sikes looked from one to the other in some surprise.

“Why, you aren’t worried about the old girl, are you, Fagin?” he asked eventually. “You’ve known her long enough to trust her, or the devil’s in it. She isn’t one to talk. Are you, Nancy?”

“I should think not!” the young lady replied, drawing her chair up to the table and resting her elbows on it.

“No, no, my dear, I know you aren’t,” the Jew said; “but—” and again the old man paused.

“But what?” Sikes asked.

“I didn’t know whether she might perhaps be feeling out of sorts, you know, my dear, like she was the other night,” the Jew replied.

At this admission, Miss Nancy burst into a loud laugh; and, swallowing a glass of brandy, she shook her head defiantly and broke into various exclamations like “Keep the game going!” “Never say die!” and the like."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_011",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "As they drew near the City, the clamor and congestion grew steadily; by the time they navigated the thoroughfares between Shoreditch and Smithfield, it had intensified into a deafening roar of activity. The morning light was as bright as it would be until dusk, and the frantic start of the day for half of London’s inhabitants was well underway.

Veering through Sun Street and Crown Street, then traversing Finsbury Square, Mr. Sikes navigated via Chiswell Street into Barbican, continuing into Long Lane and finally reaching Smithfield; from this final location emerged a chaotic symphony of clashing noises that left Oliver Twist utterly astounded.

It was the morning of the market. The earth was blanketed, nearly to the ankles, in muck and sludge; a dense vapor, constantly emanating from the steaming hides of the livestock and blending with the mist that seemed to settle upon the chimney-pots, hung oppressively overhead. Every pen in the middle of the expansive area, along with as many makeshift enclosures as could be squeezed into the open ground, was packed with sheep; tethered to posts along the gutter were lengthy rows of cattle and oxen, standing three or four abreast. Rural folk, butchers, livestock drivers, peddlers, youths, pickpockets, loafers, and outcasts of every base sort were jumbled into a single mass; the whistling of drovers, the yapping of dogs, the lowing and thrashing of oxen, the bleating of sheep, the grunting and squealing of swine, the calls of vendors, the yelling, cursing, and brawling on every side; the tolling of bells and the thunder of voices pouring from every tavern; the shoving, jostling, herding, striking, hollering, and screaming; the ghastly and jarring din echoing from every angle of the market; and the unwashed, unshaven, wretched, and filthy individuals constantly darting back and forth, plunging into and out of the mob; created a staggering and disorienting spectacle that completely overwhelmed the senses.

Mr. Sikes, pulling Oliver along, pushed his way through the densest part of the throng, paying scant notice to the myriad sights and sounds that so bewildered the lad. He gave a nod or two to an acquaintance passing by, and, declining several offers to partake in an early drink, pushed forward with determination until they had escaped the chaos and navigated through Hosier Lane into Holborn.

“Now then, youngster!” Sikes remarked, glancing up at the St. Andrew’s Church clock, “it’s nearly seven! You need to pick up the pace. Come on, don’t start dragging your feet, Lazy-legs!”

Mr. Sikes punctuated this remark with a sharp tug on his small companion’s wrist; Oliver, accelerating his gait into a sort of trot between a brisk walk and a run, managed to keep pace with the housebreaker’s long strides as best he could.

They maintained this speed until they had moved past Hyde Park Corner and were headed toward Kensington, at which point Sikes slowed his stride until an unoccupied cart, trailing some distance behind, caught up. Observing “Hounslow” painted on the side, he inquired of the driver, with as much politeness as he could muster, if he might provide them a ride as far as Isleworth.

“Hop on,” the man replied. “Is that your lad?”

“Yes; he’s my boy,” Sikes answered, staring intently at Oliver while absentmindedly sliding his hand into the pocket containing his pistol.

“Your father moves a bit too fast for you, doesn’t he, lad?”"
}

Turning down Sun Street and Crown Street, and crossing Finsbury Square, Mr. Sikes headed through Chiswell Street into Barbican, then into Long Lane, and finally into Smithfield; from this last location rose a chaotic mix of sounds that left Oliver Twist amazed.

It was market morning. The ground was covered nearly ankle-deep in filth and mud; a thick steam, constantly rising from the reeking bodies of the cattle and mixing with the fog that seemed to settle on the chimney-tops, hung heavily overhead. All the pens in the center of the large area, along with as many temporary pens as could be squeezed into the open space, were packed with sheep; tied to posts along the gutter were long lines of cattle and oxen, three or four deep. Countrymen, butchers, drovers, street vendors, boys, thieves, idlers, and vagabonds of every low status were pressed together in a mass; the whistling of drovers, the barking of dogs, the bellowing and plunging of the oxen, the bleating of sheep, the grunting and squeaking of pigs, the cries of vendors, the shouts, curses, and arguments on all sides; the ringing of bells and the roar of voices coming from every pub; the crowding, pushing, driving, beating, whooping, and yelling; the hideous and discordant noise echoing from every corner of the market; and the unwashed, unshaven, squalid, and dirty figures constantly running back and forth, bursting in and out of the throng; all combined to create a stunning and bewildering scene that completely overwhelmed the senses.

Mr. Sikes, dragging Oliver behind him, elbowed his way through the thickest part of the crowd, paying very little attention to the many sights and sounds that so astonished the boy. He nodded two or three times to a passing friend and, resisting several invitations to take a morning drink, pressed steadily forward until they were clear of the chaos and had made their way through Hosier Lane into Holborn.

“Now, kid!” said Sikes, looking up at the clock on St. Andrew’s Church, “it’s almost seven! You need to pick up the pace. Come on, don’t start lagging behind already, Lazy-legs!”

Mr. Sikes accompanied this remark with a jerk at his little companion’s wrist; Oliver, quickening his pace into a trot between a fast walk and a run, kept up with the housebreaker’s rapid strides as best he could.

They kept up this pace until they had passed Hyde Park Corner and were on their way to Kensington, when Sikes slowed down until an empty cart some distance behind caught up. Seeing “Hounslow” written on it, he asked the driver, with as much civility as he could muster, if he would give them a lift as far as Isleworth.

“Jump up,” the man said. “Is that your boy?”

“Yes, he’s my boy,” Sikes replied, looking hard at Oliver and putting his hand absentmindedly into the pocket where his pistol was kept.

“Your father walks a bit too fast for you, doesn’t he, my lad?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_012",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Bumble immediately folded his collar down once more, placed his hat and cane on a chair, and pulled another seat toward the table. As he settled himself, he gazed at the lady, who kept her eyes on the small teapot. Mr. Bumble cleared his throat again and offered a faint smile.

Mrs. Corney stood to retrieve an extra cup and saucer from the cupboard. Upon sitting back down, her gaze met that of the dashing beadle; she blushed and busied herself with preparing his tea. Mr. Bumble cleared his throat once more—this time with greater volume than before.

“Do you take it sweet, Mr. Bumble?” the matron asked, reaching for the sugar bowl.

“Extremely sweet, indeed, ma’am,” Mr. Bumble answered. He stared directly at Mrs. Corney as he spoke; if any beadle ever appeared affectionate, Mr. Bumble certainly did at that instant.

The tea was prepared and served in silence. Mr. Bumble, having laid a handkerchief across his lap to protect his fine breeches from crumbs, started to eat and drink, occasionally punctuating these activities with a heavy sigh. This did not hinder his appetite; rather, it seemed to assist his progress with the tea and toast.

“I notice you keep a cat, ma’am,” Mr. Bumble remarked, looking toward the animal resting before the hearth with her litter, “and kittens as well, I see!”

“I am so attached to them, Mr. Bumble, you have no idea,” the matron replied. “They are so joyful, so playful, and so lively that they serve as true companions to me.”

“They are delightful creatures, ma’am,” Mr. Bumble agreed, “so very domestic.”

“Indeed!” the matron added with fervor; “they are so devoted to their home that it is a genuine pleasure, I assure you.”

“Mrs. Corney, ma’am,” Mr. Bumble stated deliberately, tapping the rhythm with his teaspoon, “I mean to say this: any cat or kitten that could reside with you, ma’am, and not cherish its home, would be a fool, ma’am.”

“Oh, Mr. Bumble!” Mrs. Corney protested.

“There is no point in hiding the truth, ma’am,” Mr. Bumble declared, waving his teaspoon with a romantic gravity that made him appear doubly imposing; “I would drown such a creature myself, with pleasure.”

“Then you are a cruel man,” the matron said playfully, reaching out to take the beadle’s cup, “and a very cold-hearted one as well.”

“Cold-hearted, ma’am?” Mr. Bumble repeated. “Cold?” He surrendered his cup silently, pressed Mrs. Corney’s finger as she accepted it, struck his decorated waistcoat twice with an open hand, heaved a massive sigh, and scooted his chair a tiny distance away from the heat.

The table was circular, and since Mrs. Corney and Mr. Bumble had been seated facing one another with little room between them, both turned toward the fire, it is clear that by moving back from the hearth while remaining at the table, Mr. Bumble widened the gap between himself and Mrs. Corney. Prudent readers might admire this as an act of significant heroism on the beadle’s part, as he was tempted by the circumstances to utter romantic sentiments. While such words might suit the lighthearted, they seem far beneath the dignity of judges, members of parliament, ministers, mayors, and other public officials—and especially beneath the gravity of a beadle, who is known to be the most stern and unyielding of them all."
}

“Hard-hearted, ma’am?” Mr. Bumble asked. “Hard?” He surrendered his cup without another word, squeezed Mrs. Corney’s little finger as she took it, slapped his laced waistcoat twice, let out a massive sigh, and scooted his chair a tiny bit further from the fire.

It was a round table, and since Mrs. Corney and Mr. Bumble had been sitting opposite each other with little space between them, both facing the fire, it is clear that by moving away from the heat while staying at the table, Mr. Bumble increased the distance between himself and Mrs. Corney. Prudent readers will likely admire this as an act of great heroism on Mr. Bumble’s part, as he was tempted by the time, place, and opportunity to utter certain romantic nonsense. While such things might suit the lips of the lighthearted, they seem far beneath the dignity of judges, members of parliament, government ministers, lord mayors, and other high-ranking officials—and especially beneath the gravity of a beadle, who, as everyone knows, should be the sternest and most inflexible of them all."

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_013",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Judging by his manner, Toby was in no rush to begin their discussion. Initially, the Jew simply observed his face, hoping to discern some hint of the news he carried, but he found nothing. He appeared exhausted and ragged, yet his expression maintained that same calm ease it always held; beneath the grime, beard, and whiskers, the smug, self-assured grin of flash Toby Crackit remained unchanged. The Jew, suffering from intense impatience, tracked every bite the man took, pacing the room in a state of uncontrollable agitation. It was futile. Toby kept eating with total outward apathy until he was full; then, sending the Dodger away, he shut the door, prepared a drink of spirits and water, and settled in to speak.

“First of all, Faguey,” Toby began.

“Yes, yes!” the Jew interrupted, pulling his chair closer.

Mr. Crackit paused to sip his drink and remark that the gin was superb; then, propping his feet against the low mantel so his boots were nearly level with his eyes, he calmly continued.

“First of all, Faguey,” the burglar said, “how is Bill?”

“What!” the Jew shrieked, leaping from his chair.

“Wait, you don’t mean to say—” Toby started, his face losing its color.

“Mean!” the Jew shouted, stamping his feet in a rage. “Where are they? Sikes and the lad! Where are they? Where have they been? Where are they concealed? Why haven’t they arrived?”

“The burglary went wrong,” Toby said weakly.

“I am aware of that,” the Jew answered, yanking a newspaper from his coat and gesturing toward it. “What else?”

“They shot and wounded the boy. We fled across the fields behind the house, carrying him between us—in a direct line—through hedges and ditches. They pursued us. Damn it! The entire countryside was alerted, and the hounds were tracking us.”

“The boy!”

“Bill carried him on his back, running like the wind. We paused to share the burden; his head was drooping, and he felt cold. They were right behind us; it was every man for himself to escape the gallows! We split up and abandoned the youngster in a ditch. Whether he is living or dead, that is all I can tell you.”

The Jew waited to hear nothing further; instead, he let out a piercing cry, tangled his fingers in his hair, and bolted from the room and out of the house.

CHAPTER XXVI.
IN WHICH A MYSTERIOUS CHARACTER APPEARS UPON THE SCENE; AND MANY THINGS, INSEPARABLE FROM THIS HISTORY, ARE DONE AND PERFORMED

The old man reached the street corner before he began to overcome the impact of Toby Crackit’s report. He had not slowed his frantic pace; he was still hurrying forward in the same wild, chaotic fashion when the abrupt rush of a carriage and a loud warning from pedestrians, who noticed his peril, forced him back onto the sidewalk. Avoiding the main thoroughfares as much as he could and creeping only through side streets and lanes, he finally reached Snow Hill. Here he moved even more quickly; he did not slow down until he had turned into a court, at which point, as if realizing he was finally in his familiar territory, he resumed his customary shuffling gait and appeared to breathe with more ease."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_013",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "Judging by appearances, Toby was in no rush to start the conversation. At first, the Jew was content to watch his face patiently, hoping to glean some clue about the news he brought, but it was useless.

He looked tired and worn, yet his features held that same complacent calm they always wore. Through the dirt, beard, and whiskers, the self-satisfied smirk of flash Toby Crackit still shone through, unimpaired. The Jew, in an agony of impatience, watched every bite he took, pacing the room in irrepressible excitement. It was all for nothing. Toby continued to eat with complete outward indifference until he could eat no more. Then, ordering the Dodger out, he closed the door, mixed a glass of spirits and water, and settled in to talk.

“First and foremost, Faguey,” Toby said.

“Yes, yes!” the Jew interrupted, pulling his chair closer.

Mr. Crackit paused to take a drink of his spirits and water, declaring the gin excellent. Then, resting his feet against the low mantelpiece so his boots were level with his eyes, he calmly resumed.

“First and foremost, Faguey,” the burglar said, “how’s Bill?”

“What!” the Jew screamed, jumping from his seat.

“Wait, you don’t mean to say—” Toby began, turning pale.

“Mean!” the Jew cried, stamping furiously. “Where are they? Sikes and the boy! Where are they? Where have they been? Where are they hiding? Why haven’t they been here?”

“The job failed,” Toby said faintly.

“I know that,” the Jew replied, pulling a newspaper from his pocket and pointing to it. “What else?”

“They fired and hit the boy. We cut across the fields at the back, carrying him between us—straight as the crow flies—through hedges and ditches. They chased us. Damn it! The whole countryside was awake, and the dogs were on us.”

“The boy!”

“Bill had him on his back and ran like the wind. We stopped to carry him between us; his head hung down, and he was cold. They were right on our heels; it was every man for himself to avoid the gallows! We split up and left the youngster lying in a ditch. Alive or dead, that’s all I know about him.”

The Jew waited to hear no more. Uttering a loud yell and clutching his hair, he rushed from the room and out of the house.

CHAPTER XXVI. IN WHICH A MYSTERIOUS CHARACTER APPEARS ON THE SCENE; AND MANY THINGS, INSEPARABLE FROM THIS HISTORY, ARE DONE AND PERFORMED.

The old man had reached the street corner before he began to recover from the impact of Toby Crackit’s news. He hadn’t slowed his unusual speed, but was still pressing onward in the same wild, disordered manner when a carriage dashed past. A loud cry from pedestrians who saw his danger forced him back onto the sidewalk. Avoiding the main streets as much as possible and skulking only through side streets and alleys, he finally emerged on Snow Hill. Here he walked even faster than before, not lingering until he turned into a court. Once there, as if aware he was finally in his element, he fell into his usual shuffling pace and seemed to breathe more easily."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_014",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“May wolves rip your gullets!” Sikes snarled, gnashing his teeth. “I wish I were amidst some of you; you would howl all the hoarser for it.”

As Sikes growled this curse with the most intense savagery his desperate temperament could muster, he balanced the injured lad’s frame over his bent knee and glanced back briefly to observe those chasing him.

Little could be distinguished within the gloom and fog, yet the clamorous cries of men echoed through the atmosphere, and the baying of nearby hounds, stirred by the ringing of the alarm bell, reverberated everywhere.

“Halt, you cowardly cur!” the thief yelled, calling after Toby Crackit, who was already far ahead, utilizing his long legs to their full potential. “Halt!”

Repeating the command forced Toby to stop abruptly, as he was not entirely convinced he was out of pistol range, and Sikes was in no temperament to be trifled with.

“Give me a hand with the lad,” Sikes shouted, gesturing wildly to his partner. “Return here!”

Toby made a gesture of coming back, but as he approached sluggishly, he ventured to express significant hesitation in a voice strained by lack of air.

“Faster!” Sikes commanded, depositing the boy into a parched ditch at his feet and pulling a firearm from his coat. “Do not try to double-cross me.”

Just then, the clamor intensified. Sikes, glancing back once more, saw that the pursuers were already scaling the gate of the field he occupied, with two dogs running slightly ahead of them.

“It is finished, Bill!” Toby shouted; “abandon the boy and run for it.” Offering this final counsel, Mr. Crackit, choosing the risk of being fired upon by his associate over the certainty of capture by his foes, turned around and sprinted away. Sikes gritted his teeth, scanned the area, draped the cape that had been hastily wrapped around Oliver over the boy’s prone body, sprinted along the hedge to divert the attention of those following from the location where the child rested, paused briefly at a second hedge intersecting the first, and, tossing his pistol high into the air, vaulted over it and vanished.

“Hey, hey, over there!” a shaky voice called from behind. “Pincher! Neptune! Return here, return here!”

The dogs, who, much like their owners, appeared to have no great enthusiasm for the pursuit they were involved in, promptly obeyed the call. Three men, who had by then ventured some way into the field, paused to confer.

“My suggestion, or rather, I should state, my instructions,” declared the stoutest man of the group, “is that we immediately return home.”

“I am content with whatever suits Mr. Giles,” remarked a shorter individual, who was certainly not slender, and who appeared quite pale and very courteous, as terrified men often are.

“I would not wish to seem discourteous, gentlemen,” stated the third, who had summoned the dogs, “Mr.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_015",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Good heavens, you’re right!” the physician exclaimed. “Where is he? Lead the way. I shall stop by again on my descent, Mrs. Maylie. Is that the small window he entered through? Truly, I find it hard to believe!”

Chatting incessantly, he trailed Mr. Giles to the upper floor. While he ascended, it is worth noting that Mr. Losberne, a local surgeon recognized throughout a ten-mile radius as “the doctor,” had become stout, more from a cheerful disposition than from indulgence; he was as benevolent, warm-hearted, and peculiar an elderly bachelor as one might discover within five times that distance.

The doctor remained away far longer than he or the ladies had expected. A wide, shallow container was retrieved from the carriage; a chamber bell was sounded repeatedly; and the domestics hurried up and down the stairs constantly, all of which suggested that a significant matter was unfolding above. Finally, he reappeared, and in response to a worried question regarding his patient, he assumed a cryptic expression and shut the door securely.

“This is a highly unusual situation, Mrs. Maylie,” the doctor remarked, positioning himself against the door as if to guard it.

“He is not in peril, I trust?” the elderly woman asked.

“Well, that would not be unusual, given the situation,” the doctor answered, “though I do not believe he is. Have you laid eyes on the burglar?”

“No,” the lady replied.

“Or heard any details regarding him?”

“No.”

“I beg your pardon, ma’am,” Mr. Giles interrupted, “but I was about to inform you of him when Doctor Losberne arrived.”

The truth was that Mr. Giles had initially struggled to admit that he had merely shot a young boy. Having received such praise for his valor, he could not resist delaying the confession for a few delightful moments, during which he basked in the height of a fleeting reputation for fearless bravery.

“Rose wanted to view the man,” Mrs. Maylie stated, “but I would not permit it.”

“Hmm!” the doctor replied. “There is nothing particularly frightening about his looks. Would you object to seeing him while I am present?”

“If it is required,” the lady answered, “certainly not.”

“I believe it is required,” the doctor said; “in any case, I am certain you would deeply regret missing the chance if you delayed. He is entirely calm and at ease now. Allow me—Miss Rose, will you join us? There is no cause for alarm, I give you my word!”

With many talkative promises that they would be pleasantly surprised by the criminal’s appearance, the doctor tucked the young lady’s arm into his own, and offering his free hand to Mrs. Maylie, escorted them upstairs with great formality and grace.

“Now,” the doctor whispered, as he gently rotated the bedroom door handle, “let us see your impression of him. He has not been shaved lately, yet he does not appear fierce in the least. Wait, though! Let me first ensure he is presentable for visitors.”"
}

{

  "rewritten_text": "“Good heavens, that’s true!” the doctor said. “Where is he? Show me the way. I’ll check in again on my way down, Mrs. Maylie. That’s the small window he climbed through, is it? Well, I find that hard to believe!”

Talking the entire time, he followed Mr. Giles upstairs. While he climbed, the reader should know that Mr. Losberne, a local surgeon known for ten miles around simply as “the doctor,” had grown stout more from his good-natured personality than from overeating. He was as kind, hearty, and eccentric an old bachelor as one could find in five times that distance.

The doctor was gone much longer than he or the ladies had expected. A large, flat box was retrieved from his carriage, a bedroom bell was rung repeatedly, and the servants hurried up and down the stairs constantly, all of which suggested that something significant was happening upstairs. Eventually, he returned, and in response to an anxious question about his patient, he looked very mysterious and carefully shut the door.

“This is a very strange situation, Mrs. Maylie,” the doctor said, standing with his back to the door as if to keep it closed.

“He isn’t in danger, I hope?” the old lady asked.

“Well, that wouldn’t be a strange situation given the circumstances,” the doctor replied, “though I don’t believe he is. Have you seen the thief?”

“No,” the old lady answered.

“Or heard anything about him?”

“No.”

“I beg your pardon, ma’am,” Mr. Giles interrupted, “but I was about to tell you about him when Doctor Losberne arrived.”

The truth was that Mr. Giles had initially been unable to admit that he had only shot a boy. He had received so much praise for his bravery that he couldn’t help but delay the explanation for a few delightful minutes, during which he basked in the height of his brief reputation for fearless courage.

“Rose wanted to see the man,” Mrs. Maylie said, “but I wouldn’t allow it.”

“Hmm!” the doctor replied. “There is nothing very alarming about his appearance. Do you have any objection to seeing him while I am present?”

“If it is necessary,” the old lady replied, “certainly not.”

“Then I believe it is necessary,” the doctor said. “In any case, I am quite certain you would deeply regret not doing so if you put it off. He is perfectly quiet and comfortable now. Allow me—Miss Rose, will you permit me? There is not the slightest reason for fear, I give you my word!”

CHAPTER XXX. RELATES WHAT OLIVER’S NEW VISITORS THOUGHT OF HIM

With many talkative assurances that they would be pleasantly surprised by the criminal’s appearance, the doctor tucked the young lady’s arm into his own and, offering his other hand to Mrs. Maylie, led them upstairs with great ceremony and dignity.

“Now,” the doctor whispered as he softly turned the bedroom door handle, “let’s see what you think of him. He hasn’t been shaved recently, but he doesn’t look ferocious at all, despite that. Wait, though! Let me first make sure he is ready for visitors.”"
}

  "rewritten_text": "The lad is suffering from a severe fever and is in no state to be questioned further; that is one small mercy. We must simply do what we can, and if the outcome is poor, it is not through any failing of ours. Enter!”

“Good day, sir,” Blathers stated, stepping into the chamber behind his partner and securing the door before speaking further. “This was not an inside job.”

“And what on earth is an inside job?” the physician asked, showing his irritation.

“We refer to a robbery as an inside job, ladies,” Blathers explained, addressing them with an air of pity for their lack of knowledge, though he clearly held the doctor’s ignorance in disdain, “when the household staff is involved.”

“No one here suspected them,” Mrs. Maylie noted.

“Quite possibly not, ma’am,” Blathers answered; “yet they could have been involved regardless.”

“Even more likely for that very reason,” Duff added.

“We have determined it was a professional from the city,” Blathers continued his summary; “the quality of the work is excellent.”

“Truly impressive, indeed,” Duff muttered quietly.

“There were two individuals involved,” Blathers went on; “and they brought a boy along; the size of the window opening makes that clear. That is all we can say for now. We would like to examine the youth you have upstairs immediately, if you do not mind.”

“Perhaps they would care for a drink first, Mrs. Maylie?” the doctor suggested, his expression lightening as if a fresh idea had struck him.

“Oh! Certainly!” Rose replied with enthusiasm. “You shall have it at once, if you wish.”

“Why, thank you, miss!” Blathers said, wiping his mouth with his sleeve; “this line of work is thirsty business. Anything convenient will do, miss; please do not trouble yourself on our behalf.”

“What would you like?” the doctor inquired, trailing the young woman to the sideboard.

“A small amount of spirits, sir, if it makes no difference,” Blathers answered. “It is a chilly journey from London, ma’am; and I find that spirits always provide a warmer sensation.”

This observation was directed at Mrs. Maylie, who accepted it with great kindness. While she attended to the request, the doctor quietly exited the room.

“Ah!” Mr. Blathers remarked, gripping his wine glass by the base between his thumb and forefinger rather than the stem, and holding it against his chest; “I have witnessed many affairs of this nature during my career, ladies.”

“That break-in down the back lane at Edmonton, Blathers,” Mr. Duff said, prompting his associate’s recollection.

“That was similar to this, was it not?” Mr. Blathers replied; “that was the work of Conkey Chickweed, that was.”

“You always credited him with that,” Duff countered. “It was the Family Pet, I tell you. Conkey had no more involvement than I did.”

“Nonsense!” Blathers snapped; “I know better. Do you recall when Conkey was robbed of his own cash? What a surprise that was! Better than any storybook I have ever read!”

“What was that?” Rose asked, hoping to foster a pleasant mood in their unwanted guests.

“It was a theft, miss, that hardly anyone would have suspected,” Blathers said. “This fellow Conkey Chickweed—”

“Conkey means Nosey, ma’am,” Duff interrupted.

“Surely the lady is aware of that, is she not?”"
}

“And what the devil is an inside job?” the doctor demanded impatiently.

“We call it an inside job, ladies,” Blathers said, turning to them as if he pitied their ignorance but held the doctor’s in contempt, “when the servants are involved.”

“Nobody suspected them in this case,” Mrs. Maylie said.

“Very likely not, ma’am,” Blathers replied, “but they could have been involved regardless.”

“More likely for that very reason,” Duff added.

“We have determined it was a professional from the city,” Blathers continued his report, “because the quality of the work is top-notch.”

“Very impressive indeed,” Duff remarked quietly.

“There were two of them involved,” Blathers continued, “and they had a boy with them; that is clear from the size of the window. That is all we can say for now. We would like to see this lad you have upstairs immediately, if you please.”

“Perhaps they would like something to drink first, Mrs. Maylie?” the doctor asked, his face brightening as if a new thought had occurred to him.

“Oh, certainly!” Rose exclaimed eagerly. “You shall have it immediately.”

“Why, thank you, miss!” Blathers said, wiping his coat sleeve across his mouth. “This kind of work is thirsty business. Anything convenient will do, miss; don’t go to any trouble on our account.”

“What would you like?” the doctor asked, following the young lady to the sideboard.

“A little bit of spirits, sir, if it’s all the same to you,” Blathers replied. “It’s a cold ride from London, ma’am, and I always find that spirits warm the spirit.”

This interesting remark was directed at Mrs. Maylie, who received it very graciously. While it was being served to her, the doctor slipped out of the room.

“Ah!” said Mr. Blathers, holding his wine glass not by the stem, but gripping the base between the thumb and forefinger of his left hand and holding it against his chest. “I have seen a great many cases like this in my time, ladies.”

“That break-in down the back lane at Edmonton, Blathers,” Mr. Duff said, helping his colleague’s memory.

“That was similar to this, wasn’t it?” Blathers replied. “That was done by Conkey Chickweed, that was.”

“You always credited him with that,” Duff replied. “It was the Family Pet, I tell you. Conkey had no more to do with it than I did.”

“Nonsense!” Blathers retorted. “I know better. Do you remember when Conkey was robbed of his own money? What a surprise that was! Better than any novel I have ever read!”

“What was that?” Rose inquired, eager to encourage any sign of good humor in their unwelcome visitors.

“It was a robbery, miss, that hardly anyone would have figured out,” Blathers said. “This Conkey Chickweed—”

“Conkey means Nosey, ma’am,” Duff interrupted.

“Of course the lady knows that, doesn’t she?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_018",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Oliver believed he heard him sobbing on several occasions, yet he hesitated to break the silence with any new comment, as he could easily imagine the man's internal state, and thus he remained at a distance, pretending to be busy with his bouquet. Throughout this period, Mr. Giles, still wearing his white nightcap, remained seated on the carriage steps with his elbows resting on his knees, drying his eyes with a blue cotton handkerchief marked by white spots. That the sincere man was not pretending to be upset was clearly proven by the extremely bloodshot eyes he turned toward the young gentleman when he finally looked around to speak to him. 

"I believe it would be better for you to proceed to my mother's in the carriage, Giles," he suggested. "I prefer to walk slowly, so that I might have a moment before I see her. You can inform her that I am on my way." 

"I apologize, Mr. Harry," Giles replied, giving his disheveled face one last wipe with the cloth, "but if you would allow the postboy to deliver that message instead, I would be very grateful. It would not be suitable for the housemaids to witness me in this condition, sir; I would lose all my influence over them if they did." 

"Very well," Harry Maylie answered with a smile, "you may do as you please. Let him take the baggage if you wish, and you can walk along with us. Just be sure to swap that nightcap for a more suitable hat first, or we will be mistaken for lunatics." 

Recalling his inappropriate attire, Mr. Giles quickly removed and stowed his nightcap, replacing it with a somber, formal hat he retrieved from the carriage. Once this was finished, the postboy departed, and Giles, Mr. Maylie, and Oliver followed at a leisurely pace. 

As they traveled, Oliver looked at the newcomer with considerable interest and wonder. He appeared to be roughly twenty-five years old and of average stature; his face was open and attractive, and his manner was relaxed and charming. Despite the age gap, he resembled the elderly lady so closely that Oliver would have easily guessed they were related, even if he had not already referred to her as his mother. 

Mrs. Maylie was waiting with great anticipation to greet her son when he arrived at the cottage. Their reunion was marked by intense feeling on both sides. 

"Mother!" the young man murmured, "why did you not write to me sooner?" 

"I did write," Mrs. Maylie answered, "but upon further thought, I decided to withhold the letter until I had consulted Mr. Losberne." 

"But why," the young man asked, "why risk the possibility of what almost occurred? If Rose had—I cannot bring myself to say the word—if this sickness had ended in tragedy, how could you ever have forgiven yourself? How could I ever have found joy again!" 

"If that had been the outcome, Harry," Mrs. Maylie said, "I fear your joy would have been completely destroyed, and your arrival here, whether a day earlier or later, would have mattered very little indeed." 

"And who could be surprised if that were true, mother?" the young man replied; "or why should I use the word 'if'? It is—it is—you are aware of it, mother—you must know it!""
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_018",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "Oliver thought he heard him sob more than once, but he was afraid to interrupt him with any new comment, as he could easily guess what he was feeling. He stood to the side, pretending to be busy with his bouquet of flowers.

All this time, Mr. Giles had been sitting on the steps of the carriage wearing his white nightcap. He rested his elbows on his knees and wiped his eyes with a blue cotton handkerchief covered in white spots. It was clear that the honest man was not faking his emotions, as shown by his very red eyes when he turned to look at the young gentleman.

“I think you should go on to my mother’s in the carriage, Giles,” the young man said. “I would rather walk slowly so I have some time before I see her. You can tell her I am coming.”

“I beg your pardon, Mr. Harry,” Giles said, giving his face one last wipe with his handkerchief. “But if you would let the postboy deliver that message instead, I would be very grateful. It would not be right for the maids to see me looking like this, sir; I would never have any authority over them again if they did.”

“Well,” Harry Maylie replied with a smile, “you can do as you like. Let him go on with the luggage if you wish, and you can follow with us. But first, swap that nightcap for something more appropriate, or people will think we are mad.”

Remembering his strange appearance, Mr. Giles quickly took off his nightcap and put it in his pocket. He replaced it with a serious, sober-looking hat that he pulled from the carriage. Once this was done, the postboy drove off, and Giles, Mr. Maylie, and Oliver followed at a leisurely pace.

As they walked, Oliver looked at the newcomer with interest and curiosity. He seemed to be about twenty-five years old and of average height. His face was open and handsome, and his manner was relaxed and pleasant. Despite the difference in their ages, he looked so much like the old lady that Oliver would have easily guessed they were related, even if he hadn't already heard him call her his mother.

Mrs. Maylie was waiting anxiously to see her son when he reached the cottage. Their reunion was filled with great emotion on both sides.

“Mother!” the young man whispered. “Why didn’t you write to me sooner?”

“I did,” Mrs. Maylie replied, “but after thinking it over, I decided to hold back the letter until I had heard Mr. Losberne’s opinion.”

“But why,” the young man asked, “why take the risk of what almost happened? If Rose had—I cannot say the word—if this illness had ended differently, how could you ever have forgiven yourself? How could I ever have known happiness again!”

“If that had been the case, Harry,” Mrs. Maylie said, “I fear your happiness would have been destroyed, and your arrival here, whether a day sooner or later, would have mattered very little.”

“And who can be surprised that it is so, mother?” the young man replied. “Or why should I say ‘if’? It is—it is—you know it, mother—you must know it!”"
}

“If that had been the case, Harry,” Mrs. Maylie said, “I fear your happiness would have been destroyed, and your arrival here, whether a day sooner or later, would have mattered very little.”

“And who can be surprised that it is so, mother?” the young man replied. “Or why should I say ‘if’? It is—it is—you know it, mother—you must know it!”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_019",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "The kind girl’s eyes filled with tears as she spoke; and as one drop landed on the blossom she leaned over, shining brilliantly in its petals and enhancing its beauty, it appeared as if the overflow of her pure, youthful spirit shared a natural affinity with the most exquisite elements of the natural world.

“A being,” the young man persisted with intensity, “a being as lovely and free from deceit as one of heaven’s own angels, hovered between existence and the grave. Oh! who could expect, when the far-off realm to which she belonged partially revealed itself to her, that she would come back to the misery and misfortune of this place! Rose, Rose, to realize that you were fading like a gentle shadow cast upon the ground by a light from above; to possess no expectation that you would remain for those who stay behind; to barely find a justification for why you should; to sense that you belonged to that radiant domain where so many of the most beautiful and virtuous have taken their early departure; and yet to plead, amidst all these comforts, that you might be returned to those who cherished you—these were torments nearly too heavy to endure. They haunted me, day and night; and with them arrived such a surging flood of anxieties, worries, and self-centered sorrows, lest you should perish without ever knowing how deeply I adored you, that it nearly overwhelmed my mind and judgment. You regained your strength. Gradually, and almost moment by moment, some measure of vitality returned, and blending with the exhausted and weak current of life flowing sluggishly within you, it expanded once more into a powerful, rushing tide. I have observed you transform almost from death to life, with eyes that grew dim from their intensity and profound devotion. Do not suggest that you wish I had been spared this; for it has made my heart more compassionate toward all humanity.”

“That was not my intention,” Rose replied, sobbing; “I only wish you had departed from here, so that you might have returned to lofty and honorable endeavors; to pursuits truly deserving of you.”

“There is no endeavor more deserving of me, or more worthy of the noblest nature in existence, than the effort to capture a heart like yours,” the young man declared, grasping her hand. “Rose, my own dear Rose! For years—for years—I have loved you; hoping to achieve success, and then return triumphantly to tell you it was pursued solely for you to share; imagining, in my reveries, how I would remind you, in that joyful instant, of the many quiet signs I had provided of a boy’s affection, and ask for your hand, as if fulfilling some ancient, unspoken agreement between us! That moment has not yet come; but here, without fame achieved and no youthful dream fulfilled, I present to you the heart that has long been yours, and risk everything on the response you give to this proposal.”

“Your conduct has always been generous and honorable,” Rose said, regaining control over the feelings that stirred her. “As you trust that I am neither indifferent nor unappreciative, listen to my reply.”

“It is that I may strive to be worthy of you; is it not, dear Rose?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_019",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The gentle girl’s eyes filled with tears as she spoke; and when one drop fell onto the flower she was leaning over, glistening brightly in its petals and making it look even more beautiful, it felt as if the outpouring of her fresh, young heart naturally belonged with the loveliest things in nature.

“A creature,” the young man continued passionately, “a creature as fair and innocent as one of God’s own angels, hovered between life and death. Oh! Who could hope, when the distant world she belonged to half-opened before her, that she would return to the sorrow and calamity of this one? Rose, Rose, to know that you were fading away like a soft shadow cast upon the earth by a light from above; to have no hope that you would be spared for those of us left behind; to struggle to find a reason why you should be; to feel that you belonged to that bright realm where so many of the best and fairest have taken flight; and yet to pray, despite all those consolations, that you might be restored to those who loved you—these were distractions almost too great to bear. They were mine, day and night; and with them came such a rushing torrent of fears, anxieties, and selfish regrets, fearing you might die without ever knowing how devotedly I loved you, that it almost overwhelmed my senses and reason. You recovered. Day by day, and almost hour by hour, some measure of health returned, and mingling with the weak, spent stream of life flowing languidly within you, it swelled again into a strong, rushing tide. I have watched you change from death back to life with eyes that grew blurred from their intensity and deep affection. Do not tell me you wish I had missed this; it has softened my heart toward all mankind.”

“I didn’t mean that,” Rose said, weeping. “I only wish you had left here so you could have returned to high and noble pursuits again; pursuits truly worthy of you.”

“There is no pursuit more worthy of me, or of the highest nature that exists, than the struggle to win a heart like yours,” the young man said, taking her hand. “Rose, my own dear Rose! For years—for years—I have loved you; hoping to earn fame and then come proudly home to tell you it was pursued only for you to share; thinking in my daydreams how I would remind you, in that happy moment, of the many silent signs I had given of a boy’s affection, and claim your hand as if fulfilling some old, unspoken contract sealed between us! That time has not arrived; but here, with no fame won and no youthful vision realized, I offer you the heart that has long been yours, and stake everything on how you respond to this offer.”

“Your behavior has always been kind and noble,” Rose said, mastering the emotions that agitated her. “As you believe that I am not insensitive or ungrateful, listen to my answer.”

“It is that I may try to deserve you; is it, dear Rose?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_020",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "What course remained for Mr. Bumble? He cast a despondent glance about and crept away; as he arrived at the exit, the paupers’ snickering erupted into a high-pitched cackle of uncontrollable glee. This was the final blow. He felt diminished in their estimation; he had forfeited his rank and standing before the paupers themselves; he had plummeted from the grandeur and authority of his beadle status to the absolute nadir of a browbeaten, henpecked husband.

“All within two months!” Mr. Bumble lamented, consumed by gloomy reflections. “Two months! Barely two months have passed since I was not merely my own master, but the master of everyone else within the parochial workhouse, and now!”

It was unbearable. Mr. Bumble struck the ear of the lad who held the gate for him—having reached the entrance while lost in his musings—and wandered off into the street in a state of agitation.

He paced through various streets until the physical exertion had tempered the initial intensity of his sorrow; subsequently, a shift in his mood left him parched. He went by numerous taverns but eventually halted before one tucked away in a side street, whose parlor, as he discerned from a quick glance past the window shades, was empty except for a single patron. At that instant, a heavy rain began to fall. This settled the matter. Mr. Bumble went inside, requested a drink as he moved by the bar, and stepped into the room he had observed from the road.

The man sitting there was tall, dark-haired, and wrapped in a voluminous cloak. He appeared to be a traveler, and his worn, dusty clothing, combined with a certain gauntness in his features, suggested he had journeyed a great distance. He cast a sidelong glance at Bumble upon his arrival, yet barely bothered to incline his head in response to his greeting.

Mr. Bumble possessed sufficient self-importance for two people, even if the stranger had been more cordial; consequently, he consumed his gin-and-water quietly and perused the newspaper with an air of great ceremony and gravity.

It occurred, however—as frequently happens when individuals find themselves together in such conditions—that Mr. Bumble felt a recurring, irresistible urge to sneak a look at the stranger, only to find, upon withdrawing his gaze in embarrassment, that the stranger was simultaneously watching him. Mr. Bumble’s discomfort was deepened by the stranger’s striking gaze, which was sharp and luminous yet obscured by a scowl of wariness and doubt, unlike anything he had previously encountered and quite unpleasant to witness.

After they had met each other’s eyes repeatedly in this fashion, the stranger spoke in a grating, low tone.

“Were you searching for me,” he inquired, “when you looked through the window?”

“Not that I am conscious of, unless you happen to be Mr.—” Here Mr. Bumble paused, hoping to learn the man’s identity by letting him fill in the gap.

“I perceive you were not,” the stranger replied, a trace of subtle mockery touching his lips; “otherwise you would have known my name. You do not know it. I suggest you refrain from inquiring.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_020",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "What could Mr. Bumble do? He looked around dejectedly and slunk away; as he reached the door, the paupers’ tittering erupted into a shrill chuckle of irrepressible delight. This was the final straw. He was degraded in their eyes; he had lost his status and standing before the paupers themselves; he had fallen from the height and pomp of his position as beadle to the lowest depths of being a henpecked husband.

“All in two months!” Mr. Bumble said, filled with dismal thoughts. “Two months! Only two months ago, I was not only my own master, but everyone else’s, as far as the parish workhouse was concerned, and now!”

It was too much. Mr. Bumble boxed the ears of the boy who opened the gate for him—having reached the entrance while lost in his reverie—and walked distractedly into the street.

He walked up one street and down another until the physical exertion had calmed his initial outburst of grief; then, a shift in his mood made him thirsty. He passed many pubs but eventually paused before one in a side street, whose parlor, as he gathered from a quick glance over the blinds, was empty except for one solitary customer. It began to rain heavily at that moment, which decided him. Mr. Bumble stepped inside, ordered a drink as he passed the bar, and entered the room he had peered into from the street.

The man seated there was tall and dark, wearing a large cloak. He had the air of a stranger and appeared, by a certain haggardness in his look and the dust on his clothes, to have traveled some distance. He eyed Bumble sideways as he entered but barely bothered to nod in acknowledgment of his greeting.

Mr. Bumble had enough dignity for two, even if the stranger had been more familiar, so he drank his gin-and-water in silence and read the newspaper with a great show of pomp and circumstance.

It so happened, however—as it often does when men find themselves in such company—that Mr. Bumble felt a powerful, irresistible urge to steal a look at the stranger every now and then. Whenever he did so, he would withdraw his eyes in confusion, only to find that the stranger was at that moment stealing a look at him. Mr. Bumble’s awkwardness was increased by the remarkable expression in the stranger’s eyes, which were keen and bright but shadowed by a scowl of distrust and suspicion unlike anything he had ever seen before, and repulsive to behold.

When they had met each other’s gaze several times in this way, the stranger broke the silence in a harsh, deep voice.

“Were you looking for me,” he asked, “when you peered in at the window?”

“Not that I am aware of, unless you are Mr.—” Here Mr. Bumble stopped short, for he was curious to know the stranger’s name and thought, in his impatience, that he might fill in the blank.

“I see you were not,” the stranger said, a look of quiet sarcasm playing about his mouth, “or you would have known my name. You don’t know it. I would suggest you do not ask for it.”"
}

The man sitting there was tall, dark, and wore a large cloak. He looked like a stranger and appeared to have traveled a long distance, judging by his haggard expression and the dust on his clothes. He glanced sideways at Bumble as he entered but barely bothered to nod in response to his greeting.

Mr. Bumble possessed enough dignity for two people, even if the stranger had been friendlier. He drank his gin and water in silence and read the newspaper with a great show of self-importance.

It happened, however—as it often does when men find themselves in such company—that Mr. Bumble felt a strong, irresistible urge to steal a look at the stranger. Every time he did so, he would look away in confusion, only to find that the stranger was looking at him at that exact moment. Mr. Bumble’s discomfort grew because of the stranger’s remarkable eyes, which were keen and bright but shadowed by a scowl of distrust and suspicion. It was unlike anything he had ever seen before and was unpleasant to look at.

After they had caught each other’s gaze several times, the stranger broke the silence with a harsh, deep voice.

“Were you looking for me,” he asked, “when you peered in at the window?”

“Not that I am aware of, unless you are Mr.—” Here, Mr. Bumble stopped short. He was curious to know the stranger’s name and hoped that, in his impatience, the man might fill in the blank.

“I see you were not,” the stranger said, a look of quiet sarcasm playing around his mouth. “Otherwise, you would have known my name. You don’t know it. I would suggest you do not ask for it.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_021",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Two half-quartern loaves; a pound of the finest fresh butter; a wedge of double Gloucester; and, to finish it all, the most potent liquor you have ever tasted!”

Delivering this final praise, Master Bates drew a full-sized, well-corked wine bottle from one of his deep pockets, while Mr. Dawkins simultaneously poured a glass of undiluted spirits from his own bottle, which the sick man swallowed instantly without pausing.

“Ah!” Fagin remarked, rubbing his palms together with immense pleasure. “You will recover, Bill; you are on the mend now.”

“Recover!” Mr. Sikes retorted. “I could have perished twenty times before you would have lifted a finger to assist me. How dare you abandon a man in this condition for over three weeks, you treacherous scoundrel?”

“Just listen to him, boys!” Fagin said, lifting his shoulders. “And we have arrived to provide him with all these lovely items.”

“The items are acceptable enough,” Mr. Sikes noted, feeling slightly mollified as he surveyed the table; “but what is your excuse for leaving me here, miserable, lacking health, money, and everything else, and ignoring me all this time as if I were that dog? Chase him away, Charley!”

“I have never seen such a cheerful dog,” Master Bates exclaimed, following the order. “Sniffing the food like an elderly woman heading to the market! That dog would earn a fortune on the stage and revitalize the theater as well.”

“Be quiet,” Sikes shouted, as the animal retreated beneath the bed, still snarling. “What is your defense, you shriveled old receiver of stolen goods, eh?”

“I was out of London for over a week, my dear, working on a scheme,” the Jew answered.

“And what about the remaining two weeks?” Sikes pressed. “What about the other fortnight you left me lying here like a dying rat in a burrow?”

“I could not avoid it, Bill. I cannot provide a lengthy account in front of others, but I truly could not help it, upon my honor.”

“Upon your what?” Sikes grumbled with profound revulsion. “Here! One of you boys, slice me a portion of that pie to remove the taste of that word from my mouth, or it will surely choke me.”

“Do not lose your temper, my dear,” Fagin pleaded submissively. “I have never forgotten you, Bill; not once.”

“No! I am certain you have not,” Sikes replied with a harsh sneer. “You have been planning and scheming every hour I have spent shivering and burning here; deciding that Bill must do this, and Bill must do that, and that Bill would perform it all for a pittance as soon as he recovered, being desperate enough for your tasks. If not for the girl, I might have perished.”

“There now, Bill,” Fagin argued, seizing upon the mention. “If not for the girl! Who but old Fagin was responsible for you having such a useful girl at your side?”

“He speaks the truth there!” Nancy said, stepping forward quickly. “Leave him be; let him be.”

Nancy’s arrival shifted the tone of the discussion; the boys, catching a subtle wink from the cautious old Jew, began offering her drink, though she consumed very little, while Fagin, adopting an uncharacteristically lively mood, slowly steered Mr. Sikes."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_021",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“Two half-quartern loaves; a pound of the best fresh butter; a piece of double Gloucester; and, to top it all off, some of the finest booze you’ve ever tasted!”

Delivering this final praise, Master Bates pulled a full-sized, corked wine bottle from one of his deep pockets. At the same moment, Mr. Dawkins poured a glass of raw spirits from the bottle he was carrying, which the invalid downed without a second’s hesitation.

“Ah!” Fagin said, rubbing his hands together with great satisfaction. “You’ll be fine, Bill; you’ll be fine now.”

“Fine!” Mr. Sikes exclaimed. “I could have been finished off twenty times over before you’d have done a thing to help me. What do you mean by leaving a man in this state for three weeks or more, you two-faced vagabond?”

“Just listen to him, boys!” Fagin said, shrugging his shoulders. “And here we are, bringing him all these beautiful things.”

“The food is well enough in its own way,” Mr. Sikes remarked, feeling slightly mollified as he looked over the table, “but what do you have to say for yourself for leaving me here, miserable, broke, and in poor health, taking no more notice of me all this time than if I were that dog? Get him out of here, Charley!”

“I’ve never seen such a lively dog,” Master Bates cried, doing as he was told. “Sniffing the food like an old lady heading to the market! That dog would make a fortune on the stage and revive the drama, too.”

“Shut your mouth,” Sikes shouted as the dog retreated under the bed, still growling angrily. “What do you have to say for yourself, you withered old fence, eh?”

“I was away from London for a week or more, my dear, working on a job,” the old man replied.

“And what about the other two weeks?” Sikes demanded. “What about the other two weeks you left me lying here like a sick rat in a hole?”

“I couldn’t help it, Bill. I can’t go into a long explanation in front of company, but I couldn’t help it, on my honor.”

“On your what?” Sikes growled with intense disgust. “Here! One of you boys cut me a piece of that pie to get the taste of that out of my mouth, or it’ll choke me to death.”

“Don’t lose your temper, my dear,” Fagin urged submissively. “I’ve never forgotten you, Bill; not once.”

“No! I’ll bet you haven’t,” Sikes replied with a bitter grin. “You’ve been scheming and plotting every hour I’ve been lying here shivering and burning. Bill was to do this, and Bill was to do that, and Bill was to do it all for peanuts as soon as he got well, because he was desperate enough for your work. If it hadn’t been for the girl, I might have died.”

“There now, Bill,” Fagin remonstrated, eagerly grabbing at the word. “If it hadn’t been for the girl! Who but poor old Fagin was responsible for you having such a handy girl around?”

“He’s telling the truth there!” Nancy said, stepping forward quickly. “Leave him be; just leave him be.”

Nancy’s arrival shifted the conversation. The boys, receiving a sly wink from the cautious old man, began to offer her drinks, though she took very little. Meanwhile, Fagin, acting with unusual high spirits, gradually brought Mr. Sikes into the discussion."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_022",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Yet I did; and I never laid eyes on him again until yesterday evening.”

“And what happened then?”

“I shall explain, my lady. He returned last night. Once more they ascended the stairs, and I, concealing myself so that my silhouette would not reveal my presence, eavesdropped at the door again. The initial remarks I caught from Monks were these: ‘So the sole evidence of the lad’s heritage rests at the riverbed, and the crone who accepted those items from his mother is decaying in her grave.’ They chuckled, discussing his triumph in accomplishing this; and Monks, rambling about the boy and becoming quite frantic, declared that although he had secured the young rascal’s inheritance, he would have preferred a different outcome; for what a spectacle it would have been to dismantle the pride of his father’s legacy by dragging him through every prison in the city, and then framing him for some major crime that Fagin could easily orchestrate, after having already profited from him.”

“What does this signify!” Rose inquired.

“It is the honest truth, my lady, even if spoken by me,” the girl answered. “Then, using profanities familiar to my ears but foreign to yours, he stated that if he could satisfy his malice by ending the boy’s existence without risking his own neck, he would; but, since that was impossible, he would remain vigilant to confront him at every stage of his life; and if he exploited his origins and past, he might still ruin him. ‘In short, Fagin,’ he remarked, ‘Jew though you are, you never devised traps as cunning as those I shall set for my younger brother, Oliver.’”

“His brother!” Rose cried out.

“Those were his exact words,” Nancy stated, looking nervously about, as she had barely stopped doing since she commenced her tale, for the specter of Sikes constantly plagued her. “And there is more. When he mentioned you and the other lady, claiming it seemed orchestrated by Providence, or perhaps the devil, that Oliver should fall into your care, he laughed and noted there was some solace in that as well, for how vast a fortune would you not offer, if you possessed it, to discover the identity of your little pet.”

“You cannot mean,” Rose said, growing quite ashen, “to suggest that this was spoken seriously?”

“He spoke with bitter and furious sincerity, if any man ever has,” the girl replied, moving her head. “He is a man of intense conviction when his spite is aroused. I am acquainted with many who commit worse deeds; yet I would prefer to hear them all repeatedly than to listen to that Monks even once. It is getting late, and I must arrive home without raising suspicion that I have been on such a mission as this. I must depart immediately.”

“But how can I act?” Rose asked. “To what purpose can I apply this intelligence without your assistance? Return! Why do you desire to go back to associates you describe in such grim terms? If you share this testimony with a gentleman I can call from the adjoining room in a moment, you could be placed in a secure location within thirty minutes.”

“I must return,” the girl insisted. “I have to go back, because—how could I ever explain such matters to a pure lady such as yourself?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_022",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“But I did; and I didn’t see him again until last night.”

“And what happened then?”

“I’ll tell you, ma’am. Last night he returned. They went upstairs again, and I wrapped myself up so my shadow wouldn’t give me away, and I listened at the door once more. The first words I heard Monks say were these: ‘So the only proof of the boy’s identity is at the bottom of the river, and the old woman who took them from his mother is rotting in her grave.’ They laughed and talked about his success in doing this. Monks, getting very agitated as he spoke about the boy, said that although he had the young devil’s money safely now, he would have preferred it the other way. He said it would have been a real game to ruin the pride of his father’s will by dragging him through every jail in the city, and then setting him up for a serious crime that Fagin could easily arrange, after having made a good profit off him as well.”

“What does all this mean!” Rose asked.

“It’s the truth, ma’am, even if it comes from my mouth,” the girl replied. “Then he said, using curses that are common enough to my ears but strange to yours, that if he could satisfy his hatred by taking the boy’s life without putting his own neck in danger, he would. But since he couldn’t, he would watch for him at every turn in life; and if he could use his birth and history against him, he might still destroy him. ‘In short, Fagin,’ he said, ‘Jew that you are, you never set such traps as I will devise for my young brother, Oliver.’”

“His brother!” Rose exclaimed.

“Those were his words,” Nancy said, glancing nervously around, as she had barely stopped doing since she began speaking, for the thought of Sikes haunted her constantly. “And there is more. When he spoke of you and the other lady, and said it seemed arranged by Heaven, or the devil, that Oliver should fall into your hands, he laughed and said there was some comfort in that, too. He asked how many thousands and hundreds of thousands of pounds you wouldn’t give, if you had them, to know who your little pet really is.”

“You don’t mean,” Rose said, turning very pale, “to tell me that he was serious?”

“He spoke with harsh and angry sincerity, if a man ever did,” the girl replied, shaking her head. “He is a serious man when his hatred is stirred. I know many people who do worse things, but I would rather listen to them all a dozen times than to that Monks once. It is getting late, and I have to get home without anyone suspecting I was on an errand like this. I must return quickly.”

“But what can I do?” Rose asked. “How can I use this information without you? Go back! Why do you want to return to companions you describe in such terrible terms? If you repeat this information to a gentleman I can call in an instant from the next room, you could be taken to a place of safety in less than half an hour.”

“I want to go back,” the girl said. “I must go back, because—how can I tell such things to an innocent lady like you?”"
}
