Since he cannot, he will watch for him at every turn in life; if he uses what he knows about the boy’s birth and history, he might still harm him. ‘In short, Fagin,’ he said, ‘Jew as you are, you never set such traps as I will create for my young brother, Oliver.’”

“His brother!” exclaimed Rose.

“Those were his words,” Nancy said, glancing around nervously, as she had done almost constantly since she began speaking, for the fear of Sikes haunted her. “And there is more. When he spoke of you and the other lady, and said it seemed arranged by Heaven, or the devil, that Oliver should fall into your hands, he laughed. He said there was some comfort in that, too, for how many thousands of pounds would you not give, if you had them, to know who your little follower really is.”

“You do not mean,” said Rose, turning very pale, “to tell me that he was serious?”

“He spoke with hard, angry seriousness, if a man ever did,” the girl replied, shaking her head. “He is a serious man when his hatred is stirred. I know many people who do worse things, but I would rather listen to them all a dozen times than to that man Monks once. It is getting late, and I must reach home without anyone suspecting I was on such an errand. I must return quickly.”

“But what can I do?” said Rose. “How can I use this information without you? Return? Why do you wish to go back to companions you describe in such terrible ways? If you repeat this information to a gentleman I can call from the next room in an instant, you can be taken to a place of safety within half an hour.”

“I wish to go back,” said the girl. “I must go back, because—how can I explain such things to an innocent lady like you?”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_023",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“In what way?” the physician asked.

“Like this. It is evident that uncovering the truth behind this enigma will be immensely difficult unless we force this man, Monks, to submit. That requires cunning, and seizing him when he is away from his associates. If he were arrested now, we lack evidence to convict him. As far as we can tell, he is not involved in the gang’s criminal thefts. If he were not released, he would likely face no more than a short term for vagrancy, and thereafter he would remain so stubbornly silent that he would be useless to our investigation.”

“Then,” the doctor stated impatiently, “I ask you once more if you truly believe the pledge given to the girl remains obligatory; it was a promise made with noble motives, but really—”

“I beg you, do not debate that matter, my dear young lady,” Mr. Brownlow said, cutting off Rose as she prepared to reply. “The vow will be honored. I do not believe it will hinder our efforts in the least. However, before we decide on a specific plan, we must meet with the girl. We need to learn if she will identify Monks, provided we handle him privately rather than through the courts; or, if she refuses or is unable to do that, we must obtain a description of his appearance and his usual locations so we can track him. We cannot meet her until Sunday night, and today is Tuesday. I propose that we stay entirely inactive until then, keeping these details hidden even from Oliver.”

Though Mr. Losberne grimaced at the suggestion of waiting five full days, he had to concede that he had no superior alternative; since Rose and Mrs. Maylie both supported Mr. Brownlow, his plan was accepted by all.

“I would like,” he remarked, “to enlist the help of my associate, Grimwig. He is an eccentric fellow, yet quite sharp, and could be of significant help. He was trained as a lawyer but left the profession in frustration after handling only one brief and a routine motion in two decades, though you must decide if that is a positive recommendation.”

“I have no issue with you bringing in your friend, provided I may invite mine,” the doctor replied.

“We shall put it to a vote,” Mr. Brownlow answered, “who is he?”

“That lady’s son, and this young lady’s—very old friend,” the doctor said, gesturing toward Mrs. Maylie and finishing with a meaningful look at her niece.

Rose turned bright red, yet she voiced no protest to the suggestion, perhaps sensing she was outnumbered; consequently, Harry Maylie and Mr. Grimwig were joined to the group.

“We shall remain in the city, naturally,” Mrs. Maylie said, “as long as there is any hope of pursuing this investigation with a possibility of victory.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_023",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“How?” the doctor asked.

“Like this. It is obvious that we will have a very hard time uncovering the truth unless we can force this man, Monks, to surrender. That can only be achieved through strategy, by catching him when he is not surrounded by his associates. If he were arrested now, we have no evidence against him. As far as we know, he is not involved in any of the gang’s robberies. If he were not released, it is unlikely he would face any punishment beyond being jailed as a vagrant; and, naturally, he would then remain so tight-lipped that he might as well be deaf, dumb, blind, and mentally incapacitated for all the good he would do us.”

“Then,” the doctor said impulsively, “I ask you again: is it reasonable to consider this promise to the girl binding? It was made with the best and kindest intentions, but really—”

“Please, do not debate the point, my dear young lady,” Mr. Brownlow said, interrupting Rose as she began to speak. “The promise will be kept. I do not believe it will hinder our progress in the slightest. However, before we decide on a specific plan, we must see the girl. We need to determine if she will identify Monks, provided we handle him ourselves rather than involving the law. If she will not or cannot do that, we must get a description of his appearance and his usual haunts from her so we can track him down. She cannot be met until next Sunday night; today is Tuesday. I suggest that, in the meantime, we remain completely quiet and keep these matters secret, even from Oliver.”

Although Mr. Losberne made many sour faces at the suggestion of a five-day delay, he had to admit that no better plan occurred to him at the moment. Since both Rose and Mrs. Maylie strongly supported Mr. Brownlow, his proposal was accepted unanimously.

“I would like,” he said, “to enlist the help of my friend Grimwig. He is an odd character, but sharp, and he could be of significant help to us. He was trained as a lawyer and left the Bar in disgust because he only had one case and one routine motion in twenty years, though you must decide for yourselves if that is a recommendation.”

“I have no objection to you bringing in your friend, provided I may bring in mine,” the doctor said.

“We must put it to a vote,” Mr. Brownlow replied. “Who might he be?”

“That lady’s son, and this young lady’s very old friend,” the doctor said, gesturing toward Mrs. Maylie and finishing with a meaningful look at her niece.

Rose blushed deeply, but she did not voice any objection to the motion, perhaps feeling she was in a hopeless minority. Consequently, Harry Maylie and Mr. Grimwig were added to the group.

“We will stay in town, of course,” Mrs. Maylie said, “as long as there is the slightest chance of pursuing this investigation successfully.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_024",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“You aren’t suggesting he passed away?” Mr. Bolter exclaimed.

“No, no,” Fagin answered, “nothing quite that severe. Not nearly so bad.”

“Well, I assume he was—”

“Wanted,” Fagin interrupted. “Yes, he was sought after.”

“Is it a serious matter?” Mr. Bolter asked.

“Not particularly,” Fagin replied. “He was accused of trying to pick a pocket, and they discovered a silver snuff-box in his possession—his own, my dear fellow, his own, as he was quite fond of taking snuff himself. They postponed his hearing until today, suspecting they might identify the owner. Ah, he was worth fifty such boxes, and I would pay that much to see him returned. You really should have known the Dodger, my dear boy; you should have known him.”

“Well, I expect I shall meet him, don’t you think?” Mr. Bolter said.

“I have my doubts,” Fagin replied with a sigh. “If they fail to produce new evidence, it will likely be a minor sentence, and we will see him back in about six weeks; however, if they succeed, it means transportation. They are well aware of his cleverness; he will be sentenced for life. They will ensure the Artful Dodger receives nothing less than a life term.”

“What do you mean by transportation and a life term?” Mr. Bolter demanded. “What is the point of using such language with me? Why don’t you speak in a way I can comprehend?”

Fagin was preparing to explain these cryptic terms in plain language—which would have clarified that they referred to being sent away permanently—when the conversation was interrupted by the arrival of Master Bates, who entered with his hands in his pockets and a look of mock-sorrow on his face.

“It’s finished, Fagin,” Charley announced once he and the new acquaintance were introduced.

“What are you implying?”

“They have located the gentleman who owns the box; several others are arriving to identify him, and the Artful is definitely headed for exile,” Master Bates replied. “I require a full set of mourning clothes and a hatband to visit him before he begins his journey. To imagine Jack Dawkins—splendid Jack—the Dodger—the Artful Dodger—being sent away for a cheap, insignificant snuff-box! I never imagined he would be caught for anything less than a gold watch, chain, and seals, at the very least. Oh, why didn’t he rob some wealthy gentleman of all his valuables and depart like a gentleman, rather than a common thief without any honor or glory!”

After voicing his sympathy for his unfortunate friend, Master Bates slumped into the nearest chair, looking thoroughly dejected.

“Why do you claim he lacks honor or glory!” Fagin shouted, glaring at his student. “Wasn’t he always the most skilled among you? Is there any one of you who could match him or even approach his level of talent? Eh?”

“Not one,” Master Bates replied, his voice thick with sorrow; “not a single one.”

“Then why are you complaining?” Fagin snapped; “why are you weeping?”

“Because it won’t be on the official record, will it?” Charley retorted, goaded into defiance by his grief; “because it cannot be mentioned in the indictment; because no one will ever realize the extent of his abilities.”"
}

{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_024",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "“You don’t mean to say he died?” Mr. Bolter cried.

“No, no,” Fagin replied, “not that bad. Not quite that bad.”

“What, I suppose he was—”

“Wanted,” Fagin interrupted. “Yes, he was wanted.”

“Very seriously?” Mr. Bolter inquired.

“No,” Fagin replied, “not very. He was charged with attempted pickpocketing, and they found a silver snuff-box on him—his own, my dear, his own, for he took snuff himself and loved it. They remanded him until today because they thought they knew the owner. Ah, he was worth fifty boxes, and I’d pay the price of as many to have him back. You should have known the Dodger, my dear; you should have known the Dodger.”

“Well, but I will know him, I hope; don’t you think so?” Mr. Bolter said.

“I’m doubtful,” Fagin replied with a sigh. “If they don’t find new evidence, it will just be a summary conviction, and we’ll have him back in six weeks or so; but if they do, it’s a case of transportation. They know what a clever lad he is; he’ll get life. They’ll make the Artful nothing less than a lifer.”

“What do you mean by transportation and a lifer?” Mr. Bolter demanded. “What’s the point of talking like that to me? Why don’t you speak so I can understand you?”

Fagin was about to translate these mysterious terms into plain language—explaining that they meant “transportation for life”—when the conversation was cut short by the arrival of Master Bates, his hands in his pockets and his face twisted into a look of semi-comical misery.

“It’s all over, Fagin,” Charley said, once he and his new companion had been introduced.

“What do you mean?”

“They’ve found the gentleman who owns the box; two or three more are coming to identify him, and the Artful is booked for a trip overseas,” Master Bates replied. “I must have a full suit of mourning, Fagin, and a hatband, to visit him before he starts his travels. To think of Jack Dawkins—brilliant Jack—the Dodger—the Artful Dodger—going abroad for a common, cheap snuff-box! I never thought he’d do it for anything less than a gold watch, chain, and seals, at the very least. Oh, why didn’t he rob some rich old gentleman of all his valuables and go out like a gentleman, instead of a common thief without any honor or glory!”

With this expression of feeling for his unfortunate friend, Master Bates sat on the nearest chair, looking dejected and miserable.

“Why do you talk about him having no honor or glory!” Fagin exclaimed, shooting an angry look at his pupil. “Wasn’t he always the best among you all? Is there one of you who could touch him or come near him on any job? Eh?”

“Not one,” Master Bates replied, his voice husky with regret; “not one.”

“Then what are you talking about?” Fagin replied angrily; “what are you crying for?”

“Because it isn’t on the record, is it?” Charley said, provoked into total defiance of his mentor by his own regrets; “because it can’t come out in the indictment; because nobody will ever know half of what he was.”"
}

“Well, but I will know him, I hope; don’t you think so?” Mr. Bolter said.

“I’m doubtful,” Fagin replied with a sigh. “If they don’t find new evidence, it will just be a summary conviction, and we’ll have him back in six weeks or so; but if they do, it’s a case of transportation. They know what a clever lad he is; he’ll get life. They’ll make the Artful nothing less than a lifer.”

“What do you mean by transportation and a lifer?” Mr. Bolter demanded. “What’s the point of talking like that to me? Why don’t you speak so I can understand you?”

Fagin was about to translate these mysterious terms into plain language—explaining that they meant “transportation for life”—when the conversation was cut short by the arrival of Master Bates, his hands in his pockets and his face twisted into a look of semi-comical misery.

“It’s all over, Fagin,” Charley said, once he and his new companion had been introduced.

“What do you mean?”

“They’ve found the gentleman who owns the box; two or three more are coming to identify him, and the Artful is booked for a trip overseas,” Master Bates replied. “I must have a full suit of mourning, Fagin, and a hatband, to visit him before he starts his travels. To think of Jack Dawkins—brilliant Jack—the Dodger—the Artful Dodger—going abroad for a common, cheap snuff-box! I never thought he’d do it for anything less than a gold watch, chain, and seals, at the very least. Oh, why didn’t he rob some rich old gentleman of all his valuables and go out like a gentleman, instead of a common thief without any honor or glory!”

With this expression of feeling for his unfortunate friend, Master Bates sat on the nearest chair, looking dejected and miserable.

“Why do you talk about him having no honor or glory!” Fagin exclaimed, shooting an angry look at his pupil. “Wasn’t he always the best among you all? Is there one of you who could touch him or come near him on any job? Eh?”

“Not one,” Master Bates replied, his voice husky with regret; “not one.”

“Then what are you talking about?” Fagin replied angrily; “what are you crying for?”

“Because it isn’t on the record, is it?” Charley said, provoked into total defiance of his mentor by his own regrets; “because it can’t come out in the indictment; because nobody will ever know half of what he was.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_025",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "These reflections occupied Fagin while he sat by himself in the burglar’s quarters; keeping them at the forefront of his mind, he had seized the chance later to probe the girl with the vague suggestions he offered as they parted. She showed no sign of astonishment, nor did she pretend to miss his drift. The girl understood him perfectly. Her final look confirmed it. 

Yet she might shrink from a scheme to end Sikes’s life, which was a primary goal. “How,” Fagin pondered while slinking toward home, “might I strengthen my hold over her? What fresh leverage can I gain?”

Such minds are inventive. If he could set a spy to uncover the focus of her changed affections without forcing a confession, then threaten to expose the entire affair to Sikes—whom she feared deeply—unless she joined his plot, could he not force her to obey?

“I can,” Fagin muttered, nearly speaking the words. “She would not dare defy me then. Not for her life! I have it all figured out. The plan is prepared and shall be enacted. I will have you yet!”

He threw a sinister glance and a menacing gesture toward the place where he had left the more reckless criminal, then continued his journey, twisting his thin fingers into his ragged clothing and clutching it tightly, as if he were strangling a detested foe with every movement.

CHAPTER XLV.
NOAH CLAYPOLE IS EMPLOYED BY FAGIN ON A SECRET MISSION

The old man rose early the following morning, waiting restlessly for his new partner, who finally arrived after an agonizingly long wait and began to devour his breakfast ravenously.

“Bolter,” Fagin said, pulling a chair up to sit across from Morris Bolter.

“Well, I’m here,” Noah replied. “What is it? Don’t ask me to do anything until I’ve finished eating. That’s a major annoyance in this house. You never get enough time for your meals.”

“You are capable of talking while you eat, aren’t you?” Fagin asked, inwardly loathing his dear young associate’s gluttony.

“Oh, I can talk. I actually perform better when I talk,” Noah said, carving a massive piece of bread. “Where is Charlotte?”

“Away,” Fagin replied. “I sent her out this morning with the other young woman because I needed us to be alone.”

“Oh!” Noah remarked. “I wish you had told her to prepare some buttered toast first. Fine. Go ahead and talk. You won’t disturb me.”

There appeared to be little risk of anything distracting him, as he had clearly sat down with the intention of accomplishing a significant amount of eating.

“You performed well yesterday, my dear,” Fagin said. “Splendid! Six shillings and ninepence halfpenny on your very first day! The child-robbing business will make you wealthy.”

“Don’t forget to include three pint-pots and a milk-can,” Mr. Bolter reminded him.

“No, no, my dear. The pint-pots were brilliant strokes, but the milk-can was a true masterpiece.”

“Not bad, I suppose, for a novice,” Mr. Bolter observed with satisfaction."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_026",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "“Imagine that boy,” Fagin continued, “were to inform—to betray us all—first locating the proper authorities, then meeting them in public to sketch our faces, detail every identifying feature, and point out the hideout where we could be captured most simply. Suppose he did all this, and furthermore exposed a scheme we have all participated in, to some degree—entirely of his own volition; not captured, cornered, interrogated, or coerced by the clergyman through starvation, but by his own choice; sneaking out at night to seek those most opposed to us, and informing on us. Do you hear me?” the Jew shouted, his eyes burning with fury. “Suppose he did all this, what then?”

“What then!” Sikes answered, with a massive curse. “If he remained breathing until I arrived, I would crush his skull beneath my iron-heeled boot into as many fragments as there are strands of hair upon his head.”

“What if I were the one!” Fagin cried, nearly screaming. “I, who possess such knowledge, and could send so many to the gallows alongside myself!”

“I cannot say,” Sikes replied, gritting his teeth and turning pale at the mere thought. “I would commit some act in prison that would force them to shackle me; and if I were tried alongside you, I would attack you with those irons in open court, and bash your brains out before the crowd. I would possess such power,” the robber muttered, weighing his muscular arm, “that I could shatter your skull as if a heavy wagon had rolled over it.”

“You would?”

“Would I!” the burglar said. “Test me.”

“If it were Charley, or the Dodger, or Bet, or—”

“I do not care who,” Sikes replied irritably. “Whoever it was, I would treat them the same.”

Fagin stared intensely at the robber; then, signaling for silence, he leaned over the mattress on the floor and shook the sleeping figure to wake him. Sikes leaned forward in his seat, watching with his hands resting on his knees, seemingly puzzled as to where this questioning and setup would lead.

“Bolter, Bolter! Poor boy!” Fagin said, looking up with a look of wicked expectation, speaking deliberately and with distinct stress. “He is exhausted—exhausted from waiting for her so long,—waiting for her, Bill.”

“What do you mean?” Sikes asked, recoiling.

Fagin did not reply, but leaning over the sleeper once more, pulled him into a seated position. After his alias had been called several times, Noah rubbed his eyes, and, letting out a deep yawn, gazed drowsily around.

“Repeat that to me—once more, just so he can hear it,” the Jew said, gesturing toward Sikes as he spoke.

“Tell you what?” the groggy Noah asked, shaking himself irritably.

“That about—Nancy,” Fagin said, grabbing Sikes by the wrist, as if to stop him from departing the building before he had heard the full account. “You trailed her?”

“Yes.”

“To London Bridge?”

“Yes.”

“Where she encountered two individuals.”

“That she did.”

“A gentleman and a lady whom she had visited voluntarily before, who requested that she abandon all her associates, beginning with Monks, which she agreed to—and to describe him, which she did—and to reveal the house where we gather and frequent, which she did—and where it could be observed most effectively, which she did—and at what hour the people arrived there, which she did.”"
}

“What if I were the one!” Fagin cried, nearly screaming. “I, who possess such knowledge, and could send so many to the gallows alongside myself!”

“I cannot say,” Sikes replied, gritting his teeth and turning pale at the mere thought. “I would commit some act in prison that would force them to shackle me; and if I were tried alongside you, I would attack you with those irons in open court, and bash your brains out before the crowd. I would possess such power,” the robber muttered, weighing his muscular arm, “that I could shatter your skull as if a heavy wagon had rolled over it.”

“You would?”

“Would I!” the burglar said. “Test me.”

“If it were Charley, or the Dodger, or Bet, or—”

“I do not care who,” Sikes replied irritably. “Whoever it was, I would treat them the same.”

Fagin stared intensely at the robber; then, signaling for silence, he leaned over the mattress on the floor and shook the sleeping figure to wake him. Sikes leaned forward in his seat, watching with his hands resting on his knees, seemingly puzzled as to where this questioning and setup would lead.

“Bolter, Bolter! Poor boy!” Fagin said, looking up with a look of wicked expectation, speaking deliberately and with distinct stress. “He is exhausted—exhausted from waiting for her so long,—waiting for her, Bill.”

“What do you mean?” Sikes asked, recoiling.

Fagin did not reply, but leaning over the sleeper once more, pulled him into a seated position. After his alias had been called several times, Noah rubbed his eyes, and, letting out a deep yawn, gazed drowsily around.

“Repeat that to me—once more, just so he can hear it,” the Jew said, gesturing toward Sikes as he spoke.

“Tell you what?” the groggy Noah asked, shaking himself irritably.

“That about—Nancy,” Fagin said, grabbing Sikes by the wrist, as if to stop him from departing the building before he had heard the full account. “You trailed her?”

“Yes.”

“To London Bridge?”

“Yes.”

“Where she encountered two individuals.”

“That she did.”

“A gentleman and a lady whom she had visited voluntarily before, who requested that she abandon all her associates, beginning with Monks, which she agreed to—and to describe him, which she did—and to reveal the house where we gather and frequent, which she did—and where it could be observed most effectively, which she did—and at what hour the people arrived there, which she did.”"
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_027",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "Once the door swung open, a robust individual stepped from the carriage and took his place beside the steps, while a second man, who had been riding on the box, climbed down to stand on the opposite side. Following a gesture from Mr. Brownlow, they assisted a third man out, gripped him between them, and rushed him inside the residence. This individual was Monks.

They proceeded up the staircase in the same fashion, remaining silent, while Mr. Brownlow walked ahead to guide them into a rear chamber. At the entrance to this room, Monks, who had climbed the stairs with clear unwillingness, halted. The two men glanced toward the elderly gentleman as if awaiting his commands.

“He is aware of the choice,” Mr. Brownlow stated. “Should he waver or make any move other than what you direct, haul him out to the street, summon the police, and denounce him as a criminal in my name.”

“How can you speak of me in such a way?” Monks inquired.

“How can you force me to do so, young man?” Mr. Brownlow answered, meeting his gaze with a firm expression. “Are you foolish enough to attempt leaving this house? Release him. There, sir. You are at liberty to depart, and we are at liberty to pursue you. Yet I caution you, by everything I deem most grave and holy, that the very moment you do, I will have you arrested for fraud and theft. I am fixed and unyielding. If you choose to be the same, the consequences will rest entirely upon you!”

“By what right am I seized in the public street and dragged here by these curs?” Monks demanded, glancing between the two men standing at his sides.

“By my right,” Mr. Brownlow replied. “Those men are protected by me. If you object to being held against your will—you possessed the strength and the chance to escape while we traveled here, yet you chose to remain passive—I repeat, seek refuge in the law. I shall also turn to the law; but once you have proceeded too far to turn back, do not beg me for mercy, as the authority will have shifted to others; and do not claim that I pushed you into the abyss you chose to enter yourself.”

Monks appeared visibly unsettled and frightened. He paused.

“You must choose immediately,” Mr. Brownlow declared with absolute resolve and calm. “If you desire for me to bring my accusations in public and hand you over to a penalty whose severity I can foresee with dread but cannot alter, then once again, I say, you know the path. If not, and you seek my indulgence, along with the mercy of those you have gravely harmed, take your seat in that chair without speaking. It has been prepared for you for two full days.”

Monks mumbled some indistinct phrases but continued to waver.

“Be quick,” Mr. Brownlow urged. “One word from me, and the other option is lost forever.”

Still, the man lingered.

“I have no desire to negotiate,” Mr. Brownlow said, “and, as I am representing the vital interests of others, I have no authority to do so.”

“Is there—” Monks asked with a trembling voice,—“is there—no alternative?”

“None.”

Monks studied the old man with a worried expression; yet, finding only sternness and resolve in his face, he stepped into the room and, with a shrug of his shoulders, took his seat."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_027",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "The door opened, and a sturdy man stepped out of the carriage, positioning himself on one side of the steps, while another man, who had been sitting on the driver’s box, climbed down and stood on the other side. At a signal from Mr. Brownlow, they helped a third man out, and, holding him between them, hurried him into the house. This man was Monks.

They walked up the stairs in the same formation without speaking, and Mr. Brownlow, leading the way, brought them into a back room. At the door of this room, Monks, who had climbed the stairs with clear reluctance, stopped. The two men looked at the old gentleman as if waiting for instructions.

“He knows the alternative,” Mr. Brownlow said. “If he hesitates or moves a finger except as you command, drag him into the street, call for the police, and charge him with a felony in my name.”

“How dare you say this to me?” Monks asked.

“How dare you force me to it, young man?” Mr. Brownlow replied, meeting his gaze steadily. “Are you foolish enough to try and leave this house? Let him go. There, sir. You are free to leave, and we are free to follow. But I warn you, by everything I hold most solemn and sacred, that the moment you do, you will be arrested on charges of fraud and robbery. I am resolute and immovable. If you are determined to be the same, your fate will be on your own head!”

“By what authority am I kidnapped in the street and brought here by these thugs?” Monks asked, looking from one man to the other.

“By mine,” Mr. Brownlow replied. “These men are protected by me. If you complain about being deprived of your liberty—you had the power and opportunity to reclaim it on the way here, but you decided it was wiser to stay quiet—I say again, seek protection from the law. I will appeal to the law as well; but once you have gone too far to turn back, do not beg me for leniency when the power is no longer in my hands, and do not say I pushed you into the abyss you chose to rush into yourself.”

Monks was clearly rattled and alarmed. He hesitated.

“You must decide quickly,” Mr. Brownlow said with perfect firmness and composure. “If you want me to bring my charges publicly and consign you to a punishment that I can foresee with a shudder but cannot control, then once again, I say, you know the way out. If not, and you appeal to my forbearance and the mercy of those you have deeply injured, sit down in that chair without a word. It has been waiting for you for two whole days.”

Monks muttered something unintelligible, but still wavered.

“Be quick,” Mr. Brownlow said. “One word from me, and the alternative is gone forever.”

Still, the man hesitated.

“I have no desire to negotiate,” Mr. Brownlow said, “and, as I am representing the vital interests of others, I have no right to.”

“Is there—” Monks asked with a faltering voice—“is there no middle ground?”

“None.”

Monks looked at the old gentleman with an anxious eye, but seeing nothing in his expression but severity and determination, he walked into the room, shrugged his shoulders, and sat down."
}

“Is there—” Monks asked with a trembling voice—“is there no middle ground?”

“None.”

Monks looked at the old gentleman with an anxious eye, but seeing nothing in his face but severity and determination, he walked into the room, shrugged his shoulders, and sat down."

parts.text:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_027",
  "condition": "simplify",
  "rewritten_text": "The door opened, and a sturdy man stepped out of the coach to stand on one side of the steps, while another man, who had been sitting on the driver's box, climbed down and stood on the other side. Following a signal from Mr. Brownlow, they helped a third man out, took him between them, and hurried him into the house. This man was Monks.

They walked up the stairs in the same way without speaking, with Mr. Brownlow leading them into a back room. At the door of this room, Monks, who had climbed the stairs clearly against his will, stopped. The two men looked at the old gentleman as if waiting for instructions.

“He knows the alternative,” Mr. Brownlow said. “If he hesitates or moves a finger except as you tell him, drag him into the street, call for the police, and accuse him of being a criminal in my name.”

“How dare you say this about me?” Monks asked.

“How dare you force me to it, young man?” Mr. Brownlow replied, meeting his gaze steadily. “Are you foolish enough to try to leave this house? Let him go. There, sir. You are free to leave, and we are free to follow. But I warn you, by everything I hold most solemn and sacred, that the moment you do, I will have you arrested for fraud and robbery. I am firm and unmoving. If you are determined to be the same, the consequences will be on your own head!”

“By what authority was I kidnapped in the street and brought here by these dogs?” Monks asked, looking from one man to the other.

“By mine,” Mr. Brownlow replied. “These men are protected by me. If you complain about losing your freedom—you had the power and opportunity to regain it on the way here, but you decided it was better to stay quiet—I say again, go ahead and seek protection from the law. I will appeal to the law as well; but once you have gone too far to turn back, do not beg me for mercy when the power is no longer in my hands, and do not say that I pushed you into the disaster you brought upon yourself.”

Monks was clearly unsettled and alarmed. He hesitated.

“You must decide quickly,” Mr. Brownlow said with perfect firmness and composure. “If you want me to bring my charges publicly and send you to a punishment that I can foresee with a shudder but cannot control, then once again, I say, you know the way out. If not, and you want to appeal to my patience and the mercy of those you have deeply injured, sit down in that chair without a word. It has been waiting for you for two whole days.”

Monks muttered some unclear words, but he still wavered.

“Be quick,” Mr. Brownlow said. “One word from me, and the choice is gone forever.”

Still, the man hesitated.

“I have no desire to argue,” Mr. Brownlow said, “and since I am defending the vital interests of others, I do not have the right to.”

“Is there—” Monks asked with a trembling voice—“is there no middle ground?”

“None.”

Monks looked at the old gentleman with an anxious eye, but seeing nothing in his face but severity and determination, he walked into the room, shrugged his shoulders, and sat down."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_028",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "This conclusion, seeming the most likely, was accepted as correct; the animal, slinking beneath a seat, curled into a ball to slumber, ignored by everyone else. 

Darkness having fallen, the window covering was fastened, and a lamp was ignited and set upon the surface. The horrific occurrences of the previous forty-eight hours had left a profound mark on the trio, intensified by the peril and ambiguity of their current circumstances. They pulled their seats into a tighter circle, jumping at every noise. They conversed infrequently, and only in hushed tones, remaining as quiet and terrified as if the corpse of the slain female were situated in the adjacent chamber. 

They had been positioned in this manner for a while when a rapid rapping sounded at the entrance below. 

“Young Bates,” remarked Kags, glancing around with irritation to suppress the alarm he personally experienced. 

The rapping repeated. No, it was not him. He never rapped in that fashion. 

Crackit approached the window, and trembling violently, pulled his head back inside. There was no requirement to inform them of his identity; his pallid complexion sufficed. The canine was also instantly vigilant, running toward the entrance while whimpering. 

“We are required to admit him,” he stated, grasping the lamp. 

“Is there no alternative?” inquired the other male in a raspy tone. 

“None. He must be admitted.” 

“Do not abandon us in the gloom,” requested Kags, retrieving a lamp from the mantel and igniting it with a hand so unsteady that the rapping occurred twice more before he concluded. 

Crackit descended to the entrance and came back accompanied by a male whose lower features were concealed by a cloth, with another wrapped around his head beneath his headgear. He removed them gradually. A colorless visage, recessed sockets, gaunt cheeks, three days of stubble, withered skin, and labored, shallow respiration; it was the literal specter of Sikes. 

He placed a hand upon a seat positioned in the center of the room, but shivering as he prepared to collapse into it, and appearing to peer over his shoulder, he pulled it back against the partition—as near as it could go—and shoved it firmly against it before sitting. 

Not a syllable had been uttered. He gazed from one to another without speaking. If a glance was surreptitiously lifted and encountered his, it was immediately cast down. When his cavernous voice interrupted the quiet, all three jumped. They appeared to have never encountered its quality previously. 

“How did that animal arrive here?” he questioned. 

“By itself. Three hours prior.” 

“The evening news reports that Fagin has been captured. Is that factual, or a falsehood?” 

“Factual.” 

They returned to silence. 

“Curse you all!” exclaimed Sikes, wiping his brow. “Do you possess nothing to say to me?” 

There was a restless shifting among them, yet no one uttered a word. 

“You who manage this residence,” Sikes stated, directing his gaze toward Crackit, “do you intend to betray me, or permit me to remain here until this pursuit concludes?” 

“You are welcome to remain here, if you deem it secure,” replied the individual addressed, following a brief pause. 

Sikes moved his eyes gradually up the partition behind him—attempting to rotate his head rather than actually doing so—and asked, “Is—it—the corpse—has it been interred?” 

They gestured negatively."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_028",
  "condition": "modernize",
  "rewritten_text": "This explanation seemed the most likely, so they accepted it as fact. The dog crawled under a chair and curled up to sleep, ignored by everyone else.

Since it was now dark, they closed the shutters, lit a candle, and set it on the table. The horrific events of the past two days had left a deep mark on all three, made worse by the danger and uncertainty of their own situation. They pulled their chairs closer together, jumping at every noise. They spoke very little, and only in whispers, feeling as silent and terrified as if the murdered woman’s body were in the next room.

They had been sitting like this for some time when suddenly there was a frantic knocking at the door downstairs.

“Young Bates,” Kags said, glancing around aggressively to hide his own fear.

The knocking came again. No, it wasn’t him. He never knocked like that.

Crackit went to the window and, shaking uncontrollably, pulled his head back inside. There was no need to tell them who it was; his pale face said it all. The dog was instantly alert, running to the door and whining.

“We have to let him in,” he said, picking up the candle.

“Is there no other way?” the other man asked in a raspy voice.

“None. He has to come in.”

“Don’t leave us in the dark,” Kags said, taking a candle from the mantelpiece and lighting it with such a trembling hand that the knocking repeated twice before he finished.

Crackit went down to the door and returned followed by a man with the lower half of his face hidden by a handkerchief and another tied over his head beneath his hat. He slowly pulled them off. A blanched face, sunken eyes, hollow cheeks, three days of beard growth, wasted flesh, and short, heavy breaths; it was the very ghost of Sikes.

He placed his hand on a chair in the middle of the room, but as he was about to collapse into it, he shuddered and, seemingly glancing over his shoulder, dragged it back against the wall—as close as it would go—and sat down.

Not a word was exchanged. He looked from one to the other in silence. If anyone caught his eye, they immediately looked away. When his hollow voice finally broke the silence, all three of them jumped. It sounded like a voice they had never heard before.

“How did that dog get here?” he asked.

“Alone. Three hours ago.”

“Tonight’s paper says Fagin’s been caught. Is it true, or a lie?”

“True.”

They fell silent again.

“Damn you all!” Sikes said, wiping his hand across his forehead. “Do you have nothing to say to me?”

There was an uneasy shift among them, but no one spoke.

“You, the one who runs this house,” Sikes said, turning toward Crackit, “do you intend to sell me out, or will you let me hide here until this manhunt is over?”

“You can stay here, if you think it’s safe,” the man replied after a moment of hesitation.

Sikes slowly moved his eyes up the wall behind him, trying to turn his head without actually doing so, and asked, “Is—it—the body—is it buried?”

They shook their heads."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_029",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "He claimed he acted this way to demonstrate his trust in the mother, and his belief—only made firmer by his nearing end—that the infant would inherit her kind spirit and fine character. Should this hope prove misplaced, the funds were to be yours: for only then, when both children stood on equal footing, would he acknowledge your earlier right to his wealth, as you held no place in his affections, having repelled him with icy disdain since you were a baby.

“My mother,” Monks declared, raising his voice, “acted as a woman ought. She destroyed that testament. The message never arrived; yet she preserved it, along with other evidence, in case they ever attempted to conceal the stain. The girl’s father learned the facts from her, delivered with all the intensity that her fierce resentment—which I now admire—could muster. Driven by disgrace and humiliation, he escaped with his children to a secluded part of Wales, altering his surname so his acquaintances would never discover his hiding place; and there, not long after, he was discovered deceased in his bed. The girl had departed her home, secretly, some weeks prior; he had looked for her, walking to every nearby settlement; it was the evening he arrived home, convinced she had taken her own life to conceal her shame and his, that his weary heart gave out.”

There was a brief pause, until Mr. Brownlow resumed the account.

“Years later,” he stated, “the mother of this man—Edward Leeford—approached me. He had abandoned her at eighteen; stolen her valuables and cash; gambled, wasted, committed forgery, and escaped to London: where for two years he lived among the most wretched criminals. She was failing from a severe and terminal ailment, and hoped to reclaim him before her passing. Investigations were initiated, and thorough searches conducted. They proved fruitless for a long time, but eventually succeeded; and he returned with her to France.”

“She passed away there,” Monks added, “after a protracted sickness; and, on her deathbed, she entrusted these mysteries to me, along with her eternal and lethal loathing for everyone connected to them—though she did not need to bequeath that, as I had possessed it long before. She refused to accept that the girl had killed herself, and the infant as well, but remained convinced that a boy had been born and survived. I promised her, if he ever crossed my path, to track him down; to never let him find peace; to chase him with the most severe and unforgiving hostility; to unleash upon him the hatred I harbored, and to mock the hollow boast of that offensive will by dragging him, if possible, to the very foot of the gallows. She was correct. He finally crossed my path. I started effectively; and, were it not for chattering harlots, I would have concluded as I began!”

As the scoundrel crossed his arms firmly and whispered oaths against himself in the helplessness of his thwarted spite, Mr. Brownlow addressed the frightened group nearby, clarifying that the Jew, who had been his former partner and confidant, was promised a substantial payment for keeping Oliver trapped: a portion of which was to be surrendered, should he be saved: and that a disagreement regarding this matter had prompted their arrival at the country estate to confirm his identity."
}

If he were disappointed in this hope, then the money was to go to you; for only then, when both children were equal, would he acknowledge your prior claim on his wealth, as you had none on his heart, having repulsed him with coldness and aversion since you were an infant.

“My mother,” Monks said, in a louder voice, “did what a woman should have done. She burned this will. The letter never reached its destination, but she kept that and other proofs in case they ever tried to lie about the scandal. The girl’s father learned the truth from her, told with every bit of the violent hatred—I love her for it now—that she could add. Driven by shame and dishonor, he fled with his children to a remote corner of Wales, changing his name so his friends would never find him. Not long after, he was found dead in his bed. The girl had left home in secret weeks before; he had searched for her on foot in every nearby town and village. It was the night he returned home, convinced she had killed herself to hide her shame and his, that his heart finally gave out.”

There was a brief silence until Mr. Brownlow resumed the story.

“Years later,” he said, “this man’s mother—Edward Leeford’s mother—came to me. He had left her at eighteen, stolen her jewels and money, gambled, squandered, forged, and fled to London, where he spent two years among the lowest outcasts. She was dying of a painful, incurable disease and wanted to find him before the end. Inquiries were made and searches conducted. They were unsuccessful for a long time but eventually succeeded, and he returned with her to France.”

“She died there,” Monks said, “after a long illness. On her deathbed, she left these secrets to me, along with her unquenchable, deadly hatred for everyone involved—though she didn’t need to leave me that, as I had inherited it long before. She refused to believe the girl and the child had killed themselves, convinced instead that a boy had been born and was still alive. I swore to her that if he ever crossed my path, I would hunt him down, never let him rest, pursue him with the most relentless animosity, vent my deep-seated hatred upon him, and spit on that insulting will by dragging him, if I could, to the gallows. She was right. He finally crossed my path. I started well, and if not for some babbling women, I would have finished as I began!”

As the villain folded his arms tightly and muttered curses at his own failed malice, Mr. Brownlow turned to the terrified group, explaining that the Jew, who had been his old accomplice and confidant, had been promised a large reward for keeping Oliver trapped. A portion of this was to be paid if the boy were rescued, and a dispute over this money had led to their visit to the country house to identify him."
}

  "rewritten_text": "He did this, he explained, to show his trust in the mother, and his conviction—only made stronger by his approaching death—that the child would inherit her gentle heart and noble nature. If he were disappointed in this hope, then the money was to go to you; for only then, when both children were equal, would he acknowledge your prior claim on his wealth, as you had none on his heart, having repulsed him with coldness and aversion since you were an infant.

“My mother,” Monks said, in a louder voice, “did what a woman should have done. She burned this will. The letter never reached its destination, but she kept that and other proofs in case they ever tried to lie about the scandal. The girl’s father learned the truth from her, told with every bit of the violent hatred—I love her for it now—that she could add. Driven by shame and dishonor, he fled with his children to a remote corner of Wales, changing his name so his friends would never find him. Not long after, he was found dead in his bed. The girl had left home in secret weeks before; he had searched for her on foot in every nearby town and village. It was the night he returned home, convinced she had killed herself to hide her shame and his, that his heart finally gave out.”

There was a brief silence until Mr. Brownlow resumed the story.

“Years later,” he said, “this man’s mother—Edward Leeford’s mother—came to me. He had left her at eighteen, stolen her jewels and money, gambled, squandered, forged, and fled to London, where he spent two years among the lowest outcasts. She was dying of a painful, incurable disease and wanted to find him before the end. Inquiries were made and searches conducted. They were unsuccessful for a long time but eventually succeeded, and he returned with her to France.”

“She died there,” Monks said, “after a long illness. On her deathbed, she left these secrets to me, along with her unquenchable, deadly hatred for everyone involved—though she didn’t need to leave me that, as I had inherited it long before. She refused to believe the girl and the child had killed themselves, convinced instead that a boy had been born and was still alive. I swore to her that if he ever crossed my path, I would hunt him down, never let him rest, pursue him with the most relentless animosity, vent my deep-seated hatred upon him, and spit on that insulting will by dragging him, if I could, to the gallows. She was right. He finally crossed my path. I started well, and if not for some babbling women, I would have finished as I began!”

As the villain folded his arms tightly and muttered curses at his own failed malice, Mr. Brownlow turned to the terrified group, explaining that the Jew, who had been his old accomplice and confidant, had been promised a large reward for keeping Oliver trapped. A portion of this was to be paid if the boy were rescued, and a dispute over this money had led to their visit to the country house to identify him."
}

model_content:
{
  "passage_id": "DICKENS_OLIVER_TWIST_030",
  "condition": "paraphrase",
  "rewritten_text": "These brief remarks were exchanged privately, remaining unheard by Oliver. The man tipped his hat, cast a curious look at the boy, and unlatched a gate across from their entrance, guiding them through dim, twisting paths toward the prison cells.

“Here,” the man stated, pausing in a somber corridor where two laborers worked in absolute silence, “is the route he takes. If you move this way, you can observe the exit he uses.”

He directed them into a stone kitchen equipped with large vats for preparing prison meals, gesturing toward a door. Above it, an open grate allowed the sounds of men’s voices, hammering, and the clatter of lumber to filter through. They were constructing the gallows.

From there, they traversed several heavy gates, unbolted by other guards from within. Reaching an open courtyard, they climbed a set of cramped stairs and entered a hallway lined with sturdy doors on the left. Signaling for them to wait, the turnkey rapped on one door with his key ring. After a brief exchange of whispers, the two guards emerged, stretching as if relieved by the short break, and gestured for the visitors to accompany the jailer inside. They complied.

The prisoner sat on his cot, swaying back and forth, his features resembling a trapped animal more than a human being. His thoughts clearly drifted to his past, as he kept muttering, seemingly unaware of their presence except as figures in his delirium.

“Good lad, Charley—well done,” he mumbled. “Oliver, too, ha! ha! ha! Oliver as well—quite the gentleman now—quite the—send that boy off to bed!”

The jailer grasped Oliver’s free hand, murmuring for him not to be frightened, and watched in silence.

“Take him to bed!” Fagin shouted. “Do you hear me, any of you? He has been the—the—somehow the reason for all this. It’s worth the price to bring him to it—Bolter’s throat, Bill; forget the girl—cut Bolter’s throat as deep as you can. Saw his head off!”

“Fagin,” the jailer said.

“That’s me!” the Jew exclaimed, immediately adopting the attentive posture he had held during his trial. “An old man, my Lord; a very old, old man!”

“Here,” the turnkey said, pressing a hand to his chest to restrain him. “Someone is here to see you, I imagine to ask you a few things. Fagin, Fagin! Are you a man?”

“I won’t be one for much longer,” he answered, glancing up with a face devoid of anything but fury and fear. “Strike them all dead! What authority do they have to slaughter me?”

As he spoke, he spotted Oliver and Mr. Brownlow. Cowering into the farthest corner of his seat, he demanded to know their business.

“Calm yourself,” the turnkey said, keeping him pinned. “Now, sir, state your business. Quickly, if you would, for he deteriorates as the hour approaches.”"
}

“This,” the man said, stopping in a gloomy hallway where two workmen were silently preparing something, “is the route he takes. If you step this way, you can see the door he exits through.”

He guided them into a stone kitchen, equipped with large vats for cooking prison food, and pointed to a door. Above it was an open grate, through which came the sound of men’s voices mixed with the noise of hammering and boards being dropped. They were building the scaffold.

From there, they passed through several heavy gates, opened by other guards from the inside. After entering an open yard, they climbed a flight of narrow stairs and reached a hallway with a row of heavy doors on the left. Signaling them to wait, the guard knocked on one of the doors with his keys. After a brief whisper, the two guards inside stepped into the hallway, stretching as if relieved by the break, and gestured for the visitors to follow the jailer into the cell. They complied.

The condemned prisoner sat on his bed, rocking back and forth, his face looking more like a trapped animal’s than a man’s. His mind was clearly drifting to his past, as he kept muttering, seemingly unaware of their presence except as part of his hallucinations.

“Good boy, Charley—well done,” he mumbled. “Oliver, too, ha! ha! ha! Oliver too—quite the gentleman now—quite the—take that boy away to bed!”

The jailer took Oliver’s free hand, whispered for him not to be afraid, and watched silently.

“Take him away to bed!” Fagin shouted. “Do you hear me? He has been the—the—somehow the cause of all this. It’s worth the money to bring him up to it—Bolter’s throat, Bill; never mind the girl—cut Bolter’s throat as deep as you can. Saw his head off!”

“Fagin,” the jailer said.

“That’s me!” the old man cried, instantly adopting the listening posture he had used during his trial. “An old man, my Lord; a very old, old man!”

“Here,” the guard said, pressing his hand against Fagin’s chest to hold him down. “Someone is here to see you, I suppose to ask you some questions. Fagin, Fagin! Are you a man?”

“I won’t be one for much longer,” he replied, looking up with a face devoid of any human expression except rage and terror. “Strike them all dead! What right do they have to butcher me?”

As he spoke, he spotted Oliver and Mr. Brownlow. Shrinking into the furthest corner of his seat, he demanded to know what they wanted.

“Steady,” the guard said, still holding him down. “Now, sir, tell him what you need. Be quick, please, because he is getting worse as the time draws near.”"
}
